<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calorific Value - HCV and LCV - Experimental Determination of Calorific Value of Solid Fuels. Analysis of Coal - Proximate Analysis - Octane & Cetane Number. Biofuels - Biodiesel - Green Hydrogen. Lubricants: Classification - Solid

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Calorific Value (CV) — The Energy Currency of Fuels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calorific Value (CV)** of a fuel is defined as the total amount of heat liberated when **1 kg** of the fuel is **completely burnt** in excess of oxygen (or air), with the products of combustion cooled back to **room temperature (25 °C / 298 K)**.

**Units (as per KTU/NCERT standards):**
- SI Unit: **kJ/kg** or **J/g**
- CGS Unit: **cal/g** or **kcal/kg**
- Industrial Unit: **BTU/lb** (British Thermal Unit per pound)

**Two Varieties of Calorific Value:**

| Type | Synonym | What Happens to Water Vapour? |
|------|---------|-------------------------------|
| **HCV (Higher Calorific Value)** | Gross Calorific Value (GCV) | Water vapour is **condensed** to liquid |
| **LCV (Lower Calorific Value)** | Net Calorific Value (NCV) | Water vapour is **allowed to escape** as steam |

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Hot Cup of Tea" Model

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine boiling water in a kettle. The heat supplied boils the water, but the steam that escapes out of the spout still carries enormous **latent heat** that you can never recover in the kettle.
>
> - **HCV = Total heat** released *including* the latent heat trapped when steam re-condenses to water.
> - **LCV = Usable heat** after the steam escapes un-condensed.
>
> In a real boiler, exhaust gases leave hot (~150–250 °C). The water vapour inside them never condenses. So engineers actually **get LCV in practice**, not HCV. However, HCV is the universal *scientific benchmark* used to compare fuels.

### The Master Relationship

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + \text{Latent heat of condensation of water vapour formed}}
$$

The empirical engineering form (Dulong's rule):

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + 0.09\,H \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}
$$

where:
- **H** = mass percentage of hydrogen in the fuel
- **587 cal/g** = latent heat of condensation of steam at **25 °C, 1 atm**
- **0.09** = mass of water (in g) produced per gram of H₂ (because 2 g H₂ → 18 g H₂O, i.e., 9 g H₂O per 1 g H)

**More universal SI form:**

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + \frac{9\,H}{100} \times 2442 \;\;\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

where **2442 kJ/kg** is the latent heat of steam at 25 °C.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "Hydrogen = Hidden Heat"**: Every 1% H in the fuel adds ≈ **22 kJ/kg** to the gap between HCV and LCV. Methane (25% H) has a huge gap (~530 kJ/mol), while carbon (0% H) has *zero* gap.

---

## 1.2 Proximate Analysis of Coal — The "Coal Fingerprint"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proximate analysis** is an empirical laboratory procedure that determines the four operational/physical constituents of a coal sample — *moisture*, *volatile matter*, *ash*, and *fixed carbon*. It does **not** tell you the chemical elements (that's **ultimate analysis**).

| Component | Symbol | What It Means | Range in Bituminous Coal |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Moisture** | M | Inherent water content | 2–10% |
| **Volatile Matter** | VM | Combustible gases released on heating (out of contact with air) | 20–35% |
| **Ash** | A | Non-combustible mineral residue | 5–20% |
| **Fixed Carbon** | FC | Solid combustible carbon left after volatiles escape | 50–70% |

**Constraint Equation (always holds):**

$$
M + \text{VM} + A + \text{FC} = 100\,\%
$$

---

## 1.3 Octane and Cetane Numbers — The "Knock & Ignition" Twins

> [!NOTE]
> **Octane Number (ON)** = resistance to **knocking** in a *spark-ignition* (petrol/Gasoline) engine.
> **Cetane Number (CN)** = ease of *self-ignition* in a *compression-ignition* (diesel) engine.

| Property | Octane Number | Cetane Number |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Reference High** | Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) → **100** | n-Cetane (n-hexadecane) → **100** |
| **Reference Low** | n-Heptane → **0** | α-methyl naphthalene → **0** |
| **Knock Behaviour** | High ON = Less knock | High CN = Less knock (fast ignition) |
| **Required For** | Petrol engines (cars, bikes) | Diesel engines (trucks, locomotives) |
| **Typical Value** | Petrol: 87–98 | Diesel: 45–55 |

---

## 1.4 Biofuels — Biodiesel & Green Hydrogen

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biofuel** = fuel derived directly from **recently living biomass**, in contrast to fossil fuels (formed over millions of years).

### Biodiesel
- **Definition:** Mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids (FAME) produced by the **transesterification** of vegetable oils or animal fats with methanol/ethanol in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH / KOH).
- **Feedstocks:** Soybean oil, jatropha, palm oil, used cooking oil, algal oil.
- **Common Form:** Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME).

### Green Hydrogen
- **Definition:** Hydrogen gas (H₂) produced by the **electrolysis of water** using electricity generated entirely from **renewable sources** (solar, wind, hydro, tidal).
- **Key Reaction:** 
$$
2\,H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{Renewable electricity}} 2\,H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}
$$
- **Emission:** Zero CO₂ during production and combustion.

---

## 1.5 Lubricants — Solid Lubricants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid lubricants** are materials applied in solid/powder form between two moving surfaces to reduce friction and wear where liquid/grease lubricants fail (extreme temperatures, heavy loads, vacuum).

**Three Big Categories:**

| Type | Examples | Mechanism |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Layer-lattice solids** | Graphite, MoS₂ | Weak interlayer bonds → easy shear |
| **Metallic soaps / films** | Lead, tin, indium | Soft, low melting → smear |
| **Composite / polymeric** | PTFE (Teflon), nylon | Self-lubricating polymer chains |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamics Behind HCV and LCV

### Why Two Calorific Values?

When a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ combusts in oxygen:

$$
C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O_{(l)} \;\; (\text{HCV basis})
$$

or 

$$
C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O_{(g)} \;\; (\text{LCV basis})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **only** chemical difference between the two combustion reactions is the **physical state of water** — *liquid* (HCV) or *vapour* (LCV). The energy difference is exactly the **enthalpy of vaporization of that water** at 25 °C.

### Derivation of the 9H Rule (Numerical Form)

**Step 1.** For every 2 g of H atoms burned, 18 g of H₂O is produced.
**Step 2.** Therefore, for every 1 g of H, mass of water = 18/2 = **9 g**.
**Step 3.** Energy locked per gram of water = latent heat of vaporization = **587 cal/g** (at 25 °C).
**Step 4.** Per gram of H → 9 × 587 = **5283 cal/g of H** in the fuel.
**Step 5.** If fuel contains *H*% of H (by mass), the energy gap per gram of fuel is:

$$
\Delta = \frac{H}{100} \times 5283 = 0.09\,H \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}
$$

Hence,

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + 0.09\,H \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}
$$

In SI units (latent heat = 2442 kJ/kg):

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + \frac{9\,H}{100} \times 2442 \;\;\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

---

## 2.2 Bomb Calorimeter — The Engineering Workhorse

The **Bomb Calorimeter** is the standard ISO 1716 / ASTM D5865 apparatus for measuring **HCV of solid and liquid fuels** at constant volume.

**Construction (key parts):**

1. **Bomb (sealed steel vessel):** Holds the fuel + O₂ at ~25–30 atm.
2. **Crucible:** Thin metal cup holding the fuel pellet.
3. **Ignition wires:** Connected to a 6 V/12 V supply; ignite the fuse wire.
4. **Water jacket:** Surrounds the bomb; absorbs the heat released.
5. **Thermometer / RTD sensor:** Reads temperature rise to ±0.001 °C.
6. **Stirrer:** Ensures uniform temperature.

**Working Principle (Constant-Volume Combustion):**
The reaction inside the sealed bomb is at **constant volume**, so the heat measured equals the **internal energy change (ΔU)** of combustion, which is *very close* to the **enthalpy change (ΔH)** for solids/liquids (because Δ(pV) ≈ 0).

---

## 2.3 Proximate Analysis — Step-by-Step Laboratory Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Proximate analysis** reports four quantities — each obtained by a **separate thermal experiment** on a finely powdered (212 µm / 72-mesh) air-dried coal sample.

| Step | Determination | Procedure | Reported As |
|------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **1. Moisture (M)** | Heat 1 g coal in air oven at **105–110 °C for 1 h** | Loss in mass | % M |
| **2. Volatile Matter (VM)** | Heat dried coal in covered crucible at **925 ± 25 °C for 7 min** | Loss in mass − M | % VM |
| **3. Ash (A)** | Burn VM-free residue in muffle furnace at **700 ± 50 °C till constant mass** | Mass of residue | % A |
| **4. Fixed Carbon (FC)** | By difference | 100 − (M + VM + A) | % FC |

**Calculation Formulae:**

$$
\% M = \frac{m_1 - m_2}{m} \times 100
$$

$$
\% VM = \frac{m_2 - m_3}{m} \times 100
$$

$$
\% A = \frac{m_4}{m} \times 100
$$

$$
\% FC = 100 - (\%M + \%VM + \%A)
$$

Where:
- *m* = mass of air-dried coal taken (g)
- *m₁* = mass before oven drying
- *m₂* = mass after drying (moisture removed)
- *m₃* = mass after 925 °C heating (VM removed)
- *m₄* = mass of ash after muffle furnace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is FC calculated by difference and not by direct weighing?** Because fixed carbon cannot be isolated cleanly from ash at intermediate temperatures — burning it off to ash is the only reliable way.

### Significance in Industry

- **High VM** → easy ignition, good for domestic/thermal uses.
- **High FC** → high calorific value, coke-forming tendency.
- **High Ash** → abrasive, lowers boiler efficiency, clinkering.
- **High Moisture** → wasted transport cost, reduces CV.

**Coal Ranking (Fuel Ratio = FC/VM):**

| Coal Type | FC/VM Ratio | Use |
|-----------|-------------|-----|
| Peat | < 0.5 | Garden compost |
| Lignite | 0.5–1.0 | Power generation |
| Bituminous | 1.0–1.7 | Coking, industrial |
| Anthracite | > 10 | Domestic heating, metallurgy |

---

## 2.4 Octane Number — Extended Working

### Knocking Phenomenon

In petrol engines, the air-fuel mixture should ignite **smoothly at the spark plug**. If it **self-ignites** before the spark (due to high temperature/pressure of compression), you get a **metallic "knock"** that damages the engine.

### Reference Scale

- **Iso-octane** (branched, 8-carbon) burns smoothly → assigned **ON = 100**.
- **n-Heptane** (straight, 7-carbon) knocks violently → assigned **ON = 0**.
- A fuel with **ON = 90** knocks exactly like a mixture of 90% iso-octane + 10% n-heptane.

### Improving Octane Number

1. **Cracking** (break long chains → branched isomers).
2. **Reforming** (naphthenes → aromatics).
3. **Alkylation** (combine small olefins + isobutane → highly branched).
4. **Anti-knock additives** — e.g., **TEL** (tetraethyl lead) — now phased out for environmental reasons; replaced by **MTBE**, **ethanol**, **MMT**.

---

## 2.5 Cetane Number — Extended Working

### Diesel Engine Behaviour

Diesel engines use **compression ignition** — fuel is injected into hot compressed air and should ignite *promptly*. If the fuel has **delayed ignition**, it accumulates and explodes all at once → **diesel knock**.

- **High CN** = short ignition delay = smooth combustion.
- **Low CN** = long ignition delay = knock, smoke, rough engine.

### Reference Scale

- **n-Cetane** (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) → CN = 100.
- **α-methyl naphthalene** (aromatic, hard to ignite) → CN = 0.

### Improving Cetane Number

1. Use **straight-chain paraffins** (they ignite fast).
2. Avoid aromatics (ignite slowly).
3. **Additives**: alkyl nitrates (e.g., **2-ethylhexyl nitrate**), **DTBP** (di-tert-butyl peroxide).

> [!WARNING]
> **Inverse Trend Trap!** Octane and cetane numbers follow **opposite** trends:
> - **Branched chains** → high ON, low CN.
> - **Straight chains** → low ON, high CN.
> This is why petrol and diesel have completely different fuel chemistries.

---

## 2.6 Biodiesel Production — Transesterification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transesterification** is the reaction in which an ester (vegetable oil triglyceride) reacts with an alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst to form a new ester (biodiesel/FAME) and glycerol.

**General Reaction:**

$$
\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,ROH \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH / KOH}]{\text{50–65 °C}} 3\,R'COOR + \text{Glycerol}
$$

For methanol (R = CH₃):

$$
\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH \longrightarrow 3\,FAME + \text{Glycerol}
$$

### Key Properties of Biodiesel (vs. Diesel)

| Property | Biodiesel (FAME) | Petro-diesel |
|----------|------------------|--------------|
| Cetane Number | 45–65 | 45–55 |
| Flash Point | > 130 °C | 60–80 °C |
| Viscosity | Higher | Lower |
| Sulphur | < 10 ppm | 50–500 ppm |
| Biodegradability | > 95% in 28 days | Very slow |

### Green Hydrogen Production Routes

1. **Electrolysis (most "green" form):**
$$
2\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Electricity}} 2\,H_2 + O_2
$$
2. **Solar thermochemical splitting** — high-temperature solar concentrators decompose water.
3. **Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting** — sunlight + photocatalyst (TiO₂, perovskite).
4. **Biomass gasification + CO₂ capture** — thermochemical route.

> [!NOTE]
> Hydrogen is colour-coded by production route: **Grey** (natural gas reforming), **Blue** (grey + CCS), **Green** (renewable-powered electrolysis).

---

## 2.7 Solid Lubricants — Mechanism Deep-Dive

### Layer-Lattice Solids (Graphite, MoS₂)

Both have **strong covalent bonds within layers** but **weak van der Waals forces between layers** (in graphite) or weak S–S bonds (in MoS₂).

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a deck of cards.** The cards themselves are rigid, but they slide over each other with almost zero resistance. That is exactly how graphite and MoS₂ lubricate — the layers *shear* over each other.

### Why Graphite Requires Adsorbed Vapour?

Pure graphite in vacuum actually has **high friction** because air/water vapour molecules normally sit between the layers. In **space applications**, graphite fails; hence **MoS₂** is preferred (it works in vacuum).

### Solid Lubricant Comparison

| Lubricant | Structure | Temp Limit | Best Use |
|-----------|-----------|------------|----------|
| Graphite | Hexagonal C layers | Up to 450 °C (in air) | High-temp furnaces, locks |
| MoS₂ | S–Mo–S layers | Up to 350 °C (in air); stable in vacuum | Aerospace, vacuum |
| PTFE (Teflon) | –(CF₂–CF₂)ₙ– | Up to 260 °C | Bearings, seals, non-stick |
| WS₂ | S–W–S layers | Up to 425 °C | High-load gears |
| Mica / Talc | Sheet silicates | Up to 900 °C | Cosmetic, dusting powders |

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Relevant Quick Reference — Memorize Before the Test!**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | HCV = LCV + 0.09 H × 587 | Hydrogen-based correction | cal/g |
| 2 | HCV = LCV + (9H/100) × 2442 | SI version | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Q = (W × ΔT) / m | Bomb calorimeter HCV | cal/g or kJ/kg |
| 4 | LCV = HCV − 0.09 H × 587 | Reverse relation | cal/g |
| 5 | % M = [(m₁ − m₂)/m] × 100 | Proximate: Moisture | % |
| 6 | % VM = [(m₂ − m₃)/m] × 100 | Proximate: VM | % |
| 7 | % A = (m₄/m) × 100 | Proximate: Ash | % |
| 8 | % FC = 100 − (M + VM + A) | Proximate: Fixed Carbon | % |
| 9 | Fuel Ratio = FC / VM | Coal classification | — |
| 10 | ON ∝ (degree of branching) | More branched → higher ON | — |
| 11 | CN ∝ (chain length, straight) | Longer straight → higher CN | — |
| 12 | Triglyceride + 3 ROH ⇌ 3 FAME + Glycerol | Transesterification | — |
| 13 | 2 H₂O → 2 H₂ + O₂ | Green H₂ production | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Note: I used **vertical-bar substitutes** (`\vert`/`\mid`) — none present, but if absolute value is required, write it as `\vert x \vert` instead of `|x|`.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| Power plants | Selection of coal grade by fuel ratio and CV |
| IC engines | Petrol blended for target ON, diesel for target CN |
| Aviation | Jet fuel must have very high ON + low freezing point |
| Rocket propulsion | Liquid H₂ + liquid O₂ (LOX) — green-H₂ powered |
| Auto sector | Biodiesel blends (B5, B20, B100) for diesel trucks |
| Renewable storage | Green H₂ → fuel cell cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) |
| High-temp machinery | Solid lubricants in steel mills, glass industry |
| Space / vacuum | MoS₂ films on satellite gears |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Relation Between HCV and LCV (Starting from Combustion Equation)

Take **methane (CH₄)** as the prototype hydrocarbon.

### Step 1: Write both combustion reactions

**For HCV** (water condenses):

$$
CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H_1 = -890\,\text{kJ/mol}
$$

**For LCV** (water escapes as gas):

$$
CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O_{(g)} \quad \Delta H_2 = -802\,\text{kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 2: Form the difference

$$
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = \Delta H_1 - \Delta H_2 = (-890) - (-802) = -88\,\text{kJ/mol}
$$

The negative sign on the **magnitudes** of heat released gives:

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 88\,\text{kJ/mol}}
$$

### Step 3: Recognize the source

This 88 kJ/mol is **exactly 2 × 44 kJ/mol = 2 × latent heat of vaporization** at 25 °C.
Indeed, L_vap of water at 25 °C = 44 kJ/mol → 2 moles of water formed → 88 kJ.

### Step 4: Generalize for any CₓHᵧ

For 1 mole of CₓHᵧ, the hydrogen produces y/2 moles of water.

$$
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = \frac{y}{2} \times 44 \;\;\text{kJ/mol} \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}
$$

### Step 5: Per kg of fuel (CₓHᵧ has molar mass = 12x + y)

$$
\Delta = \frac{y/2 \times 44}{12x+y} \times 1000 \;\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 6: Connect to % Hydrogen (H)

Mass fraction of H in CₓHᵧ is y/(12x+y).
So H% = (y/(12x+y)) × 100.

Substituting into the per-kg expression:

$$
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = \frac{9 \times H}{100} \times 2442 \;\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

> ✅ **This is the final working equation**, fully derived from first principles.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Bomb Calorimeter HCV Formula

### Step 1: Energy Balance

The heat released by fuel combustion is absorbed by:
- **Water** in the calorimeter bucket
- **Bomb** itself (steel, copper)
- **Stirrer, thermometer, wires** — collectively called **accessories**

### Step 2: Mathematical Statement

$$
Q_{\text{released}} = Q_{\text{absorbed}}
$$

Let:
- *W* = water equivalent of the system (kg) = mass of water whose heat capacity equals that of the entire system (bomb + water + accessories).
- *m* = mass of fuel pellet burnt (g or kg).
- *ΔT* = rise in temperature (°C or K).

### Step 3: Heat balance equation

$$
m \times \text{HCV} = (W + m_w) \times s_w \times \Delta T - (\text{heat corrections})
$$

For a simplified textbook expression (neglecting fuse wire and acid corrections):

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + m_w)\,s_w\,\Delta T}{m}}
$$

where:
- $m_w$ = mass of water in the bucket (kg)
- $s_w$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** or **1 cal/(g·K)**
- *W* = water equivalent of bomb (kg)

### Step 4: Combine water and water equivalent

Let the effective heat capacity be $C = (W + m_w)\,s_w$, then:

$$
\text{HCV} = \frac{C \cdot \Delta T}{m} \;\;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 5: Accounting for Heat Corrections

A more rigorous version:

$$
\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + m_w)\,\Delta T - \text{fuse correction} - \text{acid correction}}{m}
$$

- **Fuse correction** = mass of fuse wire burnt × its calorific value (≈ 1400 cal/g for nichrome).
- **Acid correction** = acid formed (HNO₃ from N₂ in air) × 57,000 cal per equivalent (≈ formation energy of HNO₃).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — HCV/LCV with Coal

**Problem:** A coal sample contains 86% C, 5% H, 1% N, 1% S, 6% O and 1% ash by mass. Estimate its LCV if HCV measured in a bomb calorimeter is **7800 cal/g**. Latent heat of steam = **587 cal/g**.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta = 0.09 \times H \times 587 = 0.09 \times 5 \times 587
$$

$$
\Delta = 9 \times 5 \times 58.7 = 45 \times 58.7 = 2641.5 \;\;\text{cal/g}
$$

Wait — recalculating cleanly:

$$
\Delta = 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 = 0.45 \times 587 = 264.15 \;\;\text{cal/g}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{LCV} = 7800 - 264.15 = 7535.85 \approx 7536 \;\;\text{cal/g}
$$

**Convert to SI:**

$$
\text{LCV} = 7536 \times 4.186 = 31{,}547 \;\;\text{kJ/kg} \approx 31.5 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

> This is consistent with a **good bituminous coal** (CV = 25–35 MJ/kg). ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Bomb Calorimeter Reading

**Problem:** 0.95 g of a coal sample is burnt in a bomb calorimeter. The water equivalent of bomb + water + accessories is **2200 g**. Temperature rises from **24.50 °C to 28.20 °C**. Fuse wire correction = **15 cal**, acid correction = **10 cal**. Calculate HCV.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta T = 28.20 - 24.50 = 3.70 \;\text{°C}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{absorbed}} = (W \cdot \Delta T) - \text{corrections} = (2200 \times 3.70) - (15 + 10)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{absorbed}} = 8140 - 25 = 8115 \;\text{cal}
$$

$$
\text{HCV} = \frac{Q_{\text{absorbed}}}{m} = \frac{8115}{0.95} = 8542.1 \;\text{cal/g}
$$

Converting:

$$
\text{HCV} = 8542.1 \times 4.186 = 35{,}757 \;\text{kJ/kg} \approx 35.76 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

This value (≈ 35.8 MJ/kg) corresponds to **anthracite or high-rank bituminous coal** — ✅ sensible.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Proximate Analysis

**Problem:** In a proximate analysis, the following masses were recorded for 1.000 g of air-dried coal:

- Mass of empty crucible = 18.00 g
- Mass of crucible + coal = 19.00 g
- Mass after oven drying (105 °C, 1 h) = 18.92 g
- Mass after 925 °C heating (7 min, covered) = 18.74 g
- Mass after muffle furnace (700 °C, constant) = 18.18 g

**Calculate M, VM, A, FC.**

**Solution:**

Step 1: Initial coal mass

$$
m = 19.00 - 18.00 = 1.00 \;\text{g}
$$

Step 2: Moisture

$$
m_1 = 19.00,\; m_2 = 18.92
$$
$$
\%M = \frac{19.00 - 18.92}{1.00} \times 100 = 8.00\%
$$

Step 3: Volatile matter

$$
m_3 = 18.74
$$
$$
\%VM = \frac{18.92 - 18.74}{1.00} \times 100 = 18.00\%
$$

Step 4: Ash

$$
m_4 = 18.18
$$
$$
\%A = \frac{18.18 - 18.00}{1.00} \times 100 = 18.00\%
$$

Step 5: Fixed Carbon (by difference)

$$
\%FC = 100 - (8.00 + 18.00 + 18.00) = 56.00\%
$$

**Fuel Ratio:**

$$
\frac{FC}{VM} = \frac{56.00}{18.00} = 3.11
$$

Since FC/VM > 1.7, this is a **bituminous-to-anthracite grade coal** (good coke-forming tendency). ✅

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Octane Number Blending

**Problem:** A fuel has the same knocking tendency as a mixture of 84% iso-octane + 16% n-heptane. What is its octane number? If 20% of a straight-run naphtha (ON = 65) is blended with this fuel, what is the **approximate blended ON**?

**Solution:**

$$
\text{ON} = 84
$$

For blending with low-ON naphtha (simple linear approximation):

$$
\text{ON}_{\text{blend}} = 0.8 \times 84 + 0.2 \times 65 = 67.2 + 13 = 80.2
$$

> Real-world octane blending is **non-linear** due to synergistic effects, but linear approximation gives an order-of-magnitude estimate.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example 5 — Biodiesel Stoichiometry

**Problem:** 1000 kg of **tristearin** (C₅₇H₁₁₀O₆, molar mass = 890 g/mol) is transesterified with excess methanol. Calculate the theoretical yield of biodiesel (methyl stearate, C₁₉H₃₆O₂, M = 298 g/mol) and glycerol (C₃H₈O₃, M = 92 g/mol).

**Reaction:**

$$
C_{57}H_{110}O_6 + 3\,CH_3OH \longrightarrow 3\,C_{19}H_{36}O_2 + C_3H_8O_3
$$

**Solution:**

Moles of tristearin = $\dfrac{1000 \times 1000}{890} = 1123.6$ mol.

From stoichiometry:
- Moles of methyl stearate = 3 × 1123.6 = 3370.8 mol.
- Moles of glycerol = 1123.6 mol.

Masses:

$$
\text{Biodiesel} = 3370.8 \times 298 = 1{,}004{,}500 \;\text{g} \approx 1004.5 \;\text{kg}
$$

$$
\text{Glycerol} = 1123.6 \times 92 = 103{,}371 \;\text{g} \approx 103.4 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Mass closure check:**
- Reactants: 1000 kg tristearin + 3 × 1123.6 × 32/1000 = 1000 + 107.9 = 1107.9 kg
- Products: 1004.5 + 103.4 = 1107.9 kg ✅ (Conservation of mass verified)

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Quick Decision Tool

| Fuel / Lubricant Type | Best Use Case | Why? |
|----------------------|---------------|------|
| Anthracite (FC > 90%) | Domestic coke, metallurgy | High CV, low VM, smokeless |
| Bituminous (FC 50–70%) | Steam raising, coking | Balanced VM and FC |
| Lignite (FC 30–50%) | Power generation (near mine) | Cheap but high moisture |
| Petrol with ON 95 | High-performance cars | Resists knock at high compression |
| Diesel with CN 50 | Heavy trucks, locomotives | Quick ignition under compression |
| Biodiesel (B20) | Diesel buses, farm tractors | Reduces particulate emissions |
| Green H₂ | Fuel-cell vehicles, ammonia synthesis | Zero-carbon fuel |
| Graphite lubricant | High-temperature furnace bearings | Stable up to 450 °C |
| MoS₂ lubricant | Spacecraft, vacuum pumps | Works without adsorbed vapour |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bomb Calorimeter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```
flowchart TB
    subgraph sample["SAMPLE HOLDER"]
        A1[Fuel Pellet: 0.95 g, pressed]
        A2[Crucible: Pt or Ni dish]
        A3[Ignition wires + cotton fuse]
        A1 --> A2
        A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph bomb["STEEL BOMB - sealed"]
        B1[Oxygen at 25 atm]
        B2[Electrodes - 12 V supply]
        B3[Water jacket - 2 L]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph measure["MEASUREMENT MODULE"]
        C1[Thermometer - precision 0.001 C]
        C2[Stirrer - constant rpm]
        C3[Temperature recorder]
        C1 --> C3
        C2 --> C1
    end

    subgraph calc["DATA PROCESSING"]
        D1[Read initial T: T1]
        D2[Read final T: T2]
        D3[Compute deltaT = T2 - T1]
        D4[Apply corrections: fuse, acid]
        D5[Compute HCV = W.deltaT / m]
        D1 --> D3
        D2 --> D3
        D3 --> D4
        D4 --> D5
    end

    sample --> bomb --> measure --> calc

    style A1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333
    style B3 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#333
    style D5 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagram and not physical drawing?** A physical bomb calorimeter cross-section is hard to render accurately in Mermaid. A block-functional schematic captures the *information flow* (heat released → absorbed → measured → computed) more clearly for KTU exam purposes.

---

## 4.2 Flow Chart — Proximate Analysis Procedure

```
flowchart LR
    P0[Coal Sample<br>1.0 g, 212 micron] --> P1

    P2 --> P3
    P3{Step 2: Covered crucible<br>925 C for 7 min} --> P4[Volatile Matter VM%<br>from mass loss]
    P4 --> P5
    P5{Step 3: Muffle furnace<br>700 C to constant mass} --> P6[Ash A%<br>from residue]
    P6 --> P7[Fixed Carbon FC%<br>= 100 - M+VM+A]
    P7 --> P8{Fuel Ratio = FC/VM}
    P8 --> P9{> 10 Anthracite}
    P8 --> P10{1.0 - 1.7 Bituminous}
    P8 --> P11{0.5 - 1.0 Lignite}
    P8 --> P12{< 0.5 Peat}
```

```
flowchart TB

    Q1 --> Q2[Part 1: Heat to raise H2O to 100 C]

    Q3 --> Q4[Heat retained in gaseous products = LCV]
    Q4 --> F2[Exhaust gases at ~200 C leave boiler]

    Q3 -.Deducted.-> Q5[LOSS = 9H/100 * 2442 kJ/kg]
    Q1 -.- LCV_eq[HCV = LCV + 9H/100 * 2442]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define calorific value. Distinguish between HCV and LCV.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Calorific value** is the amount of heat released when 1 kg of a fuel is completely combusted and the products are cooled to 25 °C (1 mark).
- **HCV (Higher/Gross Calorific Value):** Heat released when the water formed is condensed back to liquid state (1 mark).
- **LCV (Lower/Net Calorific Value):** Heat released when the water formed escapes as vapour; hence LCV = HCV − latent heat of steam (1 mark).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is proximate analysis? List the four parameters it determines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Proximate analysis** is an empirical laboratory procedure that determines the operational/physical constituents of coal (1 mark).
- The four parameters (½ mark each):
  1. **Moisture content (M)** — water in coal, lost at 105 °C.
  2. **Volatile matter (VM)** — combustible gases released at 925 °C.
  3. **Ash (A)** — non-combustible residue after complete combustion.
  4. **Fixed carbon (FC)** — combustible solid residue, obtained by difference.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is calorific value? Derive the relationship between HCV and LCV of a fuel. A coal sample contains 80% C, 6% H, 2% N, 1% S, 9% O and 2% ash. If its HCV is 8200 cal/g, calculate its LCV.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Bomb Calorimeter** with a neat labelled diagram. State the formula used and the various corrections applied.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **proximate analysis** of coal in detail. Mention the principle, procedure and significance of each parameter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Octane Number** and **Cetane Number**. Discuss the relationship between the structure of hydrocarbons and these numbers, with examples.

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Question A — Part (a) Solution

**Definition [2 marks]:**
Calorific value = heat released on complete combustion of 1 kg of fuel, products cooled to 25 °C. Units: cal/g or kJ/kg.

**Derivation of HCV–LCV relation [3 marks]:**

When a hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ burns, the hydrogen atoms produce water. For every 2 g of H, 18 g of H₂O is produced; hence per gram of H, 9 g of H₂O.

Latent heat of condensation of steam at 25 °C = 587 cal/g = 2442 kJ/kg.

Therefore, the latent heat per gram of fuel = $0.09 \times H \times 587$ cal/g.

$$
\boxed{\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + 0.09\,H \times 587 \;\;\text{cal/g}}
$$

**Numerical [2 marks]:**
Given H = 6%, HCV = 8200 cal/g:

$$
\text{HCV} - \text{LCV} = 0.09 \times 6 \times 587 = 0.54 \times 587 = 316.98 \approx 317 \;\text{cal/g}
$$

$$
\text{LCV} = 8200 - 317 = 7883 \;\text{cal/g}
$$

In SI units: LCV = 7883 × 4.186 = 33,002 kJ/kg ≈ **33 MJ/kg** (typical bituminous coal). ✅

---

### Question A — Part (b) Solution

**Bomb Calorimeter Description [4 marks]:**

A bomb calorimeter measures HCV at constant volume. It consists of:
1. **Sealed steel bomb** with oxygen at 25 atm.
2. **Crucible** holding the fuel pellet.
3. **Ignition circuit** — 12 V battery, fuse wire (nichrome), cotton thread.
4. **Water bucket** surrounding the bomb (~2 L water).
5. **Insulated outer jacket** to prevent heat loss.
6. **Stirrer** for uniform temperature.
7. **Precision thermometer / RTD** (±0.001 °C).

**Procedure [2 marks]:**
1. Weigh ~1 g fuel, place in crucible.
2. Fill bomb with O₂ at 25 atm; seal.
3. Place bomb in water bucket, record initial T₁.
4. Close circuit; fuse wire ignites fuel.
5. Record maximum T₂ reached.
6. Calculate ΔT = T₂ − T₁.

**Formula and Corrections [1 mark]:**

$$
\text{HCV} = \frac{(W + m_w)\,s_w\,\Delta T - e_1 - e_2}{m}
$$

where:
- *W* = water equivalent of bomb (kg)
- *m_w* = mass of water (kg)
- *s_w* = specific heat of water = 1 cal/g·°C
- *e₁* = fuse wire correction
- *e₂* = acid (HNO₃) correction
- *m* = mass of fuel

**Corrections list [bonus for completeness]:**
- Fuse wire (nichrome): 1400 cal/g burnt
- Acid: 57,000 cal per equivalent of HNO₃ formed
- Sulphur correction (forms H₂SO₄): small

---

### Question B — Part (a) Solution

**Principle [1 mark]:**
Proximate analysis is a **thermal decomposition technique** that uses controlled heating of coal at specific temperatures to separate moisture, volatile matter, and ash, with fixed carbon obtained by difference.

**Procedure (4 steps, 1.5 marks each):**

**Step 1 — Moisture (M):**
- Weigh ~1 g of air-dried coal (212 µm, 72 mesh).
- Place in air-oven at **105–110 °C for 1 hour**.
- Cool in desiccator; weigh.
- Loss in mass = moisture.

$$
\%M = \frac{\text{loss in mass}}{\text{mass of coal}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Volatile Matter (VM):**
- Take the dried coal in a **covered platinum crucible** (lid prevents air entry).
- Heat in muffle furnace at **925 ± 25 °C for exactly 7 minutes**.
- Cool; weigh.
- Loss in mass (excluding moisture) = VM.

$$
\%VM = \frac{\text{loss in mass}}{\text{mass of dry coal}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Ash (A):**
- Take the VM-free residue.
- Burn in **muffle furnace at 700 ± 50 °C till constant mass** (free access to air).
- Cool; weigh the residue.

$$
\%A = \frac{\text{mass of ash}}{\text{mass of coal}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Fixed Carbon (FC):**

$$
\%FC = 100 - (\%M + \%VM + \%A)
$$

**Significance [1 mark]:**

| Parameter | Significance |
|-----------|--------------|
| M | Storage stability; high M → low CV, slow combustion |
| VM | Combustion ease; high VM → smoky flame, good for domestic fuel |
| A | Boiler efficiency; high A → abrasive, slagging |
| FC | Heating value; high FC → high CV, coke formation tendency |

**Fuel Ratio = FC / VM** classifies coal into peat (< 0.5), lignite (0.5–1.0), bituminous (1.0–1.7), anthracite (> 10).

---

### Question B — Part (b) Solution

**Octane Number Definition [2 marks]:**
Octane number is the percentage of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the **same knocking tendency** as the fuel under test, in a standard CFR engine. Scale: iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0.

**Cetane Number Definition [2 marks]:**
Cetane number is the percentage of n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄) in a mixture of n-cetane and α-methyl naphthalene that has the **same ignition delay** as the fuel under test. Scale: n-cetane = 100, α-methyl naphthalene = 0.

**Structure–Number Relationship [3 marks]:**

For **Octane Number** (petrol):
- **Branched chains** ignite smoothly → **high ON**.
- **Aromatics** also have high ON.
- **Straight chains** cause knock → low ON.
- *Examples:* Iso-octane (ON = 100), n-heptane (ON = 0), toluene (ON = 124).

For **Cetane Number** (diesel):
- **Long straight chains** ignite quickly → **high CN**.
- **Branched chains and aromatics** ignite slowly → low CN.
- *Examples:* n-Hexadecane (CN = 100), α-methyl naphthalene (CN = 0), 1-hexadecene (CN = 90).

**Key Inverse Trend:**

| Hydrocarbon | Octane Number | Cetane Number |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| n-Heptane | 0 | ~50 (moderate) |
| Iso-octane | 100 | ~15 (low) |
| n-Cetane | ~30 | 100 |
| Toluene | 124 | 0 |

> **Conclusion:** Octane and cetane trends are **inversely related** — no single fuel can simultaneously satisfy both engine requirements.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU 2024 Papers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HCV/LCV definitions (–1 mark)** — students often forget to specify "products cooled to room temperature" for HCV. Always mention cooling.
> 2. **Wrong latent heat value (–1 mark)** — remember 587 cal/g or 2442 kJ/kg at **25 °C**, NOT 540 cal/g (which is the boiling-point value).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — examiners strictly mark numerical accuracy. 1 cal/g = 4.186 J/g = 4.186 kJ/kg.
> 4. **In Proximate Analysis — using wrong temperatures** (–0.5 mark each):
>    - Moisture: 105–110 °C ✓
>    - VM: 925 °C, **covered** crucible ✓
>    - Ash: 700 °C, **uncovered** (free air) ✓
> 5. **Octane/Cetane reference confusion** — mixing up n-heptane with n-cetane, or α-methyl naphthalene with iso-octane. Mnemonic: **"Petrol = branches = iso-octane; Diesel = straight = n-cetane"**.
> 6. **Bomb calorimeter formula** — students often forget the *water equivalent W* and use only the mass of water. Always include W.
> 7. **Failing to define "10 µm vs 212 µm" mesh size in proximate analysis** — KTU 2024 expects the sieve size specification.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Calorific Value** = heat released per kg of fuel on complete combustion, products cooled to 25 °C.
- [x] **HCV > LCV** by the latent heat of condensation of water produced.
- [x] **Master formula:** HCV = LCV + 0.09·H·587 cal/g = LCV + (9H/100)·2442 kJ/kg.
- [x] **Latent heat of steam** at 25 °C = **587 cal/g** or **2442 kJ/kg** (this number is exam-critical).
- [x] **Bomb Calorimeter** gives **HCV** at constant volume; the formula includes water equivalent *W*, mass of water *m_w*, and corrections (fuse wire + acid).
- [x] **Proximate Analysis** gives: M (105 °C), VM (925 °C, covered), A (700 °C, uncovered), FC (by difference).
- [x] **M + VM + A + FC = 100%** — always.
- [x] **Fuel Ratio = FC/VM** classifies coal: peat (< 0.5), lignite (0.5–1), bituminous (1–1.7), anthracite (> 10).
- [x] **Octane Number**: iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0. **Branched → high ON**.
- [x] **Cetane Number**: n-cetane = 100, α-methyl naphthalene = 0. **Straight → high CN**.
- [x] **Petrol needs high ON; diesel needs high CN** — inverse trend.
- [x] **Biodiesel** = FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters), produced by **transesterification** with methanol + NaOH catalyst.
- [x] **Green Hydrogen** = H₂ produced by electrolysis of water using **renewable electricity**; zero-carbon.
- [x] **Solid Lubricants** rely on layered structures (graphite, MoS₂) or polymer chains (PTFE).
- [x] **Graphite** needs adsorbed vapour → fails in vacuum; **MoS₂** works in vacuum (used in satellites).
- [x] **Green H₂ reaction:** 2 H₂O → 2 H₂ + O₂ (renewable-electricity powered).
- [x] **Standard calorific value ranges:** Pet coke ≈ 32–35 MJ/kg; Anthracite ≈ 30–35; Bituminous ≈ 24–35; Lignite ≈ 10–20; Wood ≈ 15–18; Biodiesel ≈ 38–40; Petrol ≈ 44–46; Diesel ≈ 43–46; Methane ≈ 55; H₂ ≈ 142 MJ/kg.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semisolid and Liquid Lubricants. Properties of Lubricants - Viscosity Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Semisolid and Liquid Lubricants. Properties of Lubricants - Viscosity Index ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Lubricants — Formal Definition A **lubricant** is a substance (solid, semisolid, liquid, or gaseous) introduced between two moving surfaces in relative motion to **reduce friction, minimize wear, dissipate heat, and protect against corrosion**. In this module we focus on **semisolid** and **liquid** lubricants, which together account for over **95%** of all industrial lubrication applications. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GCCYT122 Module 1)** > *Lubricants are substances applied to reduce friction and wear between two moving surfaces. Liquid lubricants are classified as animal, vegetable, mineral (petroleum), and synthetic oils. Semisolid lubricants are predominantly greases — dispersions of a thickener in a base oil.* ### 1.2 Semisolid Lubricants — Greases A **grease** is a **semisolid to solid** dispersion of a **thickener** (typically a metallic soap) in a **base fluid** (mineral or synthetic oil), fortified with performance **additives**. **Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge in Oil"** 🧽 > Imagine a kitchen sponge soaked in cooking oil. The sponge (thickener/soap) traps the oil in a fibrous cage. When the sponge is squeezed between two surfaces (load applied), the oil bleeds out and lubricates; when pressure is released, the sponge re-absorbs the oil. That is exactly how a grease works — it is essentially a *sponge-like soap network holding oil* in place. This is why grease stays where applied and doesn't drip off vertical surfaces. **Composition of a Typical Grease:** | Component | Typical Range | Function | |---|---|---| | Base oil (mineral/synthetic) | **70 – 95%** | Actual lubrication | | Thickener (soap) | **5 – 25%** | Provides consistency/structure | | Additives | **0 – 10%** | Antioxidants, anti-wear, EP, etc. | **Common Soap Thickeners and their Drop Points:** | Soap Type | Drop Point (°C) | Application | |---|---|---| | Calcium (lime soap) | 70 – 100 | General purpose, low temperature | | Sodium (soda soap) | 150 – 180 | High temperature, ball bearings | | Lithium | 180 – 200 | Multipurpose (most common) | | Aluminum | 100 – 120 | High water resistance | | Barium | 200+ | High temperature, sea-water | | Complex soaps (Li-Ca, Al-Ba) | 230 – 290+ | Extreme conditions | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Drop Point** is the temperature at which a grease passes from the semisolid to the liquid state. It sets the **upper temperature limit** of a grease and is the single most important property governing grease selection. ### 1.3 Liquid Lubricants — Oils **Intuitive Analogy — "Motor Oil in Your Engine"** 🚗 > Think of honey and water being poured down a slanted surface. Honey flows slowly (high viscosity) and resists temperature change less; water flows fast and thins dramatically when warmed. Liquid lubricants are carefully chosen so their flow behaviour stays balanced across the working temperature range — neither too thin (metal-to-metal contact) nor too thick (energy wasted churning the oil). **Classification of Liquid Lubricants:** 1. **Animal Oils** — e.g., lard oil, tallow oil, whale oil. *Natural esters of glycerol*. Excellent lubricity but oxidize rapidly. Mostly historical. 2. **Vegetable Oils** — e.g., castor oil, coconut oil, palm oil, rapeseed oil. *Triglycerides*. Good boundary lubrication; biodegradeable; used in environmentally-sensitive applications. 3. **Mineral Oils (Petroleum-based)** — *The workhorses of industry*. Obtained by fractional distillation and refining of crude oil. Further classified by hydrocarbon type: - **Paraffinic base oils** (waxy alkanes, branched/linear) — High Viscosity Index, good oxidation stability. - **Naphthenic base oils** (cycloalkanes) — Low pour point, good low-temperature behaviour, lower VI. - **Aromatic base oils** (arenes) — Poor VI, toxic; rarely used alone as lubricants. 4. **Synthetic Oils** — Engineered molecules offering superior performance: - **Polyalphaolefins (PAO)** - **Synthetic esters** (diesters, polyolesters) - **Polyglycols** - **Silicones (polysiloxanes)** - **Phosphate esters** (fire-resistant hydraulic fluids) 5. **Blended Oils** — Mixtures of above to balance cost and performance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Most industrial lubricants (~**85%**) are **mineral oil based**. Synthetic oils dominate where extreme temperatures, long service life, fire-resistance, or biodegradability are required (aerospace, military, food-grade, arctic use). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Properties of Lubricants — The "Ten Commandments" of Lubricant Testing For KTU 2024 examinations, you must be fluent with the following lubricant properties, the *significance* of each, and the underlying principle. #### 2.1.1 Viscosity (η) The most fundamental property. It is the **internal resistance of a fluid to flow**. $$\eta = \frac{F \cdot d}{A \cdot v}$$ where $F$ = shear force (N), $d$ = distance between plates (m), $A$ = area (m²), $v$ = relative velocity (m/s), $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s). **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu = \dfrac{\eta}{\rho}$ (SI unit: m²/s, practical unit: **centistoke, cSt**). > **Why it matters:** Viscosity determines the *thickness* of the oil film (hydrodynamic lubrication) under load — governed by the Reynolds equation: $h \propto \sqrt{\eta v / P}$. #### 2.1.2 Viscosity Index (VI) ⭐ *This is the focus topic* — see Section 2.2 for deep dive. #### 2.1.3 Flash Point and Fire Point - **Flash Point** — *Lowest temperature* at which oil vapour momentarily ignites (flashes) on application of a flame. - **Fire Point** — *Lowest temperature* at which oil vapour burns continuously for at least **5 seconds**. > **Why it matters:** Determines fire hazard during storage and use. Transformer oils demand high flash points; cutting oils (intentional flash-off) need low ones. #### 2.1.4 Pour Point The **lowest temperature** at which the oil will *just flow* under standard test conditions (ASTM D97). Governs low-temperature operability. #### 2.1.5 Cloud Point Temperature at which dissolved wax first becomes visible as a cloudy haze upon cooling. #### 2.1.6 Aniline Point The temperature at which equal volumes of aniline and oil become completely miscible. **Low aniline point → high aromatic content → poor VI**. $$\text{Aromaticity} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Aniline Point}}$$ #### 2.1.7 Neutralization Number (Acid Value) mg of **KOH** required to neutralize the free acids in **1 g** of oil. Indicates oxidation degradation and corrosiveness. #### 2.1.8 Saponification Number mg of **KOH** required to saponify the fats/esters in **1 g** of oil. Indicates the *fatty/glyceride content* — relevant for vegetable/animal oils and greases. #### 2.1.9 Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom) % carbonaceous deposit left after evaporation/pyrolysis under standard conditions. Indicates tendency to form **coke** in engines. #### 2.1.10 Emulsification & Demulsibility Ability to form emulsion with water (cutting oils) *or* to separate quickly from water (turbine oils). Measured by steam-emulsion test. #### 2.1.11 Specific Gravity Used in oil identification. Mineral oils typically **0.85 – 0.95** at 15.6°C. #### 2.1.12 Iodine Number g of I₂ absorbed by **100 g** of oil. Measures degree of **unsaturation**. Vegetable oils (highly unsaturated) have high iodine numbers. ### 2.2 Viscosity Index (VI) — Detailed Theory #### 2.2.1 Conceptual Background The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an *empirical*, *dimensionless* number that quantifies the **rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature**. It was developed by **Dean and Davis (1929)** and later extended by **Dean, Davis and Bauer** for values above 100. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Definition** > *"Viscosity Index is a dimensionless number which indicates the variation of viscosity of an oil with temperature. Higher VI means smaller variation of viscosity with temperature — a better lubricant."* #### 2.2.2 The Two Reference Oils | Reference Oil | VI | Behaviour with Temperature | |---|---|---| | **Oil "A"** (naphthenic, Gulf coast crude) | **0** | Viscosity drops sharply with temperature | | **Oil "B"** (paraffinic, Pennsylvanian crude) | **100** | Viscosity drops slowly with temperature | **Construction of the VI Scale:** 1. Choose a *test oil* of known viscosity at **210°F (98.9°C)**, say $V_{210}$. 2. Find the two reference oils (VI = 0 and VI = 100) that **also have viscosity $V_{210}$ at 210°F**. 3. Read their viscosities at **100°F (37.8°C)**: - $L$ = viscosity of **VI = 0** oil at 100°F (largest value) - $H$ = viscosity of **VI = 100** oil at 100°F (smallest value) 4. Measure viscosity of the *test oil* at 100°F → $U$. 5. Compute VI by linear interpolation (or its logarithmic extension for VI > 100). #### 2.2.3 Significance for Engineering - **High VI oils** maintain thick films at high temperature (engine bearings) and remain fluid at low temperature (cold cranking). *Paraffinic and synthetic oils.* - **Low VI oils** thin out too much when hot → metal-to-metal contact, wear, failure. *Naphthenic and aromatic oils.* - **VI Improvers** (polymethacrylates, olefin copolymers) are added to base oils to push VI from ~95 to **150–200+** in modern multigrade engine oils (e.g., **SAE 5W-30**). #### 2.2.4 Alternative Modern References (ASTM D2270) Modern calculations use **40°C and 100°C** instead of 100°F and 210°F. The two reference viscosities are still denoted $L$ and $H$, but tables of $L$, $H$ for various kinematic viscosities at 100°C are tabulated in **ASTM D2270**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Master the table below. The KTU ESE almost always contains a direct 3-mark question on VI calculation. | # | Property / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dynamic Viscosity | $\eta = F \cdot d \,/\, (A \cdot v)$ | Pa·s | Internal friction | | 2 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \eta / \rho$ | m²/s (cSt) | Flow under gravity | | 3 | **VI (when $U > H$, i.e. VI $<$ 100)** | $\displaystyle \text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | Inverse of viscosity drop | | 4 | **VI (when $U < H$, i.e. VI $>$ 100)** | $\displaystyle \text{VI} = 100 + \frac{H - U}{H - Y} \times 100$ | Dimensionless | High-quality oil | | 5 | Acid Value | mg KOH per g oil | mg KOH/g | Oxidation indicator | | 6 | Saponification Value | mg KOH per g oil | mg KOH/g | Ester content | | 7 | Aniline Point | Miscibility temperature with aniline | °C | Aromaticity | | 8 | Iodine Number | g I₂ per 100 g oil | g I₂/100g | Unsaturation | | 9 | Pour Point | Lowest flow temperature | °C | Cold operability | | 10 | Flash / Fire Point | Ignition temperatures | °C | Fire safety | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the table above, $L$, $H$, $U$ and $Y$ are **kinematic viscosities at 100°F (or 40°C)** of the VI=0 reference oil, VI=100 reference oil, the test oil, and a small correction (for VI > 100 case) respectively — all chosen/measured so that each oil has the *same kinematic viscosity at 210°F (or 100°C)*. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation of the VI Formula (Case: 0 ≤ VI ≤ 100) We rely on the fact that for a fixed viscosity at 210°F, the viscosity at 100°F varies *linearly* with VI between the two reference oils. **Step 1 — Set up the linear interpolation.** Viscosity at 100°F is a linear function of VI between the two anchor points: $$\text{Viscosity at 100°F} = L - \left(\frac{L - H}{100 - 0}\right) \cdot \text{VI}$$ **Step 2 — For the test oil with viscosity $U$ at 100°F and unknown VI, write:** $$U = L - \left(\frac{L - H}{100}\right) \cdot \text{VI}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for VI:** $$L - U = \frac{(L - H) \cdot \text{VI}}{100}$$ $$\boxed{\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100} \quad \text{(valid for } U > H\text{, i.e. } \text{VI} \le 100)$$ > **Interpretation:** If $U = L$ (test oil behaves like the VI=0 naphthenic reference), $\text{VI} = 0$. If $U = H$ (test oil behaves like the VI=100 paraffinic reference), $\text{VI} = 100$. Linear in between. ### 3.2 Extension for VI > 100 (Dean–Davis–Bauer) When the test oil is *better* than the VI=100 reference, $U < H$. The arithmetic formula above would extrapolate beyond 100, but the true relationship is **logarithmic**. A *good engineering approximation* used in KTU problems is: $$\boxed{\text{VI} = 100 + \frac{H - U}{H - Y} \times 100}$$ where $Y$ is a small tabulated correction constant (often $Y \approx 0.4 \cdot H$ to $0.7 \cdot H$ depending on viscosity grade — exact values supplied in ASTM D2270 tables for the 100°C viscosity of the test oil). > For KTU exam problems, the value of $Y$ is *given in the question*. Do not memorize tables; just use the formula. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples #### **Example 1 — VI in the range 0 to 100** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** > The kinematic viscosities at 100°F of two reference oils and a test oil, all having the same viscosity **75 cSt at 210°F**, are as follows: VI = 0 oil has $L = 480$ cSt; VI = 100 oil has $H = 150$ cSt. The test oil has $U = 270$ cSt at 100°F. Calculate the VI of the test oil. **Solution (Step-by-step):** Since $U = 270 > H = 150$, the oil is of *low VI* (between 0 and 100). Apply the basic formula: $$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$ Substitute the values: $$\text{VI} = \frac{480 - 270}{480 - 150} \times 100$$ $$\text{VI} = \frac{210}{330} \times 100$$ $$\text{VI} = 0.6363\ldots \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{VI} \approx 63.6}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always explicitly state *which formula case* you are using (i.e., $U > H$ or $U < H$) and check by computing. Examiners award a mark for the case-detection step alone. #### **Example 2 — VI above 100 (Premium Paraffinic Oil)** > At 100°F, the viscosity of a VI=100 reference oil is $H = 90$ cSt, the viscosity of a VI=0 oil is $L = 320$ cSt (both having the same 100°C viscosity as the test oil). The test oil has $U = 65$ cSt at 100°F. The correction value $Y$ is given as $0.5 \cdot H = 45$ cSt. Calculate the VI. **Solution:** Here $U = 65 < H = 90$, so the test oil is *better* than the VI=100 reference → VI > 100. Apply the extended formula: $$\text{VI} = 100 + \frac{H - U}{H - Y} \times 100$$ $$\text{VI} = 100 + \frac{90 - 65}{90 - 45} \times 100$$ $$\text{VI} = 100 + \frac{25}{45} \times 100$$ $$\text{VI} = 100 + 55.56$$ $$\boxed{\text{VI} \approx 155.6}$$ > **Engineering meaning:** A VI of **156** places this oil in the *high-performance* category — typical of modern synthetic blends and multigrade engine oils with VI improvers. Such oils barely thin out even at 200°C cylinder wall temperatures. #### **Example 3 — Oil Classification by VI** > A petroleum engineer submits three oil samples with measured VIs of **30, 95, and 130**. Classify each as paraffinic, naphthenic, or aromatic-rich, and comment on their suitability as lubricants. **Solution:** | Sample | VI | Classification | Suitability | |---|---|---|---| | Oil X | 30 | **Naphthenic / aromatic** | Poor; viscosity drops steeply with T | | Oil Y | 95 | **Paraffinic** | Good; classic lubricant base | | Oil Z | 130 | **High VI (paraffinic + additives / synthetic)** | Excellent; aerospace / modern engine oil | ### 3.4 Why "Higher VI = Better Lubricant"? — A Physical Picture The Reynolds equation for hydrodynamic film thickness $h$ under load $P$: $$h \propto \sqrt{\frac{\eta \, v}{P}}$$ - At **high temperature**, low-VI oil → $\eta$ plummets → $h$ too thin → metal contact → **wear**. - At **low temperature**, low-VI oil → $\eta$ skyrockets → $h$ excessive → energy wasted in churning → **cold-start failure**. A **high-VI oil** keeps $\eta$ nearly constant across the operating range, keeping $h$ in the *safe hydrodynamic zone* at both extremes. **This is the entire reason VI was invented.** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Classification of Lubricants — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Lubricants] --> nodeB[Solid] nodeA --> nodeC[Semisolid] nodeA --> nodeD[Liquid] nodeA --> nodeE[Gaseous] nodeB --> nodeB1[Graphite] nodeB --> nodeB2[Molybdenum disulfide] nodeB --> nodeB3[PTFE] nodeC --> nodeC1[Greases] nodeC1 --> nodeC1a[Soap-thickened] nodeC1 --> nodeC1b[Non-soap thickened] nodeC1a --> nodeC1a1[Calcium] nodeC1a --> nodeC1a2[Sodium] nodeC1a --> nodeC1a3[Lithium] nodeC1a --> nodeC1a4[Aluminum] nodeD --> nodeD1[Animal oils] nodeD --> nodeD2[Vegetable oils] nodeD --> nodeD3[Mineral oils] nodeD --> nodeD4[Synthetic oils] nodeD --> nodeD5[Blended oils] nodeD3 --> nodeD3a[Paraffinic - high VI] nodeD3 --> nodeD3b[Naphthenic - low VI] nodeD3 --> nodeD3c[Aromatic - poor VI] nodeD4 --> nodeD4a[PAO] nodeD4 --> nodeD4b[Esters] nodeD4 --> nodeD4c[Silicones] nodeD4 --> nodeD4d[Polyglycols] nodeE --> nodeE1[Air bearings] nodeE --> nodeE2[Inert gas turbines] ``` ### 4.2 Viscosity Index — Reference Oil & Interpolation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph axis[Temperature Axis] direction LR T1[100 deg F or 40 deg C] T2[210 deg F or 100 deg C] end subgraph refOils[Reference Oils at Fixed 210 F Viscosity] direction LR nodeR1[Oil A
