## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "fullerene." With the help of Euler's formula, show that every closed fullerene cage must contain exactly 12 pentagonal faces.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A fullerene is a closed, hollow cage molecule composed entirely of carbon atoms arranged in 12 pentagonal and a variable number of hexagonal rings. C₆₀ is the archetypal example.
- **Euler's rule application (2 marks):** Using the three constraints — $3V = 2E$, $5F_5 + 6F_6 = 2E$, and $V - E + F = 2$ — and eliminating $V$ and $E$, we obtain $F_5 = 12$. Hence every closed fullerene has exactly 12 pentagons, independent of total carbon count.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is graphene? Mention any three remarkable properties that distinguish it from other carbon allotropes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Graphene is a single-atom-thick, 2D planar sheet of sp²-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice — the basic building block of graphite, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes.
- **Three distinguishing properties (2 marks, 1 each approx):**
  1. Exceptional **electron mobility (~ 200,000 cm²/V·s)** and ballistic transport at room temperature.
  2. Record **Young's modulus (~ 1 TPa)** and **intrinsic strength (~ 130 GPa)** — among the strongest materials ever measured.
  3. Ultra-high **thermal conductivity (~ 5000 W/m·K)** exceeding that of diamond and copper.
- (Also acceptable: zero band gap, massless Dirac fermions, 2.3% white-light absorption per layer, theoretical specific surface area of 2630 m²/g.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, the student answers **one of two** alternatives. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — C₆₀ Fullerene Structure**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the structure of C₆₀ (Buckminsterfullerene). Discuss its geometry, hybridization, bond-length distribution, and the Isolated Pentagon Rule. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

- **Discovery & name (1 mark):** Discovered in 1985 by Kroto, Curl, and Smalley (Nobel Prize 1996). Named after architect Buckminster Fuller, whose geodesic domes share the same geometry.
- **Geometry (2 marks):** C₆₀ has the shape of a *truncated icosahedron* (Archimedean solid with Ih symmetry). It contains 12 regular pentagons and 20 regular hexagons, 60 vertices, and 90 edges. Each carbon lies at the vertex shared by one pentagon and two hexagons.
- **Bond lengths (1.5 marks):** Two distinct C–C bond types:
  - **6:6 bonds** (hexagon–hexagon fusion): **1.38 Å**, double-bond character.
  - **6:5 bonds** (hexagon–pentagon fusion): **1.45 Å**, single-bond character.
  - This bond-length alternation means only the 6:6 bonds are chemically reactive in addition reactions.
- **Hybridization (1 mark):** Carbons are not pure sp²; the curvature causes pyramidalization giving a hybridization of **sp²·²⁸** (≈ 28% sp³ character). This is why C₆₀ is reactive despite being aromatic.
- **Isolated Pentagon Rule (1.5 marks):** Stable fullerenes have *no two pentagons sharing an edge*. C₆₀ is the *smallest* molecule satisfying this. Adjacent pentagons cause enormous angle strain (8-membered ring fusion problem). The IPR is the geometric reason fullerenes are stable and not mini-diamond cages.

**(b)** Using Euler's formula and the trivalent-cage constraints, derive the result that every fullerene has exactly 12 pentagonal faces. Verify your derivation for C₇₀. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (b):**

- **State the three constraints (1.5 marks):**
  1. $3V = 2E$ (each C is 3-connected)
  2. $5F_5 + 6F_6 = 2E$ (sum of polygon sides = 2 × edges)
  3. $V - E + F = 2$ (Euler), with $F = F_5 + F_6$
- **Derivation (3.5 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2 of these notes, eliminating $V$ and $E$ yields $F_5 = 12$ — independent of $V$, $F_6$, or molecular size. **(Full algebraic steps as in Section 3.2 must be reproduced.)**
- **Verification for C₇₀ (2 marks):**
  - $V = 70 \Rightarrow E = 3V/2 = 105$.
  - $F_5 = 12$ (from the result).
  - $V - E + F = 2 \Rightarrow F = 2 - V + E = 2 - 70 + 105 = 37$.
  - $F_6 = F - F_5 = 37 - 12 = 25$.
  - Check: $5(12) + 6(25) = 60 + 150 = 210 = 2E = 2(105)$ ✓
  - **Conclusion:** C₇₀ has $V = 70$, $E = 105$, $F_5 = 12$, $F_6 = 25$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Graphene Structure**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of graphene in detail. Discuss the hybridization, lattice geometry, electronic band structure, and Dirac cones. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

- **Definition & background (0.5 mark):** Graphene is a 2D, single-atom-thick sheet of sp² carbon, first isolated by mechanical exfoliation in 2004 (Geim, Novoselov — Nobel 2010).
- **Hybridization (1.5 marks):** Each carbon is **sp² hybridized** with three in-plane σ-bonds separated by 120°, and one electron in the unhybridized $p_z$ orbital perpendicular to the plane. The σ-framework forms the honeycomb skeleton; the $p_z$ electrons form a delocalised π-system above and below the plane.
- **Lattice geometry (2 marks):** A 2D honeycomb lattice is mathematically a *bipartite* lattice with a 2-atom basis (sub-lattices A and B). The hexagonal unit cell has lattice constant $a_0 = 2.46$ Å and contains 2 carbon atoms. The C–C bond length is **1.42 Å**. The unit-cell area is $A = (\sqrt{3}/2) a_0^2 \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-20}$ m².
- **Electronic band structure & Dirac cones (3 marks):**
  - Graphene's π and π* bands meet at 6 points in the Brillouin zone (K and K'). At these points, the band dispersion is *linear* in $k$: $E(k) = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \vec{k} - \vec{K} \vert$, where $v_F \approx 1 \times 10^6$ m/s.
  - The linear dispersion gives a *conical* shape (Dirac cone) — hence the name.
  - Charge carriers obey the *massless Dirac equation*, behaving as relativistic particles with zero effective mass.
  - **Result:** Graphene is a *zero-bandgap semiconductor* / semi-metal; it cannot be switched off — a major challenge for digital electronics (solved by nanoribbons, bilayer gating, or chemical functionalization).

**(b)** List and briefly explain four extraordinary properties of graphene that make it a leading candidate for next-generation electronic and structural materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (b):**

1. **Very high electron mobility** (~ 200,000 cm²/V·s at room temperature, up to 10⁶ cm²/V·s in suspended samples) — far higher than silicon (~ 1400 cm²/V·s). Enables ultra-fast transistors. **(1.75 marks)**
2. **Exceptional mechanical strength** — Young's modulus ≈ 1 TPa, intrinsic strength ≈ 130 GPa, ~ 200× stronger than structural steel. Useful in flexible electronics and structural composites. **(1.75 marks)**
3. **Very high thermal conductivity** — ~ 5000 W/(m·K), exceeding copper and diamond. Promising for heat-spreading in microelectronics. **(1.75 marks)**
4. **Optical transparency & high surface area** — Absorbs only 2.3% of white light per layer; theoretical specific surface area = 2630 m²/g. Perfect for transparent electrodes (replacing ITO) and supercapacitors. **(1.75 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing pentagon and hexagon counts in C₆₀:** Many students write "C₆₀ has 60 hexagons." It has only **20 hexagons + 12 pentagons = 32 faces**.
> 2. **Hybridization of C₆₀:** It is **NOT pure sp²**; do not write it as such. Always state **sp²·²⁸** with brief justification (curvature → pyramidalization).
> 3. **Graphene band gap:** Students often incorrectly classify graphene as a *metal*. It is a **zero-bandgap semiconductor** (semi-metal). The band gap is *zero*, but density of states at the Fermi level is also zero — distinguishing it from a true metal.
> 4. **Two distinct bond lengths in C₆₀:** Mark is lost if you state a *single* bond length for C₆₀. Always give **both** 1.38 Å and 1.45 Å.
> 5. **Euler's rule derivation:** Examiners expect you to *show all three constraints and solve them* — not just state "$F_5 = 12$."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Graphene** = 2D planar, sp², honeycomb lattice, $a_0 = 2.46$ Å, $d_{CC} = 1.42$ Å, zero band gap, Dirac cones, massless carriers with $v_F \approx 10^6$ m/s.
- [x] **C₆₀ Fullerene** = 0D cage, truncated icosahedron, 60 C, 90 bonds, 32 faces (12 pentagons + 20 hexagons), $I_h$ symmetry.
- [x] **Two bond types in C₆₀:** 6:6 bond = **1.38 Å** (double bond), 6:5 bond = **1.45 Å** (single bond).
- [x] **Hybridization in C₆₀** is sp²·²⁸ — not pure sp² — due to pyramidalization.
- [x] **Isolated Pentagon Rule (IPR):** C₆₀ is the smallest IPR fullerene; no two pentagons share an edge.
- [x] **Euler's formula for fullerenes:** $V - E + F = 2$, with $3V = 2E$ and $5F_5 + 6F_6 = 2E$.
- [x] **Topological invariant:** $F_5 = 12$ in *every* closed fullerene cage, regardless of size.
- [x] **Graphite interlayer spacing** = 3.35 Å (van der Waals gap).
- [x] **Specific surface area of graphene** = 2630 m²/g (theoretical).
- [x] **Nobel Prizes:** C₆₀ → 1996 (Kroto, Curl, Smalley); Graphene → 2010 (Geim, Novoselov).
- [x] **Engineering uses:** Fullerenes → OPV, MRI contrast, antiviral, superconductors. Graphene → transparent electrodes, supercapacitors, sensors, composites, ultrafast photonics.
- [x] **Curvature → reactivity:** As curvature increases (graphene < nanotube < fullerene), the sp² character decreases and chemical reactivity increases.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 ABS (Acrylonitrile–Butadiene–Styrene)

**Formal Definition:**
ABS is a **terpolymer** (an amorphous copolymer built from three distinct monomers) composed of **Acrylonitrile (CH₂=CH–CN)**, **1,3-Butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)**, and **Styrene (C₆H₅–CH=CH₂)**. The polybutadiene component is typically grafted onto a styrene–acrylonitrile (SAN) backbone, producing a tough, impact-resistant engineering thermoplastic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Thermoplastic:** A polymer that softens on heating and hardens on cooling, retaining processability and toughness for technical/structural use (e.g., automobile dashboards, helmets).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of ABS as a **"triple-layered sandwich"**:
- **Styrene** gives the rigidity and glossy surface (like the firm bread of a sandwich).
- **Acrylonitrile** provides chemical and heat resistance (like a layer of cheese that resists melting).
- **Butadiene** delivers the rubbery, shock-absorbing flexibility (like a soft cushioning filling).

Just as the sandwich is tougher than any single bread slice, ABS is far tougher than plain polystyrene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ABS belongs to the family of **heterophase polymers** — its rubber domains (polybutadiene) are dispersed in a hard plastic matrix (SAN), giving it the unique property of absorbing sudden impacts.

---

### 1.2 Kevlar (Poly-*para*-phenylene Terephthalamide, PPTA)

**Formal Definition:**
Kevlar is a **para-aramid** (aromatic polyamide) produced by the **low-temperature solution polycondensation** of **terephthaloyl chloride (ClOC–C₆H₄–COCl)** with **p-phenylenediamine (H₂N–C₆H₄–NH₂)** in an amide solvent system. Its structure features amide linkages (–CO–NH–) connecting *para*-substituted aromatic rings, enabling extensive hydrogen bonding and chain alignment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Aramid:** A polymer in which amide linkages (–CONH–) directly attach to aromatic rings. *Para*-aramids have substituents at 1,4-positions, allowing linear, rod-like chains that crystallize strongly.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **bundle of dry spaghetti, but each strand is also wrapped in a tight Velcro sleeve**. The rigid, rod-like Kevlar chains align parallel to each other (like spaghetti in a box), while the **hydrogen bonds between N–H and C=O groups act like Velcro** locking the strands together. This makes Kevlar **5 times stronger than steel by weight**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The aromatic rings and the *para*-linkage force the polymer chains to adopt a fully extended, rod-like conformation — this is the structural secret behind Kevlar's extraordinary tensile strength (≈**3,620 MPa** density normalized).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | ABS | Kevlar (PPTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | **1.04–1.07** | **1.44** |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 33–50 | ≈ **3,620** |
| Glass Transition (*T*<sub>g</sub>) | ≈ **105 °C** | Does not melt (decomposes ≈ **500 °C**) |
| Common Form | Pellets/granules | Fibers/yarns |
| Repeat Unit | –(CH₂–CH(C₆H₅))<sub>x</sub>–(CH₂–CH=CH–CH₂)<sub>y</sub>–(CH₂–CHCN)<sub>z</sub>– | –(–NH–C₆H₄–NH–CO–C₆H₄–CO–)<sub>n</sub>– |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ABS — Synthesis, Structure, and Property Relations

#### 2.1.1 Two-Stage Synthesis (Emulsion Graft Copolymerization)

ABS is industrially produced in **two stages**:

**Stage 1 — Polybutadiene Latex Preparation:**
1,3-Butadiene is polymerized via **emulsion polymerization** in water using potassium persulfate (K₂S₂O₈) as a free-radical initiator and a soap (sodium dodecyl sulfate) as emulsifier. This yields a milky **polybutadiene (PB) latex** of fine rubber particles (≈ 0.1 µm).

**Stage 2 — Grafting SAN onto PB:**
Styrene and acrylonitrile are added to the PB latex along with a radical initiator. The growing poly(styrene-co-acrylonitrile) chains abstract allylic H atoms from the butadiene backbone, generating radicals on the rubber. The SAN chains then **graft** onto the rubber particles, forming a *core–shell* morphology:

- **Core:** rubbery polybutadiene (impact absorber).
- **Shell:** grafted SAN (rigid, stiff, matrix former).
- **Matrix:** free SAN copolymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why grafting matters:** The SAN chains covalently bonded to the rubber surface act as *compatibilizers* — they prevent the rubber particles from coalescing and ensure the impact energy is transferred from the brittle matrix into the soft rubber particles (craze/ shear-yield toughening).

#### 2.1.2 Property–Structure Correlations in ABS

- **Acrylonitrile content (≈ 15–35%)** → increases chemical resistance, hardness, and *T*<sub>g</sub>. High CN also introduces dipole–dipole interactions.
- **Butadiene content (≈ 5–30%)** → governs impact strength and low-temperature toughness. Higher butadiene = more rubber domains.
- **Styrene content** → provides rigidity, processability, and surface gloss.
- **Molecular weight** → high *M*<sub>w</sub> gives better mechanical strength; very high *M*<sub>w</sub> lowers melt flow.

#### 2.1.3 Engineering Utility of ABS
- **Automotive:** dashboard components, interior trim, bumper parts.
- **Electronics (E&E):** housings for computers, TVs, printers (due to electroplating ability and dimensional stability).
- **Consumer goods:** LEGO bricks, luggage, helmets, toys.
- **Pipes & fittings:** drainage, vent pipes, DWV systems.
- **3D printing:** FDM filaments (due to low warping and good surface finish).

---

### 2.2 Kevlar — Synthesis, Structure, and Property Relations

#### 2.2.1 Synthesis: Low-Temperature Solution Polycondensation

Kevlar is synthesized by the **Schotten–Baumann-type condensation** between **terephthaloyl chloride** and **p-phenylenediamine** in a polar aprotic solvent mixture (NMP = *N*-methyl-2-pyrrolidone with CaCl₂) at low temperature (≈ 0–10 °C):

$$n\,\text{ClOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COCl} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{N–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NMP, }-10\,°\text{C}} [-\text{NH–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NH–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO-}]_n + 2n\,\text{HCl}$$

The byproduct **HCl** is neutralized by Ca(OH)₂ or by a tertiary amine base. Low temperature suppresses side reactions (such as hydrolysis of the acid chloride) and keeps the polymer chains linear and high in molecular weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two acid chlorides are used (not carboxylic acids):** Carboxylic acids are too unreactive for high-*M*<sub>w</sub> polycondensation; acid chlorides react readily with amines at low temperature, ensuring long, defect-free chains needed for the rigid rod architecture.

#### 2.2.2 Anisotropic Solution Spinning ("Liquid Crystal" Spinning)

The polymerization yields an **optically anisotropic (lyotropic liquid-crystalline) dope** in concentrated H₂SO₄. The rigid rod-like chains align spontaneously into nematic domains. This dope is extruded through spinnerets into a coagulation bath, where the chains solidify in their already-aligned state — producing fibers with near-perfect orientation along the fiber axis.

#### 2.2.3 Property–Structure Correlations in Kevlar

- **Aromatic backbone** → high thermal stability (no melting, char yield high).
- **Para-linkage** → linear, rod-like chains → high crystallinity → high modulus.
- **Interchain H-bonds** (N–H···O=C) → secondary bonding locking chains together.
- **Aromatic π–π stacking** → additional non-covalent reinforcement between adjacent chains.
- **No hydrolysable aliphatic groups** → excellent chemical resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tensile strength vs. impact strength:** Kevlar is exceptionally strong in **tension along the fiber axis** but is weaker in compression or transverse directions — its chains can buckle like a deck of cards sliding sideways. This is why composite laminates are used in body armor.

#### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility of Kevlar
- **Ballistics / Defense:** bullet-proof vests, helmets, spall liners.
- **Aerospace:** aircraft body composites, missile casings, space structures.
- **Automotive:** tire cords (replacing steel belts), brake pads, clutch linings.
- **Marine & ropes:** mooring lines, high-performance climbing ropes.
- **Sports goods:** racing sails, kayaks, bicycle tires, body armor for racing drivers.
- **Civil engineering:** reinforcement in concrete, bridge cable wraps.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles all KTU-relevant formulas, key unit definitions, and boundary conditions for this topic.

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Equation / Value | Units | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M_n$ | Number-average molecular weight | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | g/mol | Predicts tensile strength of polymers |
| $M_w$ | Weight-average molecular weight | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | g/mol | Reflects entanglement contribution |
| PDI | Polydispersity index | $\text{PDI} = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | dimensionless | PDI ≈ 1 → monodisperse; > 1 → polydisperse |
| Degree of polymerization | Average monomer units per chain | $X_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | unitless | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit |
| Carothers equation | Step-growth DP at conversion *p* | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | unitless | Kevlar synthesis (condensation) |
| Carothers (non-stoichiometric) | With stoichiometric imbalance *r* | $X_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | unitless | Used when monomers not in 1:1 ratio |
| Tensile strength (rule of mix) | Composite fiber strength | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | MPa | $V_f$ + $V_m$ = 1 |
| Young's modulus of fiber | Hooke's law | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | GPa | Kevlar *E* ≈ 70–130 GPa |
| H-bond energy (N–H···O=C) | Non-covalent bond | ≈ 8–40 (per H-bond) | kJ/mol | Source of interchain cohesion in Kevlar |
| ABS density | Engineering metric | ≈ **1.06** | g/cm³ | Lightweight, buoyancy in design |
| Kevlar density | Engineering metric | ≈ **1.44** | g/cm³ | Used in specific-strength calculations |

> [!TIP]
> **Specific strength** is the property most often tested in KTU. It is tensile strength divided by density: $\sigma_{sp} = \sigma / \rho$. Kevlar's specific strength exceeds steel — a classic viva question.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Carothers Equation Derivation (for Kevlar Step-Growth Polymerization)

Kevlar is made by step-growth (condensation) polymerization. The Carothers equation gives the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$ as a function of the extent of reaction *p*.

**Step 1: Define the extent of reaction.**
Let $N_0$ be the total initial number of monomer (functional group) molecules. After time *t*, let $N$ be the number of unreacted molecules still present.

$$p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$$

So,
$$N = N_0 (1-p)$$

**Step 2: Relate total molecules to original count.**
For a bifunctional A–A + B–B system (Kevlar-type), the total number of *molecules* (monomers + oligomers + polymer) remaining is *N*. The number of *bonds* formed is $\frac{N_0 - N}{2}$ (since each bond consumes two functional groups but joins two molecules into one, the number of new bonds = number of molecules lost, which is $N_0 - N$, divided by 1 — but for a system of $N_0$ bifunctional monomers, the number of linkages formed = $N_0 - N$).

For a perfectly stoichiometric system, the number of repeat units in the polymer = number of bonds formed = $N_0 - N$.

**Step 3: Compute the degree of polymerization $X_n$.**
$X_n$ = (total monomer units initially) / (total molecules remaining at extent *p*).

$$X_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)}$$

$$\boxed{X_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

**Step 4: Apply to Kevlar (stoichiometric case, r = 1).**
For a 1:1 molar ratio of terephthaloyl chloride : *p*-phenylenediamine, the imbalance ratio $r = N_A / N_B = 1$. Hence,

$$X_n = \frac{1+1}{1+1-2p} = \frac{2}{2(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

**Step 5: High-*M*<sub>w</sub> requirement.**
To reach $M_n \approx 20{,}000$ g/mol for Kevlar (repeat unit *M*<sub>0</sub> ≈ 238 g/mol), the required $X_n$ is:

$$X_n = \frac{20000}{238} \approx 84$$

Plugging back:

$$p = 1 - \frac{1}{X_n} = 1 - \frac{1}{84} = 0.988$$

Thus the reaction must proceed to **≈ 98.8% conversion** — a key reason for using highly reactive acid chlorides and rigorously dried solvents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-growth implication:** $X_n$ depends only on *p*; high molecular weight demands near-quantitative conversion. Chain-growth (like radical polymerization of styrene) does not have this restriction.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ABS vs. Kevlar

| Feature | ABS | Kevlar (PPTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Polymer class | Thermoplastic terpolymer | Aramid (polyamide) |
| Polymerization type | Chain-growth (radical) | Step-growth (condensation) |
| Number of monomers | 3 (terpolymer) | 2 (alternating) |
| Repeat unit molar mass (g/mol) | ≈ 100–150 (average) | **238.24** |
| Morphology | Amorphous + rubber domains | Semi-crystalline, highly oriented |
| Tacticity/regiochemistry | Random (atactic SAN) | Regioregular (all *para*) |
| Bonding | Covalent (with weak VdW between chains) | Covalent + H-bonding + π–π stacking |
| Chemical resistance | Moderate (improves with ↑ AN) | Excellent |
| Hydrolytic stability | Good | Excellent (no aliphatic amides) |
| Recycling | **Easily remelted & recycled** | **Not melt-recyclable** (decomposes before melting) |
| Specific strength (kN·m/kg) | ≈ 50 | ≈ **2,500** (50× higher) |
| Density (g/cm³) | **1.06** | **1.44** |
| Cost | Low–moderate | Very high |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A Kevlar-type step-growth polymerization uses *p*-phenylenediamine and terephthaloyl chloride in a 1:1 molar ratio. The repeat unit molar mass is **238 g/mol**. After 4 hours, the conversion is **99%**. Calculate (a) the number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$, and (b) the number-average molecular weight $M_n$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Apply the Carothers equation:**

$$X_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.99} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

**(b) Compute $M_n$:**

$$M_n = X_n \times M_0 = 100 \times 238 = 23{,}800 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always keep *p* as a **decimal** in the Carothers equation. Writing *p* = 99 gives $X_n = 1/(1-99)$ — a negative number! KTU examiners check this.

**Extended Question (with stoichiometric imbalance):**
If the diamine is in 2 mol% excess, then *r* = (100 − 2)/100 = 0.98. At *p* = 0.99 (conversion of the limiting monomer):

$$X_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \frac{1.98}{1.98 - 2(0.98)(0.99)} = \frac{1.98}{1.98 - 1.9404} = \frac{1.98}{0.0396} = 50$$

So even **a 2% stoichiometric imbalance** drops $X_n$ by half — a powerful lesson on monomer purity.
