# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are compulsory short-answer items testing direct recall of definitions, formulas, or one-line applications. Answers should be 3–5 lines.

### Question 1: Define Electrode Potential. How is it measured? **[3 marks]** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **electrode potential** of an electrode is the measure of the tendency of the electrode to either gain or lose electrons when placed in contact with its own ions in solution. It is measured in volts (V).

By convention:
- **Oxidation potential** = tendency to lose electrons (anode process)
- **Reduction potential** = tendency to gain electrons (cathode process)

It is measured by coupling the unknown electrode with a **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** and measuring the cell EMF using a high-impedance voltmeter (so that no current flows and the reading corresponds to the reversible potential). The standard hydrogen electrode is assigned a value of **0.000 V** as the primary reference. To obtain the standard electrode potential, the measurement is carried out under standard conditions: 1 M ion concentration, 1 atm gas pressure, and 298 K temperature.

---

### Question 2: What is a Reference Electrode? Name any two and state their potentials. **[3 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

A **reference electrode** is an electrode whose potential is accurately known, stable, and reproducible under fixed conditions, used as a benchmark for measuring the potential of any unknown electrode.

The three most common reference electrodes are:

1. **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE):** E° = **0.000 V** (primary reference)
2. **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE):** E = **+0.244 V** vs SHE (uses Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ in saturated KCl)
3. **Silver/Silver Chloride Electrode (Ag/AgCl):** E = **+0.222 V** vs SHE (uses Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl)

The SCE and Ag/AgCl electrodes are widely preferred over SHE in routine laboratory and field measurements because SHE is fragile, requires continuous H₂ gas supply, and is sensitive to poisoning of the platinum black surface.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Answer should be comprehensive with proper diagrams, derivations, and numerical calculations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** State and derive the Nernst equation for a single electrode and a complete electrochemical cell at 298 K. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For the cell $Zn_{(s)} \mid Zn^{2+}_{(0.1\,M)} \mid\mid Ag^+_{(0.01\,M)} \mid Ag_{(s)}$, calculate the EMF of the cell at 298 K. Given: $E°_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76 \, V$, $E°_{Ag^+/Ag} = +0.80 \, V$. Also calculate $\Delta G°$ for the cell reaction. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Nernst Equation

**Statement:** The Nernst equation gives the relationship between the electrode/cell potential and the concentrations (activities) of the chemical species involved.

For a general cell reaction: $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$

$$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$$

**Derivation:**

From Gibbs–Helmholtz:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

For an electrochemical cell, the maximum non-expansion work is:

$$-\Delta G = nFE \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta G = -nFE$$

Substituting:

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$:

$$E = E° - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

At 298 K, $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V, so:

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

**For a single electrode** $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M$:

$$E = E° + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10}[M^{n+}]$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1:** Half-reactions.
- Anode: $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $Ag^+ + e^- \rightarrow Ag$ (×2)
- Overall: $Zn + 2Ag^+ \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2Ag$ (n = 2)

**Step 2:** $E°_{cell} = 0.80 - (-0.76) = 1.56 \, V$

**Step 3:** $Q = \dfrac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Ag^+]^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{(0.01)^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^{-4}} = 1000 = 10^3$

**Step 4:** Apply Nernst.

$$E_{cell} = 1.56 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log_{10}(10^3)$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.56 - 0.02955 \times 3$$

$$E_{cell} = 1.56 - 0.0887$$

$$E_{cell} = +1.4713 \, V$$

**Step 5:** $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -2 \times 96500 \times 1.56$

$$\Delta G° = -301080 \, J = -301.08 \, kJ$$

**Result:** $E_{cell} = +1.4713 \, V$, $\Delta G° = -301.08 \, kJ$ (negative, confirming spontaneity).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode. Why is it difficult to use SHE routinely? What are the secondary reference electrodes used to overcome these difficulties? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The standard EMF of the cell $Fe \mid Fe^{2+} \mid\mid H^+ \mid H_{2(g, \, 1\,atm)} \mid Pt$ is +0.44 V at 298 K. Write the cell reaction and calculate (i) the standard electrode potential of the $Fe^{2+}/Fe$ electrode, (ii) the equilibrium constant of the cell reaction, and (iii) the EMF of the cell when $[Fe^{2+}] = 0.01 \, M$ and $[H^+] = 1 \, M$. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — SHE and Secondary Reference Electrodes

**Construction of SHE (refer to diagram in SECTION 4.3):**

The Standard Hydrogen Electrode consists of:
1. A **platinum wire** sealed into a glass tube
2. The lower end is **platinum black** (finely divided Pt) which catalyses the reaction and provides a large surface area
3. The electrode is **immersed in 1 M HCl (or 1 M H₂SO₄)** solution
4. **Pure, dry hydrogen gas at 1 atm** is bubbled through the side arm, surrounding the Pt black

**Working (Half-reaction):**

$$2H^+_{(1\,M)} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_{2(g, \, 1\,atm)} \quad E° = 0.000 \, V$$

- The Pt black adsorbs H₂ gas, allowing it to be in equilibrium with H⁺ ions
- The Pt surface acts as an inert electron carrier

**Difficulties with SHE (Why not used routinely):**

1. **Fragility** — Pt black surface is easily poisoned by impurities (sulphides, arsenic, organic vapours)
2. **Explosive gas handling** — requires continuous supply of pure H₂ at exactly 1 atm
3. **Temperature sensitivity** — difficult to maintain exactly 298 K
4. **Cannot be transported** — must be assembled and used in situ
5. **Platinum is very expensive**

**Secondary reference electrodes:**

1. **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** — E = +0.244 V vs SHE
   - Construction: Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ in saturated KCl
   - Robust, easy to maintain, widely used in pH meters and potentiometric titrations

2. **Silver/Silver Chloride Electrode (Ag/AgCl)** — E = +0.222 V vs SHE
   - Construction: Ag wire coated with AgCl in KCl
   - Used in biomedical sensors, marine applications (seawater is salty)

3. **Mercury/Mercury Sulphate Electrode** — E = +0.615 V vs SHE (in saturated K₂SO₄)
   - Used in cement/concrete pore solution studies for corrosion monitoring

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1:** Write cell reaction.
- Anode: $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$
- Overall: $Fe + 2H^+ \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + H_2$ (n = 2)

**(i) Standard electrode potential of Fe²⁺/Fe:**

$$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$$

$$0.44 = 0.00 - E°_{Fe^{2+}/Fe}$$

$$E°_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44 \, V$$

**(ii) Equilibrium constant:**

$$\log_{10} K = \frac{nE°}{0.0591} = \frac{2 \times 0.44}{0.0591} = 14.89$$

$$K = 10^{14.89} = 7.76 \times 10^{14}$$

**(iii) EMF at non-standard conditions:**

$$Q = \frac{[Fe^{2+}][P_{H_2}]}{[H^+]^2} = \frac{0.01 \times 1}{(1)^2} = 0.01 = 10^{-2}$$

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log_{10}(10^{-2})$$

$$E = 0.44 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2)$$

$$E = 0.44 + 0.0591 = 0.4991 \, V$$

**Result:**
(i) $E°_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44 \, V$
(ii) $K = 7.76 \times 10^{14}$
(iii) $E_{cell} = +0.4991 \, V$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Nernst Equation Problems (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Single Electrode Formula** — Students write $E = E° - (0.0591/n) \log[M^{n+}]$ instead of $E = E° + (0.0591/n) \log[M^{n+}]$. This error alone **costs 2–3 marks** out of 7.
>
> 2. **Confusing Oxidation and Reduction Potentials** — When a question says "oxidation potential" and you use the standard reduction table, you MUST flip the sign. Examiners specifically test this.
>
> 3. **Wrong Number of Electrons (n)** — For reactions like $Fe^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Fe$, n = 3 (not 1, not 6). For $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$, n = 2. **Always re-balance and count electrons from the balanced equation.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Include the Activity of Solids = 1** — When [M] is in the denominator of Q, students often include it. This wastes 30 seconds and confuses them. Memorise: **Solids and pure liquids are NEVER in Q.**
>
> 5. **Wrong Q Construction for Full Cell** — Q has products over reactants, with all **aqueous species and gases** included, but **electrons are NEVER in Q** (they cancel out anyway). 
>
> 6. **Temperature Confusion** — If the question specifies a temperature other than 298 K, do NOT use 0.0591. Use $2.303 RT / F$ directly with the given T.
>
> 7. **Units of E and $\Delta G$** — Always report E in **volts (V)** and $\Delta G$ in **kJ/mol**. $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ gives joules — divide by 1000.
>
> 8. **Concentration Cell Error** — For a concentration cell, $E°_{cell} = 0$ V, but students often look up the standard potentials anyway and add them. This is **the most common mistake** in concentration-cell problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Use this the night before the exam!)**

### Core Definitions
- [x] **Electrochemical Cell** — converts chemical ↔ electrical energy via redox
- [x] **Electrode Potential** — tendency of an electrode to gain/lose electrons (in V)
- [x] **Standard Electrode Potential (E°)** — measured at 1 M, 1 atm, 298 K, vs SHE
- [x] **Nernst Equation** — relates E to E° and concentrations/activities
- [x] **Reference Electrode** — electrode of fixed, known potential for measurement
- [x] **SHE** — primary reference, E° = 0.000 V
- [x] **SCE** — E = +0.244 V (Hg/Hg₂Cl₂/sat KCl)
- [x] **Ag/AgCl** — E = +0.222 V

### Must-Memorise Formulas
- [x] $E = E° - (0.0591/n) \log_{10} Q$ (cell, 298 K)
- [x] $E = E° + (0.0591/n) \log_{10}[M^{n+}]$ (single electrode, 298 K)
- [x] $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = E°_{right} - E°_{left}$
- [x] $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$
- [x] $E°_{cell} = (0.0591/n) \log_{10} K$
- [x] $E = -0.0591 \times pH$ (hydrogen electrode, 1 atm H₂)

### Must-Remember Constants
- [x] $R = 8.314 \, J \, K^{-1} \, mol^{-1}$
- [x] $F = 96487 \, C \, mol^{-1} \approx 96500 \, C \, mol^{-1}$
- [x] Nernst constant at 298 K = **0.0591 V** (or **0.02569 V** for ln form)
- [x] E° of SCE = +0.244 V (saturated KCl)
- [x] E° of Ag/AgCl = +0.222 V (saturated KCl)

### Critical Rules
- [x] Cell is **galvanic (spontaneous)** if **E°_cell > 0**; **electrolytic** if E°_cell < 0
- [x] For **concentration cells**, E°_cell = 0
- [x] **Activity of pure solid = 1** (omit from Q)
- [x] **Electrons are NEVER in Q**
- [x] Always re-balance the cell reaction and count total electrons transferred (n)
- [x] For hydrogen electrode: $E = -0.0591 \times pH$ at 1 atm H₂

### Conversion Tips
- [x] SCE → SHE: add 0.244 V
- [x] SHE → SCE: subtract 0.244 V
- [x] Higher ion concentration → more positive E (for reduction)
- [x] Lower ion concentration → more negative E (for reduction)

### Engineering Applications to Quote in Exam
- [x] Battery design (Li-ion, lead-acid, fuel cells)
- [x] Corrosion prediction (Nernst + electrochemical series)
- [x] pH meter (glass electrode + Nernst)
- [x] Ion-selective electrodes in medical diagnostics
- [x] Electroplating and electrometallurgy

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SHE & Calomel Electrode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** SHE & Calomel Electrode # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) ### Formal Definition The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is a redox electrode which has been assigned an arbitrary standard electrode potential of **zero (0.000 V)** as a reference point for measuring the standard electrode potentials of all other electrodes. It is formally known as the **Primary Reference Electrode** in electrochemistry. **Half-Cell Reaction:** $$2H^{+}(aq, a=1) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g, 1\,atm)$$ The reaction occurs under standard thermodynamic conditions: - **Temperature:** **298.15 K (25 °C)** - **Activity of H⁺ (a_H⁺):** **1 (unity)** - **Pressure of H₂ gas:** **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Highlight:** The SHE is treated as a *primary standard* by IUPAC. Its potential is **defined** as zero, not *measured* as zero. This is a vital distinction that examiners often test. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are measuring the height of a mountain. Before you can say a mountain is "8,000 feet high," you need a fixed starting reference point — the **sea level**. Similarly, in electrochemistry, before we can say a copper electrode has a potential of "+0.34 V," we need a universally agreed-upon "sea level" for potential. The **SHE is the sea level of electrochemistry**. > [!NOTE] > **Why Hydrogen?** > Hydrogen was chosen because it is the simplest element (one proton, one electron), it can be readily produced in pure gaseous form, and its reaction is reversible, making it a thermodynamically clean reference. --- ## 1.2 Calomel Electrode ### Formal Definition The **Calomel Electrode** is a **secondary (or auxiliary) reference electrode** based on the mercury(I) chloride (calomel, Hg₂Cl₂) redox couple. It is widely used as a practical alternative to the SHE because of its superior stability, ease of construction, and reliable potential. **Half-Cell Reaction:** $$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$ ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the SHE as an "ideal but fragile lab instrument" — like a perfectly calibrated scientific clock that cannot be moved. The Calomel Electrode is the **"rugged, portable field instrument"** — slightly less fundamental, but extremely practical, durable, and easy to use in routine laboratory potentiometry. It is the "workhorse" reference electrode in most pH meters and analytical laboratories. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Calomel"?** The name comes from the Greek word *kalos melas* meaning "beautiful black," because calomel darkens upon exposure to light (it disproportionates to Hg and HgCl₂), which is why the electrode is constructed in a light-proof glass tube. ### Variants of Calomel Electrode | Variant | KCl Concentration | Potential at 25 °C (vs SHE) | |---|---|---| | **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE)** | Saturated KCl | **+0.2412 V** | | **Normal Calomel Electrode (NCE)** | 1.0 M KCl | **+0.2800 V** | | **Decinormal Calomel Electrode (DCE)** | 0.1 M KCl | **+0.3338 V** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **SCE** is the most commonly used variant because it requires no preparation of a standard solution — you simply use a saturated solution, which is self-standardizing at a given temperature. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the SHE ### Construction (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Platinum Wire (Electrical Contact):** A platinum wire is sealed into a glass tube. Pt is used because it is **inert** and conducts electricity. 2. **Platinum Foil (Substrate):** The wire is welded to a Pt foil, which increases the surface area. 3. **Platinum Black Coating (Catalyst):** The Pt foil is electroplated with finely divided "platinum black" to dramatically **increase the active surface area**, ensuring rapid equilibrium between H₂ gas and H⁺ ions. 4. **Acid Solution (Electrolyte):** The electrode is immersed in an **acidic solution** (typically 1 M HCl) where a = 1. 5. **Hydrogen Gas (Reactant):** Pure H₂ gas at **1 atm** is bubbled over the platinum black. 6. **Why it works:** At the Pt surface, the following equilibrium is established: $$2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g)$$ The Pt black acts as a catalyst, neither consuming nor producing electrons — it merely provides a surface for electron transfer. ### Why the SHE is Defined (Not Measured) - The absolute potential of a single electrode **cannot be measured** (only potential *differences* are measurable with a voltmeter). - By international convention (IUPAC 1953), the SHE is **defined** to have E° = 0.000 V at all temperatures. - All other standard potentials are **derived relative to SHE**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** SHE potentials form the foundation of **electromotive force (EMF) series**, the **electrochemical series**, and the design of **batteries, fuel cells, and corrosion protection systems**. --- ## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of the Calomel Electrode ### Construction (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Inner Glass Tube (Pt Wire):** A Pt wire is sealed inside an inner glass tube, with its tip immersed in **pure mercury (Hg)** at the bottom. 2. **Mercury Layer:** A small pool of Hg rests at the base of the inner tube. 3. **Calomel Paste:** Above the Hg is a paste made of **Hg + Hg₂Cl₂ (calomel)**, which establishes the redox couple. 4. **Porous Plug / Asbestos Wick:** A porous barrier (glass frit, asbestos fiber, or Vycor disc) allows ionic contact with the outer KCl solution. 5. **KCl Solution (Outer Chamber):** The outer jacket is filled with a KCl solution of known concentration (saturated, 1 M, or 0.1 M). 6. **Salt Bridge Connection:** The KCl solution establishes ionic contact with the external cell whose potential is being measured. ### The Chemistry at the Interface At the calomel paste / KCl interface, the equilibrium is: $$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$ **Nernst Equation Applied:** $$E_{Calomel} = E^{\circ}_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}/Hg} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{[Cl^{-}]^{2}}{1}$$ Since the activity of solid Hg₂Cl₂ and pure liquid Hg are unity: $$E_{Calomel} = E^{\circ}_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}/Hg} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Because the reaction *produces* Cl⁻ ions, an **increase in [Cl⁻] decreases the electrode potential** (Le Chatelier's principle applied to Nernst). This is why SCE (high [Cl⁻]) has a *lower* potential than DCE (low [Cl⁻]). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | |---|---|---| | **Nernst Equation (General)** | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ | E = electrode potential (V); E° = standard potential (V); n = electrons transferred; Q = reaction quotient | | **Nernst Equation (2.303 form)** | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log Q$ | R = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**; T = 298 K; F = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** | | **SHE (defined)** | $E^{\circ}_{SHE} = 0.000\,V$ | Universal constant by IUPAC convention | | **SHE Half-Reaction** | $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \to H_{2}(g)$ | n = 2; E° = 0.000 V | | **Calomel Half-Reaction** | $Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \to 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}$ | n = 2; E° = +0.268 V (in terms of standard Cl⁻ activity) | | **Calomel Potential (general)** | $E = E^{\circ} - 0.0591\,\log[Cl^{-}]$ | Apparent simplification (Cl⁻ on product side) | | **SCE (Saturated KCl)** | $E = +0.2412\,V$ vs SHE | At 25 °C | | **NCE (1.0 M KCl)** | $E = +0.2800\,V$ vs SHE | At 25 °C | | **DCE (0.1 M KCl)** | $E = +0.3338\,V$ vs SHE | At 25 °C | | **Convert to SHE** | $E_{SHE} = E_{ref} + E_{ref\,vs\,SHE}$ | Reference → SHE conversion | | **Convert from SHE** | $E_{ref} = E_{SHE} - E_{ref\,vs\,SHE}$ | SHE → Reference conversion | | **Faraday Constant** | $F = 96{,}485\,C\,mol^{-1}$ | Used universally in electrochemistry | | **Gas Constant** | $R = 8.314\,J\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-1}$ | Universal gas constant | | **Thermal Voltage at 25 °C** | $\frac{RT}{F} = 0.02569\,V$ | $2.303 \times \frac{RT}{F} = 0.0591\,V$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These reference electrodes are critical in: > - **pH meters** (using glass electrode vs SCE) > - **Potentiometric titrations** > - **Corrosion monitoring** of pipelines, ships, and offshore structures > - **Biomedical sensors** (ion-selective electrodes) > - **Battery R&D** (half-cell characterization) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (for SHE and Calomel) ### Step 1: Gibbs Free Energy Foundation For a general electrochemical half-reaction: $$Ox + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons Red$$ The Gibbs free energy change is related to the electrode potential by: $$\Delta G = -nFE$$ At standard state: $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$ ### Step 2: General Thermodynamic Relation At any non-standard state, the change in Gibbs free energy is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ where Q is the reaction quotient. ### Step 3: Substituting the Electrochemical Forms Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ ### Step 4: Solving for E Dividing both sides by $-nF$: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ ### Step 5: Converting to log₁₀ (Base-10 Form) Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} Q$$ ### Step 6: Evaluating the Constant at 25 °C (298.15 K) Substituting R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, T = 298.15 K, F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = \frac{5705.78}{96485} \approx 0.0591$$ Therefore, at 25 °C: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10} Q$$ --- ## 3.2 Application of Nernst Equation to the SHE For the SHE half-reaction: $$2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}(g)$$ The reaction quotient is: $$Q = \frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$ The Nernst equation becomes: $$E_{SHE} = E^{\circ}_{SHE} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$$ Under standard conditions (P = 1 atm, [H⁺] = 1): $$E_{SHE} = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log(1) = 0 - 0 = 0.000\,V$$ This is the **definition**, not a measurement. --- ## 3.3 Application of Nernst Equation to the Calomel Electrode For the calomel half-reaction: $$Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$$ The reaction quotient is: $$Q = \frac{[Cl^{-}]^{2} \cdot a_{Hg}^{2}}{a_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}}}$$ Since both Hg (liquid) and Hg₂Cl₂ (solid) have unit activities: $$Q = [Cl^{-}]^{2}$$ The Nernst equation becomes: $$E_{Calomel} = E^{\circ}_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}/Hg} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log[Cl^{-}]^{2}$$ Using the identity $\log x^{2} = 2\log x$: $$E_{Calomel} = E^{\circ}_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}/Hg} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$$ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Evaluation of Calomel Electrode Potentials ### Case A: Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) - Solubility of KCl at 25 °C ≈ 4.6 M - Therefore, [Cl⁻] ≈ 4.6 M $$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(4.6)$$ $$\log(4.6) = 0.6628$$ $$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.0591 \times 0.6628$$ $$E_{SCE} = 0.268 - 0.0392 = 0.2412\,V$$ ### Case B: Normal Calomel Electrode (NCE) - [Cl⁻] = 1.0 M $$E_{NCE} = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(1.0) = 0.268 - 0 = 0.268\,V$$ (Actual measured value = +0.2800 V, the small difference arises from activity coefficients and junction potentials.) ### Case C: Decinormal Calomel Electrode (DCE) - [Cl⁻] = 0.1 M $$E_{DCE} = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(0.1)$$ $$\log(0.1) = -1$$ $$E_{DCE} = 0.268 - 0.0591 \times (-1) = 0.268 + 0.0591 = 0.3271\,V$$ (Actual measured value ≈ +0.3338 V, again due to activity corrections.) > [!NOTE] > **Why the discrepancy?** The Nernst equation in its simplified form uses *concentrations*, but the rigorous form uses *activities* ($a = \gamma \cdot c$). The difference between 0.268 V (NCE) and 0.2800 V is due to the activity coefficient $\gamma_{Cl^-}$ in 1 M KCl, which deviates from unity. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix | Problem Type | Steps to Follow | Key Formula | |---|---|---| | **Find E of a cell measured against SHE** | Identify half-reactions, write Nernst, substitute | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | | **Find E of a cell measured against SCE** | Measure $E_{obs}$, add SCE potential to convert | $E_{vs\,SHE} = E_{obs} + 0.2412\,V$ | | **Find E of a cell measured against NCE** | Measure $E_{obs}$, add NCE potential to convert | $E_{vs\,SHE} = E_{obs} + 0.2800\,V$ | | **Find [Cl⁻] from measured E** | Rearrange Nernst, solve for [Cl⁻] | $[Cl^{-}] = 10^{(E^{\circ}-E)/0.0591}$ | | **Find n (electrons transferred)** | From reaction stoichiometry | n = number of electrons in balanced half-reaction | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ExternalCircuit["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT (Voltmeter / Load)"] VM[Voltmeter Terminals] end subgraph SHEAssembly["SHE ASSEMBLY (Glass Tube)"] PT1[Pt Wire Lead] PT2[Pt Foil Substrate] PTB[Pt Black Coating
Catalyst Surface] ACID[1 M HCl Solution
aH plus = 1] end subgraph GasSystem["GAS SUPPLY SYSTEM"] H2TANK[Pure H2 Gas Cylinder] REG[Pressure Regulator] FLOW[Flow Meter] BUBB[Bubbler Inlet Tube] end H2TANK --> REG --> FLOW --> BUBB BUBB -->|Frothing H2 Gas| ACID ACID -->|Immerses| PTB PTB -->|Welded to| PT2 PT2 -->|Connected to| PT1 PT1 --> VM ACID -.->|Ionic Path| ExternalCircuit VM -.->|Returns to| ExternalCircuit ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** H₂ gas flows from the cylinder, gets regulated to exactly 1 atm, and bubbles through the 1 M HCl. The Pt black catalyzes the H⁺/H₂ equilibrium. The Pt wire carries electrons to the external circuit. Ionic conduction completes the loop through the solution. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Calomel Electrode ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InnerTube["INNER GLASS TUBE (Sealed)"] PT_C[Pt Wire Contact] HG_POOL[Mercury Pool
Pure Liquid Hg] PASTE[Calomel Paste
Hg + Hg2Cl2 Mix] end subgraph Junction["JUNCTION (Porous Barrier)"] FUSE[Glass Frit or
Vycor Disc or
Asbestos Wick] end subgraph OuterTube["OUTER GLASS TUBE"] KCLSOL[KCl Solution
Saturated 1M or 0.1M] SEAL[Rubber / Cork Seal] end subgraph ExtConn["EXTERNAL CONNECTION"] LEAD_C[Pt Lead to Voltmeter] KCLPORT[KCl Reservoir Port] end PT_C --> HG_POOL HG_POOL --> PASTE PASTE --> FUSE FUSE --> KCLSOL KCLSOL --> SEAL SEAL -.-> KCLPORT PT_C --> LEAD_C KCLSOL -.->|Ionic contact
with sample cell| ExtConn ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The inner tube contains the Hg/Hg₂Cl₂ redox couple, electrically connected via a Pt wire. The porous junction allows ionic (but not bulk solution) contact with the outer KCl solution. The outer KCl establishes a stable, reproducible chloride concentration which fixes the electrode potential. --- ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — SHE vs Calomel ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHE["SHE (Primary Reference)"] SHEDef["E0 = 0.000 V
by definition"] SHELim["Limitations:
Fragile Pt black
H2 gas handling
Platinum poisoning"] end subgraph CAL["CALOMEL (Secondary Reference)"] CALDef["E = +0.2412 V to
+0.3338 V vs SHE"] CALAdv["Advantages:
Robust glass body
No gas required
Stable potential
Easy maintenance"] end SHE -->|Conventionally
Assigned| REF[Reference Zero Point] CAL -->|Practically
Used| REF REF -->|Forms basis of| EMF[EMF Series
and All Other
Electrode Potentials] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Takeaway:** SHE is the *theoretical anchor*, Calomel is the *practical tool*. In a real KTU lab or industry setting, you will almost always use a Calomel electrode — but when you report a potential, you must convert it back to the SHE scale for standardization. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Q: Define a reference electrode. Why is the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) assigned a potential of zero volts?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **reference electrode** is an electrode whose potential is known, stable, and reproducible, used to measure the potential of other electrodes. The SHE is assigned a potential of zero volts by **IUPAC convention (1953)** because: 1. (1 Mark) It is impossible to measure the *absolute* potential of a single electrode; only potential differences can be measured. 2. (1 Mark) Therefore, an arbitrary zero reference is needed. 3. (1 Mark) The SHE, with all species in their standard states (1 M H⁺, 1 atm H₂, 25 °C), is chosen as this primary reference because of hydrogen's simplicity, reversibility, and catalytic behavior on Pt. **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Reason for zero (2) = **3 Marks** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **Q: Differentiate between a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a normal calomel electrode (NCE). Mention their standard potentials at 25 °C.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Feature | SCE | NCE | |---|---|---| | **KCl concentration** | Saturated (~4.6 M) | 1.0 M (Normal) | | **Potential at 25 °C** | +0.2412 V (1 Mark) | +0.2800 V (1 Mark) | (1 Mark) The difference in potential arises because the Nernst equation shows $E \propto -\log[Cl^{-}]$. Higher [Cl⁻] in SCE produces a lower potential, while lower [Cl⁻] in NCE produces a higher potential. **Mark Distribution:** Potentials (2) + Explanation of difference (1) = **3 Marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Main Exam, Question Paper Module 2]** **Q: (a) Describe the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). (7 Marks)** **(b) The EMF of a cell measured against a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) is +0.420 V. The same cell measured against a normal calomel electrode (NCE) gives +0.380 V. Explain why the two values differ and calculate the actual EMF of the cell with respect to SHE. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Construction and Working of SHE (7 Marks) **Construction (4 Marks):** 1. A Pt wire is sealed into a glass tube (1 Mark) 2. The wire is welded to a Pt foil (1 Mark) 3. The foil is coated with finely divided "Pt black" by electrolysis to increase surface area (1 Mark) 4. The assembly is immersed in a 1 M HCl solution (or H₂SO₄) and pure H₂ gas at 1 atm pressure is bubbled over the Pt black (1 Mark) **Working (3 Marks):** 1. At the Pt surface, the equilibrium $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons H_{2}$ is established. The Pt black acts as a catalyst (1 Mark) 2. Under standard conditions (1 M H⁺, 1 atm H₂, 25 °C), the potential is defined as 0.000 V (1 Mark) 3. The electrode is connected to an external voltmeter; the potential difference between SHE and the test electrode gives the test electrode's standard potential (1 Mark) --- ### Part (b) — EMF Analysis with SCE and NCE (7 Marks) **Explanation (3 Marks):** Both SCE and NCE are calomel electrodes, but they use different KCl concentrations. Their potentials vs SHE are different: - SCE: +0.2412 V vs SHE - NCE: +0.2800 V vs SHE When a cell's EMF is measured against a reference, the relationship is: $$E_{vs\,SHE} = E_{measured} + E_{reference\,vs\,SHE}$$ If we measure the same cell against SCE and NCE, the actual SHE-based EMF must be the same. The "measured" values differ because we are adding different reference potentials. **Calculation (4 Marks):** Measured against SCE: $$E_{SHE} = E_{SCE\,measured} + E_{SCE\,vs\,SHE} = 0.420 + 0.2412 = 0.6612\,V$$ Measured against NCE: $$E_{SHE} = E_{NCE\,measured} + E_{NCE\,vs\,SHE} = 0.380 + 0.2800 = 0.6600\,V$$ The two values (0.6612 V and 0.6600 V) differ slightly because: - Experimental uncertainties in measurement (1 Mark) - Slight variation of SCE potential with temperature and exact saturation (1 Mark) - Liquid junction potentials (1 Mark) **Average EMF vs SHE** ≈ (0.6612 + 0.6600) / 2 = **+0.6606 V** (1 Mark) --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary Exam, Module 2]** **Q: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of the calomel electrode. (7 Marks)** **(b) The standard potential of the Hg₂Cl₂/Hg electrode is +0.268 V. Using the Nernst equation, calculate the potential of a calomel electrode when (i) [Cl⁻] = 1.0 M, and (ii) [Cl⁻] = 0.1 M. Comment on the trend. (7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: ### Part (a) — Construction and Working of Calomel Electrode (7 Marks) **Construction (4 Marks):** 1. (1 Mark) An inner glass tube contains a Pt wire immersed in pure mercury (Hg) at the bottom. 2. (1 Mark) Above the mercury, a paste of Hg + Hg₂Cl₂ (calomel) is placed. 3. (1 Mark) The inner tube is fitted inside an outer tube with a porous plug (glass frit or asbestos wick) connecting them. 4. (1 Mark) The outer tube is filled with KCl solution (saturated, 1 M, or 0.1 M depending on variant). **Working (3 Marks):** 1. (1 Mark) The half-cell reaction is: $Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$ 2. (1 Mark) The Pt wire provides electrical contact; the porous plug allows ionic conduction to the KCl solution. 3. (1 Mark) The electrode potential is fixed by the chloride ion concentration in the KCl solution (via the Nernst equation). --- ### Part (b) — Nernst Calculation for Calomel Electrode (7 Marks) **Nernst Equation (2 Marks):** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log[Cl^{-}]^{2} = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$$ where E° = +0.268 V. **Case (i): [Cl⁻] = 1.0 M (2 Marks)** $$E = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(1.0)$$ $$\log(1.0) = 0$$ $$E = 0.268 - 0 = +0.268\,V$$ **Case (ii): [Cl⁻] = 0.1 M (2 Marks)** $$E = 0.268 - 0.0591 \log(0.1)$$ $$\log(0.1) = -1$$ $$E = 0.268 - 0.0591 \times (-1) = 0.268 + 0.0591 = +0.3271\,V$$ **Comment on Trend (1 Mark):** As [Cl⁻] **decreases**, the electrode potential **increases**. This is consistent with Le Chatelier's principle: when the product Cl⁻ is reduced, the equilibrium shifts to the right, releasing more electrons, hence a higher (more positive) reduction potential. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert measured EMF to SHE scale** — Many students report a value measured against SCE directly as the "EMF of the cell" without adding 0.2412 V. **Cost: 2–3 marks per question.** > > 2. **Confusing SHE definition with measurement** — The SHE potential of 0.000 V is a *convention*, not a *measurement*. Examiners specifically test this. Writing "SHE measures 0 V" loses 1 mark; writing "SHE is *defined* as 0 V" gains full credit. > > 3. **Sign convention errors in Nernst equation** — The Nernst equation for the calomel electrode: $E = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$. Students often write $+ 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$, getting the sign reversed. **Cost: 2–4 marks.** > > 4. **Forgetting activity of solids and liquids = 1** — In the calomel half-reaction, both Hg (l) and Hg₂Cl₂ (s) have unit activities. Students sometimes include them in Q, which is wrong. > > 5. **Not mentioning Pt black role** — In SHE construction, simply describing "Pt electrode" without explaining the catalytic role of *platinum black* costs 1 mark. > > 6. **Unit errors in Nernst constant** — Using 0.0592 vs 0.0591 vs 0.05916. Use 0.0591 universally for KTU exams to avoid confusion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024** ### A. Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) - ☐ SHE is the **primary reference electrode** (IUPAC convention) - ☐ Half-reaction: $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \to H_{2}(g)$ ; n = 2 - ☐ $E^{\circ}_{SHE} = 0.000\,V$ (defined, **not** measured) - ☐ Standard conditions: 1 M H⁺ (a = 1), 1 atm H₂, 25 °C - ☐ Construction: Pt wire + Pt foil + Pt black + 1 M HCl + H₂ gas - ☐ Pt black is a **catalyst** that increases active surface area - ☐ Nernst: $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{P_{H_2}}{[H^{+}]^{2}}$ ### B. Calomel Electrode - ☐ Calomel = $Hg_{2}Cl_{2}$ (mercury(I) chloride) - ☐ Half-reaction: $Hg_{2}Cl_{2}(s) + 2e^{-} \to 2Hg(l) + 2Cl^{-}(aq)$ ; n = 2 - ☐ $E^{\circ}_{Hg_{2}Cl_{2}/Hg} = +0.268\,V$ (theoretical) - ☐ Nernst: $E = E^{\circ} - 0.0591 \log[Cl^{-}]$ - ☐ **SCE** (saturated KCl): +0.2412 V - ☐ **NCE** (1.0 M KCl): +0.2800 V - ☐ **DCE** (0.1 M KCl): +0.3338 V - ☐ Higher [Cl⁻] → Lower potential - ☐ Construction: Pt wire → Hg → Hg + Hg₂Cl₂ paste → Porous junction → KCl solution - ☐ Porous plug allows ionic contact without bulk mixing ### C. Universal Constants (Commit to Memory) - ☐ $F = 96{,}485\,C\,mol^{-1}$ - ☐ $R = 8.314\,J\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-1}$ - ☐ $T = 298.15\,K$ (25 °C) - ☐ $\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0591\,V$ at 25 °C ### D. Reference Electrode Conversion Tricks - ☐ **To convert measured EMF → SHE:** $E_{SHE} = E_{measured} + E_{ref\,vs\,SHE}$ - ☐ **To convert SHE value → Reference:** $E_{ref} = E_{SHE} - E_{ref\,vs\,SHE}$ - ☐ Always add (never subtract) when going from reference to SHE. ### E. Practical Knowledge for Lab & Viva - ☐ Why is SHE not used in routine labs? → Pt poisoning, H₂ handling, fragile - ☐ Why is calomel preferred? → Robust, stable, easy to use, no gas - ☐ What is "liquid junction potential"? → Potential developed at the KCl junction - ☐ Name two other reference electrodes: **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** and **Mercury/Mercurous Sulfate** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Variants:** > **"Saturated → Smallest potential"** > **"Decinormal → Deepest (highest) potential"** > **"Normal → iN between"**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Construction and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to "Construction and Working" in Electrochemistry

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **GCCYT122 – Chemistry for Physical Science**, the topic **"Construction and Working"** in Module 2 typically refers to the architecture and operational mechanism of **Electrochemical Cells**, most prominently the **Galvanic (Voltaic) Cell** and the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. This forms the foundation of electrochemistry, electrode potentials, EMF measurements, and corrosion science.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The phrase "Construction and Working" is the standard exam-stem terminology used by KTU examiners to test whether a student understands both the **physical assembly (hardware)** and the **redox chemistry (software)** of a cell.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Galvanic Cell

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **Galvanic Cell** (or Voltaic Cell) is an electrochemical device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy via a **spontaneous redox reaction**, with the two half-reactions physically separated into two distinct half-cells connected by a **salt bridge** and an **external metallic conductor**.

The two half-cells consist of:
- An **Anode** (site of oxidation) — the **negative electrode** in a galvanic cell.
- A **Cathode** (site of reduction) — the **positive electrode** in a galvanic cell.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydraulic Dam" Model

Imagine two water reservoirs at different heights, connected by a pipe at the bottom.

| Component in Analogy | Electrochemical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Higher water reservoir | Anode (higher chemical potential / tendency to oxidize) |
| Lower water reservoir | Cathode (lower chemical potential / tendency to reduce) |
| Pipe connecting them | External wire + salt bridge (ion pathway) |
| Water flowing downhill | Electrons flowing from anode → cathode |
| Turbine in the pipe | Load (bulb, motor) doing useful electrical work |

**Why this works:** Just as water spontaneously flows downhill, electrons spontaneously flow from a metal with a **higher (more negative) reduction potential** to one with a **lower (more positive) reduction potential**. The salt bridge is like a one-way valve that lets ions migrate to maintain **charge neutrality** — without it, charge would build up and current would stop within microseconds.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **Faraday Constant (F)** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** ≈ **96,500 C mol⁻¹**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹**
> - **Standard Temperature (T)** = **298.15 K** (25 °C)
> - **Standard Pressure (P)** = **1 bar** = **10⁵ Pa**
> - **Standard Concentration** = **1 M (1 mol L⁻¹)** for solutions; **1 atm** for gases
> - **Standard State Notation (°)**: All species at unit activity, denoted by superscript "°"

### 1.5 The Daniel Cell — Prototype of a Galvanic Cell

The **Daniel Cell**, invented by John Frederic Daniell in **1836**, is the textbook prototype of a galvanic cell and the most frequently asked "Construction and Working" topic in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Daniel Cell — One-Line Definition:** A galvanic cell consisting of a **Zinc electrode immersed in ZnSO₄ solution** (anode) and a **Copper electrode immersed in CuSO₄ solution** (cathode), separated by a **porous pot or salt bridge**, with a standard EMF of **E°cell = +1.10 V**.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Daniel Cell — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The construction is best understood as a layered system:

### Layer 1 — The Anode Half-Cell (Oxidation Site)
- **Electrode:** A pure **Zinc (Zn) rod/strip** (≥ 99.9% purity) is partially immersed in an aqueous solution of **Zinc Sulphate (ZnSO₄)** at unit molarity (1 M).
- **Container:** A glass beaker or a porous ceramic pot.
- **Function:** Zn metal atoms lose two electrons each and dissolve into the solution as Zn²⁺ ions (oxidation state increases from **0 → +2**).

### Layer 2 — The Cathode Half-Cell (Reduction Site)
- **Electrode:** A pure **Copper (Cu) rod/strip** is partially immersed in an aqueous solution of **Copper Sulphate (CuSO₄)** at unit molarity (1 M).
- **Container:** A second glass beaker.
- **Function:** Cu²⁺ ions from the solution gain two electrons each and deposit onto the copper electrode as neutral Cu metal (oxidation state decreases from **+2 → 0**).

### Layer 3 — The Salt Bridge (or Porous Partition)
- **Composition:** A U-shaped glass tube filled with an **inert electrolyte** — typically **KCl, KNO₃, or NH₄NO₃** in **agar-agar gel** (for semi-solid form).
- **Selection Rule:** The salt must be highly soluble, fully dissociated, and its ions must have **nearly equal transport numbers (t₊ ≈ t₋)** to avoid liquid-junction potential.
- **Function (THREE critical roles):**
  1. **Completes the electrical circuit** by providing an ionic pathway.
  2. **Maintains electrical neutrality** — cations migrate toward the cathode, anions toward the anode.
  3. **Prevents direct mixing** of the two electrolytes, which would cause direct chemical reaction and short-circuit the cell.

### Layer 4 — External Circuit
- A **metallic wire** (usually copper) connects the Zn and Cu electrodes through a **high-resistance voltmeter** (to measure EMF without drawing current) or a **switch + load**.

### Layer 5 — Voltmeter / Galvanometer
- Measures the **potential difference (E_cell)** between the two electrodes.
- The positive terminal connects to the **cathode (Cu)**; the negative terminal connects to the **anode (Zn)**.

## 2.2 Working of the Daniel Cell — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Anodic Oxidation (Zinc Dissolution):**
- At the Zn surface, Zn atoms are oxidized: each Zn atom loses 2 electrons.
- These electrons are "pushed" into the external wire because Zn has a more negative reduction potential (−0.76 V) than Cu (+0.34 V).

**Step 2 — Electron Migration (External Circuit):**
- Electrons travel through the wire from Zn (anode) → external load → Cu (cathode).
- This directed flow of electrons constitutes the **electric current** that can power a device.

**Step 3 — Cathodic Reduction (Copper Deposition):**
- At the Cu surface, Cu²⁺ ions from the solution accept these arriving electrons and are reduced to Cu metal, which deposits on the electrode.
- This causes the Cu electrode to **gain mass** visibly over time; the Zn electrode **loses mass**.

**Step 4 — Ionic Migration (Salt Bridge / Internal Circuit):**
- As Zn²⁺ is produced at the anode, the anode solution becomes **excessively positive**.
- As Cu²⁺ is consumed at the cathode, the cathode solution becomes **excessively negative** (excess SO₄²⁻).
- The salt bridge's anions (e.g., Cl⁻, NO₃⁻) flow into the anode compartment; cations (K⁺, NH₄⁺) flow into the cathode compartment, **neutralizing the charge buildup**.

**Step 5 — Net Cell Reaction:**
- The two half-reactions sum to give the overall spontaneous reaction.
- The Gibbs free energy change is **negative (ΔG < 0)**, confirming spontaneity.

## 2.3 Half-Reactions and Net Reaction of the Daniel Cell

| Half-Cell | Half-Reaction | Standard Potential E°(V) |
|---|---|---|
| Anode (Oxidation) | Zn(s) → Zn²⁺(aq) + 2e⁻ | E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V |
| Cathode (Reduction) | Cu²⁺(aq) + 2e⁻ → Cu(s) | E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V |
| **Net Reaction** | **Zn(s) + Cu²⁺(aq) → Zn²⁺(aq) + Cu(s)** | **E°cell = +1.10 V** |

## 2.4 Sign Convention and Cell Notation (KTU Convention)

The cell is represented using IUPAC notation:

$$
\text{Zn(s)} \;\vert\; \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}, 1\text{M}) \;\Vert\; \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}, 1\text{M}) \;\vert\; \text{Cu(s)}
$$

- **Single vertical line (|)** = phase boundary.
- **Double vertical line (||)** = salt bridge.
- **Left side** = anode (oxidation), written as the **anode half-cell**.
- **Right side** = cathode (reduction), written as the **cathode half-cell**.
- By convention, the cell EMF is computed as:

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}
$$

For the Daniel cell:

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** A **positive E°cell** confirms the cell reaction is **spontaneous** (Galvanic). A **negative E°cell** means the reaction is **non-spontaneous** and would instead require an external power source (Electrolytic Cell).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **complete cheat-sheet** of formulas required for any KTU 2024 problem on Construction and Working of electrochemical cells. Memorize these and their units.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Standard cell EMF (cell potential) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Standard Gibbs free energy | J mol⁻¹ (n = electrons transferred) |
| 3 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | **Nernst Equation** (at 25 °C) | V; Q = reaction quotient |
| 4 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Nernst Equation (general form) | V |
| 5 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | Single electrode Nernst equation | V |
| 6 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \text{pH}$ | Hydrogen electrode Nernst equation | V (pH-dependent) |
| 7 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs energy under any condition | J mol⁻¹ |
| 8 | $E^{\circ} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln K$ | Relation to equilibrium constant | V; K = equilibrium constant |
| 9 | $E^{\circ} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K$ | At 25 °C | V |
| 10 | $Q = nF$ | Total charge passed | Coulombs (C) |
| 11 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass deposited (Faraday's Law) | grams; M = molar mass |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Engineers must study this:** Electrochemical cells are the operational basis of:
> - **Lithium-ion batteries** in smartphones, EVs, and laptops (cathode: LiCoO₂, anode: graphite).
> - **Lead-acid batteries** in automobiles (anode: Pb, cathode: PbO₂, electrolyte: H₂SO₄).
> - **Fuel cells** in space missions (Apollo missions used alkaline H₂-O₂ fuel cells).
> - **Corrosion protection** — sacrificial anodes (Zn on ships, Mg on underground pipelines) are reverse-galvanic-cell principles.
> - **Electro-refining** of copper, zinc, and aluminum in metallurgical industries.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for Cell EMF

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **a guaranteed 7-mark question** in every KTU ESE. Master the algebraic chain.

### Starting Point — Thermodynamic Foundation

For a general electrochemical reaction:

$$
aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD + ne^{-}
$$

The Gibbs free energy change at any instant is related to the reaction quotient $Q$ via:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$** (from the relationship between electrical work and free energy):

$$
-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $-nF$** to isolate $E_{\text{cell}}$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 3 — This is the Nernst Equation in its most general form.** It is valid at any temperature T.

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm** using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the value $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$ at T = 298.15 K:**

$$
\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form valid at 25 °C (298 K):**

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Check (for the Daniel Cell at non-standard conditions):**
> Suppose $[Zn^{2+}] = 0.01$ M and $[Cu^{2+}] = 1.0$ M. The reaction is $Zn + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + Cu$, so $Q = [Zn^{2+}]/[Cu^{2+}] = 0.01$.
> 
> Using $E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01)$
> 
> $= 1.10 - \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2)$
> 
> $= 1.10 + 0.0591 = 1.1591$ V.

## 3.2 Derivation — Relation Between E°cell and Equilibrium Constant K

**Step 1 — At equilibrium**, the cell EMF becomes zero ($E_{\text{cell}} = 0$) because no net reaction is occurring — the system is at electrochemical equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Also at equilibrium**, the reaction quotient $Q$ equals the equilibrium constant $K$:

$$
Q_{eq} = K
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute these into the Nernst equation:**

$$
0 = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent forms** (algebraic rearrangement):

$$
\log K = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}
$$

$$
K = 10^{\,n E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} / 0.0591}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A highly positive $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ implies a huge $K$ (the reaction goes nearly to completion). For example, $E^{\circ} = +1.10$ V with n = 2 gives $K \approx 10^{37}$ — the reaction is essentially irreversible.

## 3.3 Derivation — Single Electrode Potential (Reduction Form)

For a generic half-reaction:

$$
\text{Ox}^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}
$$

Applying the Nernst equation to a single electrode:

**Step 1 — Reaction quotient** for the reduction half-reaction: $Q = \dfrac{1}{[Ox^{n+}]}$ (since pure solid Red has activity = 1).

**Step 2 — Substituting into the Nernst equation:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{1}{[Ox^{n+}]}
$$

**Step 3 — Using $\log(1/x) = -\log x$:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [Ox^{n+}]
$$

**Step 4 — Generalized for half-reaction $p\text{Ox} + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons q\text{Red}$:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[\text{Red}]^{q}}{[\text{Ox}]^{p}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Always raise the species concentration to the **power of its stoichiometric coefficient** when forming $Q$ or $K$. The Nernst equation does NOT automatically use stoichiometric coefficients for the log argument; they appear inside the ratio.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When To Use Which Formula

| Problem Type | Governing Equation | Key Knowns / Unknowns |
|---|---|---|
| Calculate $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ from two $E^{\circ}$ values | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Two standard electrode potentials |
| Calculate $E_{\text{cell}}$ under non-standard concentrations | Nernst equation | $E^{\circ}$, $n$, $Q$ |
| Predict spontaneity of a reaction | Sign of $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ (positive = spontaneous) | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ |
| Calculate equilibrium constant | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K$ | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$, $n$ |
| Calculate $\Delta G$ of the cell reaction | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | $n$, $F$, $E_{\text{cell}}$ |
| Mass deposited during electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ (Faraday's Law) | $I$, $t$, $M$, $n$ |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — A Complete KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A galvanic cell is constructed with a Zn electrode in 0.5 M ZnSO₄ and a Cu electrode in 2.0 M CuSO₄ at 25 °C. Given $E^{\circ}(Zn^{2+}/Zn) = -0.76$ V and $E^{\circ}(Cu^{2+}/Cu) = +0.34$ V, calculate: (a) the standard cell EMF, (b) the cell EMF under given conditions, and (c) $\Delta G$ for the cell reaction.

### Part (a) — Standard Cell EMF

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}
$$

### Part (b) — Cell EMF Under Given Conditions

The reaction is $Zn(s) + Cu^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + Cu(s)$, with $n = 2$ and $Q = [Zn^{2+}]/[Cu^{2+}] = 0.5/2.0 = 0.25$.

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.25)
$$

$$
\log(0.25) = \log(2.5 \times 10^{-1}) = \log(2.5) - 1 = 0.3979 - 1 = -0.6021
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-0.6021)
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 1.10 - (-0.0178) = 1.10 + 0.0178 = 1.1178 \text{ V}
$$

### Part (c) — Gibbs Free Energy

$$
\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}
$$

$$
\Delta G = -(2)(96485)(1.1178) = -2.157 \times 10^{5} \text{ J mol}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta G = -215.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** confirms the reaction is **thermodynamically spontaneous** — the hallmark of a working galvanic cell.
