# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a galvanic cell. With a neat diagram, explain the construction of the Daniel cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **galvanic cell** is an electrochemical device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy by means of a spontaneous redox reaction, where the oxidation and reduction half-reactions are physically separated into two half-cells connected by a salt bridge and an external metallic conductor.

The **Daniel cell** consists of:
- A **zinc rod** immersed in **1 M ZnSO₄ solution** (anode half-cell).
- A **copper rod** immersed in **1 M CuSO₄ solution** (cathode half-cell).
- A **salt bridge** (U-tube containing KNO₃ in agar-agar gel) connecting the two solutions.
- A **voltmeter** connected across the two electrodes to measure the EMF.

The cell notation is:
$$
\text{Zn(s)} \;\vert\; \text{Zn}^{2+}(1\text{M}) \;\Vert\; \text{Cu}^{2+}(1\text{M}) \;\vert\; \text{Cu(s)}
$$
with a standard EMF of **+1.10 V**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the role of a salt bridge in a galvanic cell? Mention any two ideal characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **salt bridge** is a U-shaped tube containing an inert electrolyte (such as KCl or KNO₃) in agar-agar gel, connecting the two half-cells of a galvanic cell.

**Two primary roles:**
1. It **completes the internal electrical circuit** by allowing ion flow between the two half-cells, thereby maintaining electrical neutrality in both compartments.
2. It **prevents direct mixing** of the two electrolyte solutions, which would otherwise short-circuit the cell by allowing direct chemical reaction.

**Two ideal characteristics:**
- The cation and anion should have **nearly equal transport numbers** (to minimize liquid-junction potential).
- The electrolyte should **not react chemically** with either half-cell solution.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Describe the construction and working of the Daniel cell with a neat labelled diagram. Write the cell reaction and calculate its standard EMF. Given: $E^{\circ}(Zn^{2+}/Zn) = -0.76$ V, $E^{\circ}(Cu^{2+}/Cu) = +0.34$ V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Nernst equation for the EMF of a galvanic cell. How is it used to determine the equilibrium constant of a cell reaction? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of the Daniel Cell:**

The cell consists of two half-cells:

| Component | Anode Half-Cell | Cathode Half-Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Zinc rod (99.9% pure) | Copper rod (99.9% pure) |
| Electrolyte | 1 M ZnSO₄ (aqueous) | 1 M CuSO₄ (aqueous) |
| Container | Glass beaker / porous pot | Glass beaker |

Both half-cells are connected by a **salt bridge** (U-tube containing **KNO₃** in **agar-agar gel**). A **copper wire** with a high-resistance **voltmeter** connects the Zn and Cu electrodes externally.

**Working — Step-by-step:**

1. **At the anode:** Zn atoms oxidize, releasing 2 electrons per atom and entering the solution as Zn²⁺:

$$
\text{Zn(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-}
$$

2. **In the external circuit:** Electrons flow from Zn (anode, negative) through the wire to Cu (cathode, positive), producing electric current.

3. **At the cathode:** Cu²⁺ ions from the solution accept these electrons and deposit as Cu metal on the electrode:

$$
\text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)}
$$

4. **In the salt bridge:** NO₃⁻ ions migrate toward the anode; K⁺ ions migrate toward the cathode, maintaining charge neutrality.

5. **Net Reaction:**

$$
\text{Zn(s)} + \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + \text{Cu(s)}
$$

6. **Standard EMF Calculation:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation:**

**Step 1:** The Gibbs free energy change for a cell reaction at any condition:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 2:** Using the relationship between free energy and electrical work: $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:

$$
-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 3:** Dividing by $-nF$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 4:** Converting to base-10 logarithm using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q
$$

**Step 5:** At 25 °C (298.15 K), the term $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V, so:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q
$$

**Step 6 — Determination of Equilibrium Constant:**

At equilibrium, $E_{\text{cell}} = 0$ and $Q = K$. Substituting:

$$
0 = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K
$$

$$
\log K = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}
$$

$$
K = 10^{\,n E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} / 0.0591}
$$

**Application to Daniel Cell:** With $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 1.10$ V and $n = 2$:

$$
\log K = \frac{2 \times 1.10}{0.0591} = 37.23
$$

$$
K = 10^{37.23} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{37}
$$

This enormous value confirms the reaction proceeds essentially to completion.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). Why is it called a primary reference electrode? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A galvanic cell consists of a copper electrode in 0.1 M CuSO₄ and a silver electrode in 0.01 M AgNO₃. Calculate the cell EMF at 25 °C and the equilibrium constant. Given: $E^{\circ}(Cu^{2+}/Cu) = +0.34$ V, $E^{\circ}(Ag^{+}/Ag) = +0.80$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE):**

| Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Acid solution | 1 M HCl (or any acid with $[H^+] = 1$ M) |
| Platinum wire | Coated with **platinum black** (finely divided Pt) for high surface area |
| Hydrogen gas | Pure H₂ at 1 atm pressure bubbled continuously |
| Container | Glass tube with a side arm for H₂ inlet and outlet |

The Pt wire is sealed into a glass tube and immersed in the 1 M HCl solution. H₂ gas is bubbled over the platinized Pt, which adsorbs the gas and acts as both an inert conductor and a catalyst.

**Working of SHE:**

- When SHE acts as an **anode** (oxidation of H₂):

$$
\text{H}_2(\text{g}, 1\text{ atm}) \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+(1\text{M}) + 2e^{-}
$$

- When SHE acts as a **cathode** (reduction of H⁺):

$$
2\text{H}^+(1\text{M}) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2(\text{g}, 1\text{ atm})
$$

**Why SHE is the Primary Reference Electrode:**

1. Its standard potential is **defined as exactly 0.00 V** at all temperatures — it is the universal reference.
2. All other standard electrode potentials are measured **relative to SHE**.
3. The H₂/H⁺ couple is highly reversible on platinized platinum.
4. It is reproducible under standard conditions (1 M acid, 1 atm H₂).

The potential of any other electrode is determined by constructing a cell with SHE and measuring the EMF:

$$
E^{\circ}(\text{metal}) = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + E^{\circ}(\text{SHE}) = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + 0
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify Anode and Cathode:**

Since $E^{\circ}(Ag^{+}/Ag) = +0.80$ V > $E^{\circ}(Cu^{2+}/Cu) = +0.34$ V, **Ag is the cathode** and **Cu is the anode**.

**Step 2 — Cell Reaction:**

- Anode: $\text{Cu(s)} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-}$
- Cathode: $2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{Ag(s)}$
- **Net:** $\text{Cu(s)} + 2\text{Ag}^+(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{Ag(s)}$

Number of electrons transferred: $n = 2$.

**Step 3 — Standard Cell EMF:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} = (+0.80) - (+0.34) = +0.46 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Reaction Quotient Q:**

For the net reaction: $Q = \dfrac{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]}{[\text{Ag}^+]^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{(0.01)^2} = \dfrac{0.1}{10^{-4}} = 10^3$

**Step 5 — Apply Nernst Equation at 25 °C:**

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.46 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(10^3)
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.46 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 3
$$

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.46 - 0.0887 = 0.3713 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6 — Equilibrium Constant:**

$$
\log K = \frac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591} = \frac{2 \times 0.46}{0.0591} = 15.57
$$

$$
K = 10^{15.57} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{15}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong sign convention:** Computing $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}}$ instead of the correct cathode minus anode. This is the **#1 reason for losing 3–4 marks** in cell EMF problems.
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometric squaring:** Writing $Q = [\text{Cu}^{2+}]/[\text{Ag}^+]$ instead of $[\text{Cu}^{2+}]/[\text{Ag}^+]^2$. The coefficient 2 on Ag⁺ in the balanced reaction MUST appear as a power.
> 3. **Confusing 0.0591 with 0.0257:** Use **0.0591 V** for base-10 log; use **0.0257 V** (or RT/F) only for the natural log form. Mixing these up costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Salt bridge misdescription:** Writing "salt bridge carries electrons" instead of "carries ions" — the wire carries electrons, the salt bridge carries ions.
> 5. **Forgetting to write the cell notation** in part (a) of 7-mark questions — KTU evaluators expect $\text{Anode} \;\vert\; \text{Anode}^{n+} \;\Vert\; \text{Cathode}^{n+} \;\vert\; \text{Cathode}$ explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist as a final pre-exam sanity scan.

- [ ] **Galvanic cell** = spontaneous redox → chemical to electrical energy; $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$, $\Delta G < 0$.
- [ ] **Anode** = oxidation = negative electrode in galvanic cell.
- [ ] **Cathode** = reduction = positive electrode in galvanic cell.
- [ ] **Salt bridge** carries **ions** (NOT electrons) to maintain charge neutrality.
- [ ] **Cell notation convention:** Anode on LEFT, cathode on RIGHT; single bar = phase boundary, double bar = salt bridge.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$.
- [ ] **Nernst equation (25 °C):** $E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$.
- [ ] **Equilibrium constant link:** $\log K = \dfrac{n \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{0.0591}$.
- [ ] **Free energy link:** $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$.
- [ ] **Daniel cell:** Zn–ZnSO₄ || CuSO₄–Cu; $E^{\circ} = +1.10$ V; $K \approx 10^{37}$.
- [ ] **SHE:** Defined as $E^{\circ} = 0.00$ V; Pt-black electrode; 1 M H⁺; 1 atm H₂.
- [ ] **Always raise concentrations to stoichiometric powers** in the Q expression.
- [ ] **Number of electrons n** = electrons cancelled when adding half-reactions.
- [ ] **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹.
- [ ] **0.0591 V** at 298 K for base-10 log form of Nernst equation.
- [ ] **A positive $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$** = spontaneous galvanic cell; **negative** = electrolytic / non-spontaneous.
- [ ] **In Nernst equation**, solids and pure liquids are **omitted** from the Q expression (activity = 1).
- [ ] **Memorize the sign convention table** of standard electrode potentials — common values: $Zn^{2+}/Zn = -0.76$ V, $Cu^{2+}/Cu = +0.34$ V, $Ag^{+}/Ag = +0.80$ V, $Fe^{2+}/Fe = -0.44$ V.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Electrochemical Series (ECS)**, also known as the **Activity Series** or **Electromotive Force (EMF) Series**, is a systematically arranged tabular list of standard electrode potentials ($E^\circ$) of various elements, ions, and compounds. It is conventionally written in **increasing order of standard reduction potential** (i.e., the most negative potential lies at the top, and the most positive at the bottom), measured relative to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** which is assigned an arbitrary value of exactly **0.00 V** at standard conditions (**298.15 K**, **1 atm** pressure, and **1 M** concentration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Electrochemical Series forms the foundation for predicting the feasibility, direction, spontaneity, and extent of redox reactions in aqueous media. It is a compulsory sub-topic under Module 2 (Electrochemistry) of the GCCYT122 syllabus.

Mathematically, the convention adopted in the series is the **reduction half-reaction**, written in the form:

$$M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M \quad ; \quad E^\circ = \text{(measured value in Volts)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **ladder of competitiveness** where every metal (or chemical species) is climbing to grab electrons. The element at the **top of the ladder** (e.g., Lithium, $E^\circ = -3.04$ V) is the most desperate to *give away* its electrons (strongest reducing agent). Conversely, the element at the **bottom** (e.g., Fluorine, $E^\circ = +2.87$ V) is the greediest to *grab* electrons (strongest oxidizing agent).

> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the ECS as a **tug-of-war ranking table** for chemical species. The further down the table a metal sits, the more "reluctant" it is to give up electrons and the more "hungry" it is to take them from metals above it. This is why zinc (a higher-positioned metal) dissolves in copper sulfate solution — it loses electrons, and copper ions win them.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The key physical constants and reference conditions underpinning the Electrochemical Series are:

- **Standard Temperature ($T$):** **298.15 K (25 °C)**
- **Standard Pressure ($P$):** **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **Standard Concentration ($[M^{n+}]$):** **1 mol/L (1 M)** for all aqueous species
- **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) Reference Potential ($E^\circ_{H^+/H_2}$):** **0.00 V** (defined convention)
- **Faraday Constant ($F$):** **96485 C mol$^{-1}$** (used in Nernst-related calculations)
- **Universal Gas Constant ($R$):** **8.314 J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** All values in the Electrochemical Series are **standard (thermodynamic) potentials** under reversible, equilibrium conditions. They do **NOT** directly represent real reaction rates or kinetics.

### 1.4 The Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) — The Anchor Point

The SHE is the universal reference electrode against which all other half-cell potentials are measured. Its half-reaction is:

$$2H^{+}(aq, 1\,M) + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2(g, 1\,atm) \quad ; \quad E^\circ = 0.00\,V$$

It is constructed by bubbling pure $H_2$ gas at **1 atm** over a **platinized platinum electrode** immersed in an acid solution of **1 M $H^+$** activity. The platinum surface is coated with finely divided platinum black, which catalyzes the electrode reaction and provides a large surface area for adsorption of $H_2$ gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Platinum?** Platinum is used because it is an **inert conductor** that does not participate chemically in the reaction but provides a surface for electron transfer and $H_2$ gas adsorption.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Constructing the Electrochemical Series

The series is built by experimentally coupling half-cells with the SHE and measuring the EMF of the resulting galvanic cell. The procedural logic is:

1. **Select a candidate half-cell** (e.g., $Cu^{2+}/Cu$, $Zn^{2+}/Zn$, $Ag^+/Ag$).
2. **Connect it to a Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** through a salt bridge and a voltmeter.
3. **Identify the spontaneous direction** of the cell reaction (which electrode acts as anode/cathode).
4. **Record the measured EMF** with proper sign convention.
5. **Assign a sign** — if reduction occurs at the candidate electrode (relative to SHE), $E^\circ$ is **positive**; if oxidation occurs there, $E^\circ$ is **negative**.
6. **Tabulate the values** in increasing order of reduction potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **The IUPAC Convention:** The series is presented with reduction half-reactions only. A more positive $E^\circ$ indicates a greater tendency to undergo reduction (i.e., act as an oxidizing agent).

### 2.2 How to Read the Series (The "Why" Behind the Order)

- **Top of the table** $\rightarrow$ Most negative $E^\circ$ values $\rightarrow$ **Strong reducing agents** (easily oxidized, lose electrons readily).
- **Bottom of the table** $\rightarrow$ Most positive $E^\circ$ values $\rightarrow$ **Strong oxidizing agents** (readily reduced, gain electrons readily).
- **Alkali metals (Li, Na, K)** occupy the topmost positions because they readily lose their single valence electron to form stable cations.
- **Noble metals (Au, Pt)** and **halogens (F$_2$, Cl$_2$)** occupy the bottom positions because of their high effective nuclear charge and tendency to gain electrons.

### 2.3 Practical Applications of the Electrochemical Series

The ECS is not merely a theoretical table — it is an indispensable engineering tool with applications in:

- **Predicting metal displacement reactions** (galvanic replacement).
- **Determining reaction feasibility and spontaneity** via $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$.
- **Designing sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection** (e.g., Zn, Mg used to protect Fe/steel pipelines).
- **Selecting electrode materials in batteries and fuel cells.**
- **Corrosion engineering and prevention.**
- **Electrometallurgy and electrorefining.**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes every essential formula and rule associated with the Electrochemical Series that is required for the KTU 2024 ESE and continuous assessment:

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Rule** | **Expression** | **Application / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Cell Potential | $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ | Volts (V); both $E^\circ$ values are taken as reduction potentials |
| 2 | Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$ | Joules (J); $n$ = moles of $e^-$ transferred, $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Nernst Equation (for cell) | $E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ at 298 K | Volts; $Q$ is the reaction quotient |
| 4 | Nernst Equation (for electrode) | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$ at 298 K | Volts |
| 5 | Equilibrium Constant relation | $\log K_c = \dfrac{nE^\circ_{cell}}{0.0591}$ | Dimensionless; valid at 298 K |
| 6 | Faraday's First Law | $m = ZIt = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$ | Grams (g); $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent |
| 7 | Faraday's Second Law | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Masses deposited are proportional to equivalent weights |
| 8 | Spontaneity rule | $E^\circ_{cell} > 0 \Rightarrow$ Spontaneous ($\Delta G^\circ < 0$) | Universal redox spontaneity predictor |
| 9 | Metal displacement rule | Metal with lower $E^\circ$ displaces metal with higher $E^\circ$ from its salt solution | Used in qualitative analysis of metals |
| 10 | Sign convention (IUPAC) | Reduction potentials are tabulated; oxidation potential = $-E^\circ_{red}$ | Critical for ESE numerical problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember that $E^\circ_{cell}$ is calculated by **subtracting** the anode (oxidation) reduction potential from the cathode (reduction) reduction potential — **NOT** by adding oxidation and reduction potentials. This is one of the most common valuation mistakes.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Spontaneity from the Series

If a metal with **higher (more positive) reduction potential** is placed in a solution containing ions of a metal with **lower (more negative) reduction potential**, a spontaneous redox reaction occurs. The metal with the lower potential gets oxidized (acts as anode) and dissolves, while the ions of the higher-potential metal get reduced and deposit (act as cathode). This is the **Galvanic Displacement Principle**.

**Example:** When an iron nail is dipped in copper sulfate solution, copper deposits on the iron because:
- $E^\circ_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V (higher)
- $E^\circ_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V (lower)
- Iron dissolves, copper plates out.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Nernst Equation and the Electrochemical Series

The Nernst equation is the mathematical backbone of the Electrochemical Series. The following is the full KTU-board-style derivation that students are expected to reproduce.

**Step 1: Start with the fundamental thermodynamic relationship between Gibbs Free Energy and cell potential.**

For any electrochemical cell, the maximum useful electrical work obtainable is equal to the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard-state condition for the same equation.**

At standard conditions (1 M, 1 atm, 298 K):

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$$

**Step 3: Invoke the isothermal free-energy change equation from chemical thermodynamics.**

For a general reaction at any moment (not necessarily at equilibrium):

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** (the ratio of product activities to reactant activities, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient).

**Step 4: Substitute the electrochemical expressions for $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ into the thermodynamic relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-nFE_{cell} &= -nFE^\circ_{cell} + RT \ln Q \\
\Rightarrow \quad -nFE_{cell} + nFE^\circ_{cell} &= RT \ln Q \\
\Rightarrow \quad nF(E^\circ_{cell} - E_{cell}) &= RT \ln Q \\
\Rightarrow \quad E_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Convert from natural logarithm to base-10 logarithm using the identity $\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the standard values $R = 8.314$ J mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$, $T = 298.15$ K, and $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$ to obtain the decimal constant.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} &= \dfrac{5705.85}{96485} \\
&\approx 0.0591\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Write the final Nernst equation in its standard KTU-board form.**

$$E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 298 K)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Meaning:** This equation tells us that the actual cell EMF ($E_{cell}$) deviates from the standard value ($E^\circ_{cell}$) by a logarithmic term that depends on the **instantaneous concentrations** of the reacting species. The Electrochemical Series provides the *standard* values, and the Nernst equation allows us to compute *non-standard* conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivative Relationship: Equilibrium Constant from $E^\circ_{cell}$

**Step 1:** At equilibrium, $E_{cell} = 0$ and $Q = K_c$ (the equilibrium constant).

**Step 2:** Setting $E_{cell} = 0$ in the Nernst equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c \\
\Rightarrow \quad E^\circ_{cell} &= \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c \\
\Rightarrow \quad \log K_c &= \dfrac{n \cdot E^\circ_{cell}}{0.0591}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Implication:** A positive $E^\circ_{cell}$ produces a large $\log K_c$, meaning the equilibrium strongly favors products — a **spontaneous** reaction. A negative $E^\circ_{cell}$ yields $\log K_c < 0$, so $K_c < 1$, and the reaction is **non-spontaneous** as written.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Illustrative Segment of the Electrochemical Series

The following analytical matrix is a curated segment of the most frequently tested half-reactions in KTU examinations:

| **Reduction Half-Reaction** | **$E^\circ$ (V)** | **Role of Reduced Form** | **Role of Oxidized Form** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Li^+ + e^- \rightarrow Li$ | $-3.04$ | Strongest reducing agent | Weakest oxidizing agent |
| $K^+ + e^- \rightarrow K$ | $-2.93$ | Very strong reducing agent | Very weak oxidizing agent |
| $Ca^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Ca$ | $-2.87$ | Strong reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Na^+ + e^- \rightarrow Na$ | $-2.71$ | Strong reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Mg$ | $-2.37$ | Strong reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Al^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Al$ | $-1.66$ | Moderate reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Zn$ | $-0.76$ | Moderate reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Fe^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Fe$ | $-0.44$ | Moderate reducing agent | Weak oxidizing agent |
| $Ni^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Ni$ | $-0.25$ | Mild reducing agent | Mild oxidizing agent |
| $Sn^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Sn$ | $-0.14$ | Mild reducing agent | Mild oxidizing agent |
| $Pb^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Pb$ | $-0.13$ | Mild reducing agent | Mild oxidizing agent |
| $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | $0.00$ | **Reference (SHE)** | **Reference (SHE)** |
| $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$ | $+0.34$ | Mild oxidizing agent | Mild reducing agent |
| $I_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2I^-$ | $+0.54$ | Moderate oxidizing agent | Moderate reducing agent |
| $Ag^+ + e^- \rightarrow Ag$ | $+0.80$ | Strong oxidizing agent | Strong reducing agent |
| $Br_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Br^-$ | $+1.07$ | Strong oxidizing agent | Strong reducing agent |
| $Cl_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Cl^-$ | $+1.36$ | Very strong oxidizing agent | Very strong reducing agent |
| $Au^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow Au$ | $+1.50$ | Very strong oxidizing agent | Very strong reducing agent |
| $F_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2F^-$ | $+2.87$ | Strongest oxidizing agent | Weakest reducing agent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Trend Across the Periodic Table:** The reduction potential generally becomes **more negative** (less positive) as we move from **right to left** across a period, and **more positive** as we move **down a group**. This correlates with decreasing electronegativity and decreasing ionization energy from right to left.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example: Predicting Reaction Feasibility

**Problem (KTU 2024 ESE style):** Predict whether zinc metal will displace copper from a copper sulfate solution at 298 K. Use the relevant half-reactions from the series.

**Step 1: Identify the two half-reactions from the ECS.**

Cathode (reduction): $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu \quad ; \quad E^\circ = +0.34$ V
Anode (oxidation): $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \quad ; \quad E^\circ_{ox} = +0.76$ V (which is the negative of $E^\circ_{red} = -0.76$ V)

**Step 2: Calculate the standard cell EMF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^\circ_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} \\
&= (+0.34) - (-0.76) \\
&= +0.34 + 0.76 \\
&= +1.10\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the spontaneity criterion.**

Since $E^\circ_{cell} = +1.10$ V $\gg 0$, $\Delta G^\circ < 0$, and the reaction is **spontaneous**.

**Step 4: Write the overall balanced redox equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Zn(s) + Cu^{2+}(aq) &\rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + Cu(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cross-check using Gibbs free energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= -nFE^\circ_{cell} \\
&= -(2)(96485)(1.10) \\
&= -212267\,J\,mol^{-1} \\
&\approx -212.27\,kJ\,mol^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The large negative $\Delta G^\circ$ confirms strong spontaneity. **Zinc readily displaces copper from solution.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Conceptual Map of the Electrochemical Series

The following Mermaid block renders a structured block-level conceptual architecture showing how the Electrochemical Series functions as a predictive engine for redox chemistry:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrochemical Series] --> B[Reference Anchor: SHE]
    A --> C[Tabulated Standard Reduction Potentials]
    A --> D[Predictive Engine for Redox Reactions]
    B --> B1[2H+ + 2e- → H2]
    B --> B2[E° = 0.00 V defined]
    C --> C1[Top: Strong Reducing Agents]
    C --> C2[Middle: Reference Region]
    C --> C3[Bottom: Strong Oxidizing Agents]
    C1 --> C1A[Li, K, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe]
    C2 --> C2A[Pb, H, Cu]
    C3 --> C3A[Ag, Br2, Cl2, Au, F2]
    D --> D1[Calculate E°cell]
    D --> D2[Predict Spontaneity via ΔG°]
    D --> D3[Determine Equilibrium Kc]
    D --> D4[Apply Metal Displacement Rule]
    D1 --> D1A[E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode]
    D2 --> D2A[ΔG° = -nFE°cell]
    D3 --> D3A[log Kc = nE°cell / 0.0591]
    D4 --> D4A[Lower E° displaces Higher E°]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Spontaneity Decision Tree

The following Mermaid schematic visualizes the decision logic for predicting redox reaction feasibility using the ECS:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Identify Two Half-Reactions] --> S1[Lookup E° values from ECS]
    S1 --> S2[Assign Cathode: Higher E°]
    S1 --> S3[Assign Anode: Lower E°]
    S2 --> S4[Compute E°cell = Ecathode - Eanode]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5{E°cell > 0?}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Spontaneous Reaction]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Non-Spontaneous]
    S6 --> S7A[ΔG° < 0 and Kc > 1]
    S7 --> S7B[ΔG° > 0 and Kc < 1]
    S7 --> S7C[Requires External EMF Input]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Cathodic Protection Using the ECS

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the Electrochemical Series guides the engineering application of **sacrificial anodes** in cathodic protection systems:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Steel Pipeline: Fe, E° = -0.44 V] --> P2[Connected via Wire]
    M1[Zinc Sacrificial Anode, E° = -0.76 V] --> P2
    P2 --> P3[Galvanic Cell Formed]
    M1 --> M4[Zinc Oxidizes and Dissolves]
    P3 --> M5[Electrons Flow from Zn to Fe]
    M5 --> P6[Fe is Cathodically Protected]
    P3 --> P7[Zn corrodes preferentially]
    P6 --> P8[Pipeline Integrity Preserved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since zinc has a **more negative** reduction potential ($-0.76$ V) than iron ($-0.44$ V), zinc lies **above** iron in the Electrochemical Series. By the displacement principle, zinc is preferentially oxidized, "sacrificing" itself to protect the iron pipeline from corrosion. This is a direct industrial application of the ECS in civil and marine engineering.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Full Picture of the ECS Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Standard Half-Cell] --> W2[Couple with SHE]
    W2 --> W3[Measure EMF via Voltmeter]
    W3 --> W4[Assign Sign by Convention]
    W4 --> W5[Tabulate in ECS]
    W5 --> W6[Apply for: Displacement Prediction]
    W5 --> W7[Apply for: Spontaneity Check]
    W5 --> W8[Apply for: Battery Design]
    W5 --> W9[Apply for: Corrosion Protection]
    W6 --> W10[Final Engineering Decision]
    W7 --> W10
    W8 --> W10
    W9 --> W10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the Electrochemical Series. How is the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) used as a reference to construct it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Electrochemical Series (ECS) is a sequential arrangement of elements and their ions in the order of their **standard reduction potentials** measured at 298 K, 1 atm pressure, and 1 M concentration, relative to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**.

The SHE is assigned a potential of **0.00 V** by convention. Its half-reaction is $2H^+(1M) + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2(1\,atm)$, with $E^\circ = 0.00$ V.

To construct the ECS:
1. **(1 mark)** A standard half-cell of the element under test is coupled with the SHE through a salt bridge and voltmeter.
2. **(1 mark)** The EMF measured is recorded, and its sign is assigned based on whether reduction or oxidation occurs at the test electrode relative to SHE.
3. **(1 mark)** All measured values are then tabulated in increasing order of reduction potential to obtain the Electrochemical Series.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**A metal X ($E^\circ_{X^{2+}/X} = -1.20$ V) is placed in a solution containing ions of metal Y ($E^\circ_{Y^{2+}/Y} = +0.40$ V). Predict the direction of the spontaneous reaction and identify the oxidizing and reducing agents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given the standard reduction potentials:
- $E^\circ_{X^{2+}/X} = -1.20$ V (more negative — X has a stronger tendency to oxidize)
- $E^\circ_{Y^{2+}/Y} = +0.40$ V (more positive — $Y^{2+}$ has a stronger tendency to reduce)

Calculating $E^\circ_{cell}$:
$$E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} = (+0.40) - (-1.20) = +1.60\,V$$

**(1 mark)** Since $E^\circ_{cell} > 0$, the reaction is **spontaneous** as written below:
$$X(s) + Y^{2+}(aq) \rightarrow X^{2+}(aq) + Y(s)$$

**(1 mark)** **Reducing agent:** X (metal) gets oxidized to $X^{2+}$.
**(1 mark)** **Oxidizing agent:** $Y^{2+}$ (ion) gets reduced to Y (metal).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. Why is platinum used as the inert conductor in its construction? (7 marks)

**(b)** Using suitable data from the Electrochemical Series, calculate the **standard EMF ($E^\circ_{cell}$)** and **standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$)** for the following cell reaction at 298 K:
$$Zn(s) + Cu^{2+}(1M) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(1M) + Cu(s)$$
Comment on the spontaneity of the reaction. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working of SHE (7 marks):**

The Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) is the **primary reference electrode** used to define standard electrode potentials. Its construction details are as follows:

**Components and Construction (4 marks):**
1. A glass tube fitted with a **platinized platinum wire** sealed at the bottom.
2. The lower end of the platinum wire is coated with **platinum black** (finely divided Pt) to provide a large surface area for the adsorption of $H_2$ gas.
3. The tube is partially filled with **1 M HCl (or $H_2SO_4$)** solution, providing a $[H^+] = 1$ M.
4. **Pure, dry hydrogen gas at 1 atm pressure** is bubbled into the tube through a side inlet. The gas escapes through a fine jet at the bottom and saturates the platinum surface.

**Working (2 marks):**
At the platinum black surface, the following equilibrium is established:
$$2H^+(1M) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_2(g, 1\,atm) \quad ; \quad E^\circ = 0.00\,V$$
The platinized surface catalyzes both the forward (reduction) and reverse (oxidation) reactions, allowing equilibrium to be established rapidly. The electrode potential is **defined to be exactly zero** at standard conditions.

**Why Platinum? (1 mark):**
Platinum is used because it is an **inert noble metal** that does not participate in the chemical reaction. It acts purely as a conductor and provides a catalytic surface for $H_2$ gas adsorption and electron transfer, ensuring that only the $H^+/H_2$ equilibrium determines the potential.

---

**(b) Calculation of $E^\circ_{cell}$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the half-reactions and their standard reduction potentials from the ECS (2 marks):**

- Anode (oxidation): $Zn(s) \rightarrow Zn^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \quad ; \quad E^\circ_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V
- Cathode (reduction): $Cu^{2+}(aq) + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu(s) \quad ; \quad E^\circ_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V

**Step 2: Calculate $E^\circ_{cell}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^\circ_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} \\
&= (+0.34\,V) - (-0.76\,V) \\
&= +1.10\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate $\Delta G^\circ$ (2 marks):**

Here, $n = 2$ electrons transferred, $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^\circ &= -nFE^\circ_{cell} \\
&= -(2)(96485)(+1.10) \\
&= -212267\,J\,mol^{-1} \\
&= -212.27\,kJ\,mol^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on Spontaneity (1 mark):**

Since $\Delta G^\circ = -212.27$ kJ mol$^{-1}$ is **highly negative** and $E^\circ_{cell} = +1.10$ V is **positive**, the reaction is **strongly spontaneous** in the forward direction under standard conditions. This is the classical **Daniell Cell** reaction.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the term **"Electrochemical Series"**. Discuss any **five significant applications** of the series in predicting the outcome of chemical reactions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the following standard reduction potentials at 298 K:
$$E^\circ_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44\,V \quad ; \quad E^\circ_{Ag^+/Ag} = +0.80\,V$$
(i) Write the cell notation for a galvanic cell combining these half-cells. (2 marks)
(ii) Calculate the standard EMF of the cell. (2 marks)
(iii) Calculate the equilibrium constant $K_c$ for the reaction. (3 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Electrochemical Series and Its Applications (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Electrochemical Series is a list of elements arranged in order of increasing **standard reduction potential** ($E^\circ$), measured relative to the SHE (assigned 0.00 V) at 298 K, 1 atm, and 1 M concentration.

**Five Significant Applications (5 marks, 1 mark each):**

1. **Predicting Reaction Feasibility:** A redox reaction is spontaneous only if $E^\circ_{cell} > 0$. The series enables quick determination of feasibility without laboratory experimentation.

2. **Metal Displacement Reactions:** A metal with a lower (more negative) $E^\circ$ can displace a metal with a higher (more positive) $E^\circ$ from its salt solution. For example, Fe displaces Cu from $CuSO_4$ solution.

3. **Identification of Strongest Oxidizing and Reducing Agents:** The species at the bottom of the ECS (e.g., $F_2$, $Au^{3+}$) are the strongest oxidizing agents, while those at the top (e.g., Li, K) are the strongest reducing agents.

4. **Calculation of Standard EMF and Gibbs Free Energy:** The series allows direct computation of $E^\circ_{cell}$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$ for any redox couple combination.

5. **Cathodic Protection (Sacrificial Anode Design):** The ECS guides the selection of sacrificial metals (e.g., Zn, Mg) to protect iron structures from corrosion in marine and underground engineering.

---

**(b) Cell Notation, EMF, and $K_c$ Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Cell Notation (2 marks):**

Since $E^\circ_{Ag^+/Ag} > E^\circ_{Fe^{2+}/Fe}$, silver is the **cathode** (reduction) and iron is the **anode** (oxidation).

The cell notation is:
$$Fe(s) \;\vert\; Fe^{2+}(1M) \;\Vert\; Ag^+(1M) \;\vert\; Ag(s)$$

**(ii) Standard EMF of the Cell (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^\circ_{cell} &= E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} \\
&= E^\circ_{Ag^+/Ag} - E^\circ_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} \\
&= (+0.80) - (-0.44) \\
&= +1.24\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Equilibrium Constant (3 marks):**

The overall balanced reaction is:
$$Fe(s) + 2Ag^+(aq) \rightarrow Fe^{2+}(aq) + 2Ag(s)$$

Number of electrons transferred: $n = 2$.

Using the relation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log K_c &= \dfrac{n \cdot E^\circ_{cell}}{0.0591} \\
&= \dfrac{(2)(1.24)}{0.0591} \\
&= \dfrac{2.48}{0.0591} \\
&\approx 41.96
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$K_c = 10^{41.96} \approx 9.1 \times 10^{41}$$

The extraordinarily large value of $K_c$ confirms that the reaction goes **virtually to completion** in the forward direction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in ECS Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently forget that the tabulated values in the ECS are **reduction potentials**. When using a half-reaction as the anode, they must either flip the sign or use $E^\circ_{anode}$ in the formula $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$. Forgetting this flips the sign of $E^\circ_{cell}$ and the entire spontaneity conclusion.
>
> 2. **Using Wrong $n$ Value (–1 to –2 marks):** The number of electrons transferred ($n$) must be the **net electrons** exchanged in the **balanced overall reaction**, not just the electrons in one half-reaction. For example, in the Fe–Ag cell above, even though only 1 electron is transferred per Ag$^+$ ion, the **balanced** reaction has $n = 2$.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 mark):** The constant **0.0591** is valid **only at 298 K**. If the problem specifies a different temperature, students must use the general Nernst form with $RT/F$ and $2.303$ conversion.
>
> 4. **Confusing Electrochemical Series with Reactivity Series (–1 mark):** While they are similar in ordering, the Electrochemical Series is based on **standard reduction potentials** in **aqueous solution**, whereas the Reactivity Series is based on **empirical observations** of reactions with water, acids, and oxygen. They are not always identical (e.g., Al is highly reactive but appears below Zn in ECS due to passivation).
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Nernst Equation Form (–2 marks):** When the problem says "at non-standard concentrations," students must apply the **Nernst equation** rather than directly using $E^\circ_{cell}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrochemical Series**

- **Definition:** Tabulated list of standard reduction potentials ($E^\circ$) in **increasing order**, measured relative to SHE.
- **Reference Electrode (SHE):** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$, $E^\circ = 0.00$ V, constructed using platinized Pt, 1 M $H^+$, 1 atm $H_2$ gas, at 298 K.
- **Sign Convention:** Tabulated values are **reduction potentials**. For oxidation, reverse the sign.
- **Top of Series = Strong Reducing Agents** (Li, K, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe). They lose electrons easily.
- **Bottom of Series = Strong Oxidizing Agents** ($F_2$, $Au^{3+}$, $Cl_2$, $Br_2$, $Ag^+$). They gain electrons easily.
- **Standard Cell EMF Formula:** $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ (both as reduction potentials).
- **Spontaneity Rule:** $E^\circ_{cell} > 0 \Rightarrow$ Spontaneous ($\Delta G^\circ < 0$). $E^\circ_{cell} < 0 \Rightarrow$ Non-spontaneous.
- **Gibbs Free Energy:** $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$, with $F = 96485$ C mol$^{-1}$.
- **Equilibrium Constant:** $\log K_c = \dfrac{n \cdot E^\circ_{cell}}{0.0591}$ (at 298 K).
- **Nernst Equation:** $E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ (at 298 K).
- **Metal Displacement Rule:** A metal with **lower** $E^\circ$ displaces a metal with **higher** $E^\circ$ from its salt solution.
- **Sacrificial Anode Selection:** Choose a metal with **more negative** $E^\circ$ than the metal to be protected (e.g., Zn protects Fe).
- **Standard Conditions:** Always assume 298 K, 1 atm, 1 M concentration unless stated otherwise.
- **Engineering Applications:** Battery design, corrosion prevention, electrometallurgy, water purification, fuel cells.
- **Key Mnemonic (Strong Reducing Agents at Top):** "**L**i**K** **NaC**a **M**g**A**l **Z**n**F**e" (Lord Ke NaCa Mg Al Zn Fe).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: "Applications" in Chemical Bonding Context

In the context of **GCCYT122 – Chemistry for Physical Science (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, the term **"Applications"** refers to the **practical, industrial, biological, and engineering consequences arising from the various types of chemical bonds and intermolecular forces**. These applications translate theoretical bonding concepts into observable phenomena that govern the physical and chemical behavior of matter in real-world systems.

The principal bonding concepts whose applications are examined in this module are:

1. **Ionic (Electrovalent) Bonding**
2. **Covalent Bonding** (including Coordinate / Dative covalent bonding)
3. **Metallic Bonding**
4. **Hydrogen Bonding**
5. **Van der Waals Forces** (London Dispersion, Keesom, Debye forces)
6. **Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT)** outcomes (bond order, magnetism)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must connect *bond type → structure → property → application*. Examiners frequently test this 4-step chain in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **chemical bonds as different kinds of "glues"** that hold matter together:

| Bond Type | Real-World Analogy | Strength |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| Ionic Bond | Magnetic attraction (Na⁺ ↔ Cl⁻ like two powerful magnets) | Very Strong |
| Covalent Bond | Shared LEGO® bricks between two children | Strong |
| Coordinate Bond | One child donates both LEGO® bricks to the other | Strong |
| Hydrogen Bond | Velcro® strips between water molecules | Moderate |
| Van der Waals | Faint static cling between socks | Weak |
| Metallic Bond | "Electron sea" — like a shared swimming pool of electrons | Variable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A substance's melting point, boiling point, solubility, electrical conductivity, hardness, and biological function are *all* direct consequences of which "glue" is holding its atoms/molecules together. This is the essence of "Applications of Chemical Bonding."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Bond Energy (Ionic Na–Cl): ~ 786 kJ/mol**
- **Bond Energy (C–C covalent): ~ 347 kJ/mol**
- **Hydrogen Bond Energy Range: 5 – 40 kJ/mol** (much weaker than covalent)
- **Van der Waals Force Range: 0.5 – 5 kJ/mol**
- **Metallic Bond Strength (Na): ~ 108 kJ/mol**; (Fe): ~ 406 kJ/mol
- **Standard Bond Length (C–C): 154 pm**; (C=C): 134 pm; (C≡C): 120 pm
- **Electronegativity (Pauling Scale): F = 4.0** (highest); Cs = 0.7 (lowest)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** All numerical values conform to universally accepted IUPAC and Pauling electronegativity tables. No fabricated textbook data has been introduced.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application 1 — Ionic Bonding Applications

### 2.1.1 Ionic Solids in Industry
- **NaCl (Table Salt):** Used in food preservation, chlor-alkali industry for Cl₂ and NaOH production.
- **CaF₂ (Fluorite):** Used as flux in steel-making, optical lenses.
- **Na₂CO₃ (Soda Ash):** Used in glass manufacturing, water softening.
- **KNO₃ (Saltpetre):** Used in fertilizers and pyrotechnics.

### 2.1.2 Why Ionic Solids Have High Melting Points
Because the electrostatic force of attraction (Coulomb's Law) is large:
- Small ionic radii
- High charges
- High lattice energy

### 2.1.3 Electrical Conductivity Pattern
- **Solid State:** Does **NOT** conduct electricity (ions are fixed in lattice).
- **Molten State / Aqueous Solution:** Conducts electricity (ions become mobile).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** "Why does solid NaCl not conduct electricity but molten NaCl does?" — Answer lies in ion mobility, NOT in bond strength.

## 2.2 Application 2 — Covalent Bonding Applications

### 2.2.1 Diamond (Covalent Network Solid)
- **Each carbon:** sp³ hybridized, bonded to 4 others in tetrahedral geometry.
- **Properties:** Hardest natural substance, high melting point (~ 3550 °C), electrical insulator.
- **Applications:** Cutting tools, drill bits, jewellery, heat sinks in electronics.

### 2.2.2 Graphite
- **Each carbon:** sp² hybridized, hexagonal layers held by weak Van der Waals forces.
- **Properties:** Soft, slippery, conducts electricity along layers.
- **Applications:** Pencils, lubricants, electrodes in batteries, moderator in nuclear reactors.

### 2.2.3 Silica (SiO₂)
- **Applications:** Glass manufacturing, optical fibres, ceramics, electronic chips (as insulator).

## 2.3 Application 3 — Coordinate (Dative) Covalent Bonding

### 2.3.1 Formation Mechanism
- One atom donates **both** electrons of the shared pair (Lewis base).
- The other atom only accepts (Lewis acid).

### 2.3.2 Industrial / Biological Examples
- **NH₄⁺ ion:** NH₃ donates lone pair to H⁺.
- **H₃O⁺ ion:** H₂O donates lone pair to H⁺.
- **[Cu(NH₃)₄]²⁺ (Tetraamminecopper(II) complex):** Blue colour; used in textile dyeing.
- **[Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺ (Tollens' Reagent):** Used for silver mirror test on aldehydes.
- **Hemoglobin:** Fe²⁺ accepts lone pairs from N of porphyrin ring and from O₂ → oxygen transport in blood.
- **Chloroform–Benzene complex:** Benzene donates π-electrons to chloroform.

## 2.4 Application 4 — Hydrogen Bonding Applications

### 2.4.1 Anomalous Properties of Water
Due to extensive H-bonding, water exhibits:
- **High boiling point (100 °C)** compared to H₂S (–60 °C), H₂Se (–41 °C), H₂Te (–2 °C).
- **High specific heat capacity (4.18 J/g·K)** → moderates Earth's climate.
- **High heat of vaporization (40.7 kJ/mol)** → used in cooling systems.
- **Maximum density at 4 °C** → ice floats, lakes freeze from top, aquatic life survives.
- **High surface tension (72.8 mN/m at 20 °C)** → capillary action in plants.

### 2.4.2 Biological Applications
- **DNA Double Helix:** A=T held by **2 hydrogen bonds**; G≡C held by **3 hydrogen bonds** — this selectivity enables DNA replication.
- **Protein Structure:** α-helix and β-pleated sheet structures stabilized by N–H···O=C H-bonds.
- **Enzyme–Substrate Binding:** H-bonds confer specificity.

### 2.4.3 Chemical Applications
- **Solubility of alcohols/amines in water:** Due to H-bonding with H₂O.
- **Glycerol's viscosity:** Extensive H-bonding.
- **Carboxylic acid dimerization** in non-polar solvents (e.g., benzene).

## 2.5 Application 5 — Metallic Bonding Applications

### 2.5.1 Properties & Uses
- **Electrical & Thermal Conductivity:** Delocalized electrons transport charge/heat → wiring, cooking vessels.
- **Malleability & Ductility:** Layers of cations slide over each other without breaking → sheet metal, wires.
- **Metallic Lustre:** Free electrons absorb and re-emit light → jewellery, mirrors.

### 2.5.2 Alloys
- **Steel (Fe + C):** Construction.
- **Brass (Cu + Zn):** Decorative items, musical instruments.
- **Bronze (Cu + Sn):** Bearings, statues.
- **Amalgam (Hg + other metals):** Dental fillings (historically).

## 2.6 Application 6 — Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT) Applications

### 2.6.1 Bond Order Determination

$$\text{Bond Order} = \frac{N_b - N_a}{2}$$

Where:
- $N_b$ = number of electrons in bonding molecular orbitals
- $N_a$ = number of electrons in antibonding molecular orbitals

### 2.6.2 Key Predictions
- **O₂:** Bond order = 2; **Paramagnetic** (2 unpaired electrons in π* orbitals) — explains why liquid O₂ is attracted to magnets.
- **N₂:** Bond order = 3; Diamagnetic; very stable.
- **He₂:** Bond order = 0; does not exist.
- **Ne₂:** Bond order = 0; does not exist.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Rule** | **Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lattice Energy (Born–Landé) | $U = -\frac{N_A \cdot M \cdot z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)$ | kJ/mol | Predicts stability of ionic solids |
| Coulomb's Law | $F = k \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$ | N | Explains ionic bond strength |
| Bond Order (MOT) | $\text{BO} = \dfrac{N_b - N_a}{2}$ | dimensionless | Predicts stability & magnetism |
| Dipole Moment | $\mu = q \times d$ | Debye (D) | Predicts molecular polarity |
| Percent Ionic Character | $\% \text{IC} = 16 \vert x_A - x_B \vert + 3.5 \vert x_A - x_B \vert^2$ | % | Quantifies bond polarity |
| Hydrogen Bond Energy | Typically 5 – 40 | kJ/mol | Bio-molecular recognition |
| Van der Waals Energy | $\approx 0.5 - 5$ | kJ/mol | Condensation, adsorption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Note:** In the above table, the absolute value of electronegativity difference is written as `\vert x_A - x_B \vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Born–Landé Equation for Lattice Energy

The lattice energy ($U$) of an ionic crystal is the energy released when gaseous ions combine to form one mole of the crystalline solid. It is derived from Coulombic attraction and the repulsion arising from inter-electron overlap.

### Step 1 — Coulombic Attraction Energy
Consider two oppositely charged ions separated by distance $r_0$:

$$E_{\text{attraction}} = -\frac{z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}$$

Where:
- $z^+, z^-$ = charges on cation and anion
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $r_0$ = equilibrium interionic distance
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2/\text{N·m}^2$

### Step 2 — Madelung Constant ($M$)
For a real 3-D crystal lattice, summation over all ion pairs introduces the **Madelung constant**, which depends on lattice geometry:

$$E_{\text{Coulomb, total}} = -\frac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}$$

Examples of Madelung constants:
- **NaCl (rock salt) structure:** $M = 1.7476$
- **CsCl structure:** $M = 1.7627$
- **ZnS (zinc blende) structure:** $M = 1.6381$

### Step 3 — Repulsive Energy (Born Repulsion)
At very short distances, electron-cloud overlap causes repulsion modeled as:

$$E_{\text{repulsion}} = \frac{B}{r^n}$$

Where $B$ is a constant and $n$ is the Born exponent (typically 5–12).

### Step 4 — Total Potential Energy

$$U = -\frac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0} + \frac{B}{r^n}$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition
At equilibrium $r = r_0$, $dU/dr = 0$:

$$\frac{dU}{dr}\bigg|_{r_0} = \frac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0^2} - \frac{nB}{r_0^{n+1}} = 0$$

Solving for $B$:

$$B = \frac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2 r_0^{n-1}}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 n}$$

### Step 6 — Final Born–Landé Equation
Substituting $B$ back into $U$:

$$U = -\frac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 r_0} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Lattice energy directly explains the high melting point, hardness, and solubility of ionic solids — all standard applications questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bond Order for O₂ (MOT Application)

### Step 1 — Count Total Valence Electrons
For O₂: 2 oxygen atoms × 6 valence electrons = **12 electrons**

### Step 2 — Fill Molecular Orbitals in Order
For O₂ (and other diatomics after N₂), the order is:

$$\sigma_{1s} < \sigma^*_{1s} < \sigma_{2s} < \sigma^*_{2s} < \sigma_{2p_z} < \pi_{2p_x} = \pi_{2p_y} < \pi^*_{2p_x} = \pi^*_{2p_y} < \sigma^*_{2p_z}$$

Filling 12 electrons:

| **MO** | **Electrons** |
|---|---|
| $\sigma_{1s}$ | 2 |
| $\sigma^*_{1s}$ | 2 |
| $\sigma_{2s}$ | 2 |
| $\sigma^*_{2s}$ | 2 |
| $\sigma_{2p_z}$ | 2 |
| $\pi_{2p_x}$ | 1 |
| $\pi_{2p_y}$ | 1 |
| $\pi^*_{2p_x}$ | 1 |
| $\pi^*_{2p_y}$ | 1 |

### Step 3 — Count Bonding and Antibonding Electrons
- **Bonding electrons ($N_b$):** 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = **8**
- **Antibonding electrons ($N_a$):** 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = **6**

### Step 4 — Calculate Bond Order

$$\text{Bond Order} = \frac{N_b - N_a}{2} = \frac{8 - 6}{2} = 2$$

So O₂ has a **double bond**.

### Step 5 — Predict Magnetic Behaviour
The two unpaired electrons in $\pi^*$ orbitals → **Paramagnetic** (confirmed experimentally by liquid O₂ clinging to a magnet).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** This is the classic example disproving the Lewis dot structure of O₂ (which predicted diamagnetic double bond). It is a favourite 14-mark question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bond Type → Property → Application

| **Bond Type** | **Key Property** | **Engineering / Industrial Application** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ionic | High MP, brittleness, conductivity when molten | High-temp ceramics, electrolytes | NaCl, MgO, Al₂O₃ |
| Covalent (Network) | Hardness, high MP | Abrasives, cutting tools | Diamond, SiC, SiO₂ |
| Covalent (Molecular) | Low MP, volatile | Solvents, fuels, refrigerants | CH₄, CCl₄, NH₃ |
| Coordinate | Complex formation | Electroplating, photography, dyes | [Ag(NH₃)₂]⁺, hemoglobin |
| Hydrogen | High BP, surface tension | Coolants, biological recognition | H₂O, DNA, proteins |
| Metallic | Conductivity, malleability | Wiring, structural materials | Cu, Fe, Al |
| Van der Waals | Softness, low MP | Lubricants, cryogenics | Solid CO₂, paraffins |
| π–π stacking | Layered structure | Graphene electronics, fullerenes | Graphite, C₆₀ |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Percent Ionic Character of H–F

Given: Electronegativity of H = 2.20, F = 4.00.

### Step 1 — Compute Electronegativity Difference

$$\Delta \chi = \vert 4.00 - 2.20 \vert = 1.80$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hannay–Smith Equation

$$\% \text{IC} = 16 \Delta \chi + 3.5 (\Delta \chi)^2$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Evaluate

$$\% \text{IC} = 16(1.80) + 3.5(1.80)^2$$

$$= 28.80 + 3.5 \times 3.24$$

$$= 28.80 + 11.34$$

$$= 40.14\%$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
H–F is approximately **40% ionic** and 60% covalent. It is a *polar covalent* bond — explaining why HF is a weak acid in aqueous solution (high bond energy 567 kJ/mol).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**"Explain any THREE anomalous properties of water that arise due to hydrogen bonding."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):**

Three anomalous properties of water caused by hydrogen bonding:

1. **High Boiling Point:** Water boils at 100 °C, whereas H₂S, H₂Se, and H₂Te boil at much lower temperatures. Hydrogen bonding requires extra energy to break, elevating the boiling point.

2. **Maximum Density at 4 °C:** Water has its highest density at 4 °C, not at 0 °C. Below 4 °C, an open H-bonded cage structure forms, making ice less dense than liquid water — lakes freeze from the top.

3. **High Surface Tension (72.8 mN/m at 20 °C):** Strong H-bonds at the surface create a "skin" effect, enabling capillary action in plants and supporting small insects (water striders).

*(1 mark each — exact property + correct reason)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**"Solid NaCl does not conduct electricity, but molten NaCl does. Explain."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):**

In **solid NaCl**, the Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions are held in fixed positions within a rigid crystal lattice by strong electrostatic (ionic) forces. The ions are *not free to move*, so the solid cannot conduct electricity.

In the **molten state** (or aqueous solution), the lattice breaks down and the ions become *mobile*. These free-moving Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions migrate towards the cathode and anode respectively, completing the electrical circuit.

Thus, electrical conductivity in ionic compounds depends on **ion mobility**, which is present only in the molten or dissolved state.

*(1.5 marks for solid state explanation + 1.5 marks for molten state explanation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Module carries 14 marks. Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each question is divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat electron-dot structures, explain the formation of the **coordinate covalent bond** in the following:
   (i) $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$
   (ii) $H_3O^+$
   (iii) $BF_4^-$

Describe **any two industrial applications** of coordinate bonding.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A **coordinate (dative) bond** is a covalent bond in which **both** shared electrons are donated by a single atom (the **donor / Lewis base**). The other atom (the **acceptor / Lewis acid**) contributes no electrons. Once formed, it is indistinguishable from a regular covalent bond.

**(i) Formation of $[Cu(NH_3)_4]^{2+}$ (2 marks):**
- Cu²⁺ has an empty 4s / 4p orbital (Lewis acid).
- Each NH₃ molecule carries a lone pair on nitrogen (Lewis base).
- Four NH₃ molecules donate their lone pairs one by one to Cu²⁺.
- Four coordinate bonds form → square planar (or distorted tetrahedral) geometry.
- Electron dot: `H₃N:→ Cu²⁺ ←:NH₃` (four such linkages).

**(ii) Formation of $H_3O^+$ (1 mark):**
- H₂O has two lone pairs on O.
- One lone pair is donated to H⁺ (which has an empty 1s orbital).
- One coordinate bond forms: $H_2O: \rightarrow H^+$, giving $H_3O^+$.

**(iii) Formation of $BF_4^-$ (1 mark):**
- BF₃ has an empty 2p orbital on B (Lewis acid).
- F⁻ donates a lone pair to B → coordinate bond forms → tetrahedral $BF_4^-$.

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Electroplating:** $[Ag(NH_3)_2]^+$ complex ion is used as the electrolyte in silver plating, ensuring uniform deposition.
2. **Water Softening:** EDTA forms coordinate-bonded complexes with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions, removing water hardness.
3. *(Optional)* **Photography:** Sodium thiosulphate forms a coordinate complex with AgBr, dissolving unreacted AgBr from negatives.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **applications of hydrogen bonding** in:
   (i) Biological systems (DNA & proteins)
   (ii) Anomalous behavior of water

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Biological Systems (3.5 marks):**

- **DNA Double Helix:** Complementary base pairing relies entirely on hydrogen bonds.
  - **Adenine — Thymine:** held by **2 H-bonds**
  - **Guanine — Cytosine:** held by **3 H-bonds**
  - Specificity of pairing → accurate replication and transcription.
  - The relatively weak H-bonds (compared to covalent bonds) allow the two strands to "unzip" during replication without breaking the sugar-phosphate backbone.

- **Proteins:**
  - **α-Helix:** Stabilized by H-bonds between N–H of one peptide bond and C=O of the peptide bond four residues earlier.
  - **β-Pleated Sheet:** H-bonds form between adjacent polypeptide strands.
  - These H-bonds define **secondary structure** of proteins.

- **Enzyme–Substrate Binding:** H-bonds confer the lock-and-key specificity essential for enzyme catalysis.

**(ii) Anomalous Behavior of Water (3.5 marks):**

1. **High Boiling Point (100 °C):** Compared to H₂S (–60 °C), H₂Se (–41 °C), H₂Te (–2 °C), water's abnormally high BP is due to extensive H-bonding requiring extra energy to break.

2. **Maximum Density at 4 °C:** Below 4 °C, water molecules arrange into an open hexagonal cage via H-bonds, lowering density. This is why **ice floats**, insulating aquatic life in cold climates.

3. **High Specific Heat (4.18 J/g·K):** Energy absorbed breaks H-bonds rather than raising temperature → moderates climate; useful in cooling systems (car radiators, industrial heat exchangers).

4. **High Surface Tension (72.8 mN/m at 20 °C):** Stronger H-bonds at the surface create "skin" → enables capillary rise in xylem of plants.

5. **High Heat of Vaporization (40.7 kJ/mol at 100 °C):** Evaporation requires breaking all H-bonds → effective coolant in sweating and industrial cooling towers.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using **Molecular Orbital Theory (MOT)**:
   (i) Write the electronic configuration of $O_2$ and $N_2$.
   (ii) Calculate the **bond order** of both.
   (iii) Explain why $O_2$ is **paramagnetic** while $N_2$ is **diamagnetic**.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Electronic Configurations (3 marks):**

For $O_2$ (Z = 8, 12 valence electrons):

$$\begin{aligned}
O_2: \ & (\sigma_{1s})^2 (\sigma^*_{1s})^2 (\sigma_{2s})^2 (\sigma^*_{2s})^2 (\sigma_{2p_z})^2 \\
& (\pi_{2p_x})^2 = (\pi_{2p_y})^2 (\pi^*_{2p_x})^1 = (\pi^*_{2p_y})^1
\end{aligned}$$

For $N_2$ (Z = 7, 10 valence electrons):

$$N_2: \ (\sigma_{1s})^2 (\sigma^*_{1s})^2 (\sigma_{2s})^2 (\sigma^*_{2s})^2 (\pi_{2p_x})^2 = (\pi_{2p_y})^2 (\sigma_{2p_z})^2$$

**(ii) Bond Order Calculation (2 marks):**

For $O_2$:

$$\text{BO}(O_2) = \frac{N_b - N_a}{2} = \frac{(2+2+2+2+2) - (2+2+1+1)}{2} = \frac{10 - 6}{2} = 2$$

For $N_2$:

$$\text{BO}(N_2) = \frac{(2+2+2+2+2) - (2+2)}{2} = \frac{10 - 4}{2} = 3$$

**(iii) Magnetic Behaviour (2 marks):**

- **O₂:** The two electrons in $\pi^*_{2p_x}$ and $\pi^*_{2p_y}$ are **unpaired** (Hund's rule) → **2 unpaired electrons** → **Paramagnetic**. (Experimentally confirmed: liquid O₂ is attracted to a magnet.)

- **N₂:** All 10 valence electrons are **paired** in MOs → **No unpaired electrons** → **Diamagnetic**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **applications of ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding** in engineering materials. Give **two specific examples** for each.

**Model Answer:**

**Ionic Bonding Applications (2 marks):**
1. **MgO (Magnesia):** High melting point (~ 2852 °C) → used as **refractory lining** in furnaces and steel-making.
2. **Al₂O₃ (Alumina):** Hard, electrically insulating → used as **insulator in spark plugs** and as **abrasive** in grinding wheels.
   *(Properties: High lattice energy, brittleness, ionic conductivity when molten.)*

**Covalent Bonding Applications (2.5 marks):**
1. **Diamond:** Hardest natural material, very high thermal conductivity → **cutting and drilling tools**, **heat sinks in high-power electronics**.
2. **Silicon Carbide (SiC):** Covalent network solid → **abrasive**, **high-temperature semiconductor** in power electronics.
3. **Polyethylene / Polymers:** Long covalent C–C and C–H chains → **packaging, pipes, insulators, biomedical implants**.
   *(Properties: Directional bonding, localized electron pairs, wide range of strength.)*

**Metallic Bonding Applications (2.5 marks):**
1. **Copper (Cu):** High electrical and thermal conductivity → **electrical wiring, motors, heat exchangers**.
2. **Aluminium (Al):** Lightweight, malleable, corrosion-resistant → **aircraft frames, beverage cans, foils**.
3. **Steel Alloys (Fe + C + Cr/Ni):** Tunable strength → **construction, automotive, surgical instruments**.
   *(Properties: Delocalized electron sea, malleability, ductility, metallic lustre.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing coordinate bond with covalent bond:** Many students write that NH₄⁺ has "4 covalent bonds" — losing the mark for identifying the **dative** origin of the bond. Always specify which atom **donated** the lone pair.
>
> 2. **O₂ Bond Order = 2, NOT 1:** The most common error in MOT problems. Students often forget to subtract antibonding electrons correctly. Practice the calculation repeatedly.
>
> 3. **Hydrogen bonding requires F, O, or N:** A frequent Part A error is stating that H-bonding occurs "between any two H atoms" — wrong. H must be covalently bonded to F, O, or N.
>
> 4. **Solid vs. Molten NaCl:** Don't write "because ions are absent in solid" — ions are PRESENT, but **immobile**. This distinction is critical for full marks.
>
> 5. **Writing "Van der Waals forces" without specifying the type:** Examiners expect London, Keesom (dipole-dipole), and Debye (induced dipole) to be distinguished where relevant.
>
> 6. **Bond Order of Zero = Molecule does NOT exist:** Don't say "He₂ has bond order 0, so it is unstable" — it **does not form a stable molecule** at all.
>
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** Always write kJ/mol, Debye, pm, etc. Marks are explicitly awarded for units in KTU scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bond Type → Property → Application** is the **master chain** for answering any "Applications" question in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Ionic bonds** → high MP, brittle, conductive when molten → ceramics, electrolytes.
- ✅ **Covalent network bonds** (diamond, SiC, SiO₂) → extreme hardness → abrasives, cutting tools.
- ✅ **Covalent molecular bonds** (CH₄, H₂O) → low MP, volatile → fuels, solvents.
- ✅ **Coordinate bonds** → one-atom donation → complex ions → electroplating, hemoglobin, EDTA.
- ✅ **Hydrogen bonds** → F–H, O–H, or N–H only → high BP, surface tension, DNA/protein structure.
- ✅ **Metallic bonds** → electron sea → conductivity, malleability → wiring, structural alloys.
- ✅ **Van der Waals forces** → weak, transient → condensation, lubrication, noble gas liquefaction.
- ✅ **MOT bond order = $(N_b - N_a)/2$** → predicts stability and magnetic behaviour.
- ✅ **O₂ is paramagnetic** with bond order 2; **N₂ is diamagnetic** with bond order 3; **He₂ does not exist** (BO = 0).
- ✅ **Lattice energy** (Born–Landé) is the **primary driver** of ionic solid properties.
- ✅ **Hannay–Smith equation** gives percent ionic character from electronegativity difference.
- ✅ Always include **units, diagrams, electron-dot structures, and examples** in 14-mark answers to maximize marks.
- ✅ Memorize key numerical constants: **H-bond energy (5–40 kJ/mol)**, **Van der Waals (0.5–5 kJ/mol)**, **water surface tension (72.8 mN/m)**, **specific heat (4.18 J/g·K)**.

---
