- ☐ **Rusting of Iron (net):** $4Fe + 3O_2 + 2H_2O \to 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$.
- ☐ **Rust = Fe₂O₃·H₂O** — porous, non-protective, **reddish-brown**; **NOT** the same as protective Al₂O₃.
- ☐ **Nernst Equation (25 °C):** $E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$.
- ☐ **Faraday's Law:** $w = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$.
- ☐ **CR Formula:** $CR(mm/yr) = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW}{\rho(g/cm^3)}$.
- ☐ **EW** = M/n.
- ☐ **Mixed Potential Theory:** Total oxidation rate = Total reduction rate at E_corr.
- ☐ **Evans Diagram:** Intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines → (E_corr, i_corr).
- ☐ **ΔG = -nFE:** Negative ΔG = spontaneous corrosion.
- ☐ **Pourbaix Diagram:** Plots E vs. pH; shows zones of **immunity, corrosion, and passivity**.
- ☐ **E_{H⁺/H₂} = -0.0591 × pH** and **E_{O₂/OH⁻} = 1.23 - 0.0591 × pH** — remember these pH-dependent potentials.
- ☐ **Standard Reduction Potentials to memorize:** Ag⁺/Ag = +0.80 V, Cu²⁺/Cu = +0.34 V, H⁺/H₂ = 0.00 V, Fe²⁺/Fe = -0.44 V, Zn²⁺/Zn = -0.76 V, Mg²⁺/Mg = -2.37 V.
- ☐ **Cathodic Protection:** Connect a more active metal (Zn, Mg) to the structure — it acts as sacrificial anode.
- ☐ **Galvanic Series vs. EMF Series:** Galvanic series is empirical (real seawater), EMF is theoretical (standard conditions).
- ☐ **Engineering Examples:** Rebar in concrete (alkaline passivation), pipelines in acidic soil, ship hulls in seawater (OAC dominant).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Galvanic Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Galvanic Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Galvanic Series** is an empirically determined ranking of metals and alloys based on their relative **corrosion behaviour in a specific real-world environment** (most commonly **seawater** or a **3% NaCl solution** at ambient temperature). It arranges metals and alloys in the order of their practical tendency to corrode when they are coupled with one another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The Galvanic Series is *not* a thermodynamic series. It is a **practical engineering tool** used to predict the severity of galvanic corrosion when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in a corrosive electrolyte. The position of a metal is determined by measuring its **open-circuit corrosion potential (E_corr)** in a given environment.

### 1.2 Distinction: Galvanic Series vs. Electromotive Force (EMF) Series

| Property | EMF Series (Electrochemical Series) | Galvanic Series |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Standard reduction potential ($E^\circ$) measured in 1 M solution at 25 °C | Measured open-circuit corrosion potential in a practical environment |
| **Nature** | Thermodynamic | Empirical / Practical |
| **Coverage** | Pure metals only | Metals **and alloys** |
| **Environment** | Ideal, standardised | Specific real environment (e.g., seawater) |
| **Prediction** | Tendency to corrode thermodynamically | Actual corrosion rate in service |
| **Direction** | Noble (cathode) at top, Active (anode) at bottom | Noble at top, Active at bottom |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ladder of Loyalty"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:**
> Think of the Galvanic Series as a **"Ladder of Loyalty"** in a corrosive environment such as seawater. The metal at the **top of the ladder (Noble end)** is "lazy" and refuses to corrode — it will sit in seawater almost unchanged for years. The metal at the **bottom of the ladder (Active end)** is "self-sacrificing" — it dissolves readily to protect others. When two metals are connected and dipped into seawater, the **less noble (lower)** metal will be the one that corrodes, sacrificing itself for the more noble partner.

In a galvanic couple:

$$
\text{Anode (Active, corrodes)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrons flow} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cathode (Noble, protected)}
$$

### 1.4 Why the Galvanic Series Differs from the EMF Series

The EMF series is built on **standard reduction potentials** (thermodynamic data, e.g., $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V). However, real engineering materials are **alloys** with complex microstructures, surface films, and passivation behaviour. For instance:

- **Aluminium** in the EMF series has $E^\circ = -1.66$ V (highly active).
- In practice, aluminium forms a **protective oxide film** ($Al_2O_3$) and behaves much more nobly.
- Similarly, **stainless steel** behaves as a noble metal in seawater due to **passivation**, even though iron itself is active.

Hence, the galvanic series reflects **actual service behaviour**, not ideal thermodynamic predictions.

### 1.5 Physical Constant / Standard Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard reference environment for the published galvanic series is **flowing seawater (≈ 3.5% NaCl), at ~25 °C, with a flow rate of ~1 m/s** and dissolved oxygen near saturation. Any deviation (temperature, chloride concentration, aeration) can **shift the position of metals** in the series.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Arrangement of the Galvanic Series

The series is typically written with the **most noble (cathodic) materials at the top** and the **most active (anodic) materials at the bottom**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Top of the Series (Noble / Cathodic) — Corrosion Resistant:**
> - Graphite, Gold, Platinum
> - Silver, Titanium (passive), 18-8 Stainless Steel (passive)
>
> **Middle of the Series:**
> - Copper, Brass, Bronze, Nickel, Tin, Lead
>
> **Bottom of the Series (Active / Anodic) — Corrodes Readily:**
> - Steel, Iron, Aluminium (active), Zinc, Magnesium

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Predicting Galvanic Corrosion

When two metals $M_1$ and $M_2$ form a galvanic couple in an electrolyte:

1. **Identify the position** of both metals in the galvanic series of the given environment.
2. **Determine the direction:** The metal **lower in the series (more active)** becomes the **anode** and dissolves:
   $$M_{\text{anode}} \longrightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$
3. **The metal higher in the series (more noble)** becomes the **cathode** and supports the reduction reaction:
   $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4OH^-$$ (in neutral/alkaline solution)
4. The **greater the separation** in the series, the **higher the driving voltage** and the **more severe the corrosion**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Equation | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| Driving EMF of couple | $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Predicts severity; larger $\Delta E$ means faster anodic dissolution |
| Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $w = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass loss $w$ in grams; $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = **96 485 C mol⁻¹** |
| Corrosion current density | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/m²; current per unit anodic area |
| Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{\text{corr}} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | $i_{\text{corr}}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$, $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) |
| Galvanic series rule | $\Delta E \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Corrosion severity} \uparrow$ | Larger potential difference → higher driving force |
| Stern–Geary equation | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Polarisation resistance $R_p$ (Ω·cm²) gives $i_{\text{corr}}$ |
| Passivity factor | Effective $E$ shifted upward in series | Stainless steel, Al, Ti move "noble" due to oxide films |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Constants to Memorise:**
> - Faraday constant $F$ = **96 485 C mol⁻¹** = **26.8 A·h mol⁻¹**
> - Standard temperature = **25 °C (298.15 K)**
> - Standard state = 1 M, 1 atm, pure phases
> - Conversion: **1 mm/yr ≈ 0.04 inch/yr ≈ 2.6 mpy** (mils per year)

### 2.4 Why and How — Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of the Galvanic Series:**
> 1. **Ship hull & propeller design** — bronze propellers with steel hulls are coupled; the series tells us the *direction* of corrosion.
> 2. **Piping systems** — joining copper to galvanised steel in plumbing accelerates zinc loss.
> 3. **Cathodic protection design** — sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg, Al) are chosen because they sit at the **active end** of the series.
> 4. **Aerospace fastener selection** — avoiding direct contact between carbon-fibre composites and aluminium alloys.
> 5. **Heat exchangers** — tube-sheet material selection to minimise galvanic attack.

### 2.5 Factors That Shift the Position in the Galvanic Series

1. **Aeration** — differential aeration cells form.
2. **Temperature** — increases ionic mobility and shifts potentials.
3. **Chloride concentration** — breaks passive films on stainless steel and aluminium.
4. **pH** — affects oxide stability.
5. **Flow velocity** — high velocity removes protective films.
6. **Area ratio** — small anode : large cathode is the most dangerous combination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Example — Estimating Corrosion Severity

**Problem (Model):**
A copper pipe is joined to a galvanised iron pipe in a domestic water system. Given:
- $E_{\text{Cu}} = +0.34$ V (vs SHE)
- $E_{\text{Zn}} = -0.76$ V (vs SHE)
- Cross-sectional area of copper (cathode) = 100 cm²
- Cross-sectional area of zinc (anode) = 10 cm²
- Resistivity of water path ≈ negligible (assume $I_{\text{cell}} \approx 0.05$ A)

Calculate (a) the cell EMF, (b) the mass of zinc dissolved per day, and (c) the **anodic current density**.

#### Step (a) — Cell EMF

The cell EMF is the difference between the cathode (Cu) and anode (Zn) potentials:

$$
\Delta E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{\text{cell}} = (+0.34) - (-0.76) = +1.10 \text{ V}
$$

This is the **thermodynamic driving force**. The positive sign confirms the reaction is spontaneous.

#### Step (b) — Mass of Zinc Dissolved per Day

The anodic half-reaction is:

$$
Zn \longrightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-
$$

So $n = 2$, and molar mass $M_{Zn} = 65.38$ g mol⁻¹.

Charge passed per day:

$$
Q = I \cdot t = 0.05 \text{ A} \times (24 \times 3600) \text{ s} = 4320 \text{ C}
$$

Mass dissolved:

$$
w = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{4320 \times 65.38}{2 \times 96485}
$$

$$
w = \frac{282\,441.6}{192\,970} = 1.463 \text{ g/day}
$$

#### Step (c) — Anodic Current Density

$$
i_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I}{A_{\text{anode}}} = \frac{0.05 \text{ A}}{10 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.005 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 5000 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is an extremely high anodic current density! The small zinc anode coupled to a large copper cathode creates a **highly unfavourable area ratio** (1:10) which dramatically accelerates corrosion of zinc. This is why large copper fittings joined to small zinc-coated steel fittings fail rapidly.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Metals in Seawater

| Position | Metal / Alloy | Typical $E_{\text{corr}}$ vs SCE (V) | Corrosion Behaviour in Seawater |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top (Noble) | Graphite | +0.30 | Practically immune |
|  | Gold | +0.20 | Immune |
|  | Platinum | +0.15 | Immune |
|  | 18-8 Stainless Steel (passive) | -0.05 | Passive film intact |
|  | Titanium (passive) | -0.10 | Excellent resistance |
|  | Silver | -0.10 | Good resistance |
|  | Cu-Ni (70-30) | -0.20 | Good in seawater |
|  | Copper | -0.30 | Slow, forms patina |
|  | Brass (admiralty) | -0.35 | Dezincification possible |
|  | Lead | -0.50 | Slow attack |
|  | Tin | -0.55 | Slow attack |
|  | Cast Iron | -0.60 | Moderate attack |
|  | Steel | -0.65 | Significant rusting |
|  | Aluminium (active) | -0.75 | Pitting if film breaks |
|  | Zinc | -1.00 | Active — used as sacrificial anode |
| Bottom (Active) | Magnesium | -1.60 | Highly active |

### 3.3 The Three Corrosion-Protection Decisions Guided by the Galvanic Series

**Decision 1 — Avoid the Couple**
- If the potential separation in the series exceeds **~0.25 V**, avoid direct contact if possible.

**Decision 2 — Reverse the Polarity Using a Third Metal**
- Insert a metal **close in the series** to both as a transition piece (e.g., use brass between steel and copper).

**Decision 3 — Add a Sacrificial Anode**
- Attach a metal **lower (more active)** than both — it preferentially corrodes and protects the structure.
- Common sacrificial anodes: **Zn, Mg, Al alloys** (in order of increasing driving voltage).

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Why Smaller Anode = Faster Corrosion

Total cell current $I_{\text{cell}}$ depends on the cell resistance and the mixed potential. The total anodic current must equal total cathodic current:

$$
I_a = I_c = I_{\text{cell}}
$$

The anodic current density is:

$$
i_a = \frac{I_{\text{cell}}}{A_a}
$$

The cathodic current density is:

$$
i_c = \frac{I_{\text{cell}}}{A_c}
$$

The corrosion rate per unit anode area is therefore inversely proportional to the anode area:

$$
i_a \propto \frac{1}{A_a}
$$

For a fixed total current, **halving the anode area doubles its corrosion rate** — this is why fasteners (small anodes) corrode so quickly when fastened to large structural members (large cathodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:**
> Always maintain **anode area ≥ cathode area** in a coupled system. If the cathode is the structural component (e.g., a large copper condenser), the anode should also be large (a thick zinc block), or a separate sacrificial anode must be provided.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Position and Functional Role of the Galvanic Series

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["NOBLE END Cathodic Protected"] --> node2["Passivating Alloys Stainless Steel Titanium"]
    node2 --> node3["Precious Metals Gold Platinum Silver"]
    node3 --> node4["Copper Family Copper Brass Bronze"]
    node4 --> node5["Intermediate Tin Lead"]
    node5 --> node6["Active Alloys Steel Cast Iron"]
    node6 --> node7["ACTIVE END Anodic Corrodes Aluminium Zinc Magnesium"]
    nodeA["Environment Seawater 3.5 percent NaCl 25 C"] --> nodeB["Determines Corrosion Potential E corr"]
    nodeB --> node1
    nodeB --> node7
    nodeC["Design Output"] --> nodeD["Choose compatible pairs"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Insert transition metal"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Add sacrificial anode"]
    node1 --> nodeC
    node7 --> nodeC
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Galvanic Corrosion Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["ANODE Active Metal M1"] -->|"Oxidation: M1 -> M1 n+ + n e-"| nodeX["Electrolyte Seawater"]
    nodeX -->|"Reduction: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- -> 4OH-"| nodeC1["CATHODE Noble Metal M2"]
    nodeC1 -->|"Electron flow e-"| nodeA1
    nodeM1["Consequence: M1 dissolves mass loss"] -.-> nodeA1
    nodeM2["Consequence: M2 protected"] -.-> nodeC1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flowchart for Engineers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["Two metals to be coupled?"] --> nodeQ2{"Delta E in galvanic series > 0.25 V?"}
    nodeQ2 -->|"No"| nodeQ3["SAFE Use direct coupling"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ4{"Critical application?"}
    nodeQ4 -->|"No"| nodeQ5["Use with caution Insulate at joint"]
    nodeQ4 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ6{"Cathode area much greater than anode?"}
    nodeQ6 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ7["Add sacrificial anode Zn Mg Al"]
    nodeQ6 -->|"No"| nodeQ8["Insert transition metal close in series"]
    nodeQ8 --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ9["Monitor periodically"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Mapping — Galvanic Series vs EMF Series

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sub1["EMF SERIES Thermodynamic 1 M 25 C"]
        emf1["Au 1.50 V"] --> emf2["Ag 0.80 V"]
        emf2 --> emf3["Cu 0.34 V"]
        emf3 --> emf4["H2 0.00 V"]
        emf4 --> emf5["Pb minus 0.13 V"]
        emf5 --> emf6["Fe minus 0.44 V"]
        emf6 --> emf7["Zn minus 0.76 V"]
        emf7 --> emf8["Al minus 1.66 V"]
        emf8 --> emf9["Mg minus 2.37 V"]
    end
    subgraph sub2["GALVANIC SERIES Seawater Empirical"]
        gs1["Graphite Pt Au top"] --> gs2["Stainless Steel passive"]
        gs2 --> gs3["Cu Ni Bronze"]
        gs3 --> gs4["Cu Brass"]
        gs4 --> gs5["Pb Sn"]
        gs5 --> gs6["Steel Cast Iron"]
        gs6 --> gs7["Al active"]
        gs7 --> gs8["Zn"]
        gs8 --> gs9["Mg bottom"]
    end
    sub1 -. "Differences: Alloys, Passivation, Real env" .- sub2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> The galvanic series is fundamentally a **ranking** (ordinal data), not a physical object. A block-level architecture flow is the most appropriate engineering representation, capturing both the relative ordering and the cause-effect relationships between position, environment, and design decisions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between the Galvanic Series and the EMF Series. Why is the Galvanic Series more useful in practical corrosion studies?**

**Model Answer:**

| Galvanic Series | EMF Series |
|---|---|
| Empirical ranking in a specific environment | Standardised thermodynamic ranking |
| Includes alloys and passive metals | Only pure elements |
| Orders metals by actual corrosion behaviour in service | Orders metals by standard reduction potential |
| Example position: 18-8 stainless steel near Pt | Position: Fe below Cu, predictable from $E^\circ$ |

The galvanic series is more useful in **practical corrosion studies** because real engineering systems use **alloys**, not pure metals, and the **real environment** (seawater, soil, process fluids) often causes passivation or differential aeration that the ideal EMF series cannot capture. It directly predicts which metal in a coupled pair will corrode in a given service environment.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the effect of "area ratio" on the severity of galvanic corrosion with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

When two dissimilar metals form a galvanic couple, the **total corrosion current** $I_{\text{cell}}$ is shared over both surfaces. If the anode is small and the cathode is large, the same total current must flow through a much smaller anodic area, giving a very high **anodic current density** $i_a = I_{\text{cell}}/A_a$.

**Example:** A small **zinc rivet (anode)** in a large **copper sheet (cathode)** in seawater. The zinc rivet will corrode extremely rapidly because the area ratio is very unfavourable. **Design rule:** Always keep the **anode area ≥ cathode area** or use a separate sacrificial anode of sufficient size.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**(a)** *What is the Galvanic Series? Discuss the factors that affect the relative position of metals in the galvanic series with reference to seawater.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Galvanic Series** is an **empirically determined list** of metals and alloys arranged in the order of their **observed corrosion behaviour in a specific environment**, typically flowing seawater at ambient temperature. Metals at the **noble (top)** end resist corrosion, while those at the **active (bottom)** end corrode readily when coupled with more noble partners.

**Factors affecting position in the series:**

1. **Aeration difference** — Differential aeration cells cause the less-aerated region to act as anode.
2. **Chloride concentration** — Aggressive Cl⁻ ions disrupt passive oxide films on Al, Ti, stainless steel, shifting them towards the active end.
3. **Temperature** — Rising temperature accelerates ionic transport, altering potentials.
4. **Velocity of flow** — High-velocity flow removes protective films, activating the surface.
5. **pH of electrolyte** — Acidic pH destabilises oxide films; alkaline pH favours passivation for some metals.
6. **Biological activity** — Microbial films create new anodic/cathodic zones.
7. **Alloy composition** — Small alloying additions (Cr, Ni, Mo) shift the metal towards the noble end via enhanced passivation.
8. **Surface condition** — Polished, oxide-free surfaces are more active than oxidised ones.

---

**(b)** *A small brass plate (anode) of area 5 cm² is electrically connected to a large copper plate (cathode) of area 200 cm² in a seawater electrolyte. The measured cell current is 0.02 A. Estimate the corrosion current density at the brass anode and comment on the severity.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Anodic current density:**

$$
i_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I}{A_a} = \frac{0.02 \text{ A}}{5 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.004 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 4000 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2
$$

**Cathodic current density:**

$$
i_{\text{cathode}} = \frac{I}{A_c} = \frac{0.02 \text{ A}}{200 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.0001 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 100 \text{ }\mu A/cm^2
$$

**Area ratio:**

$$
\frac{A_c}{A_a} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 : 1
$$

**Comment on severity:**
The brass anode is **40 times smaller** than the copper cathode. A corrosion current density of **4000 µA/cm²** at the brass surface is extremely high — typical service current densities for safe operation are below **10–50 µA/cm²**. The brass plate will suffer **rapid dezincification and perforation** within a short time. This is a classic **unfavourable small-anode : large-cathode** situation and **must be avoided** in design. The remedy is either (i) to make the brass plate larger, (ii) to insulate the contact, or (iii) to attach a separate sacrificial zinc or aluminium anode.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**(a)** *Explain the concept of cathodic protection using the galvanic series. With a neat diagram, describe the sacrificial anode method and impressed current method. Give two examples of sacrificial anodes.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Cathodic Protection (CP):** A technique that converts the entire metallic structure to be protected into the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, forcing the anodic reaction (metal dissolution) to occur on a separate, expendable **sacrificial metal** or an **inert anode powered externally**.

The galvanic series helps in choosing a sacrificial anode — it must lie **below (more active than)** the metal to be protected.

**Sacrificial Anode Method (Galvanic CP):**

A metal that is **more active** (lower in the galvanic series) is electrically connected to the structure. The active metal corrodes preferentially, protecting the structure.

- **Examples of sacrificial anodes:** Zinc, magnesium, and aluminium alloys.
- **Typical applications:** Ship hulls (Zn blocks welded to steel hull), underground pipelines (Mg anodes in soil), offshore structures (Al anodes in seawater).

**Impressed Current Method:**

An **external DC power source** (rectifier) is used to drive current from an **inert anode** (e.g., graphite, Ti coated with mixed-metal oxide, scrap steel) through the electrolyte to the structure, which is the cathode. The inert anode does not dissolve.

- **Typical applications:** Long buried pipelines, large storage tank bottoms, reinforced concrete bridges.

---

**(b)** *Write notes on (i) passivity and its effect on the galvanic series, (ii) dezincification of brass.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Passivity and Its Effect on the Galvanic Series:**

Passivity is the phenomenon by which a metal forms a **thin, adherent, and protective oxide film** on its surface that drastically reduces its corrosion rate. Examples: Al → $Al_2O_3$, Cr → $Cr_2O_3$, Ti → $TiO_2$, stainless steel → mixed Cr-Fe oxide.

**Effect on the galvanic series:**
A passive metal behaves like a much more **noble** material than its standard reduction potential suggests. For example, **aluminium** has $E^\circ = -1.66$ V (highly active in the EMF series), yet in aerated water it sits **well above zinc** in the galvanic series because of its protective oxide film. Similarly, **stainless steel** moves to the **noble end** of the galvanic series when its passive film is intact, but drops dramatically to the active region if chloride ions break the film (pitting/crevice corrosion).

Thus passivity **overrides thermodynamic predictions** and is the principal reason the galvanic series differs from the EMF series.

**(ii) Dezincification of Brass:**

Brass is an alloy of **Cu (65–70%) and Zn (30–35%)**. In certain environments (especially **stagnant, acidic, or chloride-rich water**), the zinc component is **selectively leached out**, leaving a porous, mechanically weak, reddish **copper sponge** behind.

- This is a form of **selective leaching** (one of the eight forms of corrosion).
- Dezincification is most severe in **high-zinc brasses** (e.g., Muntz metal).
- **Prevention:** Use **inhibited brasses** (with 1% Sn — Admiralty brass) or **Arsenical brass** (with 0.02–0.06% As), or simply avoid brass in such environments.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing the EMF series instead of the Galvanic Series** — examiners specifically look for *alloy positions* and *seawater context*. Mentioning "1 M standard solution" loses marks.
> 2. **Confusing anode and cathode** — remember: **active metal = anode = corrodes; noble metal = cathode = protected**. Writing the reverse is an instant 1–2 mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring the area ratio** — in numerical problems, a *bare cell EMF* calculation alone (e.g., $\Delta E = 1.1$ V) is incomplete. Always discuss the *area ratio effect* and *current density*.
> 4. **Units mismatch** — report $i_{\text{corr}}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ or A/m² consistently; mixing units mid-solution is penalised.
> 5. **Skipping the practical implication** — every galvanic-corrosion question should end with a *design recommendation* (avoid coupling / insulate / use transition metal / sacrificial anode). Examiners reward the **engineering judgement** in the final line.
> 6. **Writing "Zn is below Mg in reactivity"** — this is for the EMF series. In the **galvanic series in seawater**, **Mg is at the bottom** (most active) and Zn is above it. Memorise the *practical* order.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Galvanic Series is an *empirical* ranking of metals and alloys by their **corrosion behaviour in a real environment** (seawater, 3% NaCl, 25 °C).
- **Key difference from EMF series:** Empirical ≠ Thermodynamic; Alloys ≠ Pure metals; Real env ≠ 1 M.
- **Top of series (noble):** Graphite, Pt, Au, 18-8 SS (passive), Ti (passive) — these act as **cathodes**.
- **Bottom of series (active):** Steel, Al, Zn, Mg — these act as **anodes** and corrode.
- **Driving force:** $\Delta E = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$; larger value = more severe corrosion.
- **Area ratio effect:** Small anode : Large cathode = **most dangerous** configuration.
- **Passivity:** Forms a protective oxide film; moves Al, Ti, stainless steel *noble* in the galvanic series.
- **Sacrificial anodes:** Chosen from the **active end** — Zn (seawater), Mg (soil), Al (offshore).
- **Three design remedies:** Avoid the couple, insert a transition metal, or attach a sacrificial anode.
- **Faraday's law:** $w = (I \cdot t \cdot M)/(n \cdot F)$; $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹.
- **Design rule:** Maintain $A_{\text{anode}} \geq A_{\text{cathode}}$ in any coupled system.
- **Dezincification:** Selective loss of Zn from brass; prevented by inhibited brasses (Sn or As addition).
- **Threshold for caution:** Potential separation **> 0.25 V** in the galvanic series signals a *high-risk* couple.
- **Revision mantra:** *"Active corrodes, noble survives — closer in series, longer they live."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Control Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion Control Methods** are the systematic engineering and chemical strategies employed to **prevent, retard, or eliminate the electrochemical degradation of metals** when exposed to aggressive environments. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / Outcome-Based Education framework)**, corrosion control is classified into **four principal domains**:
>
> 1. **Material Selection & Design Modifications** (alloying, structural considerations)
> 2. **Surface Protective Coatings** (metallic, organic, inorganic)
> 3. **Environmental Modification** (corrosion inhibitors, dehumidification)
> 4. **Electrochemical Protection** (cathodic protection, anodic protection)

Corrosion, fundamentally, is a **spontaneous electrochemical process** governed by the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G < 0$). The corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ and corrosion current $I_{corr}$ are derived from the **mixed potential theory** (Evans diagram) — the intersection of the anodic metal dissolution curve and the cathodic reduction curve (typically $\text{O}_2$ reduction or $\text{H}^+$ reduction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The university expects students to differentiate between *temporary* and *permanent* protection methods, justify the choice of a method based on the **Pourbaix diagram (E-pH diagram)**, and solve numerical problems on **sacrificial anode life** and **impressed current requirements**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your body fighting a disease. You have multiple lines of defense:
- **Avoid the pathogen** (don't go near sick people) → equivalent to **Material Selection** (using stainless steel instead of iron)
- **Wear a mask** → equivalent to **Protective Coatings** (paint, galvanizing)
- **Take a vaccine** (boost immunity) → equivalent to **Inhibitors** (chemicals that slow the reaction)
- **Get a pacemaker** (actively correct the heart) → equivalent to **Cathodic Protection** (external intervention)

In corrosion, we choose **one or more of these defense strategies** based on cost, environment (marine, industrial, soil), and the metal being protected. The goal is always the same: **minimize the rate at which metal atoms lose electrons** (oxidation) and become ions.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Corrosion Science

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and units are **frequently tested in KTU End Semester Examinations**:
>
> - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485\ \text{C mol}^{-1} \approx 96500\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$
> - **Standard corrosion rate unit:** **mpy (mils per year)** where $1\ \text{mil} = 0.001\ \text{inch} = 25.4\ \mu\text{m}$
> - **Equivalent weight (E):** $E = \dfrac{\text{Atomic weight}}{\text{Change in oxidation number (valency factor)}}$
> - **Standard electrode potentials (V vs SHE):** $E^\circ_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76\ \text{V}$; $E^\circ_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.44\ \text{V}$; $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34\ \text{V}$; $E^\circ_{\text{Mg}^{2+}/\text{Mg}} = -2.37\ \text{V}$
> - **Acceptable corrosion rate (industrial standard):** $< 1\ \text{mpy}$ (good); $1\text{–}5\ \text{mpy}$ (fair); $> 20\ \text{mpy}$ (unsatisfactory)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corrosion Control Methods

The complete taxonomy of corrosion control, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus, is presented below in logical engineering order.

### 2.1.1 Material Selection & Design

- **Principle:** Choose a metal/alloy whose thermodynamic nobility (high $E^\circ$) and kinetic passivity place it outside the corrosion zone of the **Pourbaix diagram**.
- **Examples:** Using **stainless steel (Fe-Cr-Ni)** in chemical plants; **titanium** in marine heat exchangers; **monel alloy (Ni-Cu)** for HF service.
- **Design rules:**
  - Avoid **galvanic couples** (dissimilar metals in electrical contact).
  - Avoid **crevices** (use welded joints over rivets).
  - Provide **drainage holes** to prevent liquid stagnation.
  - Maintain **uniform wall thickness** to prevent stress concentration.

### 2.1.2 Protective Coatings (Surface Modification)

Coatings are physical barriers that **isolate the metal from the electrolyte**, reducing the cathodic area exposed.

| Coating Type | Sub-Category | Examples | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Metallic (Anodic)** | Hot-dip galvanizing | Zn on Fe | Sacrificial protection (Zn corrodes preferentially) |
| **Metallic (Cathodic)** | Tinning | Sn on Fe | Barrier only (Sn is noble; if scratched, Fe corrodes rapidly) |
| **Metallic (Electrodeposited)** | Electroplating | Cr, Ni, Cu, Ag | Cathodic barrier coating |
| **Metallic (Electroless)** | Electroless plating | Ni-P on steel | Autocatalytic chemical reduction |
| **Organic** | Paints, varnishes | Epoxy, polyurethane, alkyd | Barrier + inhibitor pigments |
| **Inorganic** | Anodizing | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al | Thick oxide barrier layer |
| **Inorganic** | Chromate conversion | $\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ on Al, Zn | Self-healing passive film |

### 2.1.3 Corrosion Inhibitors (Environmental Modification)

Inhibitors are chemical substances added in small concentrations to the corrosive environment.

- **Anodic inhibitors (passivators):** Chromates ($\text{CrO}_4^{2-}$), nitrites ($\text{NO}_2^-$), phosphates, molybdates. They form a **passive oxide film** on anodic sites. *Risk: If concentration is insufficient, they cause pitting.*
- **Cathodic inhibitors:** Polyphosphates, zinc salts, calcium salts. They precipitate at cathodic sites, blocking $\text{O}_2$ reduction.
- **Mixed (adsorption) inhibitors:** Amines, imidazolines, thiourea derivatives. They adsorb on the metal surface, blocking both anodic and cathodic reactions.
- **Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPI):** Dicyclohexylammonium nitrite (DICHAN) — used for shipping protection.

### 2.1.4 Electrochemical Protection

This is the **most heavily tested topic in KTU exams**. It converts the protected structure into a cathode (or controlled anode) of an electrochemical cell.

#### A. Cathodic Protection (CP) — Most Widely Used

The metal to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. The two implementations are:

**1. Sacrificial Anode Method (Galvanic Cathodic Protection)**
- A more electrochemically **active metal** (Zn, Mg, or Al alloy) is electrically connected to the structure.
- The active metal becomes the anode and **corrodes preferentially** (is "sacrificed").
- No external power source required.
- Used for: ship hulls, buried pipelines, offshore platforms.
- Common anodes: **Zn (E° = -0.76 V)**, **Mg (E° = -2.37 V)**, **Al-Zn-In alloy**.

**2. Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**
- An external **DC power source** drives current through the electrolyte onto the protected structure.
- Structure is connected to the **negative terminal** → becomes cathode.
- An **inert anode** (graphite, platinized Ti, $\text{PbO}_2$, scrap Fe) is connected to the **positive terminal**.
- Used for: long pipelines, large storage tanks, reinforced concrete bridges.

#### B. Anodic Protection (AP)

- **Inverse of CP**: The metal is made the **anode** and a constant potential is applied to maintain it in the **passive region** of the Pourbaix diagram.
- **Applicable ONLY to metals that show passivation** in the medium: Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, stainless steel.
- **NOT applicable** to metals like Zn, Mg, Cu (which do not passivate).
- Used for: sulfuric acid storage tanks ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ at 96–100%), ammonium nitrate storage.

## 2.2 The Pourbaix Diagram (E-pH Diagram) — Conceptual Foundation

The Pourbaix diagram maps the **thermodynamically stable phase** of a metal as a function of electrode potential ($E$) and pH.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The Pourbaix diagram has three regions:
> - **Corrosion region:** Metal ions are stable → corrosion occurs.
> - **Immunity region:** Metal itself is stable → no corrosion.
> - **Passivation region:** Stable oxide/hydroxide film → corrosion rate is very low.
>
> **Cathodic protection** shifts the metal from the corrosion region into the **immunity region** (by lowering $E$).
> **Anodic protection** shifts the metal from the corrosion region into the **passivation region** (by raising $E$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all formulas below, the vertical bar `|` is replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Nernst equation (electrode potential) | V |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Nernst equation (at 25 °C) | V |
| 3 | $\Delta E = E_c - E_a$ | Cell potential / driving EMF for corrosion | V |
| 4 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate from weight loss (mpy formula) | mpy |
| 5 | $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion rate (SI) | mm/yr |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{\text{Atomic weight}}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | g/equiv |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot E}{F}$ | Mass of metal consumed (Faraday's 1st law) | g |
| 8 | $I = i_c \cdot A$ | Total current from current density | A |
| 9 | $t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{m \cdot F}{I \cdot E}$ | Lifetime of sacrificial anode | s |
| 10 | $\eta = E_{\text{applied}} - E_{\text{eq}}$ | Overpotential | V |
| 11 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy of corrosion | J/mol |
| 12 | $i_{\text{corr}} = i_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$ | Butler-Volmer equation | A/m² |

> **Symbol Key:**
> $W$ = weight loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = exposed area (in²), $T$ = exposure time (hr), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $m$ = mass of anode (g), $E$ = equivalent weight, $i_c$ = current density (A/m²), $\eta$ = overpotential, $n$ = number of electrons transferred.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Method Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Marine** | Ship hulls, offshore platforms | Sacrificial Zn/Mg anodes + epoxy coating |
| **Oil & Gas** | Cross-country buried pipelines | ICCP with graphite anodes |
| **Chemical Plants** | $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ storage tanks | Anodic protection with potentiostat |
| **Civil Engineering** | Reinforced concrete (rebar) | Cathodic protection using Ti mesh anode |
| **Automotive** | Car bodies, chassis | Galvanneal coating (Zn-Fe alloy) + cathodic ED-paint |
| **Aerospace** | Aluminum aircraft skins | Anodizing (Type II chromic acid) |
| **Boilers** | Steam-generating systems | Phosphate + hydrazine mixed inhibitor |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive derivations and numerical frameworks** that KTU examiners expect. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Weight Loss (mpy Formula)

### Starting Point — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

> **Faraday's First Law:** The mass $W$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ passed through the electrolyte.

$$
W \propto Q
$$

Adding the proportionality constant:

$$
W = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t
$$

where $Z = \dfrac{E}{F}$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** and $E$ is the equivalent weight.

Substituting $Z$:

$$
W = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert charge to corrosion current and time:**

For a corrosion experiment of duration $T$ hours, the total charge is $Q = I \cdot T$ (with $I$ in A and $T$ in hours). Express weight in mg by multiplying by $10^3$:

$$
W_{\text{mg}} = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot T \cdot 10^3}{96500} = \frac{E \cdot I \cdot T}{96.5}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Second Law (current → volume dissolved):**

For a metal of density $D$ and exposed area $A$ corroding uniformly, the thickness loss $\Delta x$ over time $T$ is:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}
$$

Converting $W$ from mg to g (divide by $10^3$), and converting the final thickness to **mils** (1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.0254 mm):

$$
\text{CR (mils/yr)} = \frac{\Delta x \cdot 1000}{25.4} \times \frac{8760\ \text{hr/yr}}{T\ \text{(hr)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and simplify:**

The KTU board uses the **standard empirical form**:

$$
\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}
$$

where $W$ is in **mg**, $D$ in **g/cm³**, $A$ in **in²**, and $T$ in **hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation of the constant 534:**
> 1 mpy = 1 mil/yr = $\dfrac{0.001 \times 2.54\ \text{cm}}{8760\ \text{hr}} = 2.899 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm/hr}$.
> For Fe: $E = 28$ g/equiv, $D = 7.87$ g/cm³, $n = 2$.
> Corrosion current equivalent per cm² per mpy: $I = \dfrac{2.899 \times 10^{-7} \times 7.87 \times 2 \times 96500}{28 \times 10^3} \approx 1.57 \times 10^{-6}$ A/cm².
> The 534 factor arises from converting all units and using $F = 96{,}500$ C with 8760 hours/year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lifetime of a Sacrificial Anode

A sacrificial anode of mass $m$ (grams) and equivalent weight $E$ is connected to a structure. The protective current is $I$ amperes.

**Step 1 — Total charge the anode can deliver (Faraday's law, solved for $Q$):**

$$
Q = \frac{m \cdot F}{E}
$$

**Step 2 — Time for full consumption at constant current $I$:**

$$
t = \frac{Q}{I} = \frac{m \cdot F}{I \cdot E}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to years:**

$$
t_{\text{years}} = \frac{m \cdot F}{I \cdot E \times 3600 \times 24 \times 365.25}
$$

> **Example Substitution (Mg anode, common KTU problem):**
> Mg equivalent weight $E = \dfrac{24.3}{2} = 12.15$ g/equiv.
> $F = 96{,}500$ C/equiv.
> For $m = 5$ kg $= 5000$ g, $I = 0.5$ A:

$$
t = \frac{5000 \times 96500}{0.5 \times 12.15} = \frac{4.825 \times 10^8}{6.075} = 7.94 \times 10^7\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
t = \frac{7.94 \times 10^7}{3.156 \times 10^7} \approx 2.52\ \text{years}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

The following decision matrix is the **standard KTU 14-mark question framework**.

| Environment | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Marine (submerged)** | Sacrificial anode (Zn or Al) | No external power; simple |
| **Buried pipeline (long)** | ICCP | Long distance, high current demand |
| **Acidic tank (H₂SO₄)** | Anodic protection | Steel passivates in conc. H₂SO₄ |
| **Atmospheric (urban)** | Paint + inhibitor | Cost-effective, simple |
| **Boiler water** | Phosphate + hydrazine | Prevents both scale and O₂ corrosion |
| **Reinforced concrete** | Cathodic protection (Ti anode) | Stops rebar rusting |
| **Heat exchanger (seawater)** | Cathodic protection + Cu-Ni alloy | Dual strategy |
| **Aluminium aircraft** | Anodizing + chromate primer | Lightweight, durable |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Standard

> **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style):**
> A steel pipe (area = 200 in², density = 7.86 g/cm³) is cathodically protected by a magnesium sacrificial anode. After 30 days, the weight loss of the anode is 450 g. Calculate the corrosion current and the protective current efficiency if the theoretical current (from Faraday's law) was 1.4 A.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate the actual average current from weight loss:
Faraday's law: $W = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$, so $I = \dfrac{W \cdot F}{E \cdot t}$.
For Mg: $E = 12.15$ g/equiv. Time $t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^6$ s.

$$
I_{\text{actual}} = \frac{450 \times 96500}{12.15 \times 2.592 \times 10^6} = \frac{4.3425 \times 10^7}{3.150 \times 10^7} \approx 1.379\ \text{A}
$$

Step 2 — Current efficiency:

$$
\eta_{\text{eff}} = \frac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.379}{1.4} \times 100 \approx 98.5\ \%
$$

Step 3 — Corrosion rate of the protected pipe (to verify protection):
Assuming no weight loss on the pipe (cathode), CR = 0 mpy. If partial, use mpy formula.

---

## 3.5 Conceptually Critical: Why Cathodic Protection Works — Thermodynamic Proof

For iron in aerated neutral water (pH = 7):
- Anodic: $\text{Fe} \to \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$, $E^\circ = -0.44$ V
- Cathodic: $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \to 4\text{OH}^-$, $E^\circ = +0.40$ V

For corrosion to be spontaneous: $E_{\text{cell}} = E_c - E_a > 0$, which it is (0.84 V).

By applying an external cathodic current, the **mixed potential** of the iron structure is shifted **downward** (more negative). The iron is no longer the site of oxidation — the oxidation now occurs at the sacrificial anode. The structure enters the **immunity region** of the Pourbaix diagram.

$$
E_{\text{Fe, protected}} < E_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} \Rightarrow \text{no Fe}^{2+}\text{ formed}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between sacrificial anode cathodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection. Give one example of an anode used in each."**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Sacrificial Anode (SACP) | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Power source** | None — uses galvanic potential difference | External DC rectifier required |
> | **Anode material** | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al-alloy) | Inert (graphite, platinized Ti, scrap Fe) |
> | **Driving force** | Difference in $E^\circ$ of anode and cathode | Impressed DC voltage |
> | **Application** | Short structures, ship hulls, well casings | Long pipelines, large storage tanks |
> | **Cost** | Low installation, high anode replacement | High installation, low maintenance |
>
> **Example:** For SACP → **Zinc** anode (used on ship hulls); for ICCP → **Graphite** anode (used in soil pipelines).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What are passivating inhibitors? Why must they be used in sufficient concentration?"**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Passivating inhibitors (e.g., **chromates, nitrites, phosphates, molybdates**) are **anodic inhibitors** that promote the formation of a thin, adherent, protective oxide film on the metal surface, thereby shifting the metal's potential into the **passive region** of the Pourbaix diagram.
>
> **Critical Caveat:** If the concentration is **insufficient** (below a critical threshold), they cause **localized passivation** at anodic sites, leading to **pitting corrosion**. This is because the inhibitor fails to form a complete passive film, and the small unprotected anodic area carries the entire corrosion current at high local current density. Hence, KTU examiners specifically ask: *“Always use a concentration above the critical value.”*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the mechanism of **cathodic protection by sacrificial anode method** with a neat diagram. Discuss the criteria for the selection of sacrificial anode material.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A steel structure of **10 m²** surface area is to be protected from corrosion in seawater. The required **cathodic current density is 200 mA/m²**. The sacrificial anode is **zinc** (E = 32.7 g/equiv). Calculate:
>   1. The total protective current required.
>   2. The mass of zinc anode consumed **per year**.
>   3. The number of **5-kg zinc anodes** required for **5 years** of protection.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
In sacrificial anode cathodic protection (SACP), a metal **more electrochemically active** than the structure to be protected is electrically connected to it via a wire and immersed in the same electrolyte (seawater or soil). The more active metal (anode) preferentially loses electrons and dissolves, while the protected structure receives these electrons and becomes a **cathode**. Since cathodic reactions on iron (O₂ reduction or H⁺ reduction) do not produce Fe²⁺ ions, **iron does not corrode**.

**Reactions:**

- Anode (Zn): $\text{Zn} \to \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (sacrificial dissolution)
- Cathode (Fe): $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \to 4\text{OH}^-$ (oxygen reduction — non-destructive)

**Criteria for anode selection (3 marks):**
1. The anode must have a **more negative electrode potential** than the metal to be protected (e.g., Zn, Mg, Al for protecting Fe).
2. The anode metal must have a **high electrochemical equivalent** (more coulombs per gram).
3. The anode must be **cheap, readily available, and easily fabricated**.
4. The corrosion products of the anode should be **non-toxic and adherent** (e.g., Zn(OH)₂ is harmless).
5. The anode should have a **uniform dissolution rate** (no pitting on anode itself).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Total protective current (1 mark):**

$$
I = i_c \times A = 0.200\ \text{A/m}^2 \times 10\ \text{m}^2 = 2.0\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Charge consumed per year (1 mark):**

$$
Q_{\text{year}} = I \times t = 2.0\ \text{A} \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600\ \text{s} = 6.31 \times 10^7\ \text{C/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of Zn consumed per year (2 marks):**

Using Faraday's first law: $W = \dfrac{Q \cdot E}{F}$, where $E_{\text{Zn}} = \dfrac{65.4}{2} = 32.7$ g/equiv.

$$
W_{\text{Zn/yr}} = \frac{6.31 \times 10^7 \times 32.7}{96500} = \frac{2.064 \times 10^9}{96500} = 21{,}390\ \text{g/yr} \approx 21.4\ \text{kg/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass required for 5 years (1 mark):**

$$
m_{5\text{yr}} = 21.4 \times 5 = 107\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of 5-kg anodes (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{107}{5} = 21.4 \approx 22\ \text{anodes}
$$

> **Conclusion:** **22 anodes** of 5 kg each are required for 5 years of continuous protection. (Practically, design with 10–15% extra → 24 anodes.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 style]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a Pourbaix diagram for iron, explain the concepts of **immunity, corrosion, and passivation**. How does **cathodic protection** utilize the immunity region?
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A magnesium sacrificial anode weighing **3.2 kg** is used to protect an underground pipeline. The protective current measured is **0.25 A**. Calculate:
>   1. The **total charge** delivered by the anode.
>   2. The **lifetime of the anode in years** (E for Mg = 12.15 g/equiv).
>   3. Justify whether **Zn or Mg** is a better sacrificial anode for a steel pipeline in **soil of resistivity 5000 Ω·cm**.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Pourbaix Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The Pourbaix (E-pH) diagram for iron in water is a plot of electrode potential ($E$, V on Y-axis) versus pH (X-axis). It identifies three regions:

- **Corrosion region:** Where soluble ionic species like $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, $\text{Fe}^{3+}$, $\text{HFeO}_2^-$ are thermodynamically stable. The metal dissolves.
- **Immunity region:** Where the **metallic iron (Fe)** is thermodynamically stable. No corrosion occurs because the metal cannot ionize.
- **Passivation region:** Where stable solid oxides/hydroxides ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$, $\text{FeOOH}$) form a protective film. The metal is covered by an inert barrier.

**Cathodic Protection Link (4 marks):**
Cathodic protection works by applying an external current or using a sacrificial anode to **drive the electrochemical potential of iron downward** (toward more negative values). When the potential is shifted below the **Fe / Fe²⁺ equilibrium line**, the metal enters the **immunity region** of the Pourbaix diagram, where the **thermodynamically stable phase is metallic iron**. Hence, even if oxygen is present, no iron ions are produced → corrosion is **thermodynamically impossible**. The two methods (SACP and ICCP) are simply different engineering means to achieve this potential shift.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Total charge (1 mark):**

First, find the theoretical charge the anode can deliver:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{m \cdot F}{E} = \frac{3200 \times 96500}{12.15} = 2.541 \times 10^7\ \text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Lifetime (3 marks):**

$$
t = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{I} = \frac{2.541 \times 10^7}{0.25} = 1.0165 \times 10^8\ \text{s}
$$

Converting to years:

$$
t = \frac{1.0165 \times 10^8}{3.156 \times 10^7} \approx 3.22\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 3 — Justification: Mg vs Zn in soil (3 marks):**

- **Mg** has $E^\circ = -2.37$ V → driving potential of **1.93 V** vs Fe (very high).
- **Zn** has $E^\circ = -0.76$ V → driving potential of **0.32 V** vs Fe (low).
- For **high-resistivity soil** (5000 Ω·cm), the **IR drop** is large. Only Mg (with its much higher driving voltage) can push the necessary protective current through the resistive electrolyte. Zn would be ineffective in such soil.
- Hence, **Mg is the better sacrificial anode for high-resistivity soils**; Zn is preferred in low-resistivity environments (seawater, moist clay).
- *Practical note:* Despite this, Mg is rarely used in seawater because it corrodes **too rapidly** and produces flammable H₂ gas — Zn is preferred there.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in numerical problems** — Forgetting to convert **hours → seconds** (Faraday's law uses seconds), or **in² → m²** in area conversions. Always write the units explicitly.
> 2. **Valency factor error** — Zn is divalent (n=2, E=32.7), but **Al is trivalent (n=3, E=9)**. Many students wrongly use atomic weight directly.
> 3. **Confusing anodic and cathodic protection** — Remember: **CP is for ANY metal; AP is ONLY for passivating metals** (Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti, stainless steel). Writing "AP for Cu" is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Galvanizing vs Tinning** — In galvanizing (Zn, anodic coating), if scratched, **Zn corrodes first** protecting Fe. In tinning (Sn, cathodic coating), if scratched, **Fe corrodes faster** because Sn is noble. Many students mix these up.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram in 7-mark questions** — A "neat labelled diagram" is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always include one.
> 6. **Writing "corrosion inhibitor" without specifying anodic/cathodic/mixed** — Generic answers get only 1 of 3 marks in Part A. Be specific.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently state:**

- [x] Corrosion control methods are classified into **4 main categories**: Material/Design, Coatings, Inhibitors, Electrochemical Protection.
- [x] **Sacrificial anode CP** uses **Zn, Mg, Al** — requires no external power; suitable for short structures in low-resistivity electrolytes.
- [x] **Impressed current CP (ICCP)** uses an external DC source with an **inert anode** (graphite, platinized Ti); suitable for long pipelines.
- [x] **Anodic Protection** is **only** for **passivating metals** (Fe, Cr, Ni, Ti) — uses a **potentiostat** to hold potential in the passive region.
- [x] **Galvanizing (Zn)** is an **anodic coating** — sacrificial. **Tinning (Sn)** is a **cathodic coating** — only barrier.
- [x] **Anodic inhibitors** = chromates, nitrites, molybdates. **Cathodic inhibitors** = polyphosphates, Zn salts. **Mixed inhibitors** = amines, imidazolines.
- [x] **Pourbaix Diagram** regions: **Immunity** (low E) → CP shifts here. **Passivation** (mid E, neutral pH) → AP shifts here. **Corrosion** (high E or extreme pH) → where we want to AVOID.
- [x] **Faraday's Law for corrosion rate:** $W = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$; **mpy formula:** $\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$.
- [x] **Equivalent weight** $E = \dfrac{\text{Atomic weight}}{n}$ (n = valency factor / change in oxidation state).
- [x] **Acceptable corrosion rate** in industry: **< 1 mpy** = good; **> 20 mpy** = unsatisfactory.
- [x] Always include a **neat labelled diagram** in any 7-mark question (cathodic protection, galvanic cell, or Pourbaix sketch).
- [x] **Nernst equation** (at 25 °C): $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log\dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$.
- [x] **Selection rule for sacrificial anode:** The anode must have **E° more negative** than the protected metal **AND** high electrochemical equivalent.
- [x] **Inhibitor pitfall:** Insufficient concentration of **anodic inhibitors** causes **pitting corrosion**.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cathodic Protection — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical technique employed to mitigate the corrosion of a metal surface by converting it into the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell. This is achieved either by coupling it with a more electrochemically active (more anodic) metal, or by supplying an external direct current (DC) from an independent source, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation/dissolution) reaction responsible for metallic deterioration.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cathodic protection is classified as a **"corrosion-control by electrochemical intervention"** method. It is one of the most widely deployed, economically viable, and reliable anti-corrosion strategies in the modern engineering industry — particularly for buried, submerged, and structurally embedded metallic assets.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bodyguard Effect"

Imagine a **VIP celebrity (the metal structure — say, an underground oil pipeline)** who is being attacked by a group of "rust bullies" (corrosive ions like Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, dissolved O₂). Now, the security team has two options:

- **Option A — The Bodyguard Sacrifices Himself:** A loyal, slightly weaker bodyguard (a more reactive metal like **Zn, Mg, or Al**) steps in front of the VIP. Every time the rust bullies strike, the bodyguard takes the hit and corrodes, leaving the VIP completely unharmed. Once the bodyguard is destroyed, a new one replaces him. This is the **Sacrificial Anode Method**.

- **Option B — A Bouncer Powered by the Grid:** An electric bouncer (a rectifier powered by AC mains or solar panels) is positioned nearby. He continuously pushes the rust bullies away using an electric force field, keeping the VIP safe indefinitely — as long as the power supply lasts. This is the **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Underlying Logic:** In both cases, the protected structure is forced to act as a **cathode**, meaning the oxidation (anodic, metal-loss) reaction is forced to occur *elsewhere* — either on the expendable sacrificial anode or on the inert impressed-current anode.

## 1.3 The Electrochemical Premise — Why Does Corrosion Happen?

Corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical process**. On a metal surface exposed to an electrolyte (moisture, soil, seawater), countless microscopic **anodic and cathodic sites** form. The local galvanic cells drive the following half-reactions:

**At the Anode (Metal Dissolution — The Destructive Reaction):**
$$\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^{-}$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction Reactions — The Innocent Reaction):**
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \quad \text{(in neutral/alkaline media)}$$
$$2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(in acidic media)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Goal of Cathodic Protection:** Eliminate the anodic reaction at the protected metal by either (a) shifting the corrosion current entirely onto a sacrificial partner, or (b) overwhelming the anodic dissolution with an external current that polarizes the metal cathodically until its anodic current density approaches **zero**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Protection potential for steel (vs Cu/CuSO₄) | **−850 mV** | Industry standard (NACE SP0169) |
| Protection potential for steel (vs Ag/AgCl) | **−780 mV** | Marine/seawater applications |
| Protection potential for steel (vs SCE) | **−770 mV** | Saturated Calomel reference |
| Minimum protective current density (steel in soil) | **10–20 mA/m²** | Resistivity dependent |
| Minimum protective current density (steel in seawater) | **80–150 mA/m²** | Higher due to Cl⁻ activity |
| Minimum protective current density (steel in concrete) | **0.2–2 mA/m²** | Passivated environment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Requirements for Cathodic Protection

For any CP system to function correctly, **three mandatory conditions** must be satisfied:

1. **Electrical Continuity:** The protected metal structure must be in **metallic (electrical) continuity** so that it behaves as a single equipotential cathode.
2. **Electrolyte Path:** There must be a continuous **ionic conduction path** (soil, water, concrete pore solution) between the anode and the protected cathode.
3. **DC Circuit Completion:** A complete **direct-current electrical circuit** must exist — electrons flow from anode → through external wire → to the protected cathode → through electrolyte → back to the anode.

## 2.2 The Two Industrial Methods of Cathodic Protection

### 2.2.1 Method I — Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

Also known as the **Galvanic Anode Method**.

- The protected metal is connected via a wire to a metal/alloy that is **more electrochemically active** (more negative reduction potential).
- This active metal acts as the anode and **sacrifices itself** by dissolving preferentially.
- **No external power source is required** — the system is self-driven by the natural galvanic potential difference.

**Common Sacrificial Anode Materials:**

| Anode | Electrode Potential (V vs SHE) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | −2.37 V | Buried pipelines, low-resistivity soils |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | −0.76 V | Marine structures, ship hulls |
| **Aluminium alloys (Al-Zn-In)** | −1.10 V | Offshore platforms, seawater |
| **Manganese alloys** | −1.50 V | Specialized soil conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** The anode must be located **closer to the structure in the electrolyte path** so that current preferentially flows into the structure rather than dissipating into the bulk electrolyte.

### 2.2.2 Method II — Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

- An **external DC power source** (rectifier, solar panel, thermoelectric generator, or wind turbine) drives current from an **inert (impressed current) anode** to the protected structure.
- The inert anodes are made of non-consumable materials: **graphite, high-silicon cast iron, mixed-metal oxide (MMO), platinized titanium, or scrap steel**.
- This method is suitable for **large structures** and **high-resistivity electrolytes**.

**Block Schematic of an ICCP System:**

$$\text{AC Mains} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier (AC to DC)} \rightarrow (+) \text{Inert Anode} \rightarrow \text{Electrolyte} \rightarrow (-) \text{Protected Cathode} \rightarrow \text{Back to Rectifier}$$

## 2.3 Critical Cathodic Protection Criteria (Industry Standards)

For effective protection, **any one** of the following empirical criteria must be satisfied (NACE/AMPP standards):

1. **Potential Criterion:** A structure-to-electrolyte potential of at least **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** (with IR drop considered).
2. **Polarization Criterion:** A minimum of **100 mV of cathodic polarization** between the structure and a stable reference electrode.
3. **Current Density Criterion:** An impressed current density sufficient to suppress anodic reaction.
4. **Tafel Slope Criterion:** The structure is polarized to the **most negative (active) potential** in the anodic polarization curve.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Driving potential of the galvanic cell | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{E_{cell}}{R_{total}}$ | Ohm's law applied to CP circuit (current output) | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of anode consumed (Faraday's law) | kg (W = mass consumed) |
| 4 | $i_{prot} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{A_{protected}}$ | Required protective current density | A/m² |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi R_L} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{d}{r}\right)$ | Dwight's equation — earth resistance of a vertical anode | Ohms (Ω) |
| 6 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Simplified Dwight equation for anode-to-earth resistance | Ohms (Ω) |
| 7 | $I_{req} = i_{prot} \cdot A_{surface}$ | Total current required for protection | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{anode}}$ | Number of anodes required | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \cdot u \cdot 8760}{I_{anode}}$ | Anode design life equation | Years |
| 10 | $V_{total} = I \cdot (R_a + R_c + R_w)$ | Total voltage drop across CP circuit | Volts (V) |

**Where:**
- $\rho$ = soil/electrolyte resistivity (Ω·m)
- $L$ = anode length (m)
- $d$ = anode diameter (m)
- $r$ = distance to remote earth (m)
- $M$ = molar mass of anode (kg/mol)
- $n$ = valency of dissolving ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $u$ = utilization factor of anode (typically 0.85 for Mg, 0.50 for Zn)
- $R_c, R_w, R_a$ = cathode, wire, and anode resistances respectively

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Cathodic protection is mandated by international standards for **cross-country oil/gas pipelines, offshore drilling platforms, ship hulls, reinforced concrete bridges, water storage tanks, well casings, and underground cable sheaths**. The global CP market is valued in the multi-billion-dollar range, making it a critical topic for chemical, civil, and mechanical engineers.

## 2.5 Selection Criteria — SACP vs ICCP

| Feature | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| External power | Not required | Required (rectifier) |
| Anode consumption | Yes (sacrificial) | Minimal (inert) |
| Current output | Limited (low) | Adjustable, high |
| Soil resistivity | < 5000 Ω·cm preferred | Up to 50,000 Ω·cm |
| Installation cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Operating cost | Anode replacement | Electricity + maintenance |
| Best suited for | Small, localized structures | Large, complex networks |
| Typical structures | Ship hulls, small tanks | Long pipelines, offshore platforms |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Sacrificial Anode Consumed (Faraday's Law Applied to CP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A buried steel pipeline is protected by zinc sacrificial anodes. If the protective current required is 5.0 A and the system is designed to operate for 8 years, calculate the total mass of zinc consumed. (Atomic mass of Zn = 65.38 g/mol; n = 2; F = 96,485 C/mol).

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Electrochemical Reaction

At the zinc sacrificial anode, the oxidation half-reaction is:

$$\text{Zn}_{(s)} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

Therefore, for every mole of zinc dissolved, **2 moles of electrons** are released (n = 2).

### Step 2 — Express Total Charge (Coulombs) Transferred

The total charge $Q$ transferred over the operating time $t$ is given by:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Substituting** $I = 5.0\ \text{A}$ and $t = 8\ \text{years}$:

$$t = 8 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 252,288,000\ \text{seconds}$$

$$Q = 5.0 \times 252,288,000 = 1.2614 \times 10^9\ \text{C}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Moles of Electrons Transferred

Using Faraday's constant, the number of moles of electrons is:

$$n_{e^-} = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{1.2614 \times 10^9}{96,485} = 13,072.6\ \text{mol } e^{-}$$

### Step 4 — Relate Moles of Electrons to Moles of Zinc Dissolved

From the stoichiometry of the half-reaction: 2 mol e⁻ ↔ 1 mol Zn

$$n_{Zn} = \dfrac{n_{e^-}}{2} = \dfrac{13,072.6}{2} = 6,536.3\ \text{mol}$$

### Step 5 — Convert Moles of Zinc to Mass

$$W = n_{Zn} \times M_{Zn} = 6,536.3 \times 65.38 = 427,344\ \text{g} = 427.34\ \text{kg}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Utilization Factor

Real-world anodes are not 100% efficient. Assuming a utilization factor $u = 0.85$ for zinc (industry standard):

$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{theoretical}}{u} = \dfrac{427.34}{0.85} = 502.75\ \text{kg}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **502.75 kg of zinc** will be consumed over 8 years.

$$\boxed{W_{actual} \approx 502.75\ \text{kg Zn}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Sacrificial Anodes Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A 2.5 km long, 30 cm diameter buried steel pipeline in soil of resistivity 1500 Ω·cm requires a protective current density of 15 mA/m². If each zinc anode can deliver 250 mA, calculate the number of anodes required.

### Step 1 — Calculate the External Surface Area of the Pipeline

$$A_{surface} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 2,500 = 2,356.2\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Total Protective Current Required

$$I_{req} = i_{prot} \times A_{surface} = 0.015 \times 2,356.2 = 35.34\ \text{A} = 35,343\ \text{mA}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Number of Anodes

$$N = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{35,343}{250} = 141.4$$

### Step 4 — Round Up to the Next Whole Number

$$\boxed{N = 142\ \text{zinc anodes}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** In real installations, anodes are typically spaced uniformly along the pipeline at calculated intervals. The spacing here would be approximately $2,500 / 142 \approx 17.6$ metres between successive anodes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Anode-to-Earth Resistance (Dwight's Equation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A vertical magnesium anode of length 1.5 m and diameter 0.10 m is installed in soil of resistivity 2000 Ω·cm. Calculate the anode-to-earth resistance.

### Step 1 — Apply Dwight's Equation

The Dwight equation for a vertical rod anode is:

$$R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 2 — Convert Units to SI

$$\rho = 2000\ \Omega \cdot \text{cm} = 20\ \Omega \cdot \text{m}, \quad L = 1.5\ \text{m}, \quad d = 0.10\ \text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$R_a = \dfrac{20}{2\pi \times 1.5} \left[ \ln\left(\dfrac{8 \times 1.5}{0.10}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Inner Logarithm

$$\dfrac{8L}{d} = \dfrac{12.0}{0.10} = 120$$

$$\ln(120) = 4.7875$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Bracketed Term

$$4.7875 - 1 = 3.7875$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Final Result

$$R_a = \dfrac{20}{9.4248} \times 3.7875 = 2.1221 \times 3.7875 = 8.04\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_a \approx 8.04\ \Omega}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Design Life of a Sacrificial Anode System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Each magnesium anode weighs 8.0 kg, delivers 0.5 A, and has a utilization factor of 0.50. Calculate the design life in years.

### Step 1 — Apply the Anode Life Equation

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{m_{anode} \times u \times 8760}{I_{anode}}$$

**Where:** 8760 = hours per year, $u$ = utilization factor, $I_{anode}$ in amperes.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 0.50 \times 8760}{0.5} = \dfrac{35,040}{0.5} = 70,080\ \text{hours}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Hours to Years

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{70,080}{8760} = 8.0\ \text{years}$$

$$\boxed{T_{life} = 8.0\ \text{years}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SACP vs ICCP Performance Parameters

| Parameter | SACP (Zinc) | SACP (Magnesium) | ICCP (MMO Anode) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Driving voltage | 0.2 – 0.3 V | 0.6 – 0.7 V | 6 – 50 V (variable) |
| Current per anode | 5 – 250 mA | 5 – 100 mA | Up to 100 A |
| Anode life | 5 – 15 years | 5 – 20 years | 20 – 50 years |
| Soil resistivity limit | < 1500 Ω·cm | < 5000 Ω·cm | < 50,000 Ω·cm |
| Cost per ampere-year | High | Moderate | Low |
| Maintenance | Periodic replacement | Periodic replacement | Annual inspection only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
