# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define cathodic protection. Mention the two methods employed for it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Cathodic protection is an electrochemical technique used to control the corrosion of a metal surface by making it the cathode of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution. (2 marks)

The two methods employed are:
1. **Sacrificial Anode Method (SACP)** — where a more active metal like Zn, Mg, or Al is galvanically coupled to the protected structure. (0.5 marks)
2. **Impressed Current Method (ICCP)** — where an external DC power source drives current from an inert anode to the protected structure. (0.5 marks)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between sacrificial anode and impressed current cathodic protection methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| External power source | Not required (1) | Required (1) |
| Anode | Consumable (Zn, Mg) | Inert (MMO, graphite) |
| Driving force | Natural galvanic potential | External DC rectifier |
| Suitable for | Small structures, low resistivity (1) | Large structures, any resistivity (1) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the principle and working of **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** with a neat schematic diagram. Discuss the materials used for impressed current anodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A buried steel pipeline of length 3.0 km and diameter 40 cm is to be protected using magnesium sacrificial anodes. The protective current density required is 20 mA/m² and each Mg anode can deliver 300 mA. The anode has a mass of 7.5 kg, utilization factor of 0.5, and operates at a current of 0.3 A. Calculate:
1. The number of anodes required.
2. The design life of each anode in years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle of ICCP (3 marks):**
In ICCP, an **external DC power source** (rectifier) supplies current between an **inert anode** (placed in the electrolyte) and the **protected structure** (made the cathode). The rectifier forces electrons onto the structure, shifting its electrochemical potential to a more negative value until all anodic dissolution reactions are thermodynamically and kinetically suppressed.

**Schematic Description (2 marks):**

$$\text{AC Mains} \rightarrow \text{Transformer} \rightarrow \text{Rectifier} \rightarrow (+) \text{Anode} \rightarrow \text{Electrolyte} \rightarrow (-) \text{Structure} \rightarrow \text{Back to Rectifier}$$

**Anode Materials (2 marks):**
- **High-silicon cast iron** — durable, used in soil
- **Graphite** — economical, used in fresh water
- **Mixed-Metal Oxide (MMO)** — long life, used in all environments
- **Platinized titanium** — premium, marine applications
- **Scrap steel** — used in low-resistivity soils

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate the external surface area of the pipeline (1 mark):**
$$A = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.40 \times 3{,}000 = 3{,}769.9\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total protective current required (1 mark):**
$$I_{req} = i_{prot} \times A = 0.020 \times 3{,}769.9 = 75.40\ \text{A} = 75{,}398\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the number of anodes (2 marks):**
$$N = \dfrac{I_{req}}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{75{,}398}{300} = 251.3$$

Rounded up:
$$\boxed{N = 252\ \text{magnesium anodes}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the design life of each anode (3 marks):**

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{m \cdot u \cdot 8760}{I_{anode}} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 0.5 \times 8760}{0.3}$$

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{32{,}850}{0.3} = 109{,}500\ \text{hours}$$

$$T_{life} = \dfrac{109{,}500}{8760} = 12.5\ \text{years}$$

$$\boxed{T_{life} = 12.5\ \text{years}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** What are the **requirements** for effective cathodic protection? Explain the **sacrificial anode method** in detail with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **mass of metal consumed** in a sacrificial anode system using Faraday's law. A zinc anode of mass 5.2 kg operates at a current of 0.4 A. Given the atomic mass of Zn = 65.4 g/mol, n = 2, and utilization factor = 0.85, calculate the **lifetime of the anode in years**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Requirements for Effective CP (3 marks):**
1. The protected metal must be in metallic (electrical) continuity.
2. A continuous electrolyte path (soil, water) must exist.
3. The driving voltage (natural galvanic or impressed) must exceed the structure's corrosion potential shift requirement.
4. The protective current must polarize the structure to at least −850 mV vs CSE.
5. The system must be designed to provide adequate current for the entire design life.

**Sacrificial Anode Method (4 marks):**
In the sacrificial anode method, the structure to be protected is electrically connected to a metal/alloy that is **more electrochemically active**. Because of the potential difference, the active metal (anode) dissolves preferentially, supplying electrons to the protected structure (cathode). The method requires no external power and is best suited for small structures, ship hulls, and underground tanks in low-resistivity electrolytes.

**Examples of sacrificial anodes (1 mark):** Mg (−2.37 V), Zn (−0.76 V), Al alloys (−1.10 V).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation of Mass Consumed (4 marks):**
According to Faraday's law of electrolysis, the mass of metal deposited or dissolved is:

$$W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Where:** $W$ = mass consumed (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol.

For maximum utilization, the actual mass required is:
$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{theoretical}}{u}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
Rearranging for time $t$:

$$t = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M} = \dfrac{5{,}200 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{0.4 \times 65.4}$$

$$t = \dfrac{1.0034 \times 10^9}{26.16} = 3.8362 \times 10^7\ \text{seconds}$$

Converting to years:
$$t = \dfrac{3.8362 \times 10^7}{3.156 \times 10^7} = 1.215\ \text{years}$$

Applying the utilization factor 0.85:
$$T_{life} = \dfrac{1.215}{0.85} = 1.43\ \text{years}$$

$$\boxed{T_{life} \approx 1.43\ \text{years}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cathodic Protection Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap (2 marks lost frequently):** Students often mix Ω·cm and Ω·m when using Dwight's equation. Always convert soil resistivity to **SI units (Ω·m)** before substitution.
> 2. **Utilization Factor Oversight (1–2 marks lost):** The Faraday calculation gives *theoretical* mass; the *actual* anode must account for the utilization factor $u$. Many students omit this step and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Rounding Direction Error (1 mark lost):** When calculating the number of anodes, always **round up** to the next whole integer (e.g., 141.4 → 142). Rounding down would under-protect the structure.
> 4. **Confusion between Anode and Cathode Roles (2–3 marks lost):** In SACP, students sometimes wrongly assume the *protected metal* is the anode. Remember: the **sacrificial metal dissolves (anode)**; the **structure receives electrons (cathode)**.
> 5. **Neglecting IR Drop (1 mark lost):** For complete accuracy, the protection potential of −850 mV must be measured *with IR drop eliminated or considered*. Mention this in theory questions for full marks.
> 6. **Mermaid Diagram Label Errors:** When drawing schematics, always clearly label which terminal of the rectifier connects to the anode and which to the cathode. Reverse polarity will *accelerate* corrosion catastrophically.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cathodic Protection**

- 🔹 **Core Definition:** CP is an electrochemical method that converts the protected metal into the **cathode** of a galvanic or impressed current cell.
- 🔹 **Two Methods:** **SACP** (sacrificial anodes, no external power) and **ICCP** (rectifier-driven, inert anodes).
- 🔹 **Standard Protection Potential (Steel):** **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode (NACE criterion).
- 🔹 **Most Common Sacrificial Anode:** **Zinc** for marine, **Magnesium** for soil, **Aluminium alloys** for offshore.
- 🔹 **Most Common ICCP Anode:** **Mixed-Metal Oxide (MMO)** or high-silicon cast iron.
- 🔹 **Faraday's Law for CP:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — governs anode life calculations.
- 🔹 **Dwight's Equation:** $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ — calculates anode-to-earth resistance.
- 🔹 **Number of Anodes:** Always **round up** the calculated value.
- 🔹 **Utilization Factor ($u$):** Typical values — Mg ≈ 0.50, Zn ≈ 0.85, Al ≈ 0.80.
- 🔹 **Hours per Year Conversion:** Use **8760 h/year** (or $3.156 \times 10^7$ s/year) for time conversions.
- 🔹 **SACP Driving Voltage:** Only 0.2–0.7 V (limited by galvanic potential difference).
- 🔹 **ICCP Driving Voltage:** Adjustable, 6–50 V (limited by rectifier capacity).
- 🔹 **Applications:** Pipelines, ship hulls, offshore platforms, storage tanks, reinforced concrete, well casings.
- 🔹 **Standard Codes to Mention in Answers:** **NACE SP0169** (pipelines), **EN 12495** (marine), **ISO 12696** (concrete).
- 🔹 **Reverse Polarity Hazard:** Reversed ICCP connections will *accelerate* corrosion by 10–100×.
- 🔹 **Current Density Range:** Soil (10–20 mA/m²) < Concrete (0.2–2 mA/m²) < Seawater (80–150 mA/m²).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sacrificial Anodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sacrificial Anodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cathodic Protection — The Governing Principle

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metal to be protected is deliberately forced to behave as the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidative, metal-dissolving) half-reaction on its surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Cathodic protection is a method of corrosion control in which the metal surface is made the cathode of an electrochemical cell, either by galvanic coupling to a more active metal (sacrificial method) or by an externally impressed direct current (impressed current method).*

The two commercially and academically important variants mandated by the GCCYT122 Module 2 syllabus are:

1. **Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP)** — also called *Galvanic Cathodic Protection*.
2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — also called *Impressed Current Method*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Self-Sacrificing Bodyguard":**
> Imagine an important VIP (the steel pipeline) is being attacked by a bully (corrosion). A loyal bodyguard (a more active metal like zinc, magnesium, or aluminium) offers to take all the hits. The bully, finding the bodyguard easier to attack (lower electrode potential), repeatedly strikes *him* instead. The VIP walks away unharmed. Eventually, the bodyguard "falls" — he corrodes away completely — but the VIP is preserved.
>
> This is **Sacrificial Anodic Protection**. The bodyguard is *consumed* in the process.
>
> Now imagine a *second* scenario: the VIP hires an armed security detail that uses a *tas*er* (external DC source) to shock the bully continuously, paralyzing him so he cannot attack. The security detail itself wears a bulletproof vest (inert anode) and is never seriously harmed.
>
> This is **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection**. The power comes from outside, and the "anode" is essentially indestructible in the short term.

## 1.3 Why Cathodic Protection Works — The Thermodynamic Basis

For any metal M submerged in an electrolyte, the corrosion reaction proceeds only if the **open-circuit potential (E_corr)** lies *above* the **equilibrium potential of the anodic reaction** (E_M/M^n+) and *below* the **equilibrium potential of the cathodic reaction** (E_H+/H₂ or E_O₂/H₂O).

By making the metal a cathode, we **polarize its surface potential in the negative direction**, so that:

$$E_{\text{protected}} \le E_{\text{eq}}(\text{M}^{n+}/\text{M})$$

When this condition is met, the net anodic current on the protected surface becomes zero — the metal cannot dissolve.

## 1.4 The Two Methods at a Glance

| Feature | Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP) | Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Driving Force** | Galvanic potential difference | External DC rectifier |
| **Anode Type** | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) | Inert (graphite, MMO, Pt, Si–Fe–Cr) |
| **Power Source** | None (self-powered) | AC-to-DC rectifier (mains or solar) |
| **Anode Life** | Finite (consumed) | Long (essentially inert) |
| **Current Range** | Low to moderate (limited by ΔE) | Wide (adjustable from rectifier) |
| **Typical Use** | Small/medium structures | Large/remote structures |
| **Symbolic Analogy** | "Bodyguard who takes punches" | "Taser that stuns the attacker" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP) — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Principle
A metal **M₁** (the structure to be protected, e.g., **iron/steel**) is electrically connected to a more *electrochemically active* metal **M₂** (the sacrificial anode, e.g., **Zn, Mg, or Al**). Because M₂ has a more negative standard reduction potential, it preferentially undergoes **oxidation** (corrosion), while the electrons liberated flow through the metallic connection to M₁, forcing M₁ to act as the **cathode**.

### 2.1.2 Half-cell Reactions (Steel protected by Zinc in aerated neutral water)

At the **Sacrificial Anode (Zn)** — Anodic dissolution:

$$\text{Zn} \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \quad E^{\circ} = -0.76\ \text{V vs SHE}$$

At the **Protected Steel Surface** — Cathodic reactions (oxygen reduction in neutral/alkaline medium):

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \quad E^{\circ} = +0.40\ \text{V vs SHE}$$

In acidic medium (less common but possible):

$$2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad E^{\circ} = 0.00\ \text{V vs SHE}$$

### 2.1.3 Requirements of a Good Sacrificial Anode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner-Weighted Criteria for Sacrificial Anode Selection:**
> 1. **Electrochemically more active** than the base metal (more negative E°).
> 2. **Anode efficiency (utilization factor)** must be high (≥ 50% for Zn, ≥ 50% for Mg, ~ 50–95% for Al-alloys).
> 3. **Stable potential** in the working environment (predictable).
> 4. **Uniform corrosion** (no localized pitting of the anode itself).
> 5. **Low cost** and easy availability.
> 6. **High theoretical capacity** — ampere-hours per kilogram.

### 2.1.4 Practical Sacrificial Anode Materials

| Sacrificial Anode | $E^{\circ}$ (V vs SHE) | Theoretical Capacity (A·h/kg) | Density (g/cm³) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | −2.37 | **2200** | 1.74 | Soil (high-resistivity), buried tanks |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | −0.76 | **820** | 7.14 | Seawater, ship hulls, pipelines |
| **Aluminium (Al-alloy, e.g., Al–Zn–In)** | −1.66 | **2700** | 2.71 | Marine (offshore platforms) |

### 2.1.5 Limitations of SAP
- Driving voltage limited to galvanic potential difference (typically **≤ 0.25 V for Zn–Fe**, **≤ 0.7 V for Mg–Fe**).
- Anode is consumed — needs periodic replacement.
- Ineffective in **high-resistivity electrolytes** (e.g., dry, rocky soil).
- Limited current output → unsuitable for very large structures.

---

## 2.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.2.1 Principle
An **external DC power source** (rectifier converting AC mains to DC, or a solar-powered DC system) is used. The **positive terminal** of the rectifier is connected to an **inert (impressed current) anode**, and the **negative terminal** is connected to the **structure to be protected**. The rectifier *forces* electrons to flow into the protected structure, polarizing it strongly in the negative direction so that the anodic dissolution reaction is thermodynamically and kinetically suppressed.

### 2.2.2 Half-cell Reactions

At the **Inert Anode (e.g., graphite or MMO)** — Anodic reactions (the anode itself is *not* consumed, but electrolyte is oxidized):

In neutral/alkaline aerated medium:

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-}$$

In chloride-rich (marine) medium:

$$2\text{Cl}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}_2 + 2e^{-}$$

At the **Protected Structure (Cathode)** — Cathodic reactions:

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

### 2.2.3 Common ICCP Anode Materials

| Inert Anode | Notes | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Graphite** | Cheap, brittle, low current density | Buried pipelines, ground beds |
| **High-Silicon Cast Iron (Si–Fe–Cr)** | Wear-resistant | Underground/underwater |
| **Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO) on Ti substrate** | Premium, very low wear rate (~ mg/A·yr) | Marine, deep well |
| **Platinum (Pt) plated on Ti or Nb** | Excellent but expensive | Critical marine structures |
| **Lead–Antimony (Pb–Sb) alloy** | Used in concentrated sulfate media | Sulfuric acid plant coolers |

### 2.2.4 Advantages of ICCP
- **Adjustable current and voltage** — can be tuned to the structure's exact need.
- **Single installation can protect very large structures** (entire pipeline networks, ship hulls, jetties).
- **Anode is essentially permanent** (lifetime limited by mechanical wear, not consumption).
- **Effective even in high-resistivity soils** if voltage is increased.

### 2.2.5 Disadvantages of ICCP
- Requires **continuous electrical power** (rectifier failure → loss of protection).
- Higher **initial cost** (rectifier, cabling, monitoring).
- Risk of **over-protection** → hydrogen embrittlement, disbonding of coatings.
- Requires **regular monitoring** by qualified personnel.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for the KTU ESE. Memorize the symbols, the units, and the limiting forms.

### Table A — Fundamental Electrochemical Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis (mass liberated) | $m$ in g; $I$ in A; $t$ in s |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Electrochemical equivalent | g/C or kg/A·h |
| 3 | $CR = \dfrac{3270 \cdot I}{A \cdot \rho \cdot n}$ | Corrosion rate from current density (mpy formula) | mpy (mils per year) |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot I}{A \cdot \rho \cdot n}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | mm/year |
| 5 | $t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M \cdot \eta}$ | Anode lifetime (with efficiency η) | seconds (convert to years) |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{soil}} \cdot L}{A}$ | Soil/electrolyte resistance (Ohm's law) | Ohms |
| 7 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's law for circuit | Volts |
| 8 | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | A/m² or mA/cm² |
| 9 | $E_{\text{drive}} = \vert E_{\text{anode}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{cathode}}^{\circ} \vert$ | Driving voltage in SAP | Volts |
| 10 | $m_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{theoretical}}}{\eta}$ | Mass required accounting for anode efficiency | kg |

> **Important constants to memorize:**
> - **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 in simple KTU problems)
> - **1 year = 31,536,000 s** (use 3.15 × 10⁷ for quick KTU approximations)
> - **Equivalent weight of Zn = 65.4 / 2 = 32.7 g/eq**
> - **Equivalent weight of Mg = 24.3 / 2 = 12.15 g/eq**
> - **Equivalent weight of Al = 27.0 / 3 = 9.0 g/eq**
> - **Density of Fe (steel) = 7.86 g/cm³**

### Table B — Selection Decision Matrix (SAP vs ICCP)

| Selection Criterion | Choose **SAP** when… | Choose **ICCP** when… |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Structure size | Small to medium | Large to very large |
| Electrolyte resistivity | Low (seawater, moist soil) | High (rocky/dry soil also OK) |
| Power availability | None / unreliable | Mains AC or solar available |
| Budget | Low initial cost acceptable | Higher initial cost acceptable |
| Maintenance access | Easy periodic replacement | Remote; long-life preferred |
| Current demand | ≤ ~ 5 A | > 5 A (any value possible) |
| Coating of structure | Bare, small uncoated area | Large coated + small bare area |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Touchpoints where these systems save trillions of dollars globally:**
> - **Cross-country oil & gas pipelines** (e.g., 10,000+ km pipelines protected by ICCP ground-beds every 30–50 km).
> - **Marine — ship hulls, offshore drilling platforms, jetties, ballast tanks** (Zn/Al-alloy hull anodes on virtually every commercial ship).
> - **Underground LPG/domestic gas distribution networks.**
> - **Reinforcing steel (rebar) in concrete bridge decks and parking structures.**
> - **Storage tank bottoms** (double-bottom tanks with reference cells and Zn anodes).
> - **Water-works — ductile-iron water mains, water-heater magnesium rods.**
> - **Power plant condensers and heat-exchanger tube sheets.**

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cathodic Protection Design Equation

The governing equation for the mass of sacrificial metal required is obtained by applying **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** to the corrosion of the sacrificial anode.

### Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's First Law

The mass *m* of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge *Q* passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q$$

Introducing a proportionality constant *Z* (the electrochemical equivalent):

$$m = Z \cdot Q$$

Since charge $Q = I \cdot t$:

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Faraday's Second Law → Expression for Z

The mass liberated per unit charge is proportional to the **equivalent weight** of the substance, and the universal constant is Faraday's constant *F* (the charge carried by one mole of electrons, 96,485 C).

$$Z = \dfrac{\text{Equivalent weight}}{F} = \dfrac{M / n}{F} = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per atom/ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant

### Step 3 — Combine to Get the Master Equation

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Anode Efficiency (Utilization Factor η)

In practice, **not all** of the current passing through the anode contributes usefully to protective electron supply. Some current is "wasted" on side reactions, localized pitting of the anode, or back-currents. This is captured by the **anode utilization factor η** (0 < η ≤ 1, typically 0.50–0.95).

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{theoretical}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \eta}$$

### Step 5 — Express the Anode Lifetime

Rearranging for the time *t* over which a given mass *m* of sacrificial anode will last:

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \eta}{I \cdot M}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

> **Problem:**
> A buried steel pipeline is to be protected by a **magnesium sacrificial anode** system. The protective current required is $I = 0.50\ \text{A}$. If the total mass of magnesium installed is $m = 25.0\ \text{kg}$ and the anode utilization factor is $\eta = 0.55$, calculate the **service life** of the anode system in years.
>
> **Given:**
> - $I = 0.50\ \text{A}$
> - $m = 25.0\ \text{kg} = 25{,}000\ \text{g}$
> - $\eta = 0.55$
> - For Mg: $M = 24.3\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$ (since Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻)
> - $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation** (anode lifetime form, derived in §3.1):

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \eta}{I \cdot M}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values** (watch unit consistency — *m* in grams, *I* in amperes):

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.55}{0.50 \times 24.3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$25{,}000 \times 2 = 50{,}000$$

$$50{,}000 \times 96{,}485 = 4.824 \times 10^{9}$$

$$4.824 \times 10^{9} \times 0.55 = 2.653 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$0.50 \times 24.3 = 12.15$$

**Step 5 — Divide to find time in seconds:**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{2.653 \times 10^{9}}{12.15} = 2.184 \times 10^{8}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert seconds to years** (1 year = 31,536,000 s = 3.1536 × 10⁷ s):

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{2.184 \times 10^{8}}{3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \approx 6.92\ \text{years}$$

> **Final Answer (with units):**
> $$\boxed{t_{\text{life}} \approx 6.9\ \text{years}}$$

---

## 3.3 Reverse Problem — Mass Required for a Given Life

> **Problem:**
> An aluminium-alloy sacrificial anode is to protect a marine steel structure. The required current is $I = 2.5\ \text{A}$ and the design life is 8 years. Anode utilization factor is $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate the **mass of Al-alloy** required.
>
> For Al-alloy: $M = 27.0\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 3$ (Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻).

**Step 1 — Use the master mass equation:**

$$m_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \eta}$$

**Step 2 — Convert time to seconds:**

$$t = 8 \times 3.1536 \times 10^{7} = 2.523 \times 10^{8}\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$m = \dfrac{2.5 \times 2.523 \times 10^{8} \times 27.0}{3 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.85}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$2.5 \times 2.523 \times 10^{8} = 6.308 \times 10^{8}$$

$$6.308 \times 10^{8} \times 27.0 = 1.703 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$3 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.85 = 2.460 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$m = \dfrac{1.703 \times 10^{10}}{2.460 \times 10^{5}} = 6.92 \times 10^{4}\ \text{g} \approx 69.2\ \text{kg}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$\boxed{m_{\text{Al-alloy}} \approx 69.2\ \text{kg}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SAP vs ICCP

| Analytical Parameter | Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP) | Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driving EMF | Fixed by galvanic couple (0.2–0.7 V typical) | Variable, set by rectifier (0–50 V typical) |
| Equivalent Circuit | Anode ∥ Soil Resistance ∥ Cathode | Rectifier + Cable Resistance + Anode + Cathode |
| Polarization Curve Behavior | Limited to natural ΔE; cannot exceed open-circuit | Fully polarizes to any desired E_protect |
| Governing Equation | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_{\text{electrolyte}}}$ | $I = \dfrac{V_{\text{rectifier}} - E_{\text{back}}}{R_{\text{circuit}}}$ |
| Anode Mass Equation | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \eta}$ | Not applicable (anode is inert) |
| Energy Source | Chemical (anode dissolution) | Electrical (rectifier from AC mains) |
| Current Density at Cathode | Limited (typ. 10–100 mA/m²) | Wide range (1–500 mA/m²) |
| Failure Mode | Anode exhaustion (predictable) | Power failure (sudden) |
| Cost Component Dominant | Anode replacement (recurring) | Electricity + rectifier (operational) |
| Suitability for Coated Structures | Poor (small exposed area) | Excellent (coatings reduce current demand) |
| Inspection Method | Anode weight loss, visual | Reference electrode (Cu/CuSO₄) potential, rectifier output |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Sacrificial Anode: Zn, Mg, or Al-alloy] -->|Direct metallic connection: wire or weld| nodeB[Protected Metal: Steel or Iron Structure]
    nodeB -->|Electrons received become cathode| nodeC[Protected Cathodic Surface: no corrosion]
    nodeA -->|Anodic dissolution Zn to Zn2 plus 2e| nodeD[Corrosion Products: Zn2 plus, Mg2 plus]
    nodeE[Electrolyte: soil, seawater, or concrete pore water] -->|Ionic current completes circuit| nodeA
    nodeE -->|Ionic current completes circuit| nodeB
    nodeF[Periodic anode replacement required] -.->|Consumable maintenance| nodeA
```

**Figure 4.1:** *Functional flow of electrons and ions in a Sacrificial Anodic Protection system. The sacrificial anode dissolves continuously, and the protected steel is forced to act as the cathode of the galvanic couple.*

---

## 4.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AC Mains or Solar Source] -->|Stepped down and rectified| nodeB[DC Rectifier Unit: 0 to 50 V adjustable]
    nodeB -->|Positive terminal cable| nodeC[Inert Anode: Graphite, MMO-Ti, Pt-Ti, or Si-Fe-Cr]
    nodeB -->|Negative terminal cable| nodeD[Protected Metal Structure: Pipeline or Hull]
    nodeC -->|Minimal dissolution: O2 or Cl2 evolution| nodeE[Anode Reaction Products: oxygen gas or chlorine gas]
    nodeD -->|Polarized to protective potential| nodeF[Protected Cathodic Surface: no corrosion]
    nodeG[Electrolyte: soil or seawater] -->|Ionic path completes circuit| nodeC
    nodeG -->|Ionic path completes circuit| nodeD
    nodeH[Reference Electrode: Cu-CuSO4 or Ag-AgCl] -.->|Continuous potential monitoring| nodeD
    nodeI[Automatic Controller: optional] -.->|Adjusts rectifier output| nodeB
```

**Figure 4.2:** *Functional flow in an Impressed Current Cathodic Protection system. The rectifier provides the driving voltage, the inert anode survives indefinitely, and the protected structure receives a continuous stream of protective electrons. A reference electrode provides feedback for monitoring and control.*

---

## 4.3 Combined Comparison — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeSubA[Sacrificial Anodic Protection SAP]
    nodeSubA --> nodeA1[Active Anode: Mg, Zn, or Al]
    nodeSubA --> nodeA2[No External Power Source]
    nodeSubA --> nodeA3[Direct Electrical Connection]
    nodeSubA --> nodeA4[Anode is Consumed over Time]

    nodeSubB[Impressed Current Cathodic Protection ICCP]
    nodeSubB --> nodeB1[Inert Anode: Graphite or MMO]
    nodeSubB --> nodeB2[External DC Rectifier]
    nodeSubB --> nodeB3[Cable Connection through Rectifier]
    nodeSubB --> nodeB4[Anode is Essentially Permanent]

    nodeCommon[Common Outcome]
    nodeA4 --> nodeCommon[Protected Structure acts as Cathode]
    nodeB4 --> nodeCommon[Protected Structure acts as Cathode]
```

**Figure 4.3:** *Side-by-side architectural comparison of the two cathodic protection philosophies. Both achieve the same end (protected cathode), but differ fundamentally in the *source* of the protective electrons and the *fate* of the anode.*

---

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Start: Identify Structure to be Protected]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q1. Distinguish between Sacrificial Anodic Protection and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP) | Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power source** | Self-powered; uses galvanic potential difference between dissimilar metals. | Requires external DC power source (rectifier). |
| **Anode material** | Active metal (Mg, Zn, Al) — *consumed* over time. | Inert material (graphite, MMO, Pt) — *not consumed*. |
| **Driving voltage** | Limited to galvanic couple (≈ 0.2–0.7 V). | Adjustable from rectifier (0–50 V). |
| **Application** | Small structures, ship hulls, water heaters. | Large pipelines, offshore platforms, storage tanks. |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic anode replacement. | Periodic rectifier servicing, continuous power. |

---

### **Q2. Mention any four requirements of a good sacrificial anode.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A good sacrificial anode must satisfy the following requirements:
1. Its **standard electrode potential must be more negative** (more active) than the metal to be protected, so that it preferentially corrodes.
2. It must have a **high electrochemical equivalent** (high ampere-hour capacity per kg).
3. It should have a **high anode utilization factor (η)**, meaning the bulk of its dissolution is uniform and useful.
4. It should be **cheap, readily available, and easy to fabricate** into the required shapes.
5. (Bonus) It should form **non-passivating corrosion products** that do not insulate the anode surface.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** *(14 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, mechanism, and essential components** of *Sacrificial Anodic Protection* with a suitable sketch. Discuss the criteria for selecting a sacrificial anode material, with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is *Impressed Current Cathodic Protection*? With the help of a block diagram, explain its working. Compare it with sacrificial anodic protection. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Principle:**
Sacrificial anodic protection is a method of corrosion control in which a more electrochemically active metal (the *sacrificial anode*) is electrically connected to the metal structure to be protected. Because the anode has a more negative reduction potential, it preferentially oxidizes (corrodes), and the electrons it releases flow through the metallic connection to the protected structure, suppressing the corrosion of the latter.

**Mechanism (for steel protected by zinc in aerated neutral water):**

- **At the zinc anode (oxidation):**
$$\text{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \quad E^{\circ} = -0.76\ \text{V}$$

- **At the steel surface (reduction of dissolved oxygen):**
$$\text{O}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}(aq) \quad E^{\circ} = +0.40\ \text{V}$$

- **Overall cell potential:**
$$E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} = E_{\text{cathode}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{anode}}^{\circ} = (+0.40) - (-0.76) = +1.16\ \text{V}$$

The cell EMF drives a continuous protective current. The steel structure is held at a potential where its anodic dissolution is thermodynamically unfavourable.

**Essential Components:**
1. **Sacrificial anode** (Zn, Mg, or Al-alloy).
2. **Metallic connection** (insulated copper cable or direct weld).
3. **Electrolyte medium** (soil, seawater, or concrete pore water) that completes the ionic circuit.
4. **Protected structure** (the cathode).

**Criteria for Anode Selection:**
- More negative $E^{\circ}$ than the base metal.
- High theoretical capacity (A·h/kg).
- High utilization factor (η ≥ 0.5).
- Uniform, non-passivating corrosion behaviour.
- Low cost and easy availability.

**Examples:**
- **Magnesium** (high driving voltage, $E^{\circ} = -2.37$ V) → soils, buried pipelines.
- **Zinc** ($E^{\circ} = -0.76$ V) → marine applications, ship hulls.
- **Aluminium alloys** (Al–Zn–In, $E^{\circ} = -1.66$ V) → offshore platforms.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Definition:**
Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) is a method in which an external DC power source (rectifier) is used to force a continuous protective current from an inert anode through the electrolyte to the protected structure, which acts as the cathode.

**Working (with reference to a buried pipeline):**
- The **positive terminal** of the DC rectifier is connected to an **inert ground-bed anode** (e.g., graphite or MMO on titanium).
- The **negative terminal** is connected to the **pipeline**.
- The rectifier drives current from the anode, through the soil electrolyte, into the pipeline.
- At the pipeline surface, the current polarizes the metal in the negative direction, suppressing the anodic dissolution of iron.
- A **Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** is used to monitor the pipeline–soil potential continuously.

**Half-cell reactions in ICCP:**

- **At the inert anode (oxidation of water):**
$$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{O}_2(g) + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4e^{-}$$

- **At the protected structure (oxygen reduction):**
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

**Comparison with SAP:**

| Aspect | SAP | ICCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Power source | Galvanic (self) | External DC |
| Driving voltage | Limited | Adjustable |
| Anode life | Finite | Very long |
| Application size | Small–medium | Large–very large |
| Cost | Low initial, recurring replacement | High initial, low operating |

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** *(14 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Part B, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of *Impressed Current Cathodic Protection*. List the materials commonly used as impressed current anodes and state any **two advantages** of ICCP over SAP. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is *Sacrificial Anodic Protection*? Mention its **four essential components** and explain its applications. A magnesium sacrificial anode of mass **20 kg** is used to protect an underground pipeline. If the protective current is **0.8 A** and the anode utilization factor is **0.50**, calculate the **service life of the anode in years**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Working of ICCP (block diagram description):**
A DC rectifier (input: 230 V AC mains; output: adjustable 0–50 V DC) is connected with its **positive terminal** to a buried **inert anode** (e.g., MMO-coated titanium ground-bed) and its **negative terminal** to the **pipeline**. The rectifier forces a continuous current to flow from the anode, through the soil, into the pipeline, and back to the rectifier via the cable. The pipeline surface is polarized to a protective potential of typically **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode**, at which anodic dissolution of iron is thermodynamically forbidden.

**Common ICCP Anode Materials:**
1. **Graphite** — cheap, used in soil.
2. **Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO) on titanium** — premium marine/underground use.
3. **High-Silicon Cast Iron (Si–Fe–Cr)** — used in aggressive soils.
4. **Platinum (Pt)-plated titanium or niobium** — high-end marine.
5. **Lead–antimony alloy (Pb–Sb)** — sulfuric acid coolers.

**Two Advantages of ICCP over SAP:**
1. **Adjustable current and voltage** — can protect very large structures, regardless of natural galvanic limitations.
2. **Anode is essentially permanent** (inert), so operating and maintenance cost is lower over the long term.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** *(Numerical)*

**Definition:**
Sacrificial anodic protection is a corrosion-control method in which a more active metal is electrically coupled to the structure, sacrificing itself by corrosion to protect the base metal.

**Four Essential Components:**
1. The **sacrificial anode** (active metal).
2. The **protected structure** (base metal).
3. The **electrolyte** (soil or water).
4. The **metallic connection** between anode and structure.

**Applications:**
- Buried oil and gas pipelines.
- Ship hulls and offshore platforms.
- Domestic water-heater magnesium rods.
- Reinforcing steel in concrete bridge decks.
- Underground storage tank bottoms.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $m = 20\ \text{kg} = 20{,}000\ \text{g}$
- $I = 0.8\ \text{A}$
- $\eta = 0.50$
- For Mg: $M = 24.3\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$

**Formula:**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \eta}{I \cdot M}$$

**Substitution:**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.50}{0.8 \times 24.3}$$

**Numerator:**

$$20{,}000 \times 2 = 40{,}000$$

$$40{,}000 \times 96{,}485 = 3.859 \times 10^{9}$$

$$3.859 \times 10^{9} \times 0.50 = 1.930 \times 10^{9}$$

**Denominator:**

$$0.8 \times 24.3 = 19.44$$

**Division:**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{1.930 \times 10^{9}}{19.44} = 9.93 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s}$$

**Convert to years:**

$$t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{9.93 \times 10^{7}}{3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3.15\ \text{years}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$\boxed{t_{\text{life}} \approx 3.15\ \text{years}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Loss of Marks in KTU Board Exams (Cathodic Protection):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Sacrificial Anodic Protection" with "Anodic Protection":**
>    *Sacrificial anodic protection* (a *misnomer* that survives from older texts) is actually a **cathodic protection** method — the *protected structure* is the *cathode*. *Anodic protection* is a *completely different* technique in which the protected metal is made the *anode* to form a passive oxide film (used for stainless steel in H₂SO₄). **Do not mix these two up in your answer** — examiners *will* deduct 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the efficiency factor η in numerical problems:**
>    Students often compute $m = (I \cdot t \cdot M) / (n \cdot F)$ without dividing by η. The question **always** specifies η. Missing it costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in time conversion:**
>    Faraday's law requires *t* in **seconds**. The trap: writing *t* in hours or years and computing charge as $I \cdot t$ in inconsistent units. Always convert *t* to seconds: $1\ \text{year} = 3.1536 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s}$.
>
> 4. **Choosing the wrong *n* in $m = I t M / n F$:**
>    - Zn → Zn²⁺, so $n = 2$.
>    - Mg → Mg²⁺, so $n = 2$.
>    - Al → Al³⁺, so $n = 3$.
>    - Fe → Fe²⁺ (typical), so $n = 2$.
>    Get this wrong, and the entire numerical answer is wrong.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:**
>    A neat labelled diagram is worth **1 to 2 marks** by itself. Even a simple box-and-arrow schematic earns full marks. **Always draw.**
>
> 6. **Writing "Zn protects Fe" without explaining *why*:**
>    KTU examiners want the **electrode-potential argument** explicitly: "Zn has a more negative standard reduction potential (–0.76 V) than Fe (–0.44 V); therefore Zn is preferentially oxidized."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Cathodic Protection**

- ✅ **Cathodic protection (CP)** makes the metal to be protected the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, suppressing its anodic dissolution.
- ✅ There are **two methods**: *Sacrificial Anodic Protection (SAP)* and *Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)*.
- ✅ In **SAP**, an **active metal** (Zn, Mg, Al) is galvanically coupled to the structure. The active metal **dissolves (sacrifices) itself**.
- ✅ In **ICCP**, an **inert anode** (graphite, MMO, Pt) is connected via an **external DC rectifier** to the structure. The rectifier supplies the protective electrons.
- ✅ **SAP** is self-powered, simple, and ideal for small/medium structures in low-resistivity electrolytes (seawater, moist soil).
- ✅ **ICCP** is adjustable, suitable for **large/remote** structures, and works even in **high-resistivity** soils.
- ✅ **Sacrificial anodes** in common use: **Mg** (soils, E° = –2.37 V), **Zn** (seawater, E° = –0.76 V), **Al-alloy** (offshore, E° ≈ –1.66 V).
- ✅ **Inert anodes** in common use: graphite, high-Si cast iron, MMO on Ti, Pt-plated Ti/Nb, Pb–Sb alloy.
- ✅ **Driving voltage in SAP** is limited to the **galvanic potential difference** between anode and cathode (≈ 0.2–0.7 V typically).
- ✅ **Driving voltage in ICCP** is **adjustable from the rectifier** (0 to 50 V), independent of the natural couple.
- ✅ **The master equation** for sacrificial anode mass or life is:
$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \eta}$$
- ✅ **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** (use 96,500 for quick approximations).
- ✅ **Anode efficiency η** is always ≤ 1; typical values: Zn ≈ 0.90, Al-alloy ≈ 0.85, Mg ≈ 0.50.
- ✅ **Cathodic protection criteria** (practical safeguard potentials vs Cu/CuSO₄ reference): for steel in soil, target **−0.85 V**; for steel in seawater, target **−0.80 V**.
- ✅ **Do NOT confuse "sacrificial anodic protection" with "anodic protection"** — the latter is a different technique (used for passivating alloys in aggressive acids).
- ✅ **Applications** to remember: buried pipelines (oil/gas/water), ship hulls, offshore platforms, water-heater anodes, rebar in concrete, underground storage tanks.
- ✅ **For numerical problems**, always: identify $M$, $n$, $I$, $t$ (in seconds), $F$, and $\eta$; substitute carefully; convert *t* to years at the end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating of Copper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroplating of Copper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroplating of Copper** is an **electrolytic process** in which a thin, uniform, and adherent layer of metallic copper is deposited onto the surface of a conducting substrate (the *cathode*) by passing a **direct electric current** through an electrolytic cell containing an aqueous solution of a copper salt (typically **acidified copper sulphate, CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄**).

In this process, the **object to be plated acts as the cathode** (negatively charged electrode), while a **pure copper bar acts as the anode** (positively charged electrode). The copper ions migrate from the anode → through the electrolyte → and get reduced/deposited onto the cathode surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology — The Nernst-Electrolysis Pairing**
> Copper electroplating is the textbook case study for **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** under controlled electrolysis. The KTU examiner expects you to link the **deposition mass (m)**, **current (I)**, **time (t)**, **equivalent weight**, and **Faraday's constant (F = 96500 C mol⁻¹)** in every numerical.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Copper Paint Factory" 🎨

Imagine a busy kitchen where a chef is making a perfect chocolate layer cake:

- 🍫 **The chocolate (Cu²⁺ ions in solution)** floats around in a syrupy **batter (electrolyte)**.
- 🧊 **The cold plate (cathode)** is the cake base — when dipped in, the chocolate **sticks onto it** layer by layer.
- 🔥 **The hot spoon (anode)** keeps replenishing fresh chocolate back into the batter to keep the concentration steady.

The **electric current** is the "chef" controlling how thick the layer becomes, and **Faraday's Laws** are the "recipe book" that tells us *exactly* how much chocolate will stick per minute based on the chef's stirring speed (current).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of it as a "reverse rusting." In rusting, iron loses electrons and dissolves. In electroplating, we *force* copper ions to *gain* electrons and stick to a surface — a controlled, directed electrochemical deposition.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Faraday's Constant (F)** | **96500 C mol⁻¹** | Charge carried by 1 mole of electrons |
| **Atomic mass of Cu (M)** | **63.5 g mol⁻¹** | Used in mass calculations |
| **Valency (n) of Cu²⁺** | **2** | Each Cu²⁺ accepts 2 electrons |
| **Density of Cu (ρ)** | **8.96 g cm⁻³** | Needed for thickness calculation |
| **Electrolyte concentration** | ~150–250 g/L CuSO₄ + 50 g/L H₂SO₄ | Standard acid-copper bath |
| **Operating current density** | 2–4 A/dm² | Typical for decorative plating |
| **Operating temperature** | 25–50 °C | Affects deposit uniformity |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Cell Setup — Component-Wise Logic

The copper electroplating cell consists of **four essential components**. Each plays a non-negotiable role:

### ⚙️ Component 1: The Anode (Positive Electrode) — *The Sacrificial Source*
- **Material:** Pure electrolytic-grade copper (≥ 99.9% purity).
- **Function:** Continuously dissolves to maintain Cu²⁺ concentration in the bath.
- **Half-Reaction (Oxidation):**

$$Cu_{(s)} \longrightarrow Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

- **Why pure copper?** Impurities would introduce unwanted ions (e.g., Fe²⁺, Zn²⁺) that co-deposit and ruin the layer.

### ⚙️ Component 2: The Cathode (Negative Electrode) — *The Substrate to be Plated*
- **Material:** The object to be plated (steel, brass, nickel, ABS plastic pre-coated with conductive paint, etc.).
- **Function:** Receives the copper deposit.
- **Half-Reaction (Reduction):**

$$Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow Cu_{(s)}$$

### ⚙️ Component 3: The Electrolyte — *The Ion Highway*
- **Composition:** Acidified CuSO₄·5H₂O solution (≈ 200 g/L) + H₂SO₄ (≈ 50 g/L).
- **Role of H₂SO₄:**
  1. Increases electrical conductivity of the bath.
  2. Prevents hydrolysis of Cu²⁺ to form basic copper salts.
  3. Ensures smooth, fine-grained deposits.
- **Possible competing reaction at cathode:**

$$2H^{+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow H_{2(g)} \uparrow \quad \text{(side reaction, lowers current efficiency)}$$

### ⚙️ Component 4: The Power Source — *The Electron Pump*
- A regulated **DC rectifier** supplies controlled voltage (typically 2–6 V) and current.
- A **variable resistance (rheostat)** + **ammeter** in series controls current.
- A **voltmeter** is connected in parallel to monitor cell voltage.

---

## 2.2 The Two Operational Phases of Copper Electroplating

### 🔹 Phase A: Surface Preparation (Pre-Treatment) — *Often Worth 70% of Plating Quality*
A poorly prepared surface will cause **peeling, blistering, or pitting**. The standard sequence is:

1. **Mechanical cleaning** — Buffing, sandblasting, or polishing to remove scale.
2. **Degreasing** — Alkaline cleaning (NaOH/Na₂CO₃) to remove oils/grease.
3. **Acid pickling** — Dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl dip to remove oxides/rust.
4. **Water rinsing** — Distilled water rinse between every step to prevent cross-contamination.
5. **Strike coating (optional)** — A thin initial copper layer from a high-throwing-power bath to ensure adhesion.

### 🔹 Phase B: Actual Electroplating — *The Deposition Step*
- The cleaned cathode and copper anode are immersed in the electrolyte.
- DC current is applied — Cu²⁺ ions discharge at the cathode and deposit.
- Duration is calculated using **Faraday's Law** to achieve a target thickness.

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting the Quality of Copper Deposit

| Factor | Effect on Deposit |
|---|---|
| **Current density ↑** | Faster deposition but rough, burnt, or powdery deposit if too high |
| **Temperature ↑** | Improves smoothness, but excessive heat dulls the layer |
| **Electrolyte concentration** | Low Cu²⁺ → hydrogen evolution; High Cu²⁺ → rough, granular deposit |
| **Agitation/stirring** | Prevents concentration polarization, yields finer grain |
| **pH control** | Acidic (pH ≈ 1–3) prevents hydroxide contamination |
| **Additives** | Brighteners, levelers, and wetting agents refine grain structure |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **minimum required to answer any KTU 2024 numerical or theoretical question on copper electroplating.** Memorize these cold.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary/Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Faraday's 1st Law (Mass)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $m$ = mass (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $n$ = valency electrons, $F$ = 96500 C/mol | Theoretical mass deposited |
| 2 | **Equivalent Weight** | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | $E$ = equivalent weight (g/eq) | For Cu, $E = 63.5/2 = 31.75$ g/eq |
| 3 | **Faraday's 2nd Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$ | Mass ratio = equivalent weight ratio | Compare two metals in series |
| 4 | **Current Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Expressed as % | KTU favourite 3-mark question |
| 5 | **Thickness of deposit** | $t = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | $t$ = thickness (cm), $\rho$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (cm²) | Reverse-calc plating time |
| 6 | **Charge (Q)** | $Q = I \cdot t$ | $Q$ in Coulombs | Used as input in Faraday eqn |
| 7 | **Number of Faradays** | $\#F = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{96500}$ | Dimensionless | Moles of electrons passed |

### 🔢 Quick-Reference Substitutions for Copper
- $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol
- $n_{Cu} = 2$ (Cu²⁺ state)
- $E_{Cu} = 31.75$ g/eq
- $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g/cm³

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 🛠️

Copper electroplating is **not just a classroom exercise** — it underpins several industrial processes:

- 🖥️ **Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs):** Copper plating creates conductive traces on non-conductive substrates.
- 🚗 **Automotive parts:** Decorative + corrosion-resistant finish on bumpers, badges, and trim.
- 🏗️ **Undercoat for nickel/chromium plating:** Copper's softness allows it to "level" rough surfaces, giving a mirror-smooth final chrome layer.
- ⚡ **Electrical connectors:** Improved surface conductivity and solderability.
- 🛡️ **EMI/RFI shielding:** Copper-coated plastic enclosures block electromagnetic interference.
- 💎 **Art & jewellery:** Affordable decorative finish on base metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Modern *acid copper sulphate* baths (used since the 1930s) dominate over cyanide-based copper baths due to environmental concerns, higher current efficiency (95–99%), and superior throwing power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Mass Deposited (Faraday's 1st Law)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme boards for any electrolysis/plating topic. Practice it until you can reproduce it blind.

### **Statement:**
> *The mass of substance deposited (or dissolved) at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.*

### **Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Charge carried by 1 mole of electrons:**
By definition, **1 Faraday (F) = Charge of 1 mole of electrons**.

$$F = N_A \cdot e^{-}$$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹ (Avogadro's number) and $e^{-} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electronic charge).

$$F = (6.022 \times 10^{23}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \approx 96500 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Charge required to deposit 1 mole of a metal with valency *n*:**
Since each ion requires **n electrons** to be reduced:

$$1 \text{ mole of } M^{n+} + n \text{ moles of } e^{-} \longrightarrow 1 \text{ mole of } M$$

$$\text{Charge needed} = n \cdot F \text{ Coulombs per mole of metal}$$

**Step 3 — Charge required to deposit *m* grams (where m is the actual mass):**
Mass deposited is proportional to charge passed (Faraday's 1st Law postulate):

$$\text{If } n \cdot F \text{ C deposit } \frac{M}{1} \text{ g (1 mole)},$$

$$\text{then } Q \text{ C deposit } m \text{ g such that:}$$

$$\frac{m}{Q} = \frac{M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging to get mass deposited:**

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $Q = I \cdot t$ (since $I$ = current in amperes, $t$ = time in seconds):**

$$\boxed{\,m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}\,}$$

This is **Faraday's 1st Law of Electrolysis** in its working form. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thickness of Copper Deposit from Mass

**Step 1 — Start with the volume of deposit:**
Volume is mass divided by density:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Volume in terms of plating area and thickness:**
For a uniform deposit of thickness $t$ on area $A$:

$$V = A \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$A \cdot t = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for thickness $t$:**

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Combine with Faraday's Law to get *time required for a target thickness*:**
Substitute $m$ from Faraday's Law:

$$t = \frac{I \cdot t_{plating} \cdot M}{\rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}$$

Solving for the plating time $t_{plating}$:

$$\boxed{\,t_{plating} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot t_{thickness} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}\,}$$

This is the **planner's equation** — used by KTU problems to ask "how long to plate?"

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Current Efficiency (η)

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass** (if 100% of current were used for Cu²⁺ reduction):

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Actual mass** (measured experimentally by weighing the cathode before and after):

$$m_{actual} = m_{after} - m_{before}$$

**Step 3 — Define efficiency as a ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{useful output}}{\text{ideal output}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and finalize:**

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is η < 100% in real plating?** Side reactions — primarily **hydrogen evolution** at the cathode — consume electrons that would otherwise deposit copper. This is the *core reason* KTU asks for the acid bath (it suppresses H⁺ discharge).

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Type KTU 2024:** This is a 7-mark Part B sub-question pattern.

### **Problem:**
An iron article of surface area **200 cm²** is to be copper-plated to a thickness of **0.005 cm** using a current of **2.0 A**. Calculate:
**(a)** The mass of copper deposited.
**(b)** The time required for plating (assume 100% current efficiency).

Given: $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96500$ C/mol.

### **Solution:**

**Part (a): Mass of copper deposited**

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t_{thickness}$$

$$m = 8.96 \times 200 \times 0.005$$

$$m = 8.96 \times 1.0$$

$$\boxed{m = 8.96 \text{ g}}$$

**Part (b): Time required for plating**

Rearranging Faraday's 1st Law:

$$t = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

$$t = \frac{8.96 \times 2 \times 96500}{2.0 \times 63.5}$$

$$t = \frac{1729280}{127}$$

$$t = 13616.4 \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t = \frac{13616.4}{3600} \approx 3.78 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.78 \text{ hours} \approx 3 \text{ h } 47 \text{ min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry **at least 4 significant figures** in intermediate steps; round off only in the final answer. **Always mention the units** in the final boxed answer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — *Why Copper is Special*

| Property | Copper (Cu) | Nickel (Ni) | Silver (Ag) | Chromium (Cr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Atomic mass (g/mol) | 63.5 | 58.7 | 107.9 | 52.0 |
| Valency (n) | 2 | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| Equivalent weight | 31.75 | 29.35 | 107.9 | 17.33 |
| Current efficiency (%) | 95–99 | 90–95 | 95–100 | 10–25 |
| Bath type | Acid sulphate (easy) | Watts bath | Cyanide (toxic) | Chromic acid (toxic) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Main use | Undercoat | Mid-layer | Conductivity | Hard decorative top |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why copper is the preferred undercoat:** Its **high current efficiency** + **self-leveling** ability + **low cost** make it ideal as a base for subsequent nickel-chrome plating — known as the **"triple-layer decorative system"** in industry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Copper Electroplating Cell — Complete Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Rectifier Power Source] --> AM[Ammeter]
    AM --> RH[Rheostat Variable Resistance]
    RH --> AN[Anode Pure Copper Bar 99.9 percent]
    RH --> CT[Cathode Object to be Plated]
    AN <-->|Electrolyte Path| CT
    CT --> AN

    subgraph BATH[Electrolyte Bath Acid Copper Sulphate Solution]
        CUSO4[CuSO4 200 g per litre]
        H2SO4[H2SO4 50 g per litre]
        IONS[Cu2 plus and SO4 2 minus ions in solution]
        CUSO4 --> IONS
        H2SO4 --> IONS
    end

    AN -.->|Cu dissolves Cu to Cu2 plus| BATH
    BATH -.->|Cu2 plus deposits on cathode| CT

    VM[Voltmeter Parallel] --- DC
    AN --- VM
    CT --- VM
```

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Copper Plating Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Mechanical Polishing Buffing Sandblasting] --> S2[Step 2 Alkaline Degreasing NaOH Na2CO3]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Acid Pickling Dilute H2SO4 or HCl to remove rust]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Water Rinsing Distilled water]
    S4 --> S5{Optional Strike Coating}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Step 5a High Throwing Power Initial Layer]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Step 5 Final Acid Copper Bath]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 6 Apply DC Current 2 to 4 A per dm2]
    S8 --> S9[Step 7 Monitor Current Time and Temperature]
    S9 --> S10[Step 8 Final Rinsing and Drying]
    S10 --> S11[Step 9 Quality Inspection Thickness Adhesion Appearance]
```

---

## 4.3 Electrode Reactions — Reduction & Oxidation Sites

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANODE_REGION[Anode Positive Electrode Oxidation]
        A1[Pure Copper Bar] --> A2[Cu gives Cu2 plus and 2 electrons]
        A2 --> A3[Cu2 plus enters the electrolyte]
    end

    subgraph ELECTROLYTE[Electrolyte Cu2 plus ions migrate]
        E1[Cu2 plus ions in solution] --> E2[Drift towards cathode]
        E2 --> E3[H plus also present side reaction possible]
    end

    subgraph CATHODE_REGION[Cathode Negative Electrode Reduction]
        C1[Substrate object] --> C2[Cu2 plus gains 2 electrons]
        C2 --> C3[Cu deposited as thin uniform layer]
    end

    ANODE_REGION --> ELECTROLYTE
    ELECTROTE --> CATHODE_REGION

    E3 -.->|Competing| CH[2H plus and 2 electrons gives H2 gas]
    CH -.->|Reduces Current Efficiency| CATHODE_REGION
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass-to-Time Calculation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Target Thickness t cm] --> C1
    INPUT2[Surface Area A cm2] --> C1
    INPUT3[Density rho g per cm3] --> C1
    C1[Compute Mass m equals rho A t] --> C2
    INPUT4[Current I A] --> C3
    C2 --> C3[Apply Faraday m equals I t M over nF]
    INPUT5[Molar mass M g per mol] --> C3
    INPUT6[Valency n] --> C3
    C3 --> OUTPUT1[Plating Time t equals m nF over I M]

    INPUT7[Actual mass measured] --> C4
    C2 --> C4[Current Efficiency eta equals m actual over m theoretical times 100]
    C4 --> OUTPUT2[Efficiency percentage]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Define electroplating. Write the anode and cathode reactions involved in the electroplating of copper.**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Electroplating is the process of depositing a thin, adherent layer of one metal onto the surface of another metal (or non-metal) by passing direct current through an electrolytic solution containing ions of the plating metal.

**Anode (oxidation) — Pure copper bar:**

$$Cu_{(s)} \longrightarrow Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

**Cathode (reduction) — Object being plated:**

$$Cu^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow Cu_{(s)}$$

The **electrolyte is acidified CuSO₄ solution**, and the **anode is sacrificial** — it continuously dissolves to maintain the Cu²⁺ concentration. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Mention the role of H₂SO₄ in the acid copper plating bath.**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance deposited (or liberated) at an electrode is **directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge** passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q \implies m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Role of H₂SO₄ in the bath:**
1. Increases the **electrical conductivity** of the solution, reducing cell voltage.
2. Prevents **hydrolysis of Cu²⁺** ions into basic copper salts/oxides that would spoil the deposit.
3. Suppresses **hydrogen evolution** at the cathode, improving current efficiency.
4. Maintains an **acidic pH (1–3)**, essential for smooth, fine-grained deposits.

**(3 marks)**

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> ⛔ **Most common mistakes students make in copper plating questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert time units** (minutes → seconds or hours → seconds) — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. Using **n = 1 instead of n = 2** for Cu²⁺ (copper is +2 state, not +1).
> 3. **Mixing up density and atomic mass** — density (g/cm³) is for volume; atomic mass (g/mol) is for Faraday's law.
> 4. **Ignoring current efficiency** in problems that state "actual" or "experimental" mass — failing to compute η.
> 5. Not **writing units** in the final answer — KTU deducts half-mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Not balancing half-reactions** or **omitting electron count** — partial marking is strict.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **electrolysis process** used for copper plating with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of (i) anode, (ii) cathode, (iii) electrolyte, and (iv) current density. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copper plating bath operates at a current of **5 A** for **45 minutes** and deposits **4.4 g of copper** at the cathode. Calculate:
- (i) Theoretical mass of copper that *should* be deposited.
- (ii) The current efficiency.
- (iii) The equivalent weight of copper. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Process Description:** Copper electroplating is an electrolytic process where a thin layer of copper is deposited on a metallic substrate using an external DC source and an acidified CuSO₄ electrolyte.

**Setup (description matches Section 4.1 diagram):**
- A **rectifier** (DC source) provides constant current.
- The **anode** is a pure copper rod; the **cathode** is the article to be plated.
- Both are dipped in **acidified CuSO₄ solution** in an inert tank (glass/PP).
- An **ammeter** (in series) and **voltmeter** (in parallel) monitor the process.

**Roles:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| (i) **Anode** | Sacrificial source of Cu²⁺ ions — keeps bath concentration constant |
| (ii) **Cathode** | Site of reduction; receives the Cu deposit |
| (iii) **Electrolyte** | Conducts ions between electrodes; provides Cu²⁺ for deposition |
| (iv) **Current density** | Controls deposition rate & quality (2–4 A/dm² for smooth, bright deposit) |

**Reactions:**

$$Cu \rightarrow Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \text{ (anode)}$$

$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu \text{ (cathode)}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for roles, 2 for reactions)**

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $I = 5$ A, $t = 45$ min $= 2700$ s, $m_{actual} = 4.4$ g, $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96500$ C/mol.

**(i) Theoretical mass:**

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{5 \times 2700 \times 63.5}{2 \times 96500}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{857250}{193000}$$

$$\boxed{m_{theoretical} \approx 4.442 \text{ g}}$$

**(ii) Current efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \frac{4.4}{4.442} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 99.05\%}$$

**(iii) Equivalent weight of copper:**

$$E = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{63.5}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{Cu} = 31.75 \text{ g/eq}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 for sub-parts)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the quality of copper electrodeposit**. Explain how **Faraday's laws** are applied in determining the thickness of a copper coating on a metal surface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An iron sheet of area **400 cm²** is to be coated with copper of thickness **0.003 cm** by electrolysis using a current of **3 A** with current efficiency of **90%**. Calculate:
- (i) The mass of copper deposited.
- (ii) The actual time required for plating. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Factors affecting copper deposit quality:**

1. **Current density** — Optimum: 2–4 A/dm². Too high → rough/burnt deposit; too low → slow plating, wastage of bath.
2. **Temperature** — Optimum: 25–50 °C. Higher temps improve diffusion and leveling.
3. **Electrolyte concentration** — CuSO₄ ~200 g/L. Low → H₂ evolution; High → granular deposit.
4. **pH** — Acidic pH (1–3) prevents hydroxide contamination.
5. **Agitation** — Mechanical/air stirring prevents concentration polarization.
6. **Additives** — Brighteners, levelers refine the grain structure.
7. **Substrate preparation** — Cleanliness determines adhesion.
8. **Purity of anode** — Impurities co-deposit and spoil the layer.

**Application of Faraday's Laws in thickness calculation:**

Step 1: Faraday's 1st Law gives the **mass deposited**:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Step 2: Thickness is related to mass by volume/density:

$$t_{thickness} = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{\rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}$$

Step 3: Solving for plating time $t$:

$$t = \frac{t_{thickness} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

This is the **planning equation** used industrially to specify plating time for a given coating thickness.

**(7 marks: 4 for factors + 3 for derivation/application)**

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $A = 400$ cm², $t_{thickness} = 0.003$ cm, $I = 3$ A, $\eta = 90\%$, $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96500$ C/mol.

**(i) Mass of copper deposited:**

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t_{thickness}$$

$$m = 8.96 \times 400 \times 0.003$$

$$\boxed{m = 10.752 \text{ g}}$$

**(ii) Actual time required (with 90% efficiency):**

Since efficiency = (actual mass / theoretical mass) × 100, the *theoretical mass* must be 10.752 g. But because only 90% of the current does useful work, we need to deposit **more theoretical mass** to get the same *actual* mass... actually let's re-derive carefully.

**Correct approach:** The *theoretical* mass using Faraday's law must equal the *actual* mass. So:

$$m_{actual} = \eta \times m_{theoretical} \implies m_{theoretical} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\eta}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = \frac{10.752}{0.90} = 11.947 \text{ g}$$

Now apply Faraday's 1st Law to find the **actual plating time**:

$$t = \frac{m_{theoretical} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$$

$$t = \frac{11.947 \times 2 \times 96500}{3 \times 63.5}$$

$$t = \frac{2305910}{190.5}$$

$$t = 12104.4 \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t = \frac{12104.4}{3600} \approx 3.36 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.36 \text{ h} \approx 3 \text{ h } 22 \text{ min}}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for mass + 4 for time with efficiency correction)**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Core Definition:** Electroplating = electrolytic deposition of a metal layer on a substrate using a DC current.
>
> ✅ **Cell Components:** Anode (pure Cu) + Cathode (object) + Electrolyte (acid CuSO₄) + DC source.
>
> ✅ **Key Half-Reactions:**
> - Anode: $Cu \to Cu^{2+} + 2e^-$
> - Cathode: $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \to Cu$
>
> ✅ **Faraday's 1st Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — *master this.*
>
> ✅ **Equivalent weight of Cu:** $E = 63.5 / 2 = 31.75$ g/eq.
>
> ✅ **Current Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$
>
> ✅ **Thickness Equation:** $t_{thickness} = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$
>
> ✅ **Time-Planning Equation:** $t_{plating} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot A \cdot t_{thickness} \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$
>
> ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $F = 96500$ C/mol, $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$.
>
> ✅ **Role of H₂SO₄:** Conductivity + prevents hydrolysis + suppresses H₂ evolution.
>
> ✅ **Role of anode:** Sacrificial — maintains constant Cu²⁺ concentration in the bath.
>
> ✅ **Current density optimum:** 2–4 A/dm² for smooth decorative copper.
>
> ✅ **Competing reaction:** $2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2 \uparrow$ — lowers current efficiency.
>
> ✅ **Pre-treatment steps:** Polish → Degrease → Acid pickle → Rinse → Plate (in that order).
>
> ✅ **Applications:** PCB fabrication, decorative finishing, undercoat for Ni-Cr plating, EMI shielding.
>
> ✅ **Industrial bath:** Acid copper sulphate (200 g/L CuSO₄ + 50 g/L H₂SO₄) — preferred over toxic cyanide baths.
>
> ✅ **Self-leveling property:** Copper's softness allows it to fill surface micro-defects, producing a mirror finish.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroless Plating of Copper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroless Plating of Copper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroless Plating (Autocatalytic Chemical Reduction):** Electroless plating is a non-galvanic, autocatalytic deposition process in which a metal ion (M^n+) present in an aqueous solution is chemically reduced to its metallic form (M⁰) by a suitable reducing agent, **without the application of an external electric current**, on a suitably prepared and catalytically active substrate surface. The deposited metal itself acts as a catalyst, allowing the reaction to continue autocatalytically until the substrate is withdrawn from the bath.

For **Copper Electroless Plating**, the metal ion undergoing reduction is **Cu²⁺ (cupric ion)**, most commonly supplied via **Copper(II) Sulphate Pentahydrate (CuSO₄·5H₂O)**, while the standard reducing agent is **Formaldehyde (HCHO)**, used in a strongly alkaline medium (pH > 11) maintained by **Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Self-Growing Paint"**
> Imagine dipping a **clean iron nail** into a special "smart paint bucket". Instead of using an electric brush (electroplating) to spread paint on the nail, the paint itself is a *liquid metal* that *spontaneously* glues itself onto the nail wherever it touches. The freshly painted spot then attracts more "paint" from the bucket and grows thicker by itself — *no electrician, no wire, no switch*. That is essentially **electroless copper plating** — a chemical reaction that "paints" copper atoms onto a surface in a self-sustaining way.

- **Standard Reduction Potential of Cu²⁺/Cu couple:** **+0.34 V** (vs. SHE).
- **Operating bath temperature:** **25 – 70 °C** (commonly 40 – 60 °C).
- **Operating pH range:** **11.0 – 13.5** (strongly alkaline).
- **Typical deposition rate:** **5 – 25 µm/hour**.
- **Avogadro's number (referenced for mole calculations):** **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just use Electroplating?** In electroplating, you *force* the current through a complex-shaped object — but the electric field concentrates at edges and corners, giving *uneven* coatings. Electroless plating relies purely on chemistry, so it gives a **uniform thickness** even on tiny holes, vias, and 3-D surfaces — this is why the entire modern **PCB (Printed Circuit Board) industry** depends on it.

### 1.3 Why Copper Specifically?

| Property | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Excellent electrical conductivity (5.96 × 10⁷ S/m) | Used for circuit traces on PCBs |
| High thermal conductivity (~400 W/m·K) | Heat-sink coatings |
| Good solderability | Allows components to be soldered onto boards |
| Low cost (cheaper than Ag/Au) | Economical for mass production |
| EMI/RFI shielding capability | Used as shielding layer in electronics |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Redox Logic

Electroless copper plating is fundamentally a **galvanic displacement-less redox reaction** happening at a *catalytic interface*. The driving force is the difference in electrochemical potential between the reducing agent couple and the Cu²⁺/Cu couple.

**Step-by-step logical flow:**

- **Step 1 — Surface Preparation:** The substrate (e.g., ABS plastic, epoxy, copper-clad laminate) is cleaned, etched, and *sensitized/activated* using **SnCl₂ (stannous chloride)** followed by **PdCl₂ (palladium chloride)**. This leaves nanoscopic **Pd⁰ nuclei** on the surface — these are the *catalytic seeds*.

- **Step 2 — Cathodic Half-Reaction (Reduction of Cu²⁺):** At the catalytic Pd/Cu surface:
  $$\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{0}_{(s)}$$

- **Step 3 — Anodic Half-Reaction (Oxidation of Formaldehyde):** In alkaline solution, formaldehyde exists as methylene glycol anion (CH₂(OH)O⁻), which donates electrons:
  $$\text{HCHO}_{(aq)} + 3\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{HCOO}^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} + 2e^{-}$$

- **Step 4 — Overall (Net) Reaction:** Adding the two half-reactions:
  $$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{HCHO} + 4\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{0} + 2\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + \text{H}_{2}\uparrow$$

- **Step 5 — Autocatalysis:** Once the first layer of Cu⁰ deposits, it *itself* becomes catalytic, so the reaction continues without further Pd⁰.

- **Step 6 — Complexation:** Free Cu²⁺ would spontaneously precipitate as **Cu(OH)₂** in alkaline medium. To prevent this, **complexing agents** such as **EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid)**, **Rochelle salt (Sodium Potassium Tartrate, KNaC₄H₄O₆·4H₂O)**, or **Quadrol** are added. They sequester Cu²⁺ and slowly release it for controlled reduction.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Equations You Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Typical Units / Values | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nernst Equation (cell EMF) | $E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ at 25 °C | Volts (V) | Predicts feasibility of the redox couple |
| 2 | Standard Cell Potential | $E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$ | V (vs. SHE) | Determines driving force for the bath |
| 3 | Thickness of Deposit | $t = \dfrac{m}{\rho \times A} = \dfrac{M \times I \times T}{n \times F \times \rho \times A}$ | micrometres (µm) | Calculate required plating time |
| 4 | Faraday's 1st Law of Electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{M \times I \times t}{n \times F}$ | grams (g) | Mass of metal deposited |
| 5 | Faraday's Constant | $F = 96485$ | C mol⁻¹ | Used in all electrochemical calculations |
| 6 | Density of Copper | $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ | g cm⁻³ | Needed in thickness formula |
| 7 | Molar mass of Cu | $M_{Cu} = 63.55$ | g mol⁻¹ | Stoichiometry |
| 8 | Deposition Rate | $R = \dfrac{k[\text{Cu}^{2+}]^{a}[\text{HCHO}]^{b}}{[\text{Complex}]^{c}}$ | µm h⁻¹ | Empirical rate law |
| 9 | Activation Energy (Arrhenius) | $k = A e^{-E_{a}/RT}$ | E_a ≈ 30–60 kJ mol⁻¹ | Bath temperature optimization |
| 10 | Stability Constant of Complex | $K_{f} = \dfrac{[\text{Cu(EDTA)}^{2-}]}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}][\text{EDTA}^{4-}]}$ | Dimensionless | Controls free Cu²⁺ concentration |

### 2.3 Bath Composition — A Typical Electroless Copper Bath

| Component | Chemical | Function | Typical Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Copper source | CuSO₄·5H₂O | Supplies Cu²⁺ | 3 – 10 g L⁻¹ |
| Reducing agent | HCHO (37% formalin) | Provides electrons | 2 – 10 mL L⁻¹ |
| Complexing agent | EDTA / Rochelle salt | Prevents Cu(OH)₂ ppt | 10 – 30 g L⁻¹ |
| pH adjuster | NaOH | Maintains alkalinity | pH 11 – 13.5 |
