| Stabilizer | 2,2′-Bipyridyl / Thiourea | Prevents bath decomposition | 1 – 50 mg L⁻¹ |
| Surfactant | Polyethylene glycol | Wetting action | 0.1 – 1 g L⁻¹ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Manufacturing** (through-hole plating of vias and multilayer boards).
- **EMI/RFI Shielding** of plastic enclosures in mobile phones and computers.
- **Decorative Coatings** on plastics (replaces toxic chromium).
- **Microelectronics** — ULSI interconnects, chip packaging.
- **Aerospace & Defence** — Radar-absorbing composite coatings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cell Potential of the Electroless Copper Bath

We use the **Nernst Equation** to verify the thermodynamic feasibility of the formaldehyde–copper redox couple.

**Step 1 — Identify the half-reactions and their standard reduction potentials:**

Cathode: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $\;E^{\circ}_{cathode} = +0.34$ V

Anode (reverse of oxidation): $\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{HCHO} + 3\text{OH}^{-}$, $\;E^{\circ}_{anode} = -0.22$ V (formal potential in alkaline medium, pH 14)

**Step 2 — Compute the standard cell EMF:**

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = (+0.34) - (-0.22) = +0.56 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Nernst Equation for the actual bath condition:**

For the overall reaction $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{HCHO} + 4\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu} + 2\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$, with $n = 2$ electrons transferred:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$$

$$E_{cell} = 0.56 - \frac{0.0591}{2}\log\frac{[\text{HCOO}^{-}]^{2}}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}][\text{HCHO}]^{2}[\text{OH}^{-}]^{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of Spontaneity:**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $E_{cell}$ is **positive (+0.56 V)**, the reaction is **thermodynamically spontaneous** ($\Delta G < 0$). This confirms that formaldehyde *can* reduce Cu²⁺ in alkaline solution without external electrical energy.

**Step 5 — Calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(+0.56)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -1.081 \times 10^{5} \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -108.1 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The large negative $\Delta G^{\circ}$ confirms a highly spontaneous, exergonic process.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculating Plating Time for a Required Thickness

**Problem:** Calculate the time required to deposit a copper layer of **25 µm** thickness on a PCB of area **0.05 m²** (i.e., 500 cm²), using an electroless bath that delivers a **constant deposition rate of 10 µm h⁻¹**.

**Step 1 — Recall the thickness formula:**

$$t = \frac{R \times T}{1000} \quad \text{where } R = \text{rate in } \mu\text{m h}^{-1}, \;T = \text{time in hours}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to find T:**

$$T = \frac{t \times 1000}{R} = \frac{25 \times 1000}{10} = 2500 \text{ minutes of consideration...}$$

Wait — let us restate units clearly. The rate $R$ is already in µm h⁻¹, so:

$$T = \frac{\text{required thickness}}{\text{rate}} = \frac{25 \;\mu\text{m}}{10 \;\mu\text{m h}^{-1}} = 2.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minutes:**

$$T = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ minutes} = 2 \text{ hours } 30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Verify mass of Cu deposited using Faraday's Law:**

Mass per unit area: $m/A = t \times \rho = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} \times 8.96 \text{ g cm}^{-3} = 2.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g cm}^{-2}$

Total mass: $m = 2.24 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 = 1.12$ g of Cu

**Step 5 — Verify moles of Cu:**

$$n_{Cu} = \frac{1.12}{63.55} = 0.01763 \text{ mol}$$

This is the amount of copper that must be reduced; the bath must supply **at least 2 × this many moles of HCHO** (since 2 HCHO per Cu²⁺), i.e., **0.0353 mol HCHO** ≈ **0.86 mL of 37% formalin** (density ~1.09 g mL⁻¹, M(HCHO) = 30).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Activation Energy from Rate vs. Temperature Data

Suppose a study reports the following initial deposition rates $R$ at different temperatures for an electroless copper bath:

| Temperature (K) | Rate R (µm h⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 303 | 4.2 |
| 313 | 6.8 |
| 323 | 10.5 |

Using the **Arrhenius equation in its two-temperature form**:

$$\ln\left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Pick two data points (T₁ = 303 K, T₂ = 323 K, R₁ = 4.2, R₂ = 10.5):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{10.5}{4.2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{303}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the LHS and RHS components:**

LHS: $\ln(2.5) = 0.9163$

RHS bracket: $\frac{1}{323} - \frac{1}{303} = \frac{303 - 323}{323 \times 303} = \frac{-20}{97869} = -2.0437 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for E_a:**

$$0.9163 = -\frac{E_a}{8.314}\times(-2.0437\times 10^{-4})$$

$$0.9163 = \frac{E_a \times 2.0437 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314}$$

$$E_a = \frac{0.9163 \times 8.314}{2.0437 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{7.617}{2.0437 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 37278 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx 37.3 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This value is typical for a surface-chemical reaction (chemical-controlled, not diffusion-controlled), confirming that **the rate-limiting step is the surface catalytic reaction**, not mass transport.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electroless vs. Electroplating

| Parameter | Electroplating | Electroless Plating |
|---|---|---|
| External power source | **Required** (DC rectifier) | **Not required** |
| Coating uniformity on complex shapes | Poor (edge build-up) | **Excellent (conformal)** |
| Deposition rate | 25 – 50 µm h⁻¹ | 5 – 25 µm h⁻¹ |
| Equipment cost | High | Low |
| Throwing power | Low | **Very high** |
| Bath stability | Stable | Sensitive (auto-decomposition risk) |
| Selectivity | Conductive surfaces only | Catalytic surfaces only |
| Typical application | Decorative chrome, jewellery | PCB through-hole, EMI shielding |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Electroless Copper Plating of a PCB

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw PCB Substrate] --> B[Step 1: Mechanical Cleaning]
    B --> C[Step 2: Degreasing with Alkaline Cleaner]
    C --> D[Step 3: Etching and Surface Roughening]
    D --> E[Step 4: Sensitization using SnCl2 Solution]
    E --> F[Step 5: Activation using PdCl2 Solution]
    F --> G[Step 6: Acceleration / Reduction Step]
    G --> H[Step 7: Dip into Electroless Copper Bath]
    H --> I{Bath Parameters pH 11 to 13.5 Temp 25 to 70 C}
    I --> J[Step 8: Autocatalytic Cu Deposition]
    J --> K[Step 9: Rinsing in Deionized Water]
    K --> L[Step 10: Drying and Quality Inspection]
    L --> M[End: Copper Plated PCB Ready]

    subgraph "Bath Chemistry Loop"
        X1[CuSO4 Source] --> X3[Mixing Tank]
        X2[Formaldehyde HCHO] --> X3
        X4[EDTA Complexing Agent] --> X3
        X5[NaOH pH Adjuster] --> X3
        X3 --> X6[Continuous Filtration and Replenishment]
        X6 --> H
    end
```

### 4.2 Reaction Mechanism — Redox at the Catalytic Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cathode[Reduction Half Reaction on Cu Surface]
        A1[Cu2+ ion from bath] --> A2[Approaches catalytic surface]
        A2 --> A3[Accepts 2 electrons from surface]
        A3 --> A4[Deposits as Cu0 atom]
        A4 --> A5[Newly formed Cu becomes catalytic site]
    end

    subgraph Anode[Oxidation Half Reaction at Same Surface]
        B1[HCHO in alkaline solution] --> B2[Forms CH2OH O minus anion]
        B2 --> B3[Donates 2 electrons to surface]
        B3 --> B4[Forms HCOO minus formate ion]
        B4 --> B5[Releases H2O byproduct]
    end

    A5 --> Anode
    B3 --> A2
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Electro[Electroplating System]
        E1[DC Rectifier Power Supply] --> E2[Anode: Copper Metal]
        E2 --> E3[Electrolyte Bath]
        E3 --> E4[Cathode: Substrate to be Plated]
        E1 -.External current.-> E4
    end

    subgraph ElectroLess[Electroless Plating System]
        N1[Reducing Agent in Bath] --> N2[Electrolyte with Cu2+ Ions]
        N2 --> N3[Substrate Pre-coated with Pd0 Catalyst]
        N3 --> N4[Autocatalytic Deposition of Cu]
        N4 --> N5[Cu Layer is Itself Catalytic]
    end

    Electro --- X[Same End Result: Cu Coating] --- ElectroLess
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** When asked to "explain the process with a diagram" in a 7-mark question, you should sketch (i) the **process flow** (4.1), (ii) the **two half-reactions at the interface** (4.2), and (iii) write the **net ionic equation**. A 7-mark answer typically gets full credit only if all three are present.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
**"What is electroless plating? How does it differ from electroplating?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electroless plating is a non-galvanic method of depositing a metal coating on a substrate by means of a **chemical reducing agent** present in the plating solution, **without the use of external electrical energy**. The metal ion is reduced at a catalytically active surface, and the freshly deposited metal itself acts as a catalyst, allowing the process to continue autocatalytically.

**Differences:**

- **Power source:** Electroplating requires an external DC source; electroless plating does not.
- **Uniformity:** Electroless plating produces highly uniform coatings on complex geometries, whereas electroplating tends to over-plate edges and corners.
- **Mechanism:** Electroplating uses forced electron flow through an external circuit; electroless plating uses *in-situ* chemical reduction.

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks differences — total 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**
**"Why is formaldehyde (HCHO) used as the reducing agent in electroless copper plating rather than sodium hypophosphite?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Formaldehyde is the **preferred** reducing agent specifically for **copper** electroless plating because:

- It is a **strong reducing agent only in alkaline medium (pH > 11)**, which matches the operating window of the Cu bath, and it gives a **high standard cell EMF of +0.56 V**, providing a strong thermodynamic driving force.
- It selectively reduces **Cu²⁺** to Cu⁰ without contaminating the deposit with phosphorus or other foreign elements.
- **Sodium hypophosphite (NaH₂PO₂)**, although widely used for **nickel** electroless plating, gives a lower driving force with Cu²⁺ and would co-deposit **phosphorus** (forming Cu–P alloys of inferior conductivity). Hence it is unsuitable for high-purity copper deposits required in PCBs.

*(1.5 marks — reason 1 & 2; 1.5 marks — explanation of why hypophosphite fails for Cu; total 3 marks)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** A Module-2 question carries **14 marks** and almost always has an **internal choice** (i.e., you may answer **either** Q (A) **or** Q (B)). Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **process of electroless plating of copper on a PCB substrate**. Discuss the role of **(i) SnCl₂ sensitization**, **(ii) PdCl₂ activation**, and **(iii) EDTA complexing agent**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write the **anodic and cathodic half-reactions** involved in the electroless deposition of copper using formaldehyde. Hence deduce the **overall reaction** and compute the **standard cell EMF (E°cell)** and the **standard Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°)** for the same. Given: $E^{\circ}(\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}) = +0.34$ V; $E^{\circ}(\text{HCOO}^{-}/\text{HCHO},\text{OH}^{-}) = -0.22$ V. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Process Steps (3 marks):**
  1. **Mechanical cleaning** of the drilled PCB holes to remove dust and burrs.
  2. **Degreasing** in alkaline cleaner to remove oils.
  3. **Etching / conditioning** to micro-roughen the epoxy surface for better adhesion.
  4. **Sensitization (SnCl₂ step):** Substrate is dipped in acidic SnCl₂ solution. Sn²⁺ ions adsorb on the surface as a thin film.
  5. **Activation (PdCl₂ step):** Substrate is dipped in PdCl₂ solution. The adsorbed Sn²⁺ reduces Pd²⁺ to **Pd⁰ nuclei**:
     $$\text{Sn}^{2+} + \text{Pd}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{Sn}^{4+} + \text{Pd}^{0}$$
     These Pd⁰ sites act as **catalytic seeds** for Cu deposition.
  6. **Acceleration:** Removes excess tin and exposes Pd⁰.
  7. **Electroless copper bath dip** (pH 11–13.5, 40–60 °C) — Cu deposits autocatalytically on the Pd/Cu sites for the required duration.

- **Role of EDTA (1 mark):** EDTA acts as a **complexing/chelating agent** that binds free Cu²⁺ as [Cu(EDTA)]²⁻. This (i) prevents the precipitation of **Cu(OH)₂** in the alkaline bath, and (ii) controls the **release rate of free Cu²⁺**, ensuring a slow, uniform deposition.

- **Neat labelled diagram (3 marks):** A clean schematic showing the sequence of tanks (cleaner → etchant → SnCl₂ → PdCl₂ → accelerator → electroless Cu bath) and the labelling of input/output.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Cathodic half-reaction (reduction of Cu²⁺) (1 mark):**
  $$\text{Cu}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{0}_{(s)}, \quad E^{\circ}_{cathode} = +0.34 \text{ V}$$

- **Anodic half-reaction (oxidation of HCHO in alkaline medium) (1 mark):**
  $$\text{HCHO}_{(aq)} + 3\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)} \longrightarrow \text{HCOO}^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} + 2e^{-}, \quad E^{\circ}_{anode} = -0.22 \text{ V}$$

- **Overall reaction by adding the two (1 mark):**
  $$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{HCHO} + 4\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{0} + 2\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$$

- **Standard cell EMF calculation (2 marks):**
  $$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode} = (+0.34) - (-0.22) = +0.56 \text{ V}$$

- **Standard Gibbs free energy change (2 marks):**
  $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(+0.56) = -108.06 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

  Since $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$, the reaction is **spontaneous** and thermodynamically favourable.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the **composition and function** of each component of a typical **electroless copper plating bath**. Why is **alkaline pH essential** for this process? (7 marks)

**(b)** A printed circuit board of total conductive area **750 cm²** is to be plated with copper to a thickness of **20 µm** using an electroless bath. Calculate the **(i) mass** of copper deposited, **(ii) moles of Cu²⁺ consumed**, and **(iii) minimum volume of 37% formalin (HCHO) solution** required. Given: density of Cu = 8.96 g cm⁻³, M(Cu) = 63.55 g mol⁻¹, density of formalin ≈ 1.09 g mL⁻¹, M(HCHO) = 30 g mol⁻¹, formalin is 37% w/w HCHO. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Bath composition (5 marks — 1 mark per component):**

| Component | Example | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Copper salt | CuSO₄·5H₂O | Source of Cu²⁺ ions |
| Reducing agent | HCHO (formalin) | Supplies electrons for Cu²⁺ reduction |
| Complexing agent | EDTA / Rochelle salt | Prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation; controls free Cu²⁺ |
| Alkali | NaOH | Maintains pH 11–13.5; activates HCHO |
| Stabilizer | 2,2′-Bipyridyl, thiourea | Prevents homogeneous decomposition of bath |
| Surfactant | PEG, wetting agent | Improves wetting of substrate surface |

- **Why alkaline pH is essential (2 marks):**
  - Formaldehyde exhibits reducing power **only in alkaline medium**; in acidic/neutral pH, it is *not* a strong enough reductant to reduce Cu²⁺ (E°cell becomes negative).
  - At high pH, formaldehyde exists predominantly as the **methylene glycol anion (CH₂(OH)O⁻)**, which has a much lower oxidation potential.
  - The alkaline pH also activates the catalytic Pd surface and helps dissolve Cu(OH)₂ into the complexed form.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert thickness to cm:**
$$t = 20\;\mu\text{m} = 20 \times 10^{-4}\text{ cm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ cm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of copper deposited (2 marks):**

$$m = \rho \times V = \rho \times A \times t = 8.96 \times 750 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$m = 8.96 \times 1.5 = 13.44 \text{ g of Cu}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate moles of Cu²⁺ consumed (2 marks):**

$$n_{Cu} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{13.44}{63.55} = 0.2115 \text{ mol Cu}^{2+}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric moles of HCHO required (1 mark):**

From the overall reaction, **2 mol HCHO per 1 mol Cu²⁺**:

$$n_{HCHO} = 2 \times 0.2115 = 0.4230 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of pure HCHO (1 mark):**

$$m_{HCHO} = 0.4230 \times 30 = 12.69 \text{ g of pure HCHO}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of 37% formalin solution required (0.5 mark):**

$$m_{\text{formalin}} = \frac{12.69}{0.37} = 34.30 \text{ g}$$

**Step 7 — Volume of 37% formalin (0.5 mark):**

$$V_{\text{formalin}} = \frac{34.30}{1.09} = 31.47 \text{ mL} \approx 31.5 \text{ mL}$$

**Final Answer:** Mass of Cu = **13.44 g**, Moles of Cu²⁺ = **0.2115 mol**, Volume of formalin ≈ **31.5 mL**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):**
> - ❌ **Unit errors in thickness:** Many students write $t = 20$ cm instead of converting µm → cm. Always convert to **cm** for CGS-based mass/volume equations.
> - ❌ **Forgetting the stoichiometric factor of 2** between HCHO and Cu²⁺. Marks lost in step 4.
> - ❌ **Forgetting to convert % w/w formalin into pure HCHO** before dividing by density.
> - ❌ **Missing the role of Pd⁰** in their answers. KTU examiners specifically look for the Sn²⁺/Pd²⁺ reduction equation.
> - ❌ **Sign convention for ΔG°:** Always state clearly that $E_{cell} > 0$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$ → spontaneous.
> - ❌ **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** A neatly labelled flow chart earns you 1–2 *easy* marks.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electroless Plating of Copper (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Electroless plating = autocatalytic chemical reduction of metal ions *without* external current.

- **Standard Bath:** CuSO₄·5H₂O (source) + HCHO (reducing agent) + EDTA/Rochelle salt (complexing agent) + NaOH (pH > 11) + stabilizer + surfactant.

- **Two key pre-treatment steps:** **SnCl₂ sensitization** → **PdCl₂ activation** → leaves catalytic **Pd⁰** nuclei.

- **Cathodic reaction:** $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $E^{\circ} = +0.34$ V.

- **Anodic reaction:** $\text{HCHO} + 3\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O} + 2e^{-}$, $E^{\circ} = -0.22$ V (alkaline).

- **Overall reaction:** $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2\text{HCHO} + 4\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Cu} + 2\text{HCOO}^{-} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}$.

- **Standard cell EMF:** **+0.56 V** → highly spontaneous; $\Delta G^{\circ} = -108.06$ kJ mol⁻¹.

- **Why HCHO not NaH₂PO₂?** Because HCHO gives a pure Cu deposit; hypophosphite would co-deposit P (used for Ni, not Cu).

- **Why alkaline pH?** HCHO's reducing power is activated only in alkaline medium; otherwise $E_{cell} < 0$.

- **Role of EDTA:** (i) Prevents Cu(OH)₂ precipitation, (ii) controls free [Cu²⁺] for uniform deposition.

- **Key constants to memorize:** $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹, $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ g cm⁻³, $M_{Cu} = 63.55$ g mol⁻¹.

- **Industrial applications:** PCB through-hole plating, EMI/RFI shielding, decorative coatings, ULSI interconnects.

- **Key advantages over electroplating:** No external power, uniform thickness, excellent throwing power, works on non-conductive substrates.

- **Key disadvantages:** Slower deposition rate, sensitive bath chemistry, risk of bath decomposition (need stabilizers).

- **Quick numerical mnemonic:** For plating time, **T (h) = thickness (µm) ÷ rate (µm h⁻¹)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Spectra - Molecular Energy Levels - Beer Lambert's Law - Numerical Problems - Electronic Spectroscopy - Principle
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Spectroscopy?

**Spectroscopy** is the branch of physical chemistry that deals with the interaction of electromagnetic radiation (EMR) with matter. When a molecule absorbs or emits light of a specific energy, it undergoes a transition between quantized energy levels. By analyzing the wavelengths of light absorbed or emitted, we can deduce structural and electronic information about the molecule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GCCYT122 - Module 3):** Spectroscopy is foundational to analytical chemistry. For KTU 2024, you must master (i) the classification of spectra, (ii) the energy-level hierarchy of molecules, (iii) Beer–Lambert's law with numerical problem-solving, and (iv) the principles of electronic (UV-Vis) spectroscopy.

## 1.2 Types of Spectra — A Formal Classification

A **spectrum** is a plot of intensity of absorbed or emitted radiation as a function of wavelength (λ), frequency (ν), or wavenumber (ν̄).

### 1.2.1 Based on the Source / Mode of Interaction

| Spectrum Type | Definition | When Observed |
|---|---|---|
| **Emission Spectrum** | Produced when an excited species *releases* photons and returns to a lower energy state | Atoms in flame, discharge tubes, excited gases |
| **Absorption Spectrum** | Produced when a species *absorbs* specific photons from a continuous source | Molecules/atoms placed between source and detector |
| **Fluorescence / Phosphorescence Spectrum** | Photons emitted at a *longer wavelength* after absorption (Stokes-shifted) | Fluorescent dyes, phosphors |

### 1.2.2 Based on the Appearance of the Spectrum

| Spectrum Type | Appearance | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Spectrum** | Unbroken band of all wavelengths | Hot, dense bodies (e.g., tungsten lamp, sun's core) |
| **Line Spectrum** | Sharp, discrete lines on a dark background | Excited isolated atoms (e.g., hydrogen Balmer series) |
| **Band Spectrum** | Closely spaced lines forming a *band* with sharp head | Excited molecules (rotational-vibrational structure) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase" Model:** Think of a molecule as a building with floors (energy levels). **Continuous radiation** is like a steady stream of rain falling on the building. The molecule (building) **absorbs** only those raindrops (photons) whose energy exactly matches the height between two specific floors. The transmitted light carries "gaps" — these gaps form the **absorption spectrum**. The light re-emitted when the molecule "jumps down" forms the **emission spectrum**.

## 1.3 Molecular Energy Levels — The Hierarchy

Unlike atoms, molecules have *multiple* types of energy because they possess more degrees of freedom. The total molecular energy is the sum of four independent contributions:

$$E_{\text{molecular}} = E_{\text{electronic}} + E_{\text{vibrational}} + E_{\text{rotational}} + E_{\text{translational}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Energy Hierarchy (from largest to smallest spacing):**
> - **Electronic energy (Eₑ):** ~1–10 eV (UV-Vis region, λ ≈ 200–800 nm)
> - **Vibrational energy (Eᵥ):** ~0.05–0.5 eV (IR region, λ ≈ 1–25 µm)
> - **Rotational energy (Eᵣ):** ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ eV (Microwave region, λ > 100 µm)
> - **Translational energy (Eₜ):** Quasi-continuous; does not produce a spectrum

| Energy Type | Quantum Number | Typical Spectral Region | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electronic | n (principal) | UV & Visible | ~10¹⁵ Hz |
| Vibrational | v (0, 1, 2, …) | Infrared (IR) | ~10¹³ Hz |
| Rotational | J (0, 1, 2, …) | Microwave | ~10¹⁰ Hz |
| Translational | — | — | Continuous |

## 1.4 Beer–Lambert's Law — The Workhorse of Quantitative Analysis

**Beer–Lambert's Law (also called Beer's Law)** is the mathematical foundation of all absorption-based quantitative analysis in UV-Vis spectroscopy.

**Formal Statement:** *The absorbance of a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of the light through the solution.*

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

where:
- **A** = Absorbance (also called optical density, O.D.) — dimensionless
- **ε** = Molar absorptivity (or molar extinction coefficient) — units: **L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹** or **M⁻¹ cm⁻¹**
- **c** = Concentration of the absorbing species — units: **mol L⁻¹** (M)
- **l** = Optical path length — units: **cm**

The relationship between absorbance (**A**) and transmittance (**T**) is:

$$A = -\log_{10} T = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0} \quad \text{and} \quad \%T = 100 \times T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Frosted Glass" Model:** Imagine a beam of light passing through clear water (I₀ is the full intensity). Now drop one drop of ink — the light dims slightly. Drop ten drops — it dims ten times more. Drop a hundred — the light barely gets through. The absorbance is like the *darkness* of the glass, and the concentration is the *number of ink drops*. This direct proportionality is exactly what Beer–Lambert's Law captures.

## 1.5 Electronic Spectroscopy — The Underlying Principle

**Electronic (UV-Vis) spectroscopy** studies the absorption of ultraviolet (200–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) radiation by molecules, leading to transitions of electrons (typically π, n, or σ electrons) from the **ground state (HOMO)** to an **excited state (LUMO)**.

The energy of the absorbed photon must match the energy gap:

$$\Delta E_{electronic} = E_{excited} - E_{ground} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Three things examiners ALWAYS test:**
> 1. The hierarchy of molecular energy levels.
> 2. Beer–Lambert's law derivations and numericals.
> 3. Types of electronic transitions (σ → σ*, n → π*, π → π*, n → σ*) and selection rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Spectra

### 2.1.1 Continuous Spectrum
A body heated to high temperature (e.g., the filament of a tungsten lamp) emits **all wavelengths** of radiation because its energy levels are not quantized at the macroscopic scale. It serves as the **light source** in absorption spectrophotometers.

### 2.1.2 Line Spectrum
Atoms (e.g., hydrogen, sodium, mercury) possess **discrete, sharply defined** energy levels. When excited, they emit photons of *exact* energies corresponding to transitions between these levels. The result is **sharp lines** at characteristic wavelengths.
- Example: Na-D line at **589.0 nm and 589.6 nm** (yellow doublet).
- Example: H-Balmer series (visible region): Hα at 656.3 nm (red), Hβ at 486.1 nm (blue-green).

### 2.1.3 Band Spectrum
Molecules have a vast number of closely spaced rotational and vibrational sub-levels superimposed on each electronic state. Every electronic transition is therefore accompanied by **many simultaneous vibrational and rotational transitions**, producing a *band* that appears continuous at low resolution but shows fine structure at high resolution.

## 2.2 Molecular Energy Levels — Why the Hierarchy Matters

### 2.2.1 Translational Energy
- Free motion of the molecule as a whole through space.
- Energy is **not quantized** to any meaningful extent (the "particle in a box" levels are too closely spaced to resolve).
- Does **not** produce a spectrum — the spectrum is a continuous background.

### 2.2.2 Rotational Energy (Microwave Spectroscopy)
- Rotation of the molecule about its center of mass.
- Allowed only for molecules with a **permanent dipole moment** (heteronuclear diatomics like HCl, NO, CO).
- Selection rule: **ΔJ = ±1**
- Energy: $E_J = B J(J+1)$, where B is the rotational constant.

### 2.2.3 Vibrational Energy (IR Spectroscopy)
- Periodic stretching and bending of bonds.
- Selection rule: **Δv = ±1** (harmonic approximation); anharmonicity allows Δv = ±2, ±3 (overtones).
- **IR active** only if the vibration causes a change in dipole moment.
- Energy: $E_v = h\nu_0 \left(v + \frac{1}{2}\right)$ for a harmonic oscillator.

### 2.2.4 Electronic Energy (UV-Vis Spectroscopy)
- Promotion of an electron from a bonding or non-bonding orbital to an anti-bonding orbital.
- Selection rule: **ΔS = 0** (spin must be conserved — singlet → singlet, triplet → triplet).
- The Laporte selection rule applies to centrosymmetric molecules: **g ↔ u allowed; g ↔ g, u ↔ u forbidden** (relaxed in practice via vibronic coupling).
- Energy: $\Delta E_e \sim 1$–$10$ eV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Beer–Lambert's Law fail at high concentrations?** Beyond ~0.01 M, solute–solute interactions, refractive index changes, and aggregation cause deviations. The "linear regime" (where the law holds) is typically c < 0.01 M for most systems.

## 2.3 Beer–Lambert's Law — Full Mathematical Framework

### 2.3.1 Derivation Logic (Why does it work?)

Consider a thin slice of absorbing solution of thickness **dx**. The fraction of light *absorbed* in that slice is proportional to the **number of absorbing molecules** encountered, which is proportional to **c·dx**:

$$-\frac{dI}{I} = k' \, c \, dx$$

Integrate from x = 0 (where I = I₀) to x = l (where I = I):

$$-\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = k' c \int_0^l dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k' c l$$

Converting natural log to base-10 log (using 2.303):

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{k'}{2.303} \, c \, l = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

Therefore: $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$

### 2.3.2 Why Absorbance is Additive

If a solution contains *n* non-interacting absorbing species, the total absorbance is the **sum** of individual absorbances at the chosen wavelength:

$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i \, c_i \, l$$

This additivity is the basis of **multi-component analysis** by UV-Vis spectroscopy.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — Every formula here is examinable.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | Beer–Lambert's law | A dimensionless, ε in **L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹** |
| 2 | $A = \log_{10}(I_0/I) = -\log_{10} T$ | Absorbance definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T = I/I_0$ | Transmittance (fraction) | 0 ≤ T ≤ 1 |
| 4 | $\%T = 100 \times T$ | Percent transmittance | 0 to 100 % |
| 5 | $A_1/A_2 = c_1/c_2$ (same l, same ε) | Two-point proportionality | Used in dilution problems |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = A/(c \cdot l)$ | Calculate molar absorptivity | For unknown samples |
| 7 | $c = A/(\varepsilon \cdot l)$ | Calculate unknown concentration | Most common numerical type |
| 8 | $\Delta E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy | h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** |
| 9 | $E_{molecule} = E_e + E_v + E_r + E_t$ | Total molecular energy | Hierarchy: Eₑ ≫ Eᵥ ≫ Eᵣ ≫ Eₜ |
| 10 | $E_v = h\nu_0(v + 1/2)$ | Vibrational energy (harmonic) | v = 0, 1, 2, … |
| 11 | $E_J = BJ(J+1)$ | Rotational energy (rigid rotor) | J = 0, 1, 2, … |
| 12 | $\nu = c/\lambda = c \cdot \tilde{\nu}$ | Wave-number relationship | $\tilde{\nu}$ in **cm⁻¹** |
| 13 | $A_{mix} = \varepsilon_1 c_1 l + \varepsilon_2 c_2 l$ | Two-component mixture | Simultaneous equations |
| 14 | Limit of detection | $c_{min} \approx 0.001/\varepsilon$ (for A = 0.001, l = 1) | Practical sensitivity |

### 2.4.1 Critical Constants to Memorize

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant (h) | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴** | J·s |
| Speed of light (c) | **2.998 × 10⁸** | m·s⁻¹ (often written as **3.0 × 10⁸**) |
| Avogadro's number (Nₐ) | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| 1 eV | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | J |
| Conversion | $\tilde{\nu}(\text{cm}^{-1}) = 1/\lambda(\text{cm}) = 10^7/\lambda(\text{nm})$ | — |

## 2.5 Electronic Spectroscopy — The Underlying Principle

### 2.5.1 Why Electronic Transitions?

When a molecule absorbs a UV or visible photon, an **electron** in a lower-energy orbital is promoted to a higher-energy orbital. The energy gap ΔE = E_LUMO − E_HOMO determines the wavelength of maximum absorption (λ_max).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Staircase-Climbing" Model:** Electrons sit on the *lowest available step* (HOMO). When a photon strikes with enough energy, the electron jumps to a *higher step* (LUMO). The energy of the photon must match the *exact height* of the step. If it is too low → no jump. If too high → no jump (the photon is wasted, transmitted through).

### 2.5.2 The Four Fundamental Electronic Transitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — KTU asks this almost every year.**

| Transition | Type of Electron | Typical λ_max (nm) | Intensity (ε) | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| σ → σ* | Alkane C–C, C–H | < 150 (far UV) | ~10³ | CH₄, ethane |
| n → σ* | Saturated with lone pairs (O, N, S, halogens) | 150–250 | 100–3000 | Alcohols, alkyl halides |
| π → π* | Unsaturated (C=C, C=O) | 170–250 (isolated) | 10⁴–10⁵ (strong) | Ethylene, benzene (255 nm) |
| n → π* | C=O, N=O, C=N with lone pairs | 270–350 | 10–100 (weak) | Acetone (280 nm) |

### 2.5.3 Chromophores and Auxochromes

- **Chromophore:** The functional group responsible for the electronic absorption (e.g., C=C, C=O, –N=N–).
- **Auxochrome:** A substituent that *modifies* the λ_max and intensity of the chromophore (e.g., –OH, –NH₂, –Cl). Auxochromes usually cause a **red shift (bathochromic shift)** or **blue shift (hypsochromic shift)**.

| Effect | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Bathochromic shift (red shift)** | λ_max shifts to *longer* wavelength (lower energy) | Phenol (270 nm) vs. Benzene (255 nm) |
| **Hypsochromic shift (blue shift)** | λ_max shifts to *shorter* wavelength (higher energy) | Aniline in acid (decreased λ) |
| **Hyperchromic effect** | Increase in ε (intensity) | Conjugation extension |
| **Hypochromic effect** | Decrease in ε (intensity) | Aggregation of dye molecules |

### 2.5.4 Selection Rules for Electronic Transitions

1. **Spin Selection Rule:** $\Delta S = 0$ — the spin multiplicity must not change during the transition (singlet → singlet allowed; singlet → triplet forbidden but weakly observed via intersystem crossing).
2. **Laporte Selection Rule (for centrosymmetric molecules):** Transitions between states of the same parity (g→g, u→u) are forbidden; only g↔u are allowed.
3. **Orbital Symmetry Rule:** The transition dipole moment integral $\langle \psi_i \vert \hat{\mu} \vert \psi_f \rangle \neq 0$ — there must be a non-zero overlap of the initial and final wavefunctions with the dipole operator.

### 2.5.5 Instrumentation (Block Diagram Concept)

A typical **UV-Vis Spectrophotometer** has the following components in series:

**Light Source → Monochromator → Sample Compartment (Cuvette) → Detector → Amplifier → Recorder/Display**

| Component | Common Examples |
|---|---|
| Source (UV) | Deuterium lamp (160–375 nm) |
| Source (Visible) | Tungsten-halogen lamp (350–2500 nm) |
| Monochromator | Prism or grating + slits |
| Cuvette | Quartz (UV) or glass (visible); l = 1 cm standard |
| Detector | Photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Beer–Lambert's Law

Let an incident monochromatic beam of intensity **I₀** pass through a solution of thickness **l** containing an absorbing species of concentration **c**.

**Step 1 — Define the differential absorption in an infinitesimal slice.**

Consider a slice of thickness **dx** at depth **x**. The decrease in intensity **dI** is proportional to:
- the incident intensity **I(x)** at that slice,
- the concentration **c** of the absorbing species,
- the slice thickness **dx**.

$$-dI \propto I \cdot c \cdot dx$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constant.**

We introduce a constant of proportionality **k'** (depends on wavelength and the absorbing species):

$$-dI = k' \, I \, c \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k' \, c \, dx$$

Integrate both sides. On the left, the limits are from I₀ (at x = 0) to I (at x = l). On the right, the limits are from 0 to l:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -k' \, c \int_{0}^{l} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both integrals.**

The left side is a standard logarithmic integral:

$$\left[\ln I\right]_{I_0}^{I} = \ln I - \ln I_0 = \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

The right side is trivial:

$$-k' \, c \cdot l$$

**Step 5 — Combine the results.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k' \, c \, l$$

Multiply both sides by **−1**:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k' \, c \, l$$

**Step 6 — Convert from natural log to base-10 log.**

Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{k'}{2.303} \, c \, l$$

**Step 7 — Identify the physical quantities.**

Define:
- $\log_{10}(I_0/I) \equiv A$ (Absorbance)
- $k'/(2.303) \equiv \varepsilon$ (Molar absorptivity)

This gives the famous result:

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \, c \, l}$$

**Step 8 — Verify units.**

$$[\varepsilon] = \frac{[A]}{[c][l]} = \frac{\text{dimensionless}}{(\text{mol L}^{-1})(\text{cm})} = \text{L mol}^{-1}\text{ cm}^{-1} = \text{M}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$$

Units check: ✓

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Standard Beer–Lambert Calculation

**Problem:** A solution of a transition-metal complex has a concentration of **5.0 × 10⁻⁴ M**. The molar absorptivity at 450 nm is **ε = 2.0 × 10³ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**. The cuvette path length is **l = 1.0 cm**.
Calculate (a) the absorbance, (b) the transmittance (T), and (c) the percent transmittance (%T).

**Solution:**

**(a) Absorbance:**

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, l = (2.0 \times 10^3) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.0)$$

$$A = 2.0 \times 5.0 \times 10^{3-4} = 10 \times 10^{-1} = 1.0$$

$$\boxed{A = 1.0}$$

**(b) Transmittance:**

$$A = -\log_{10} T \implies T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-1.0} = 0.1$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.10}$$

**(c) Percent Transmittance:**

$$\%T = 100 \times T = 100 \times 0.10 = 10\%$$

$$\boxed{\%T = 10\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The solution absorbs 90% of the incident light at 450 nm.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Unknown Concentration

**Problem:** The absorbance of a CuSO₄ solution at 635 nm was measured as **A = 0.450** using a **1.0 cm** cuvette. The molar absorptivity at this wavelength is **ε = 50 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**. Calculate the concentration of the Cu²⁺ solution.

**Solution:**

Rearrange Beer–Lambert's law for concentration:

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, l} = \frac{0.450}{50 \times 1.0} = \frac{0.450}{50}$$

$$\boxed{c = 9.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M} = 9.0 \text{ mM}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Multi-Component (Two Components) Mixture

**Problem:** A solution contains two absorbing species **X** and **Y**. At λ₁ = 400 nm, the molar absorptivities are ε_X = 200 and ε_Y = 50 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹. At λ₂ = 500 nm, ε_X = 80 and ε_Y = 400 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹. The measured absorbances (l = 1 cm) are:
- A(400 nm) = 0.500
- A(500 nm) = 0.840

Find the concentrations of X and Y.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write Beer–Lambert's law for each wavelength.**

$$A_{400} = \varepsilon_X^{400} c_X l + \varepsilon_Y^{400} c_Y l$$

$$0.500 = 200 \, c_X + 50 \, c_Y \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$A_{500} = \varepsilon_X^{500} c_X l + \varepsilon_Y^{500} c_Y l$$

$$0.840 = 80 \, c_X + 400 \, c_Y \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the simultaneous equations.**

Multiply (i) by 8 to align the c_X terms with (ii):

$$4.000 = 1600 \, c_X + 400 \, c_Y \quad \text{...(i×8)}$$

Subtract (ii) from (i×8):

$$4.000 - 0.840 = 1600 \, c_X - 80 \, c_X + 400 \, c_Y - 400 \, c_Y$$

$$3.160 = 1520 \, c_X$$

$$c_X = \frac{3.160}{1520} = 2.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

Substitute c_X back into (i):

$$0.500 = 200 \times (2.08 \times 10^{-3}) + 50 \, c_Y$$

$$0.500 = 0.416 + 50 \, c_Y$$

$$50 \, c_Y = 0.084$$

$$c_Y = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{c_X = 2.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}, \quad c_Y = 1.68 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 4 — Photon Energy & Wavelength

**Problem:** A molecule undergoes a π → π* transition when it absorbs light of wavelength **λ = 254 nm**. Calculate (a) the frequency, (b) the wavenumber, and (c) the energy of this photon in eV.

**Given:** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s, 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

**Solution:**

**(a) Frequency:**

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{254 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$\nu = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{2.54 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.181 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 1.181 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}}$$

**(b) Wavenumber:**

$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{254 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}} = \frac{1}{2.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}}$$

$$\tilde{\nu} = 3.937 \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^{-1} \approx 39370 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{\nu} = 39370 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$

**(c) Energy in eV:**

$$E = h\nu = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (1.181 \times 10^{15})$$

$$E = 7.83 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Converting to eV:

$$E = \frac{7.83 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV}} = 4.89 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{E = 4.89 \text{ eV}}$$

**Interpretation:** This photon energy (~4.89 eV) lies in the UV region and is sufficient to promote a π electron to π* in a conjugated system (e.g., benzene absorbs at 254 nm with E ≈ 4.9 eV).

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 5 — Dilution & Proportionality

**Problem:** A stock solution of KMnO₄ has absorbance A₁ = 1.20 at 525 nm. It is diluted 1:4 (one part stock + three parts water). What is the new absorbance, assuming Beer–Lambert's law is obeyed?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine the dilution factor.**

1 part stock + 3 parts water → total 4 parts. So c₂ = c₁/4.

**Step 2 — Apply the proportionality (ε and l constant).**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{c_2}{c_1} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{A_1}{4} = \frac{1.20}{4} = 0.30$$

$$\boxed{A_2 = 0.30}$$

**Sanity check:** Transmittance changes from T₁ = 10⁻¹·² = 6.3% to T₂ = 10⁻⁰·³ = 50% — consistent with a 4× dilution of a strongly absorbing solution.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Distinguish between line spectrum, band spectrum, and continuous spectrum. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Spectrum Type | Appearance | Origin | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Line Spectrum** | Sharp, isolated lines | Discrete electronic transitions in *isolated atoms* | Sodium D-line at 589 nm; H-Balmer series |
| **Band Spectrum** | Closely packed lines forming bands, often with a sharp edge | Electronic transitions in *molecules* accompanied by vibrational/rotational substructure | Cyanogen (CN) band spectrum in comets; I₂ vapor |
| **Continuous Spectrum** | Unbroken distribution of all wavelengths | Emission from a hot, dense body with quasi-continuous energy levels | Tungsten filament, sun's photosphere, black-body radiation |

**Marking Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition of each (≈1.5 lines each) + 0.5 mark each for example + 0.5 for proper distinction.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: State Beer–Lambert's law. What are its limitations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** *When a beam of monochromatic light passes through a homogeneous absorbing medium, the absorbance of the medium is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of the light through the medium.*

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

**Limitations (any three, 1 mark each):**

1. **Valid only for dilute solutions (typically c < 0.01 M):** At high concentrations, solute–solute interactions alter ε.
2. **Valid only for monochromatic radiation:** Polychromatic light causes deviation.
3. **Applies to single, non-interacting species:** Scattering, fluorescence, and chemical reactions cause deviations.
4. **Stray light** in the instrument reduces accuracy at very high absorbances (A > 2).
5. **pH, temperature, and solvent** can shift λ_max and change ε.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** In Part B, every module question carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. Most 14-mark questions are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the various types of molecular energy levels. Explain how the spacing between energy levels determines the type of spectroscopy used to probe each.

**(b) [7 marks]** A solution shows an absorbance of **0.624** at λ = 480 nm in a 1.0 cm cuvette. The molar absorptivity is **ε = 4650 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**. Calculate:
- (i) The concentration of the solution.
- (ii) The percent transmittance.
- (iii) The frequency and energy (in eV) of the absorbed photon.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Molecular Energy Levels [7 marks]**

The total energy of a molecule is the sum of four independent contributions:

$$E_{molecule} = E_{electronic} + E_{vibrational} + E_{rotational} + E_{translational}$$

| Energy Type | Cause | Order of Magnitude | Spectral Region | Spectroscopic Technique |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electronic (Eₑ)** | Promotion of e⁻ to higher orbital | 1–10 eV | UV-Vis (200–800 nm) | UV-Vis spectroscopy |
| **Vibrational (Eᵥ)** | Bond stretching/bending | 0.05–0.5 eV | IR (1–25 µm) | Infrared spectroscopy |
| **Rotational (Eᵣ)** | Rotation about COM | 10⁻⁴–10⁻³ eV | Microwave (>100 µm) | Microwave spectroscopy |
| **Translational (Eₜ)** | Free motion through space | Quasi-continuous | — | No spectrum (continuous) |

**Key points to mention for full marks (KTU):**
1. Each level has finer sub-structure — every electronic state contains many vibrational states, each containing many rotational states. (1 mark)
2. Selection rule for vibration: Δv = ±1 (anharmonicity permits overtones). (1 mark)
3. Selection rule for rotation: ΔJ = ±1, applicable only to molecules with permanent dipole moment. (1 mark)
4. Pure rotational spectra occur in the **microwave** region (10⁹–10¹¹ Hz). (1 mark)
5. Vibrational spectra appear in the **IR** region (10¹²–10¹⁴ Hz). (1 mark)
6. Electronic transitions span the **UV-Vis** region (10¹⁴–10¹⁵ Hz). (1 mark)
7. Translational energy is essentially continuous and does not contribute to discrete spectral lines. (1 mark)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:** A = 0.624, ε = 4650 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹, l = 1.0 cm, λ = 480 nm.
Constants: h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s, 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

**(i) Concentration [2 marks]:**

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \, l} = \frac{0.624}{4650 \times 1.0} = 1.342 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}$$

$$\boxed{c = 1.342 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M} = 1.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ M}}$$

**(ii) Percent Transmittance [2 marks]:**

$$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-0.624} = 0.2377$$

$$\%T = 100 \times 0.2377 = 23.77\%$$

$$\boxed{\%T = 23.77\%}$$

**(iii) Frequency and Energy [3 marks]:**

**Frequency:**

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{480 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$\nu = 6.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$$

**Energy in Joules:**

$$E = h\nu = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (6.25 \times 10^{14})$$

$$E = 4.141 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Energy in eV:**

$$E = \frac{4.141 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.585 \text{ eV}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 6.25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}, \quad E = 2.585 \text{ eV}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** This photon energy (~2.6 eV) corresponds to an **n → π\*** or extended π → π* transition in the visible region (480 nm = blue), giving the solution a complementary color (likely yellow-orange).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** State Beer–Lambert's law. Derive it mathematically. List any three conditions for its validity.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the principle of UV-Vis (electronic) spectroscopy. With a neat energy-level diagram, describe the four fundamental electronic transitions (σ → σ\*, n → σ\*, π → π\*, n → π\*) and arrange them in order of increasing energy and wavelength.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Beer–Lambert's Law Derivation [7 marks]**

**Statement [1 mark]:** *The absorbance of a monochromatic beam in a homogeneous absorbing medium is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and to the path length of the radiation through the medium.*

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

**Derivation [4 marks]:** *(The student should reproduce the integration shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

Consider a thin slice dx at depth x, with intensity I(x). The decrease in intensity is:

$$-dI \propto I \, c \, dx \implies -dI = k' \, I \, c \, dx$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to l (intensity changes from I₀ to I):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -k' \, c \int_0^l dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k' \, c \, l$$

Converting to log₁₀ (multiply by 1/2.303):

$$2.303 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k' \, c \, l$$

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{k'}{2.303} \, c \, l = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

Therefore: $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$

**Conditions for Validity [2 marks — any 3 expected]:**

1. **Monochromatic radiation** must be used.
2. **Low concentrations** (typically c < 0.01 M) to avoid solute–solute interactions.
3. **Homogeneous solution** with no turbidity, scattering, or fluorescence.
4. The absorbing species must **not undergo association, dissociation, or reaction** with the solvent.
5. **No stray light** reaching the detector.

---

**Part (b) — Principle of UV-Vis Spectroscopy [7 marks]**

**Principle [2 marks]:** When a molecule absorbs UV or visible radiation (λ = 200–800 nm), an electron in a lower-energy orbital (HOMO) is promoted to a higher-energy orbital (LUMO). The energy of the absorbed photon must exactly match the energy gap:

$$\Delta E = E_{LUMO} - E_{HOMO} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Energy-level diagram (textual representation) [2 marks]:**

```
HOMO  (π*)        ←————————  π → π* transition (high ε)
LUMO  (π*)        ←————————  n → π* transition (low ε)
HOMO  (π)         ←————————  n → σ* transition
LUMO  (σ*)        ←————————  σ → σ* transition (very high energy)
HOMO  (n, σ)      ←————————  Ground state
```

**The Four Transitions (Table) [3 marks]:**

| Transition | Type of Electron | λ_max (nm) | Intensity (ε, L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹) | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **σ → σ\*** | σ-bonding e⁻ | < 150 | ~10³ (moderate) | Alkanes — CH₄, C₂H₆ |
| **n → σ\*** | Lone-pair e⁻ (O, N, S, X) | 150–250 | 100–3000 (moderate) | H₂O, alcohols, alkyl halides |
| **π → π\*** | π-bonding e⁻ | 170–250 (isolated) | 10⁴–10⁵ (very strong) | Alkenes, alkynes, arenes |
| **n → π\*** | Lone-pair on C=O, N=O | 270–350 | 10–100 (weak, "forbidden" character) | Aldehydes, ketones, nitroso |

**Order of Increasing Energy (and Decreasing Wavelength):**

$$\boxed{\text{n} \to \pi^* < \pi \to \pi^* < \text{n} \to \sigma^* < \sigma \to \sigma^*}$$

(So the order of increasing **wavelength** is the reverse: σ → σ* < n → σ* < π → π* < n → π*.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ε units:** Writing "cm²/mol" or omitting the unit L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹. KTU strictly expects the standard form. **Lose 0.5 mark** if units are missing.
> 2. **Using A = εcl incorrectly:** Some students write A = εc/l or A = εl/c. Double-check the numerator/denominator.
> 3. **Mixing up the four transitions:** σ → σ* and n → σ* involve σ framework electrons; π → π* and n → π* involve π systems. Don't interchange n and σ.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert nm → m in E = hc/λ calculations:** A = 1 mark deducted in numericals.
> 5. **Sign of absorbance:** A is always positive; if you get a negative A, recheck whether you used I₀/I or I/I₀.
> 6. **Deviations from Beer–Lambert's law:** State at least 2–3 reasons for deviations to score full marks in theory questions.
> 7. **Spin selection rule:** "Singlet → triplet transitions are forbidden" is a must-mention in selection-rule questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

### 🔬 Types of Spectra
- **Emission** vs **Absorption** — same physics, opposite direction.
- **Line spectrum** = atoms (sharp, discrete).
- **Band spectrum** = molecules (close-packed lines, sharp edge).
- **Continuous spectrum** = hot dense bodies (black-body).
- Continuous sources are used in **absorption** instruments; line emitters are used in **emission** instruments.

### ⚛️ Molecular Energy Levels
- Hierarchy: **Eₑ ≫ Eᵥ ≫ Eᵣ ≫ Eₜ**.
- Eₜ is continuous → no spectrum.
- IR (vibrational) requires a **change in dipole moment**.
- Microwave (rotational) requires a **permanent dipole moment**.
- UV-Vis (electronic): electrons jump from HOMO → LUMO.

### 📊 Beer–Lambert's Law
- **$A = \varepsilon c l$** — memorize, derive, apply.
- A is dimensionless; ε in **L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**; c in **mol L⁻¹**; l in **cm**.
- $A = \log(I_0/I)$; $T = I/I_0$; $A = -\log T$.
- **Additivity:** $A_{total} = \Sigma \varepsilon_i c_i l$ — used in multi-component analysis.
- **Valid only for dilute solutions, monochromatic light, homogeneous media.**

### 🌈 Electronic Spectroscopy
- **Four transitions (in order of increasing E):** n → π* < π → π* < n → σ* < σ → σ*.
- **Bathochromic shift (red shift)** = λ_max ↑ ; **Hypsochromic (blue shift)** = λ_max ↓.
- **Hyperchromic** = ε ↑ ; **Hypochromic** = ε ↓.
- **Chromophore** = light-absorbing group; **Auxochrome** = modifying group.
- **Selection rules:** ΔS = 0; Laporte (g↔u) for centrosymmetric molecules.

### 🧮 Critical Numerical Conversions
- λ(nm) → λ(m): multiply by 10⁻⁹.
- Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) = 10⁷ / λ(nm).
- 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.
- A = 1.0 → T = 10% (general: %T = 10⁻ᴬ × 100).
- Transmittance to absorbance: A = −log(T).

### 🧪 Practical Tips for KTU
- In numericals, always show **units explicitly** — KTU examiners reward this.
- For "compare" questions, use a **table** for clean presentation.
- For derivation questions, **draw a thin-slice diagram** (in your answer sheet) showing the differential element dx and the light path.
- For multi-component mixtures, set up **simultaneous equations** with Beer–Lambert applied at two different wavelengths.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electronic Spectroscopy — Foundational Definition

**Electronic Spectroscopy** (UV-Visible Spectroscopy) is the branch of molecular spectroscopy that deals with the interaction of ultraviolet (200–400 nm) and visible (400–800 nm) electromagnetic radiation with matter, resulting in the excitation of valence electrons from the **ground state** (HOMO — Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital) to the **excited state** (LUMO — Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The energy gap ($\Delta E$) between HOMO and LUMO determines the wavelength of absorption. According to the Planck-Einstein relation, $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. A smaller energy gap means longer wavelength absorption (bathochromic shift), and a larger gap means shorter wavelength absorption (hypsochromic shift).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Staircase Electron"

Think of a molecule as a multi-storey building, and an electron as a person sitting on the ground floor (ground state). When light (energy packet — photon) strikes the building, the electron absorbs this energy and jumps to a higher floor (excited state). Just as a person can only step on specific floors (allowed energy levels), the electron can only jump to specific permitted orbitals. The **type of floor** the electron lands on depends on the **type of orbital it started from** — this is the basis of classifying electronic transitions.

## 1.2 Types of Electronic Transitions

There are **four principal types** of electronic transitions observed in organic molecules, all involving the excitation of electrons from a bonding or non-bonding orbital to an antibonding orbital.

| Transition Type | Notation | Energy Required | Wavelength Range (nm) | Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sigma to Sigma-star | $\sigma \to \sigma^*$ | Very High | < 150 (Far UV) | Very Strong |
| n to Sigma-star | $n \to \sigma^*$ | High | 150–250 (Near UV) | Weak to Moderate |
| Pi to Pi-star | $\pi \to \pi^*$ | Moderate | 200–400 (Near UV) | Very Strong |
| n to Pi-star | $n \to \pi^*$ | Low | 300–700 (UV-Visible) | Weak |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic:** "**S**igma transitions are **S**trongest energy; **n→π\* transitions are the weakest**." Conjugation always involves $\pi \to \pi^*$ transitions.

### 1.2.1 Detailed Description of Each Transition

**(a) $\sigma \to \sigma^*$ Transition**
- Occurs in saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) like methane, ethane.
- Requires high energy because $\sigma$ bonds are tightly held.
- Example: Methane absorbs at **125 nm**.

**(b) $n \to \sigma^*$ Transition**
- Occurs in saturated compounds containing lone pairs (alcohols, amines, halides).
- The non-bonding ($n$) electron gets promoted to the antibonding $\sigma^*$ orbital.
- Example: Methanol absorbs at **183 nm**; ethyl chloride at **170 nm**.

**(c) $\pi \to \pi^*$ Transition**
- Occurs in unsaturated compounds (alkenes, alkynes, aromatics).
- Moderate energy required.
- Example: Ethylene absorbs at **165 nm**; benzene at **255 nm**.

**(d) $n \to \pi^*$ Transition**
- Occurs in compounds with both lone pairs and $\pi$ systems (carbonyls, nitro groups).
- Lowest energy transition; appears at longer wavelengths.
- Example: Acetone absorbs at **280 nm** (R-band).
- These are **forbidden** by Laporte selection rules but occur weakly due to vibronic coupling.

## 1.3 Role of Conjugation in Absorption Maxima

**Conjugation** is the phenomenon of alternating single and double (or triple) bonds, or the presence of lone pairs adjacent to $\pi$ systems, which delocalises $\pi$ electrons across the molecular framework.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Electron Highway"

Imagine a single $\pi$ bond as a **one-lane road**: the electron can only move in a small, confined area. When you create a **conjugated system** (alternating double bonds), it's like expanding to a **multi-lane highway**: the electron has far more room to move, and the energy barrier between HOMO and LUMO shrinks. This means **less energy is needed** to excite the electron, and thus the molecule absorbs at **longer wavelengths**.

### Quantitative Effect — The Woodward-Fieser Rules

For conjugated dienes, the empirical base value is **217 nm**. Each substituent (alkyl group, ring residue, exocyclic double bond, polar group) adds a defined increment. For enones (α,β-unsaturated carbonyls), the base is **215 nm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solute-Solvent Effects on $n \to \pi^*$ Transitions:**
> - **Bathochromic shift (red shift):** $\lambda_{max}$ increases with conjugation.
> - **Hypsochromic shift (blue shift):** $\lambda_{max}$ decreases (e.g., polar protic solvents H-bond with lone pair of $n \to \pi^*$ transition, raising the n-orbital energy).
> - **Hyperchromic effect:** Increase in absorption intensity ($\varepsilon$ increases).
> - **Hypochromic effect:** Decrease in absorption intensity.

## 1.4 Instrumentation — UV-Visible Spectrophotometer

A **double-beam spectrophotometer** is the standard instrument. The principal components are:

1. **Radiation Source:** Deuterium lamp (190–400 nm, UV) and Tungsten-Halogen lamp (350–2500 nm, Visible).
2. **Monochromator:** Prism or diffraction grating that isolates monochromatic light.
3. **Beam Splitter:** Divides light into reference and sample beams.
4. **Sample Compartment:** Holds the sample in a transparent cuvette (quartz for UV; glass for visible).
5. **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube (PMT) or photodiode.
6. **Recorder/Display:** Plots absorbance vs wavelength.

## 1.5 Applications of UV-Visible Spectroscopy

1. **Quantitative Analysis:** Determination of metal ions, organic compounds, pharmaceuticals using Beer-Lambert Law.
2. **Qualitative Analysis:** Identification of functional groups and chromophores.
3. **Kinetics:** Monitoring reaction progress by tracking absorbance vs time.
4. **pKa Determination:** From absorption-pH curves.
5. **Purity Verification** of solvents and drugs.
6. **DNA/Protein Analysis:** Nucleic acids absorb at **260 nm**; proteins at **280 nm** (aromatic amino acids).
7. **Colourimetry in Industry:** Dyes, paints, beverages.

## 1.6 Vibrational Spectroscopy — IR Spectroscopy — Principle

**Infrared (IR) Spectroscopy** is the study of the interaction of infrared radiation (wavenumber range **4000–400 cm⁻¹** or wavelength **2.5–25 μm**) with molecular vibrations.

### Core Principle

A molecule absorbs IR radiation when the **frequency of incident radiation exactly matches** the natural vibrational frequency of a bond, AND the vibration produces a **change in dipole moment** ($\partial \mu / \partial q \neq 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for IR Activity:** A vibration is IR active **only if** it causes a change in the dipole moment of the molecule. Hence, **homonuclear diatomic molecules** (H₂, N₂, O₂) are **IR inactive** (no dipole moment), while **heteronuclear diatomic molecules** (HCl, CO) are IR active.

## 1.7 Number of Vibrational Modes

A molecule containing **N atoms** has a total of **3N degrees of freedom**. Of these:
- **3** are translational (movement of centre of mass).
- **2** (for linear) or **3** (for non-linear) are rotational.
- The remaining are vibrational.

| Molecule Type | Formula for Vibrational Modes |
|---|---|
| **Non-linear** molecule | $3N - 6$ |
| **Linear** molecule | $3N - 5$ |

## 1.8 Vibrational Modes of CO₂ and H₂O

### CO₂ (Linear, N = 3)
- Vibrational modes = $3(3) - 5 = 4$
- The four modes are:
  1. **Symmetric Stretching ($\nu_1$):** 1388 cm⁻¹ — **IR inactive** (no dipole change).
  2. **Asymmetric Stretching ($\nu_3$):** 2349 cm⁻¹ — **IR active**.
  3. **Bending (degenerate, $\nu_2$):** 667 cm⁻¹ — appears as a doublet due to Fermi resonance with $\nu_1$.

### H₂O (Non-linear, N = 3)
- Vibrational modes = $3(3) - 6 = 3$
- The three modes are:
  1. **Symmetric Stretching ($\nu_1$):** 3652 cm⁻¹ — IR active.
  2. **Asymmetric Stretching ($\nu_3$):** 3756 cm⁻¹ — IR active.
  3. **Bending ($\nu_2$):** 1596 cm⁻¹ — IR active.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CO₂ has IR active modes:** Despite being a homonuclear diatomic-style linear molecule, the asymmetric stretch and bending modes produce oscillating dipoles perpendicular to the molecular axis, making them IR active. The symmetric stretch keeps the dipole symmetrically zero, hence IR inactive.

## 1.9 Applications of IR Spectroscopy

1. **Identification of Functional Groups:** O-H (~3300 cm⁻¹), C=O (~1700 cm⁻¹), C-H (~3000 cm⁻¹), N-H (~3500 cm⁻¹).
2. **Structural Elucidation:** Fingerprint region (1500–400 cm⁻¹) for unique molecular signature.
3. **Distinguishing Isomers:** cis vs trans, ortho vs meta vs para.
4. **Detection of Intermolecular H-bonding.**
5. **Study of Reaction Mechanisms.**
6. **Quality Control in Pharmaceuticals and Polymers.**
7. **Environmental Analysis** of pollutants and greenhouse gases.

## 1.10 Thermal Analysis — Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) — Principle

**Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is an analytical technique in which the **mass of a sample is continuously monitored as a function of temperature** (or time at constant temperature) under a controlled atmosphere.

### Core Principle
- A sensitive **microbalance** (with µg-level accuracy) measures the mass of a sample placed in a crucible (alumina, platinum, or ceramic).
- The sample is heated at a controlled rate (typically **5–20 °C/min**) from room temperature to 1000 °C or higher.
- The instrument records a **thermogram** showing % mass loss vs temperature.
- Mass changes occur due to **dehydration, decomposition, oxidation, or volatilisation** of components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** TGA is purely a **quantitative technique** — it does not identify the species lost; identification requires coupling with **FTIR** or **mass spectrometry (TGA-FTIR / TGA-MS)**.

### Types of TGA
1. **Dynamic (Isothermal) TGA:** Sample heated at linear rate; mass vs temperature recorded.
2. **Static TGA:** Sample held at constant temperature; mass vs time recorded.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Analysis of Electronic Transitions

The molecular orbital (MO) diagram of a typical organic molecule shows energy levels in the order:
$$\sigma < \pi < n < \pi^* < \sigma^*$$
(where lone pair 'n' energy lies between bonding and antibonding MOs).

The **wavelength of maximum absorption ($\lambda_{max}$)** for each transition is determined by the HOMO-LUMO gap and influenced by **auxochromes** (electron-donating/withdrawing substituents) and **chromophores** (the actual $\pi$-system).

### Selection Rules for Electronic Transitions

1. **Laporte Selection Rule:** In a centrosymmetric molecule, transitions between states of the same parity (g→g or u→u) are **forbidden**; only g→u or u→g are allowed. This is why d-d transitions in transition metals are weak.
2. **Spin Selection Rule:** Transitions involving a change in spin multiplicity ($\Delta S \neq 0$) are **forbidden**. Singlet → Triplet transitions are very weak.

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Conjugation Effect

When a molecule contains **alternating single and double bonds**, the p-orbitals overlap laterally to form a continuous $\pi$-molecular orbital spread over multiple atoms. This delocalisation:

1. **Lowers the HOMO energy** (slightly) but more importantly **lowers the LUMO energy** substantially.
2. **Reduces the HOMO-LUMO gap** ($\Delta E$).
3. Therefore, **lower energy (longer wavelength)** is required for $\pi \to \pi^*$ excitation.

The energy of a conjugated polyene follows a particle-in-a-box-like relation:
$$\Delta E = \frac{h^2 (n+1)}{8 m_e L^2}$$
where **L** is the length of the conjugated system. Longer conjugation → smaller $\Delta E$ → longer $\lambda_{max}$.

## 2.3 Detailed Instrumentation Block Flow

A modern **double-beam UV-Vis spectrophotometer** functions as follows:
1. The **light source** emits a continuous spectrum of UV-visible radiation.
2. The **monochromator** (prism + slits) selects a specific narrow wavelength band.
3. The **chopper/beam splitter** divides this monochromatic light into two beams:
   - **Reference beam** → passes through blank (solvent).
   - **Sample beam** → passes through sample in cuvette.
4. Both beams strike the **detector** alternately, and the electronic circuit computes the ratio $I / I_0$.
5. The output is **absorbance (A)** displayed against wavelength.

## 2.4 Theoretical Basis of IR Spectroscopy

Molecules are not rigid; atoms undergo **continuous vibrations** about their equilibrium positions. These vibrations are quantized, and the energy levels are given by the **harmonic oscillator model**:
$$E_v = \left(v + \frac{1}{2}\right) h \nu_0 \quad ; \quad v = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
where $v$ is the vibrational quantum number.

The **selection rule** for harmonic oscillator transitions is $\Delta v = \pm 1$. The **fundamental transition** is $v = 0 \to v = 1$, giving the strongest IR band.

The frequency of vibration depends on the **bond strength (k)** and the **reduced mass ($\mu$)** of the bonded atoms, as given by **Hooke's Law** for molecular vibrations.

## 2.5 Analysis of Vibrational Modes — Stretching vs Bending

There are two broad categories of molecular vibrations:

**(a) Stretching Vibrations** (change in bond length):
- **Symmetric Stretching:** Atoms move in the same direction; dipole may or may not change.
- **Asymmetric Stretching:** Atoms move in opposite directions; dipole change is guaranteed.

**(b) Bending Vibrations** (change in bond angle):
- **Scissoring:** In-plane bending (two atoms approach each other).
- **Rocking:** In-plane bending (both atoms move in same direction).
- **Wagging:** Out-of-plane bending (both atoms move in same direction, perpendicular to plane).
- **Twisting:** Out-of-plane bending (atoms move in opposite directions, perpendicular to plane).

## 2.6 Theoretical Analysis of TGA

TGA involves:
1. **Heat transfer** to the sample (conduction, convection, radiation).
2. **Mass change** due to physical/chemical processes:
   - **Dehydration** (loss of water, e.g., CuSO₄·5H₂O loses water in steps).
   - **Decarboxylation** (loss of CO₂ from carboxylic acids).
   - **Decomposition** (breaking of bonds, e.g., CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂).
   - **Oxidation** (in O₂ atmosphere).
   - **Sublimation** (e.g., naphthalene sublimes at ~80 °C).

The **derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) curve** is obtained by plotting $dm/dT$ vs $T$, which gives **peaks** corresponding to the **rate of mass loss**, helping to resolve overlapping steps.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planck-Einstein Relation | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $E$ in J; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; $\lambda$ in m | Energy of absorbed photon |
| 2 | Wavenumber | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\nu}{c}$ | $\tilde{\nu}$ in cm⁻¹ | Standard unit in IR spectroscopy |
| 3 | Beer-Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon \, c \, l$ | $A$ = absorbance (no units); $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹); $c$ in mol/L; $l$ in cm | Quantitative analysis |
| 4 | Transmittance | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | $I$ = transmitted; $I_0$ = incident | Fraction of light transmitted |
| 5 | Absorbance-Transmittance | $A = -\log T = \log \dfrac{I_0}{I}$ | Dimensionless | Direct conversion |
| 6 | Hooke's Law (Vibration) | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | $\nu$ in Hz; $k$ in N/m; $\mu$ in kg | Frequency of bond vibration |
| 7 | Wavenumber form of Hooke's Law | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | $\tilde{\nu}$ in cm⁻¹ | Used in IR spectroscopy |
| 8 | Reduced Mass | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 \cdot m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | $m$ in kg or amu | For diatomic systems |
| 9 | Vibrational Modes (Linear) | $3N - 5$ | $N$ = number of atoms | For CO₂, CO, HCN |
| 10 | Vibrational Modes (Non-linear) | $3N - 6$ | $N$ = number of atoms | For H₂O, CH₄, benzene |
| 11 | Conjugated Polyene $\Delta E$ | $\Delta E = \dfrac{h^2 (n+1)}{8 m_e L^2}$ | $L$ = conjugation length; $n$ = quantum number | Particle-in-a-box model |
| 12 | TGA Mass Loss | $\%\text{ Loss} = \dfrac{m_0 - m_T}{m_0} \times 100$ | $m_0$ = initial mass; $m_T$ = mass at T | Quantitative TGA analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** UV-Vis spectroscopy is used in **water quality monitoring** (COD/BOD analysis), **solar cell characterization** (band-gap determination), and **pharmaceutical purity testing**. IR spectroscopy is essential in **polymer identification**, **forensic analysis**, and **air quality monitoring** (FTIR for greenhouse gases). TGA is critical in **polymer thermal stability**, **pharmaceutical formulation**, and **ceramic processing**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer-Lambert Law

The Beer-Lambert Law relates the absorbance of a solution to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length of light through the solution.

### Step 1: Definition of Transmittance
Let $I_0$ be the intensity of incident light and $I$ the intensity of transmitted light. The transmittance $T$ is defined as the ratio of transmitted to incident light intensity:
$$T = \frac{I}{I_0}$$

### Step 2: Absorbance Definition
Absorbance $A$ is defined as the negative logarithm (base 10) of transmittance:
$$A = -\log_{10} T = -\log_{10} \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \log_{10} \left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

This definition is chosen because absorbance is **additive** for multiple absorbing species and is **linearly related to concentration**.

### Step 3: Differential Form (Lambert's Law)
Consider a thin slice of solution of thickness $dx$ that absorbs a fraction $dI$ of light. The fraction absorbed is proportional to thickness:
$$\frac{-dI}{I} = k_1 \, dx$$

Integrating over total path length $l$:
$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{-dI}{I} = k_1 \int_{0}^{l} dx \implies \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k_1 l$$

### Step 4: Beer's Law (Concentration Dependence)
The fraction of light absorbed is also proportional to the molar concentration $c$ of the absorbing species:
$$\frac{-dI}{I} = k_2 \, c \, dx$$

### Step 5: Combined Form (Beer-Lambert Law)
Combining both proportionalities and converting natural log to base-10 logarithm:
$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{k_2}{2.303} \cdot c \cdot l = \varepsilon \, c \, l$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \, c \, l}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law**, where $\varepsilon$ is the **molar absorptivity** (or extinction coefficient) with units of L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹.

### Numerical Example
A solution has $\varepsilon = 5000$ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹, $c = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L, and cuvette path length $l = 1$ cm.
$$A = 5000 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 = 1.0$$
$$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-1.0} = 0.10 \quad \text{(10% transmittance)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hooke's Law for Molecular Vibrations

### Step 1: Model a Bond as a Spring
Consider two atoms connected by a chemical bond. The bond behaves as a **spring** with force constant $k$ (a measure of bond stiffness). The atoms undergo **simple harmonic motion** about their equilibrium separation.

### Step 2: Equation of Motion
For a reduced mass $\mu$ undergoing simple harmonic motion:
$$F = -k x = \mu \, a = \mu \, \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$

The equation of motion becomes:
$$\mu \, \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + k \, x = 0$$

### Step 3: Solution of the Differential Equation
The general solution is:
$$x(t) = A \cos(2\pi \nu t) + B \sin(2\pi \nu t)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants determined by initial conditions.

### Step 4: Frequency Expression
Substituting back into the equation of motion, we obtain the natural frequency of vibration:
$$\nu = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}$$

### Step 5: Converting to Wavenumber (IR convention)
Since IR spectra use wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ (cm⁻¹):
$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{\nu}{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}}$$

### Numerical Application
Calculate the IR stretching frequency of C=O bond. Given:
- $k \approx 1200$ N/m (typical for C=O)
- $\mu = \dfrac{(12 \times 16)}{(12 + 16) \times 6.022 \times 10^{26}}$ kg = $\dfrac{192}{28 \times 6.022 \times 10^{26}} = 1.14 \times 10^{-26}$ kg

$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm/s})} \sqrt{\frac{1200}{1.14 \times 10^{-26}}}$$
$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{1.885 \times 10^{11}} \sqrt{1.05 \times 10^{28}}$$
$$\tilde{\nu} = \frac{1.026 \times 10^{14}}{1.885 \times 10^{11}} \approx 1727 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

This matches the **experimental value** of C=O stretch (~1700–1750 cm⁻¹) very well.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Vibrational Modes

### Step 3.3.1: Degrees of Freedom
A molecule with $N$ atoms has $3N$ degrees of freedom (each atom can move independently in 3D space: $x$, $y$, $z$).

### Step 3.3.2: Subtraction of Non-Vibrational Motions

**(a) Translational Motion (3 degrees):** The entire molecule can move in $x$, $y$, $z$ directions as a unit. This accounts for 3 degrees of freedom.

**(b) Rotational Motion:** 
- For a **non-linear** molecule: rotation about 3 mutually perpendicular axes (3 degrees).
- For a **linear** molecule: rotation about 2 axes perpendicular to the bond axis (2 degrees); rotation about the bond axis is meaningless.

### Step 3.3.3: Final Formula

**For Non-linear Molecules:**
$$\text{Vibrational modes} = 3N - 3 - 3 = 3N - 6$$

**For Linear Molecules:**
$$\text{Vibrational modes} = 3N - 3 - 2 = 3N - 5$$

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Examples: CO₂ and H₂O

### 3.4.1: CO₂ (Linear, N = 3)
Using the formula for linear molecules:
$$\text{Modes} = 3N - 5 = 3(3) - 5 = 4$$

| Mode | Type | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | IR Active? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\nu_1$ | Symmetric stretch | 1388 | **No** | No dipole change |
| $\nu_2$ | Bending (degenerate pair) | 667 | **Yes** | Dipole change perpendicular to axis |
| $\nu_3$ | Asymmetric stretch | 2349 | **Yes** | Dipole change along axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fermi Resonance in CO₂:** The symmetric stretch ($\nu_1$, 1388 cm⁻¹) and the overtone of the bending mode ($2\nu_2$, 1334 cm⁻¹) have similar energies, leading to coupling that splits the symmetric stretch into **two bands** at 1285 and 1388 cm⁻¹.

### 3.4.2: H₂O (Non-linear, Bent, N = 3)
Using the formula for non-linear molecules:
$$\text{Modes} = 3N - 6 = 3(3) - 6 = 3$$

| Mode | Type | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | IR Active? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\nu_1$ | Symmetric stretch | 3652 | **Yes** | Dipole change |
| $\nu_2$ | Bending | 1596 | **Yes** | Dipole change |
| $\nu_3$ | Asymmetric stretch | 3756 | **Yes** | Dipole change |

All three modes of H₂O are IR active, consistent with the bent geometry that gives a permanent dipole moment.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study

| Property | UV-Vis Spectroscopy | IR Spectroscopy | TGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Region of EM Spectrum** | 200–800 nm (UV-Vis) | 2.5–25 μm (4000–400 cm⁻¹) | N/A (thermal energy) |
| **Energy Transition** | Valence electrons | Molecular vibrations | Physical/chemical change |
| **Origin of Spectrum** | HOMO → LUMO | $v = 0 \to v = 1$ vibrational | Mass loss vs T |
| **Selection Rule** | $\Delta S = 0$; Laporte allowed | Change in dipole moment | No selection rule |
| **Output** | Absorbance vs $\lambda$ | %T vs $\tilde{\nu}$ | Mass loss vs T |
| **Quantitative Tool** | Beer-Lambert Law | Hooke's Law (for assignment) | % mass loss |
| **Sample Phase** | Solution/gas | Solid, liquid, gas (neat/KBr) | Solid (typically) |
| **Information Gained** | $\lambda_{max}$, $\varepsilon$, concentration | Functional groups, structure | Thermal stability, composition |
| **Limitation** | Cannot identify unknowns fully | Complex spectra; overlapping | Cannot identify volatile products |

---

## 3.6 Detailed TGA Thermogram Analysis (Example: CaC₂O₄·H₂O)

A classic KTU-style problem involves the TGA of calcium oxalate monohydrate, which decomposes in three distinct steps:

| Step | Temperature Range (°C) | Reaction | Theoretical Mass Loss |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100–200 | CaC₂O₄·H₂O → CaC₂O₄ + H₂O | ~12.3% |
| 2 | 300–400 | CaC₂O₄ → CaCO₃ + CO | ~19.2% |
| 3 | 600–800 | CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ | ~30.1% |

Each step appears as a **plateau-to-plateau transition** on the TGA curve, with the DTG curve showing a **peak** at the maximum rate of mass loss.

---

## 3.7 Effect of Conjugation on $\lambda_{max}$ — Quantitative Table

| Compound | Structure | Conjugation | $\lambda_{max}$ (nm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ethylene | CH₂=CH₂ | 1 C=C | 165 |
| Butadiene | CH₂=CH-CH=CH₂ | 2 C=C (conjugated) | 217 |
| Hexatriene | CH₂=CH-CH=CH-CH=CH₂ | 3 C=C (conjugated) | 258 |
| Octatetraene | 4 C=C (conjugated) | 4 C=C | 290 |
| $\beta$-Carotene | 11 C=C (conjugated) | 11 | 480 (orange) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** As conjugation length increases, $\lambda_{max}$ shifts to **longer wavelengths** (lower energy) — the **bathochromic effect**.
