# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a DTA Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Programmable Temperature Controller] --> FURN[Furnace]
    FURN --> SAMP[Sample Crucible with Thermocouple]
    FURN --> REF[Reference Crucible with Thermocouple]
    SAMP --> AMP1[Thermocouple Amplifier Sample]
    REF --> AMP2[Thermocouple Amplifier Reference]
    AMP1 --> SUM[Differential Amplifier Subtractor]
    AMP2 --> SUM
    AMP1 --> REC1[Temperature Recorder Channel 1 Ts]
    AMP2 --> REC2[Temperature Recorder Channel 2 Tr]
    SUM --> REC3[Differential Signal Recorder Delta T]
    REC3 --> OUT[DTA Thermogram Plot]
    GAS[Atmosphere Control N2 or Air Inlet] --> FURN
    AT[Ambient Temperature Sensor] --> PA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The Programmable Temperature Controller (PTC) is the brain that ramps the furnace temperature linearly. The two thermocouples generate small mV signals that are amplified and fed into a **subtractor (differential amplifier)**. The output of the subtractor is the $\Delta T$ signal that traces the DTA thermogram.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Gas Chromatograph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CG[Carrier Gas Cylinder He or H2 or N2] --> PR[Pressure Regulator]
    PR --> FC[Flow Controller]
    FC --> INJ[Sample Injector Gas Sampling Valve]
    INJ --> COL[GC Column Packed or Capillary Inside Column Oven]
    COL --> DET[Detector TCD or FID or ECD]
    DET --> AMP[Electrometer Amplifier]
    AMP --> REC[Recorder or Integrator]
    REC --> PC[Data System Chromatogram]
    OVEN[Column Oven with Temperature Programmer] -.controls.-> COL
    OVENC[Oven Temperature Programmer] --> OVEN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU answers, always show the following functional blocks in order:**
> 1. Carrier gas supply (with regulator and flow controller)
> 2. Sample injection system (syringe or gas sampling valve)
> 3. Column (inside a temperature-controlled oven)
> 4. Detector (TCD, FID, or ECD)
> 5. Amplifier and recorder / data system
>
> A neat, **left-to-right** block diagram with labelled arrows scores full marks.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EGS[Electron Gun Tungsten or LaB6 or FEG] --> ACC[Acceleration Anode 0.5 to 40 kV]
    ACC --> CL1[Condenser Lens 1]
    CL1 --> CL2[Condenser Lens 2]
    CL2 --> AP1[Objective Aperture]
    AP1 --> OL[Objective Lens Final Demagnification]
    OL --> SCAN[Scan Coils X and Y Deflection]
    SCAN --> SPEC[Specimen Stage with Sample]
    SPEC --> SE[Secondary Electron Detector]
    SPEC --> BSE[Backscattered Electron Detector]
    SPEC --> EDS[EDS Detector for X-rays]
    SE --> AMP[Signal Amplifier]
    BSE --> AMP
    EDS --> EDSAMP[EDS Pulse Processor]
    AMP --> CRT[Display Monitor Raster Scan Sync]
    CRTG[Sweep Generator] --> CRT
    CRTG --> SCAN
    EDSAMP --> EDSPC[EDS Computer Elemental Spectrum]
    VAC[Vacuum System Turbomolecular Pump] --> SPEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The **scan generator** drives both the scan coils *and* the CRT/monitor synchronously. The beam rasters the specimen line by line, and a corresponding line is drawn on the display. Each point's intensity is determined by the detector signal. The result is a **magnified, real-time image** of the surface.

---

## 4.4 DTA Thermogram of CaC₂O₄·H₂O — Schematic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room Temp Baseline Flat] --> B[Step 1 Endothermic Peak at 150 C Dehydration Loss of H2O]
    B --> C[Return to Baseline]
    C --> D[Step 2 Endothermic Peak at 450 C Loss of CO]
    D --> E[Plateau CaCO3 Stable Region]
    E --> F[Step 3 Endothermic Peak at 700 C Loss of CO2 forming CaO]
    F --> G[Final Baseline CaO Stable]
    N1{N2 Atmosphere} -.all three peaks downward endothermic.-> A
    AIR{Air or O2 Atmosphere} -.step 2 becomes exothermic due to CO combustion.-> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid tip:** In the KTU answer sheet, you may replace this Mermaid block with a hand-drawn thermogram showing three downward peaks labelled (1), (2), (3) — examiners accept either, but a hand-drawn peak is faster and scores better if neat.

---

## 4.5 Exhaust Gas Analysis — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Vehicle Engine Exhaust] --> PROBE[Heated Sampling Probe]
    PROBE --> DRY[Water Removal Trap Nafion]
    DRY --> SAMP[Sample Loop of GC Injector]
    SAMP --> COL[GC Column Molecular Sieve or Porapak]
    COL --> TCDFID[TCD or FID Detector]
    TCDFID --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> CALIB[Calibration with Standard Gas]
    CALIB --> RESULT[Concentrations of CO CO2 NOx CxHy H2 O2 in ppm or percent]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and require crisp 4–6 sentence answers. Marks are awarded for the keyword hits and a labelled diagram where applicable.

### Question 1: Define Differential Thermal Analysis. What is a DTA thermogram? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)** is a thermoanalytical technique in which the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) between a sample and an inert reference material (such as calcined $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$) is recorded as a function of programmed temperature, while both are subjected to identical heat treatment in a furnace.

The plot of $\Delta T$ (vertical axis) versus programmed temperature $T$ (or time $t$) is called the **DTA thermogram**. A flat baseline indicates no thermal event; **downward peaks** correspond to **endothermic** processes (e.g., dehydration, melting) and **upward peaks** to **exothermic** processes (e.g., oxidation, recrystallisation). The peak temperature $T_p$ is characteristic of the substance, and the peak area $A$ is proportional to the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ of the process.

---

### Question 2: List any three components of a Gas Chromatograph and state the function of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The three essential components of a Gas Chromatograph are:

1. **Carrier gas supply system** — supplies a chemically inert, dry, high-purity gas (He, H$_2$, or N$_2$) at a constant, controlled flow rate. It acts as the mobile phase that transports the vaporised sample through the column.

2. **Column (packed or capillary)** — contains the stationary phase. The mixture is separated here based on differences in volatility and partition behaviour between the mobile and stationary phases. Capillary columns give higher resolution.

3. **Detector (TCD / FID / ECD)** — senses the eluting components and converts their concentration into an electrical signal. The signal is plotted against time to give the **chromatogram**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often accept answers that include **sample injector**, **column oven with temperature programmer**, and **recorder/integrator** as alternative third components. Always end with a labelled block-diagram reference to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** and provide an **internal choice**. You must answer **one** of the two sub-questions (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. A typical structure is:
> - Sub-part (a) — 7 marks — Theory / Principle / Diagram
> - Sub-part (b) — 7 marks — Application / Derivation / Numerical

---

### Question A: Differential Thermal Analysis and its Application `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a)** Define DTA. Draw a neat labelled **block diagram of a DTA instrument** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA) is a thermoanalytical technique in which the temperature difference between a sample and an inert reference is measured as a function of programmed temperature.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PTC[Programmable Temperature Controller] --> FRN[Furnace]
    FRN --> SCR[Sample Crucible + Thermocouple]
    FRN --> RCR[Reference Crucible + Thermocouple]
    SCR --> AM1[Amplifier for Sample]
    RCR --> AM2[Amplifier for Reference]
    AM1 --> SUB[Subtractor Differential Amplifier]
    AM2 --> SUB
    AM1 --> R1[Recorder Channel 1 Ts vs t]
    AM2 --> R2[Recorder Channel 2 Tr vs t]
    SUB --> R3[Recorder Channel 3 Delta T vs t]
    R3 --> TG[DTA Thermogram]
    GAS[Atmosphere Control N2 or O2] --> FRN
```

**Function of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Programmable Temperature Controller | Linear heating at constant rate $\beta$ (e.g., 10 K/min) |
| Furnace | Provides uniform, controlled heat to both sample and reference |
| Sample crucible + thermocouple | Holds the sample; generates mV signal proportional to $T_s$ |
| Reference crucible + thermocouple | Holds inert reference (e.g., $\alpha$-Al$_2$O$_3$); generates mV signal for $T_r$ |
| Amplifiers | Boost the small thermocouple voltages to measurable levels |
| Subtractor (Differential Amplifier) | Computes $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ in real time |
| Recorders / Data system | Plots $T_s$, $T_r$ and $\Delta T$ against time/temperature |
| Atmosphere control | Maintains inert (N$_2$, Ar) or oxidising (air, O$_2$) environment |

**(b)** Discuss the application of DTA to the study of thermal decomposition of **CaC₂O₄·H₂O**. Draw a schematic thermogram and label the peaks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Calcium oxalate monohydrate, CaC₂O₄·H₂O, decomposes in **three discrete stages** in an inert (N₂) atmosphere:

| Step | Temperature (°C) | Reaction | Peak Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 – 200 | $\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Endothermic (downward) |
| 2 | 400 – 500 | $\text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{CO}$ | Endothermic (downward) |
| 3 | 600 – 800 | $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$ | Endothermic (downward) |

**Schematic Thermogram (description):**
- Flat baseline from 25 °C to ≈ 100 °C.
- **Peak I** at ≈ 150 °C, sharp downward (dehydration) — returns to baseline.
- Plateau 200 – 400 °C (anhydrous CaC₂O₄ stable).
- **Peak II** at ≈ 450 °C, sharp downward (CO loss) — returns to baseline.
- Plateau 500 – 600 °C (CaCO₃ stable).
- **Peak III** at ≈ 700 °C, broad downward large peak (CO₂ loss) — returns to baseline.
- Final flat region above 800 °C (CaO stable).

**In air atmosphere**, the CO released in step 2 is oxidised:
$$2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2$$
This exothermic reaction partly **offsets** the endothermic decomposition in step 2, often **eliminating** the downward peak (or even producing a small upward peak).

**Significance:**
- Identifies the sample by its characteristic $T_p$ values.
- Quantifies water of crystallisation from peak area (Step 1).
- Used in quality control of cement, minerals, and pharmaceutical compounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **omit** the inert-vs-oxidising comparison — losing 2 marks.
> - Students sometimes confuse **DTA with DSC** — remember DTA measures $\Delta T$ (°C), DSC measures heat flow $dq/dt$ (mW).
> - Forgetting to **label the x-axis** ($T$ or $t$) and **y-axis** ($\Delta T$ with up/down direction) costs 1 mark.

---

### Question B: Gas Chromatography and its Application `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** State the principle of Gas Chromatography. Draw a **neat labelled block diagram of a GC instrument** and explain the working of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Gas Chromatography is a separation technique in which a **gaseous mobile phase** (carrier gas — He, H₂, or N₂) is used to transport vaporised analytes through a column containing a stationary phase. Separation is based on differences in the **volatility** of the components and their **partition coefficients** $K$ between the mobile and stationary phases. Components with lower $K$ (lower affinity for stationary phase) elute first.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CG[Carrier Gas Cylinder] --> RG[Pressure Regulator]
    RG --> FC[Flow Controller]
    FC --> INJ[Sample Injector]
    INJ --> COL[GC Column in Oven]
    COL --> DET[Detector TCD or FID or ECD]
    DET --> AM[Amplifier]
    AM --> REC[Recorder or Integrator]
    REC --> PC[Data System]
    OP[Oven Temperature Programmer] -.controls.-> COL
```

**Working of each component:**

1. **Carrier gas supply:** Provides a clean, inert, dry gas at a constant flow rate (≈ 25–50 mL/min for packed columns).
2. **Sample injector:** Introduces a small, vaporised sample (≈ 0.1–10 µL) into the carrier-gas stream via a **microsyringe** (liquid) or a **gas sampling valve** (gas).
3. **Column oven + temperature programmer:** Maintains the column at the desired isothermal temperature or ramps it for temperature-programmed runs. The column contains the stationary phase; this is where separation occurs.
4. **Detector:**
   - **TCD (Thermal Conductivity Detector):** Measures change in thermal conductivity of gas; universal.
   - **FID (Flame Ionisation Detector):** Burns analyte in H₂/air flame; current is proportional to carbon content. Excellent for hydrocarbons.
   - **ECD (Electron Capture Detector):** Selective for halogenated, nitro compounds.
5. **Recorder / Integrator / PC:** Plots **detector signal vs time** — the chromatogram — and integrates peak areas for quantification.

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **analysis of the chemical composition of exhaust gases** using Gas Chromatography. Mention the typical components determined. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Vehicle exhaust is a multi-component gas mixture. GC with a **Porapak Q packed column** (or a molecular-sieve column) is the **standard technique** for its speciation. The procedure is:

**Block diagram of the analysis setup:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Exhaust Pipe] --> PRB[Heated Sampling Probe 100 C]
    PRB --> DRY[Water Trap Nafion Dryer]
    DRY --> SL[Sample Loop 0.5 to 5 mL]
    SL --> CG[Carrier Gas He or N2]
    CG --> INJV[Gas Sampling Valve]
    INJV --> COL[GC Column Porapak Q or Molecular Sieve]
    COL --> TCDFID[TCD or FID Detector]
    TCDFID --> INT[Integrator]
    INT --> CAL[Calibration Curve from Standard Gases]
    CAL --> RES[Reported Concentrations in ppm or volume percent]
```

**Steps:**
1. **Sampling:** A heated probe (≈ 100 °C) prevents condensation of heavy hydrocarbons. Sample is drawn into a conditioned vessel.
2. **Drying:** Water vapour is removed using a **Nafion membrane dryer** or a cold trap to avoid damaging the column.
3. **Injection:** A known volume (0.5 – 5 mL) is admitted into the carrier-gas stream via a **gas-sampling valve** with a fixed sample loop.
4. **Separation:** Components separate in the GC column. Hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₆, C₆H₁₄ …) on Porapak Q; permanent gases (H₂, O₂, N₂, CO, CO₂) on a **molecular-sieve 5 Å column**.
5. **Detection:** TCD detects permanent gases; FID detects hydrocarbons with high sensitivity.
6. **Calibration & Quantification:** Peak areas are compared with a **calibration curve** prepared from certified standard gas mixtures.

**Typical components determined and their typical ranges:**

| Component | Formula | Detector | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon monoxide | CO | TCD / FID after methanation | 0.1 – 5 % |
| Carbon dioxide | CO₂ | TCD | 5 – 15 % |
| Unburnt hydrocarbons | C$_x$H$_y$ | FID | 50 – 5000 ppm |
| Methane | CH₄ | FID | 50 – 500 ppm |
| Oxides of nitrogen | NO, NO₂ | ECD | 100 – 3000 ppm |
| Hydrogen | H₂ | TCD | 0.1 – 2 % |
| Oxygen | O₂ | TCD | 0.5 – 5 % |

**Engineering significance:**
- Compliance verification with **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** and **Euro-VI** emission standards.
- Evaluation of **catalytic converter efficiency** (HC, CO → CO₂).
- Comparative testing of petrol vs diesel engines.
- Indoor air-quality monitoring in tunnels and underground parking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students forget to **mention water removal** — examiners often test this. Lose 1 mark.
> - Mixing up **TCD vs FID** (TCD = universal but less sensitive; FID = carbon-specific, very sensitive) costs 1 mark.
> - Omitting the **calibration step** is a common mistake; always mention "compared with standard gas mixture" to gain 1 mark.
> - Drawing the block diagram **without arrows or labels** is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Alternate Question Set (For Self-Practice)

### Question 2: SEM — Principle and Instrumentation `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** State the principle of a **Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM)**. Explain the various **electron–sample interactions** that produce useful signals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of SEM:** A focused beam of high-energy electrons (0.5 – 40 keV) is accelerated from an electron gun and demagnified by a series of electromagnetic condenser and objective lenses. The beam is then deflected by **scan coils** to raster across the specimen surface in a line-by-line pattern. The interaction of these primary electrons with atoms of the sample produces a variety of **secondary signals**, which are collected by suitable detectors and used to reconstruct an image of the surface on a synchronously scanned display.

The de Broglie wavelength of the electron is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V + 0.978 \times 10^{-6} V^2}} \text{nm}$$

For $V = 30$ kV, $\lambda \approx 0.007$ nm — orders of magnitude smaller than visible light — enabling **nanometre-scale resolution**.

**Electron–Sample Interactions:**

| Signal | Energy (eV) | Origin | Detector | Information |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Secondary Electrons (SE)** | < 50 | Inelastic scattering from outer-shell electrons | Everhart–Thornley detector | Surface topography (most common imaging mode) |
| **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** | Up to $E_0$ | Elastic scattering from nuclei | Solid-state BSE detector | Atomic-number (Z) contrast |
| **Characteristic X-rays** | Element-specific | Inner-shell ionisation | EDS detector | Elemental composition |
| **Auger electrons** | 50 – 2000 | Surface atom relaxation | Auger detector | Surface chemistry |
| **Cathodoluminescence** | Visible | Band-gap transitions | PMT | Optical/electronic properties |

The **most-used** signal for imaging is the **SE signal** because it provides high surface-detail contrast and has a high signal-to-noise ratio.

**(b)** Draw a **neat labelled block diagram of SEM** and describe the function of its main components. List **four applications** of SEM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EG[Electron Gun] --> AN[Anode High Voltage 0.5 to 40 kV]
    AN --> C1[Condenser Lens 1]
    C1 --> C2[Condenser Lens 2]
    C2 --> AP[Objective Aperture]
    AP --> OL[Objective Lens]
    OL --> SC[Scan Coils X and Y]
    SC --> SP[Specimen on Stage]
    SP --> SD[Secondary Electron Detector]
    SP --> BD[Backscattered Electron Detector]
    SP --> ED[EDS X-ray Detector]
    SD --> SA[Signal Amplifier]
    BD --> SA
    SA --> CR[T Display Monitor]
    SG[Sweep Generator] --> CR
    SG --> SC
    ED --> EP[EDS Pulse Processor and Computer]
    VP[Vacuum Pump System] --> SP
```

**Functions of main components:**

1. **Electron gun:** Emits electrons by **thermionic emission** (W filament, LaB₆) or **field emission** (FEG).
2. **Anode & high-voltage supply:** Accelerates electrons to 0.5 – 40 keV.
3. **Condenser and objective lenses:** Electromagnetic lenses demagnify the source to a 1 – 10 nm spot.
4. **Objective aperture:** Stops off-axis electrons to reduce aberration and control beam current.
5. **Scan coils:** Deflect the beam in a raster pattern across the specimen.
6. **Specimen stage:** Holds the sample; allows translation, tilt, and rotation.
7. **Detectors (SE, BSE, EDS):** Convert emitted signals into electrical pulses.
8. **Sweep generator:** Provides synchronised scanning for both the beam and the display CRT/monitor.
9. **Vacuum system:** Maintains high vacuum (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa) to prevent electron scattering and sample contamination.

**Four Applications of SEM:**
1. **Materials science:** Fracture-surface analysis, grain-size measurement, failure analysis of metals.
2. **Nanotechnology:** Imaging of nanoparticles, nanowires, 2D materials (graphene, MoS₂).
3. **Biological sciences:** Topography of cells, tissues, insects (after sputter-coating with gold/palladium).
4. **Semiconductor industry:** Defect inspection, line-width measurement in integrated-circuit fabrication.
5. *(Bonus)* Forensic science — gunshot-residue analysis; pharmaceutical — tablet-surface uniformity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Failing to **mention the vacuum system** in SEM — examiners often test this concept. Lose 1 mark.
> - Confusing **SEM with TEM** — SEM images *surface*; TEM images *thin section* in transmission. Cost: 1 mark.
> - Listing vague applications like "studying materials" without specifics — examiners want **named** examples. Cost: 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision checklist** for the KTU ESE. Read this 30 minutes before the exam.

### DTA
- ✅ DTA measures $\Delta T = T_s - T_r$ vs $T$ (or $t$).
- ✅ Downward peak = endothermic; upward peak = exothermic.
- ✅ Reference material = $\alpha$-Al$_2$O₃ (calcined alumina), inert.
- ✅ Heating rate is **constant** and **programmed**; typical $\beta$ = 5–20 K/min.
- ✅ $\Delta H = K \cdot A / m$ — peak area is proportional to enthalpy.
- ✅ **CaC₂O₄·H₂O gives 3 endothermic peaks in N₂** (water loss → CO loss → CO₂ loss).
- ✅ In **air**, the second peak becomes exothermic due to CO combustion.
- ✅ DTA differs from DSC: DTA → $\Delta T$ (°C); DSC → $dq/dt$ (mW).
- ✅ Block diagram: PTC → Furnace → (Sample + Reference) → Amplifier → Subtractor → Recorder.

### Gas Chromatography
- ✅ Carrier gas = mobile phase (He, H₂, N₂) — must be **inert** and **dry**.
- ✅ Stationary phase = high-boiling liquid on inert support, or solid (GSC vs GLC).
- ✅ Column types: **packed** (mm-bore, 1–5 m) vs **capillary** (open tubular, 10–100 m).
- ✅ $K = C_s / C_m$ — distribution constant.
- ✅ $H = A + B/u + Cu$ — Van Deemter equation (Eddy + Longitudinal + Mass transfer).
- ✅ $u_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{B/C}$ — optimum velocity.
- ✅ $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$ — number of theoretical plates.
- ✅ Detectors: **TCD** (universal), **FID** (carbon compounds, hydrocarbons), **ECD** (halogens, NO$_x$).
- ✅ **Exhaust analysis:** Components = CO, CO₂, H₂, O₂, NO$_x$, C$_x$H$_y$, CH₄.
- ✅ Sample must be **dried** (Nafion trap) and **heated** (sampling probe) to prevent condensation.
- ✅ Block diagram: Carrier gas → Injector → Column (in oven) → Detector → Amplifier → Recorder.

### SEM
- ✅ SEM uses a **focused electron beam** to scan a surface in a **raster** pattern.
- ✅ Resolution limit comes from de Broglie wavelength ($\lambda \propto 1/\sqrt{V}$).
- ✅ Typical accelerating voltage = 0.5 – 40 kV.
- ✅ **Most common signal: Secondary Electrons (SE) — for topography.**
- ✅ **BSE = Z-contrast**; **Characteristic X-rays = elemental analysis (EDS)**.
- ✅ SEM requires **high vacuum** (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa) and **conductive samples** (non-conductors are sputter-coated with Au/Pd).
- ✅ Resolution: ~1 nm (FEG-SEM) to ~10 nm (W-filament SEM).
- ✅ SEM ≠ TEM: SEM = **surface** imaging; TEM = **transmission** through thin section.
- ✅ Block diagram: Electron gun → Condenser lenses → Objective lens → Scan coils → Specimen → Detectors → Display.
- ✅ Applications: Materials science, nanotechnology, biology, semiconductors, forensic, pharmaceutical.

### Universal KTU Tips
- ✅ Always draw **labelled block diagrams** with arrows.
- ✅ Memorise **3 standard reactions** of CaC₂O₄·H₂O and the **3 standard components** of exhaust gas (CO, CO₂, C$_x$H$_y$).
- ✅ Be ready to differentiate **DTA vs DSC**, **SEM vs TEM**, **TCD vs FID**.
- ✅ Use **S.I. units** (K, J, m, m/s) and **standard nomenclature** to avoid examiner deductions.
- ✅ Always mention **reference material** (in DTA), **carrier gas** (in GC), and **vacuum system** (in SEM).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for the three peaks of CaC₂O₄·H₂O:** *"**H**₂O, **C**O, **C**O₂ — like a child H-C-C saying 'HCC, I am being heated!'"*

---

**End of Module 3 Premium Notes — GCCYT122 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Instrumentation and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instrumentation and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Analytical Instrumentation — The Foundation

**Analytical Instrumentation** refers to the systematic assembly of physical and chemical tools, electronic devices, and detection systems used to **identify**, **quantify**, and **characterize** the structure, composition, and properties of matter at molecular and atomic levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GCCYT122 Module 3):**
> Analytical instrumentation encompasses the design, working principle, components, and applications of spectroscopic techniques (UV-Vis, IR, NMR), chromatographic methods (GC, HPLC), electroanalytical techniques, and thermal analysis methods used in modern chemistry laboratories.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective Toolbox" Approach

Imagine you are a **forensic detective** at a crime scene. A crime scene contains clues — fingerprints, blood stains, hair samples. To solve the case, you need:
- A **magnifying glass** (Microscopy)
- A **chemical kit** to test blood type (Wet chemistry)
- A **fingerprint database** (Spectroscopy — comparing with known spectra)
- A **camera** with special filters (UV-Vis, IR detectors)

Similarly, in chemistry, when you have an unknown compound, you use different **instruments** as your "detective tools." Each tool gives different information:
- **UV-Vis**: "What's the color/absorbing structure?"
- **IR**: "What functional groups are present?"
- **NMR**: "How are atoms connected?"
- **Chromatography**: "Is my sample pure? What are the components?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** No single instrument gives complete information. A complete analysis of a molecule requires combining data from multiple techniques — this is called **complementary analysis**.

## 1.3 Classification of Analytical Instruments

Analytical instruments are classified based on the type of interaction between matter and energy:

| **Class** | **Energy Type** | **Examples** | **Information Obtained** |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Spectroscopic** | Electromagnetic radiation | UV-Vis, IR, NMR, AAS | Structure, functional groups, electronic states |
| **Chromatographic** | Mobile phase transport | GC, HPLC, TLC | Separation, purity, quantification |
| **Electrochemical** | Electrical signals | Potentiometry, Conductometry, Voltammetry | Ion concentration, redox behavior |
| **Thermal** | Heat flow | TGA, DTA, DSC | Thermal stability, phase transitions |
| **X-ray based** | X-ray radiation | XRD, XRF | Crystal structure, elemental composition |
| **Mass-based** | Ion mass/charge | Mass Spectrometry | Molecular weight, fragmentation pattern |

## 1.4 The Universal Instrumentation Block Diagram

Every analytical instrument — regardless of its specific type — follows a common **architectural pattern**:

> Source → Wavelength/Energy Selector → Sample Holder → Detector → Signal Processor → Readout/Display

**Why this matters:** Understanding this universal pattern means you can transfer your knowledge from UV-Vis to IR, from GC to HPLC, because they all share this underlying architecture.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Speed of light (c):** $c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Planck's constant (h):** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **Avogadro's number (Nₐ):** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Frequency-Wavelength relationship:** $\nu = c/\lambda$
- **Energy-Frequency relationship:** $E = h\nu$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 GCCYT122, focus primarily on **UV-Visible Spectroscopy**, **IR Spectroscopy**, and a working knowledge of **Chromatography** and **Electrochemical methods**. NMR is introduced at a conceptual level.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UV-Visible Spectroscopy — Instrumentation and Applications

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Principle

UV-Vis spectroscopy is based on the **absorption of ultraviolet or visible light** by molecules, causing **electronic transitions** from the ground state to an excited state.

**The Governing Equation:**

$$E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{hcN_A}{\lambda} = \Delta E_{electronic}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Energy absorbed (J)
- $h$ = Planck's constant = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $\nu$ = Frequency of radiation (Hz)
- $c$ = Speed of light = $3.0 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)

**Range of operation:**
- **UV region:** $\lambda$ = **190–400 nm**
- **Visible region:** $\lambda$ = **400–800 nm**

### 2.1.2 Types of Electronic Transitions (Order of Energy)

$$\sigma \rightarrow \sigma^* > n \rightarrow \sigma^* > \pi \rightarrow \pi^* > n \rightarrow \pi^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory trick:** Transitions with **star (\*)** are excited states. The order is from **highest to lowest energy** going from $\sigma \rightarrow \sigma^*$ down to $n \rightarrow \pi^*$.

### 2.1.3 Instrumentation Block Diagram (UV-Vis Spectrophotometer)

**Source** → **Monochromator** → **Sample compartment** → **Detector** → **Amplifier** → **Recorder/Display**

**Component-wise breakdown:**

1. **Radiation Source:**
   - **Tungsten-Halogen lamp** for visible region (320–2500 nm)
   - **Deuterium lamp** for UV region (190–400 nm)
   - **Xenon arc lamp** covers both UV and Visible (190–800 nm)

2. **Monochromator (Wavelength Selector):**
   - Uses a **prism** or **diffraction grating** to isolate a narrow band of wavelengths
   - **Slit width** controls the bandwidth

3. **Sample Holder:**
   - **Quartz cuvettes** for UV region (glass absorbs UV)
   - **Glass or plastic cuvettes** for visible region

4. **Detector:**
   - **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** — most sensitive
   - **Photodiode** — used in modern instruments
   - Converts light intensity into electrical signal

5. **Recorder:**
   - Displays **Absorbance (A)** vs. **Wavelength (λ)**
   - Produces the characteristic **UV-Vis spectrum**

### 2.1.4 The Beer-Lambert Law (Quantitative Foundation)

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

Where:
- $A$ = Absorbance (no units)
- $\varepsilon$ = Molar absorptivity / Molar extinction coefficient ($\text{L mol}^{-1} \text{ cm}^{-1}$)
- $c$ = Concentration of the solution ($\text{mol L}^{-1}$)
- $l$ = Path length of the cuvette (cm)

**Transmittance Relationship:**

$$A = -\log T = \log\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

Where $I_0$ = incident intensity, $I$ = transmitted intensity.

### 2.1.5 Applications of UV-Vis Spectroscopy

- **Quantitative analysis** of metal ions, organic compounds, pharmaceuticals
- **Determination of concentration** of colored solutions
- **Study of reaction kinetics** (monitoring absorbance vs. time)
- **Detection of impurities** in pharmaceutical formulations
- **DNA/Protein analysis** at 260 nm and 280 nm
- **Colorimetry** — identification of colored complexes

---

## 2.2 IR (Infrared) Spectroscopy — Instrumentation and Applications

### 2.2.1 Fundamental Principle

IR spectroscopy probes the **vibrational modes** of molecules. When IR radiation frequency matches the natural vibrational frequency of a bond, **absorption** occurs.

**Selection Rule:** For a vibration to be IR active, there must be a **change in dipole moment** during the vibration.

**Vibrational Modes:**
- **Stretching:** Symmetric and Asymmetric
- **Bending:** Scissoring, Rocking, Wagging, Twisting

For a **non-linear molecule** with N atoms: **Number of vibrational modes = 3N − 6**
For a **linear molecule**: **Number of vibrational modes = 3N − 5**

### 2.2.2 IR Regions

| **Region** | **Wavelength** | **Wavenumber (cm⁻¹)** | **Information** |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Near IR** | 0.78–2.5 μm | 12800–4000 | Overtone bands |
| **Mid IR** | 2.5–25 μm | 4000–400 | **Fundamental vibrations (most useful)** |
| **Far IR** | 25–1000 μm | 400–10 | Skeletal vibrations, lattice modes |

**Wavenumber definition:** $\bar{\nu} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{\nu}{c}$ (in cm⁻¹)

### 2.2.3 Instrumentation — FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared) Spectrometer

**Modern FTIR uses the Michelson Interferometer:**

1. **IR Source:** Globar (silicon carbide) — emits broad IR radiation
2. **Michelson Interferometer:**
   - **Beam splitter** divides light into two paths
   - **Fixed mirror** and **Moving mirror** recombine beams
   - Creates **interferogram** as a function of mirror position
3. **Sample compartment** (KBr pellets or liquid cells)
4. **Detector:** DTGS (Deuterated Triglycine Sulfate) or MCT (Mercury Cadmium Telluride)
5. **Fourier Transform** converts interferogram → IR spectrum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FTIR over Dispersive IR?**
> - **Fellgett's advantage:** Multiplex (Fellgett) — measures all frequencies simultaneously → **faster**
> - **Jacquinot's advantage:** Higher throughput (no slits) → **better S/N ratio**
> - **Connes' advantage:** Internal He-Ne laser calibration → **highly accurate wavenumbers**

### 2.2.4 Characteristic IR Absorption Frequencies

| **Bond** | **Compound Type** | **Wavenumber (cm⁻¹)** | **Intensity** |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| O–H (free) | Alcohols, Phenols | 3650–3590 | Sharp, strong |
| O–H (H-bonded) | Alcohols, Carboxylic acids | 3550–3200 | Broad, strong |
| N–H | Amines, Amides | 3500–3300 | Medium |
| C–H (sp³) | Alkanes | 3000–2850 | Strong |
| C–H (sp²) | Alkenes, Aromatics | 3100–3000 | Medium |
| C≡C | Alkynes | 2260–2100 | Variable |
| C≡N | Nitriles | 2260–2220 | Medium |
| C=O | Aldehydes, Ketones, Acids | 1750–1680 | Very strong |
| C=C | Alkenes | 1680–1600 | Variable |
| C–O | Alcohols, Ethers | 1300–1000 | Strong |

### 2.2.5 Applications of IR Spectroscopy

- **Identification of functional groups** in organic molecules
- **Structural elucidation** of unknown compounds
- **Quality control** in pharmaceutical industry
- **Detection of impurities** and adulterants
- **Study of reaction mechanisms** (intermediates)
- **Polymer characterization** (crystallinity, orientation)
- **Forensic analysis** of drugs, paints, fibers

---

## 2.3 Chromatography — Instrumentation and Applications

### 2.3.1 Fundamental Principle

Chromatography separates components of a mixture based on their **differential distribution** between a **stationary phase** and a **mobile phase**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The core equation governing retention:**
> $$R_f = \frac{\text{Distance traveled by solute}}{\text{Distance traveled by solvent front}}$$
> Used in **TLC (Thin Layer Chromatography)**, where $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$.

### 2.3.2 Types of Chromatography (KTU Focus)

**A. Gas Chromatography (GC):**

- **Principle:** Volatile samples carried by an inert gas (He, N₂) mobile phase through a liquid-coated capillary column
- **Detector:** Flame Ionization Detector (FID), Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD)
- **Applications:** Analysis of hydrocarbons, drugs, environmental pollutants, forensic samples

**B. High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC):**

- **Principle:** Liquid mobile phase (water, methanol, acetonitrile) pushed under high pressure through a packed column
- **Detector:** UV-Vis, Refractive Index (RI), Fluorescence
- **Applications:** Pharmaceutical analysis, biomolecules, food additives, pesticides

**C. Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC):**

- **Principle:** Stationary phase = silica gel on glass plate; mobile phase = solvent
- **Applications:** Quick purity check, reaction monitoring, identifying components

### 2.3.3 Instrumentation Block Diagram (HPLC)

**Solvent Reservoir** → **Pump (high pressure)** → **Injector** → **Column (oven)** → **Detector** → **Data System**

---

## 2.4 Electrochemical Methods — Overview

### 2.4.1 Potentiometry

**Principle:** Measures **potential difference** between indicator and reference electrode at **zero current**.

**Nernst Equation (Foundation):**

$$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

At standard conditions (T = 298 K):

$$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$$

Where:
- $E_{cell}$ = Cell potential (V)
- $E°_{cell}$ = Standard cell potential (V)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- $T$ = Temperature (K)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C mol⁻¹**
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient

**Application:** pH measurement using **glass electrode**.

### 2.4.2 Conductometry

**Principle:** Measures **electrical conductivity** of a solution.

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = G \cdot \frac{l}{A}$$

Where:
- $\kappa$ = Specific conductivity ($\text{S cm}^{-1}$)
- $\rho$ = Resistivity ($\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$)
- $G$ = Conductance (S)
- $l$ = Distance between electrodes (cm)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of electrode ($\text{cm}^2$)

**Molar conductivity:**

$$\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \times 1000}{c}$$

Where $c$ = molar concentration (mol/L); $\Lambda_m$ has units $\text{S cm}^2 \text{ mol}^{-1}$.

**Applications:** Conductometric titrations (acid-base, precipitation), water quality analysis, degree of dissociation.

---

## 2.5 Thermal Analysis Methods

| **Technique** | **Full Name** | **Measures** | **Application** |
|--------------|---------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **TGA** | Thermogravimetric Analysis | Mass change vs. temperature | Thermal stability, decomposition |
| **DTA** | Differential Thermal Analysis | Temperature difference (sample vs. reference) | Phase transitions |
| **DSC** | Differential Scanning Calorimetry | Heat flow difference | Melting point, heat of fusion, glass transition |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula/Concept** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Used In** |
|------|---------------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Energy of radiation | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Joules (J) | All spectroscopy |
| 2 | Wavenumber | $\bar{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ | cm⁻¹ | IR spectroscopy |
| 3 | Beer-Lambert Law | $A = \varepsilon c l$ | dimensionless | UV-Vis |
| 4 | Absorbance-Transmittance | $A = -\log T = \log(I_0/I)$ | dimensionless | UV-Vis |
| 5 | Nernst Equation | $E = E° - (0.0592/n)\log Q$ | Volts (V) | Potentiometry |
| 6 | Specific conductivity | $\kappa = G \cdot l/A$ | S cm⁻¹ | Conductometry |
| 7 | Molar conductivity | $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/c$ | S cm² mol⁻¹ | Conductometry |
| 8 | Vibrational modes (non-linear) | $3N - 6$ | — | IR |
| 9 | Vibrational modes (linear) | $3N - 5$ | — | IR |
| 10 | Retention factor (TLC) | $R_f = d_{solute}/d_{solvent}$ | dimensionless | Chromatography |
| 11 | Number of electrons (TGA/Redox) | $n = \Delta m \cdot F / (M \cdot I \cdot t)$ | — | Faraday's Law |
| 12 | Planck-Einstein | $E = N_A h \nu$ | J/mol | Photochemistry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** UV-Vis is used in **water quality monitoring** (chemical oxygen demand), IR in **polymer quality control**, HPLC in **drug purity testing**, and conductometry in **industrial process control** — all directly relevant to B.Tech engineering applications.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Beer-Lambert Law (From First Principles)

**Statement to prove:** $A = \varepsilon c l$

### Step 1: Setting up the differential equation

Consider a thin slice of absorbing solution of thickness $dl$. Let the intensity of light entering this slice be $I$, and the intensity leaving be $I - dI$.

The fractional decrease in intensity is proportional to:
- The concentration of the absorbing species ($c$)
- The thickness of the slice ($dl$)

**Mathematically:**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k \cdot c \cdot dl$$

> **Logic:** The negative sign indicates that intensity **decreases** as the light passes through more absorbing material. The proportionality constant $k$ is characteristic of the absorbing species.

### Step 2: Integration across the entire path length

Integrating from $I_0$ (intensity at $l = 0$) to $I$ (intensity at $l = l$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -kc \int_{0}^{l} dl$$

Evaluating both integrals:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -kcl$$

### Step 3: Converting to natural logarithm form

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-kcl}$$

### Step 4: Introducing Transmittance and Absorbance

**Transmittance (T):**

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0}$$

Therefore:

$$T = e^{-kcl}$$

**Absorbance (A):**

$$A = -\log_{10} T = -\log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 5: Converting to base-10 logarithm

Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{kcl}{2.303}$$

**Defining molar absorptivity:** $\varepsilon = k/2.303$

### Step 6: Final form of the Beer-Lambert Law

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$

**Where:**
- $A$ = Absorbance (dimensionless)
- $\varepsilon$ = Molar absorptivity ($\text{L mol}^{-1} \text{ cm}^{-1}$)
- $c$ = Concentration ($\text{mol L}^{-1}$)
- $l$ = Path length (cm)

> **Validity Conditions:**
> 1. Monochromatic light (single wavelength)
> 2. Dilute solutions (typically $c < 0.01$ M)
> 3. No scattering, no fluorescence, no chemical reaction

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Detailed)

**Statement to derive:** $E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$

### Step 1: Gibbs free energy and cell potential

The fundamental relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

For standard conditions:

$$\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$$

### Step 2: Applying the reaction isotherm

The general Gibbs free energy change for any reaction:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

Where $Q$ is the reaction quotient.

### Step 3: Substituting

Replacing $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G°$:

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 4: Simplification

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:

$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Step 5: Converting to common logarithm

Using $\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$:

$$E = E° - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

### Step 6: At standard temperature T = 298 K

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, $T = 298$ K:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0592$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (UV-Vis)

**Problem:** A solution has an absorbance of 0.45 at 540 nm in a 1 cm cuvette. If the molar absorptivity is $1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{ cm}^{-1}$, calculate the concentration.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given values**
- $A = 0.45$
- $\varepsilon = 1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{ cm}^{-1}$
- $l = 1$ cm
- $c = ?$

**Step 2: Apply Beer-Lambert Law**

$$A = \varepsilon c l$$

**Step 3: Rearrange for concentration**

$$c = \frac{A}{\varepsilon \cdot l}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values**

$$c = \frac{0.45}{1.5 \times 10^4 \times 1}$$

**Step 5: Calculate**

$$c = \frac{0.45}{15000} = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol L}^{-1}$$

**Step 6: Convert to more useful units**

$$c = 3.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol L}^{-1} = 30 \text{ μmol L}^{-1} = 30 \text{ μM}$$

**Answer:** $c = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mol/L

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Nernst Equation)

**Problem:** Calculate the electrode potential of a Cu²⁺/Cu electrode in a solution of 0.01 M Cu²⁺ at 25°C. Given: $E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the half-reaction**

$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$$

**Step 2: Identify the number of electrons**

$$n = 2$$

**Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation**

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[Cu]}{[Cu^{2+}]}$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary condition**

For pure solid Cu, activity $[Cu] = 1$:

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values**

$$E = 0.34 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.01}$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the logarithm**

$$\log \frac{1}{0.01} = \log 100 = 2$$

**Step 7: Final calculation**

$$E = 0.34 - 0.0296 \times 2 = 0.34 - 0.0592 = 0.2808 \text{ V}$$

**Answer:** $E = +0.2808$ V

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem Matrix (Common KTU Problem Types)

| **Problem Type** | **Formula Used** | **Key Steps** | **Common Pitfall** |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Find concentration from absorbance | $c = A/(\varepsilon l)$ | 1. Identify A, ε, l. 2. Plug into formula. | Mixing cm with m for path length |
| Calculate % Transmittance | $T = 10^{-A} \times 100$ | 1. Calculate $10^{-A}$. 2. Multiply by 100. | Forgetting to convert to % |
| Cell potential at non-standard conditions | $E = E° - (0.0592/n)\log Q$ | 1. Write balanced equation. 2. Identify Q. 3. Plug in. | Confusing log with ln |
| Molar conductivity | $\Lambda_m = 1000\kappa/c$ | 1. Convert c to mol/L. 2. Apply formula. | Using wrong unit for c |
| Number of vibrational modes | $3N-6$ or $3N-5$ | 1. Count atoms (N). 2. Check if linear. | Not identifying linear vs. non-linear |
| Wavelength from energy | $\lambda = hc/E$ | 1. Use $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. 2. Solve. | Unit conversion (nm, Å, m) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Tungsten Deuterium Lamp] --> B[Monochromator Prism Grating Slit]
    B --> C[Sample Compartment Quartz Cuvette]
    C --> D[Detector Photomultiplier Tube]
    D --> E[Amplifier Signal Processor]
    E --> F[Recorder Display Absorbance vs Wavelength]
    F --> G[Output UV-Vis Spectrum]
```

**Component Functional Mapping:**

- **Source (A):** Provides continuous UV-Vis radiation; tungsten for visible, deuterium for UV
- **Monochromator (B):** Isolates a narrow wavelength band; uses diffraction grating in modern instruments
- **Sample Compartment (C):** Holds the solution in a quartz cuvette (1 cm path length typical)
- **Detector (D):** Converts light signal to electrical signal; photomultiplier provides high sensitivity
- **Amplifier (E):** Boosts weak electrical signal from detector
- **Recorder (F):** Plots absorbance as a function of wavelength, generating the spectrum
- **Output (G):** Final UV-Vis spectrum used for analysis

---

## 4.2 FTIR Spectrometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR Source Globar SiC] --> B[Michelson Interferometer Beam Splitter Fixed Mirror Moving Mirror]
    B --> C[Sample KBr Pellet ATR]
    C --> D[Detector DTGS MCT]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter]
    E --> F[Fourier Transform Computer]
    F --> G[IR Spectrum Absorbance vs Wavenumber]
```

**Key Architectural Notes:**

- The **Michelson Interferometer** is the heart of FTIR; it produces an **interferogram** (intensity vs. mirror position) which is then mathematically transformed via **Fourier Transform** into the conventional IR spectrum
- The moving mirror is translated with **micron precision** to create the optical path difference
- A **He-Ne laser** provides an internal wavelength calibration standard

---

## 4.3 HPLC — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solvent Reservoir Mobile Phase] --> B[High Pressure Pump]
    B --> C[Injector Sample Loop]
    C --> D[Column Packed C18 Silica in Oven]
    D --> E[Detector UV-Vis RID Fluorescence]
    E --> F[Data System Chromatogram]
```

**Architectural Function:**

- **Solvent Reservoir:** Stores the mobile phase (water/methanol/acetonitrile mixtures)
- **High Pressure Pump:** Generates pressures of 50–400 bar to push mobile phase through the packed column
- **Injector:** Introduces a precise volume (typically 1–100 μL) of sample into the mobile phase stream
- **Column:** The "heart" of separation; packed with stationary phase particles (e.g., C18 reversed-phase)
- **Detector:** Continuously monitors the eluate for separated components
- **Data System:** Produces the chromatogram (Detector response vs. Time)

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Analytical Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What do you want to know?] --> B{Concentration of a known species?}
    A --> C{Structure of an unknown compound?}
    A --> D{Purity of a sample?}
    A --> E{Thermal stability?}
    B --> F[UV-Vis Spectrophotometry Beer Lambert Law]
    C --> G{Functional groups?}
    C --> H{Complete structure?}
    G --> I[IR Spectroscopy]
    H --> J[NMR Spectroscopy]
    D --> K[Chromatography HPLC GC TLC]
    E --> L[TGA DSC DTA]
```

**Usage Notes:**

- This flowchart helps in **method selection** for chemical analysis
- For **quantitative analysis** of a known absorbing species → UV-Vis
- For **functional group identification** → IR
- For **complete structural elucidation** → NMR + IR + MS (combined)
- For **separation and purity** → Chromatography
- For **thermal behavior** → TGA/DSC

---

## 4.5 Spectroscopic Technique Comparison (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electromagnetic Spectrum] --> B[Gamma Rays High Energy]
    A --> C[X-Rays]
    A --> D[UV Region 190-400 nm]
    A --> E[Visible Region 400-800 nm]
    A --> F[IR Region 2500-25000 nm]
    A --> G[Microwaves]
    A --> H[Radio Waves]
    D --> I[UV-Vis Electronic Transitions]
    E --> I
    F --> J[IR Vibrational Transitions]
    H --> K[NMR Nuclear Spin Transitions]
```

**Energy Hierarchy:**

$$\text{UV-Vis} > \text{IR} > \text{Microwave} > \text{Radio (NMR)}$$

> **Memory Aid:** UV-Vis involves **electronic transitions** (highest energy); IR involves **vibrational transitions** (moderate energy); NMR involves **nuclear spin transitions** (lowest energy).
