# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between point and non-point sources of water pollution with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Point Source | Non-Point Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Pollution discharged at a specific, identifiable location via pipe/drain | Pollution spread over a wide area with no single entry point |
| **Identification** | Easy to monitor and regulate | Difficult to monitor, diffuse |
| **Examples** | (i) Industrial effluent from a factory, (ii) Municipal sewage outfall | (i) Agricultural fertilizer runoff, (ii) Urban stormwater runoff |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each; 1 mark each for two examples = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - July 2022]**
**Define BOD. Why is BOD measured at 20 °C for 5 days?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen (in mg/L) consumed by aerobic microorganisms to biochemically oxidize the organic matter in a water sample under standard conditions. *(1.5 marks)*
- **20 °C:** It is a standard reference temperature (close to average water temperature) chosen to ensure consistent microbial activity and allow comparison of results across laboratories. *(0.75 marks)*
- **5 days:** It represents the **carbonaceous BOD** phase (carbon oxidation completes in ~5–7 days; nitrification starts after 7 days). Five days is a practical, internationally standardized incubation period. *(0.75 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing BOD with COD — they are *not* the same. BOD measures *biodegradable* organics; COD measures *total* organics (biodegradable + non-biodegradable). Always state which.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using incorrect units (mg vs. mg/L, or mL vs. L) — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for unit errors.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert the dilution factor to a *decimal*, not a *percentage* (e.g., 1%, not 0.01 — this causes a 100× calculation error).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to mention **specific disease names** (Minamata, Itai-Itai, Blue Baby Syndrome) — naming the disease earns full marks, generic "health problems" loses marks.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Classify the major sources of water pollution. Discuss the effects of heavy metal pollution on human health with specific examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of water pollution — Classification:**

Water pollution sources are broadly classified into **point sources** and **non-point (diffuse) sources**.

1. **Point sources** — pollutants discharged from a single, identifiable location:
   - **Industrial effluents** (tanneries, electroplating units, paper mills, chemical plants)
   - **Domestic sewage** outfalls from municipal treatment plants
   - **Oil refinery** and **petrochemical** discharges
   - **Thermal power plant** cooling water and ash pond effluents

2. **Non-point sources** — pollutants entering water bodies from widespread areas:
   - **Agricultural runoff** (fertilizers, pesticides)
   - **Urban stormwater** runoff carrying oils, heavy metals, litter
   - **Atmospheric deposition** of SO₂, NOₓ (acid rain precursors)
   - **Mining, quarrying**, and construction site erosion

*(3 marks for classification with examples)*

**Effects of heavy metal pollution on human health:**

Heavy metals are **non-biodegradable** and **bioaccumulative** — they persist in the environment and concentrate in the food chain.

| Metal | Source | Specific Disease | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mercury (Hg)** | Chlor-alkali industry, coal combustion | **Minamata disease** | Severe neurological damage, vision/hearing loss, paralysis |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | Ni-Cd batteries, electroplating | **Itai-Itai disease** | Kidney failure, bone demineralization, painful joints |
| **Lead (Pb)** | Lead pipes, paints, gasoline | **Plumbism / Saturnism** | Nervous system damage, learning disabilities, anemia |
| **Arsenic (As)** | Pesticides, natural geology | **Arsenicosis** | Skin lesions, cancers (skin, lung, bladder) |
| **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** | Electroplating, leather tanning | Carcinogen | Lung cancer, kidney damage |

Heavy metals enter the body via **drinking water, food chain, and inhalation**, and are stored in **bones, kidneys, and liver**, causing **chronic toxicity** rather than acute poisoning.

*(4 marks for effects with specific disease names and metal sources)*

---

**(b) Explain the principle and procedure of the BOD test. A 7.5 mL sewage sample is diluted to 150 mL. The initial DO is 7.8 mg/L and after 5 days it drops to 4.2 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the sewage. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of BOD Test:**
The BOD test measures the **biologically mediated oxidation of organic matter** in a water sample by aerobic microorganisms. The oxygen consumed during 5 days at 20 °C — measured as the drop in dissolved oxygen (DO) of the diluted sample — is taken as the BOD₅.

**Procedure (Concise Steps):**
1. Take a known volume $V_s$ of the wastewater sample in a BOD bottle.
2. Dilute it with **oxygen-saturated dilution water** containing phosphate buffer, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, and FeCl₃ (to support microbial growth).
3. Determine the **initial DO ($D_1$)** of the diluted sample using the Winkler method.
4. Seal the bottle and incubate at **20 °C for 5 days** in a BOD incubator.
5. Measure the **final DO ($D_2$)** after 5 days.
6. Calculate BOD₅ using the formula:

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}
$$

where $P = V_s / (V_s + V_w)$ is the dilution factor.

*(3 marks for principle and procedure)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $V_s = 7.5$ mL
- Total volume $= 150$ mL → $V_w = 150 - 7.5 = 142.5$ mL
- $D_1 = 7.8$ mg/L
- $D_2 = 4.2$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Dilution factor:**

$$
P = \dfrac{7.5}{150} = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — DO drop:**

$$
\Delta D = 7.8 - 4.2 = 3.6 \text{ mg/L}
$$

**Step 3 — BOD calculation:**

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{3.6}{0.05} = 72 \text{ mg/L}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A BOD₅ of 72 mg/L indicates **weak to medium-strength sewage**; further treatment is required.

*(4 marks for numerical solution — 1 mark dilution, 1 mark DO drop, 2 marks final BOD with units)*

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

#### **[KTU University Exam - July 2023]**

**(a) What is eutrophication? Explain the stages of eutrophication and its harmful effects on aquatic life. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Eutrophication is the **excessive enrichment of a water body with nutrients** (primarily nitrates and phosphates), leading to **excessive growth of algae and aquatic plants**, followed by **oxygen depletion** and degradation of water quality.

**Stages of Eutrophication:**

1. **Nutrient Influx:** Excess nitrates (from fertilizer runoff, sewage) and phosphates (from detergents, agriculture) enter the lake or river.
2. **Algal Bloom:** The nutrients trigger rapid, uncontrolled growth of algae, forming a visible scum on the water surface.
3. **Light Blockage:** The dense algal mat **blocks sunlight** from reaching submerged plants, reducing photosynthesis.
4. **Plant Death:** Submerged plants die due to lack of light.
5. **Decomposition:** Aerobic bacteria decompose the dead algae, consuming dissolved oxygen.
6. **Oxygen Depletion:** DO drops sharply, causing **hypoxia** (< 2 mg/L) or **anoxia** (0 mg/L).
7. **Fish Kill:** Aquatic animals (fish, invertebrates) die of suffocation.
8. **Lake Succession:** The lake becomes a **swamp → marsh → dry land** over decades.

**Harmful Effects on Aquatic Life:**
- **Mass mortality of fish** and other aerobic organisms.
- **Loss of biodiversity** — sensitive species disappear first.
- **Release of toxins** — some algae (e.g., *Microcystis*, *Anabaena*) release **cyanotoxins** harmful to fish, livestock, and humans.
- **Bad odor and taste** in drinking water.
- **Clogging of water treatment filters** and increased treatment cost.

*(4 marks for stages + 3 marks for effects)*

---

**(b) Discuss the phenomenon of biomagnification with a suitable example. Why are persistent organic pollutants (POPs) particularly dangerous? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Biomagnification — Definition:**
Biomagnification is the **progressive increase in the concentration of a toxic, persistent substance** in the tissues of organisms at **successively higher trophic levels** in a food chain.

**Conditions Required for Biomagnification:**
- The pollutant must be **persistent** (resistant to degradation).
- It must be **lipophilic** (fat-soluble) so it accumulates in fatty tissue.
- It must be **non-biodegradable** or very slowly metabolized.

**Classic Example — DDT in an Aquatic Food Chain:**

| Trophic Level | Organism | DDT Concentration |
|---|---|---|
| Water | — | 0.003 ppb |
| Primary producer | Algae/Phytoplankton | 0.04 ppm |
| Primary consumer | Zooplankton, small fish | 0.5 ppm |
| Secondary consumer | Larger fish | 2.0 ppm |
| Tertiary consumer | Fish-eating birds (e.g., osprey) | 25 ppm |

The concentration increases by a factor of nearly **10 million** from water to bird tissue. Affected birds lay eggs with **thin, brittle shells**, causing **reproductive failure** — the iconic example from *Silent Spring*.

**Why Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) are Dangerous:**

1. **Long environmental half-life** — persist for years to decades (e.g., DDT half-life ≈ 15 years in soil).
2. **Long-range transport** — vaporize in warm regions, condense in cold regions (grasshopper effect), contaminating the Arctic and Antarctic.
3. **Bioaccumulation** in fatty tissue of organisms.
4. **Biomagnification** up the food chain.
5. **Endocrine disruption** — interfere with hormones (estrogen, testosterone).
6. **Carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic** effects on humans.
7. **Stockholm Convention (2001)** — international treaty to eliminate/restrict 12 initial POPs (the "dirty dozen") including DDT, dioxins, PCBs, aldrin, chlordane, heptachlor, hexachlorobenzene, and mirex.

*(4 marks for biomagnification with example + 3 marks for POPs dangers)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Sources & Effects**

### 📌 Core Concepts (Must Memorize)
- [ ] **BOD₅ formula:** $B = (D_1 - D_2)/P$, where $P = V_s/(V_s + V_w)$
- [ ] **COD formula:** $(a - b) \times N \times 8 \times 1000 / V$ (factor **8** for O₂)
- [ ] **BOD test conditions:** 20 °C, 5 days, in dark, oxygen-saturated dilution water
- [ ] **COD oxidant:** K₂Cr₂O₇ in 50% H₂SO₄; titrant: FAS (Mohr's salt)
- [ ] **BIS 10500 DO standard:** ≥ 5 mg/L for aquatic life
- [ ] **BOD > COD is impossible** (COD includes non-biodegradable + biodegradable)

### 📌 Specific Disease–Pollutant Pairings
- [ ] **Hg (Mercury)** → **Minamata disease** (Japan, 1956)
- [ ] **Cd (Cadmium)** → **Itai-Itai disease** (Japan, 1950s)
- [ ] **NO₃⁻ > 45 mg/L** → **Blue Baby Syndrome** (Methemoglobinemia)
- [ ] **F⁻ > 1.5 mg/L** → **Fluorosis** (skeletal, dental)
- [ ] **As (Arsenic)** → **Arsenicosis** (skin cancer)
- [ ] **Pb (Lead)** → **Plumbism** (anemia, neurological damage)

### 📌 Numerical Problem Hot-Spots
- [ ] **Always convert mL to decimal fraction** for $P$ (e.g., 10 mL in 1000 mL → $P = 0.01$, not 1%).
- [ ] **Units are critical:** BOD/COD expressed in **mg/L = ppm** (for dilute aqueous solutions).
- [ ] **Typical BOD₅ values:** untreated domestic sewage = 200–400 mg/L; treated effluent < 30 mg/L.
- [ ] **COD > BOD always**; ratio COD/BOD indicates biodegradability (lower = more biodegradable).

### 📌 Classification Mastery
- [ ] **Point sources** = single identifiable discharge (factory pipe, sewage outfall).
- [ ] **Non-point sources** = diffuse, widespread (agricultural fields, urban roads, atmosphere).
- [ ] **Pollutants by nature:** Physical, Chemical (organic + inorganic), Biological.

### 📌 High-Yield Terminology
- [ ] **Bioaccumulation** = buildup in a single organism over time.
- [ ] **Biomagnification** = increase across trophic levels.
- [ ] **Eutrophication** = nutrient enrichment → algal bloom → oxygen depletion.
- [ ] **Thermal pollution** = hot water discharge → reduced DO (Henry's Law).
- [ ] **POPs (Stockholm Convention, 2001)** = 12 initial "dirty dozen" pollutants.

> **Final Exam Strategy:** KTU 2024 Module 4 questions typically combine (i) a **3-mark conceptual question** (definition, classification, disease naming) and (ii) a **14-mark question with internal choice** (theory + numerical). Practice at least **3 BOD numericals** and **2 COD numericals** before the exam, and memorize the **disease–pollutant pairings** verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Greenhouse Gases
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Gases

# Greenhouse Gases – Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Greenhouse Gas (GHG)** is a gaseous component of the atmosphere — both natural and anthropogenic — that **absorbs and re-emits infrared (IR) radiation** within the thermal infrared range, thereby contributing to the **greenhouse effect** and regulating the Earth's atmospheric temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):** Greenhouse gases are polyatomic molecules possessing a **permanent or transient dipole moment** that allows them to vibrate at frequencies corresponding to the Earth's outgoing infrared radiation (wavelength range ≈ **4 µm to 100 µm**), enabling absorption and re-emission of this energy.

The principal GHGs recognized under the **IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change)** and the **UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change)** are:

| S.No | Greenhouse Gas | Chemical Formula | Primary Source |
|:----:|:---------------|:----------------:|:---------------|
| 1 | Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation |
| 2 | Methane | CH₄ | Wetlands, livestock, rice paddies, landfills |
| 3 | Nitrous Oxide | N₂O | Agricultural soils, industrial processes |
| 4 | Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) | CCl₂F₂, CClF₃ | Refrigerants, aerosols (now banned) |
| 5 | Hydrofluorocarbons | HFCs | Air conditioners, refrigeration |
| 6 | Water Vapour | H₂O | Evaporation (most abundant natural GHG) |
| 7 | Ozone | O₃ | Tropospheric photochemistry |
| 8 | Sulphur Hexafluoride | SF₆ | Electrical insulation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blanket" Analogy for Engineering Students:**
> Imagine Earth wrapped in a **selective infrared blanket** made of greenhouse gas molecules. **Short-wave solar radiation** (visible light, UV) from the Sun passes *through* this blanket and warms the Earth's surface. The warm Earth then tries to cool itself by emitting **long-wave infrared (IR) radiation** back into space. The GHG molecules — acting like the fibres of the blanket — **absorb this outgoing IR radiation**, vibrate, and **re-emit** it in all directions, with a significant fraction sent back to Earth's surface. This traps heat, raising the **mean global surface temperature** above the natural radiative equilibrium value.

**In one line:** GHGs are *spectroscopically active* gases that are transparent to incoming sunlight but opaque to outgoing Earth radiation — an asymmetric transmissivity that produces net warming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only certain molecules?** The fundamental rule is **molecular symmetry**. Diatomic homonuclear molecules like **N₂** and **O₂** are IR-inactive because they possess no dipole moment. In contrast, asymmetric polyatomic molecules like **CO₂** (linear but with two different bond dipoles that can bend) and **CH₄** (tetrahedral, non-linear) have vibrational modes that change the dipole moment, making them **IR-active**.

## 1.3 The Physics of Greenhouse Effect in One Statement

The Earth's energy balance is governed by:

$$ \text{Incoming Solar Radiation} = \text{Reflected Solar} + \text{Outgoing IR (OLR)} $$

Without GHGs, Earth's mean surface temperature would be approximately **–18 °C (255 K)**. With natural GHGs, the actual mean surface temperature is approximately **+15 °C (288 K)**. The **33 K difference** is the natural greenhouse effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Point:** The enhanced greenhouse effect (anthropogenic warming) is the *additional* warming caused by human-emitted GHGs (primarily CO₂, CH₄, N₂O) that pushes the global mean temperature above the natural baseline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Greenhouse Gases – Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of the Greenhouse Effect (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Step 1 – Solar Incidence:** The Sun emits radiation approximating a **blackbody at 5778 K**, with peak emission in the visible region (~0.5 µm). Roughly **1361 W/m²** reaches the top of the atmosphere (the **solar constant, S₀**).

2. **Step 2 – Atmospheric Transmission:** Short-wave radiation passes largely unimpeded through the atmosphere because the major constituents (N₂, O₂, Ar) do not absorb visible light.

3. **Step 3 – Surface Absorption:** ~70% of the incoming solar radiation is absorbed by the Earth's surface, heating it. The surface re-emits as a **blackbody at ~288 K**, with peak emission in the IR region (~10 µm) per **Wien's Displacement Law**.

4. **Step 4 – IR Absorption by GHGs:** Outgoing IR photons encounter GHG molecules. Molecules with the correct vibrational frequencies absorb these photons, transitioning from the ground vibrational state to excited vibrational states.

5. **Step 5 – Vibrational Relaxation & Re-emission:** Excited molecules lose energy through collisions and re-emit IR photons in **random directions** (isotropic emission). Roughly **half** of these re-emitted photons are directed back toward Earth, causing **downward IR flux** and warming.

6. **Step 6 – Radiative Forcing:** The net change in energy flux at the tropopause due to a GHG is called **radiative forcing (ΔF)**, measured in **W/m²**.

## 2.2 Molecular Basis of IR Absorption

For a molecule to absorb IR radiation, **two conditions** must be met:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for IR Activity:** A vibrational mode is IR-active if and only if it produces a **change in the dipole moment (∂µ/∂Q ≠ 0)**.

- **CO₂** has 3N – 5 = 4 normal modes (linear): symmetric stretch (IR-inactive), asymmetric stretch (IR-active, ν₃ ≈ 2349 cm⁻¹), and two bending modes (IR-active, ν₂ ≈ 667 cm⁻¹).
- **CH₄** (tetrahedral, 3N – 6 = 9 modes) has IR-active stretching (≈ 3019 cm⁻¹) and bending (≈ 1306 cm⁻¹) modes.
- **N₂O** has strong absorption near **1285 cm⁻¹** and **2224 cm⁻¹**.
- **CCl₂F₂ (CFC-12)** absorbs broadly in the **IR window region (8–12 µm)** where Earth's surface emission peaks — making it an exceptionally potent GHG.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Use |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------------|:------|:----|
| 1 | Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Blackbody emission) | $E = \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Surface/emitter radiant exitance |
| 2 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | m | Peak emission wavelength |
| 3 | Solar Constant | $S_0 = 1361$ | W/m² | Top-of-atmosphere solar irradiance |
| 4 | Earth albedo reflection | $F_{\text{ref}} = \alpha \cdot S_0 / 4$ | W/m² | Reflected short-wave flux (α ≈ 0.30) |
| 5 | Radiative Forcing (single gas) | $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\dfrac{C}{C_0}\right)$ | W/m² | CO₂ forcing (IPCC formula) |
| 6 | Global Warming Potential | $\text{GWP}_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot [x(t)]\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_r \cdot [r(t)]\,dt}$ | dimensionless | Comparative warming index |
| 7 | CO₂-equivalent emissions | $E_{\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}} = \sum_i m_i \cdot \text{GWP}_i$ | kg CO₂-eq | Standardised emission metric |
| 8 | Bond Dipole Rule | $\dfrac{\partial \mu}{\partial Q} \neq 0$ | — | IR activity criterion |
| 9 | Vibrational Frequency | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | cm⁻¹ | Hooke's law for diatomic approx. |
| 10 | Beer–Lambert Law | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | — | Atmospheric IR absorption |

Where:
- $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W m⁻² K⁻⁴ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant)
- $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (Wien's displacement constant)
- $C, C_0$ = current and pre-industrial CO₂ concentration (ppm)
- $a_x, a_r$ = radiative efficiencies of gas *x* and reference (CO₂)
- $TH$ = time horizon (typically **100 years**)
- $k$ = bond force constant, $\mu$ = reduced mass

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU engineers must know GHGs:** Every thermal power plant, refrigeration cycle, cement kiln, and metallurgical process is now evaluated under **carbon accounting** standards (ISO 14064, GHG Protocol). Chemical engineers select refrigerants based on **GWP** (low-GWP alternatives like HFO-1234yf, CO₂ in transcritical cycles). Civil engineers evaluate embodied carbon in concrete. Environmental engineers design **carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS)** systems. The chemistry of GHGs underpins all **climate policy, ESG reporting, and sustainable development** frameworks.

## 2.5 The Major GHGs – Comparative Analysis

### A. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** Effectively **infinite** on human timescales (no chemical sink other than slow carbonate–silicate weathering).
- **Pre-industrial concentration:** ~**280 ppm**; Current (2024): **~422 ppm** (Mauna Loa).
- **Anthropogenic share:** ~**74%** of total GHG emissions.
- **Major chemistry:** Dissolves in seawater to form carbonic acid → **ocean acidification** (pH dropped from 8.2 to 8.1 since pre-industrial era).

### B. Methane (CH₄)
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** ~**12 years** (oxidised to CO₂ by OH radicals).
- **GWP-100:** **28–34** (recent estimates 80–84 with climate-carbon feedback).
- **Sources:** Enteric fermentation in ruminants, paddy rice cultivation, natural gas leakage, landfills, wetlands.
- **Sink:** Tropospheric reaction with hydroxyl radical (·OH).

### C. Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)
- **Lifetime:** ~**114 years**; **GWP-100:** **265–298**.
- **Sources:** Synthetic fertilisers (Haber–Bosch ammonia), biomass burning, adipic acid production.
- **Stratospheric chemistry:** Photolysis produces NO → catalytically destroys **stratospheric ozone**.

### D. Fluorinated Gases (CFCs, HFCs, SF₆)
- **Extremely long lifetimes** (CFC-11: 52 years; SF₆: 3,200 years).
- **GWPs:** CFC-12 ≈ 10,900; HFC-134a ≈ 1,430; SF₆ ≈ 23,500.
- **Regulated under:** Montreal Protocol (1987) for CFCs; Kigali Amendment (2016) for HFCs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Greenhouse Gases – Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Earth's Radiative Equilibrium Temperature

We start with the assumption that Earth behaves as a **blackbody radiator** with a defined albedo.

**Step 1:** The Sun delivers the solar constant $S_0 = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ perpendicular to its rays at the top of the atmosphere.

**Step 2:** Because Earth is a sphere of cross-section $\pi R^2$ but radiates from its entire surface area $4\pi R^2$, the average insolation per unit area is $S_0 / 4$.

**Step 3:** Of this incoming radiation, a fraction $\alpha$ (the **Bond albedo**, ≈ 0.30) is reflected back to space by clouds, ice, and surface. The absorbed fraction is $(1 - \alpha)$.

**Step 4:** At thermodynamic equilibrium, the absorbed solar power must equal the emitted IR power:

$$ (1 - \alpha) \cdot \frac{S_0}{4} = \sigma T_e^4 $$

**Step 5:** Substituting numerical values:

$$ T_e = \left[ \frac{(1 - 0.30)(1361)}{4 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8}} \right]^{1/4} $$

**Step 6:** Numerator: $0.70 \times 1361 = 952.7$ W/m². Dividing by 4 gives 238.2 W/m².

$$ T_e = \left[ \frac{238.2}{5.670 \times 10^{-8}} \right]^{1/4} $$

**Step 7:** Computing the inside of the bracket: $238.2 / (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) = 4.201 \times 10^{9}$ K⁴.

**Step 8:** Taking the fourth root:

$$ T_e = (4.201 \times 10^9)^{0.25} \approx 254.6 \text{ K} \approx -18.5 \text{ °C} $$

**Conclusion:** Without an atmosphere, Earth's mean temperature would be **254.6 K**. The observed value is **288 K**, giving a natural greenhouse warming of **33.4 K**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "33 K Greenhouse" is the natural baseline.** Anthropogenic GHGs add approximately **+1.1 °C** of further warming (IPCC AR6, 2021) above this baseline.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – CO₂ Radiative Forcing (Myhre et al. Equation)

The IPCC uses an empirical logarithmic relationship between CO₂ concentration and radiative forcing:

$$ \Delta F = A \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) $$

**Step 1:** The constant $A = 5.35$ W/m² is empirically derived from radiative transfer models and line-by-line calculations for CO₂.

**Step 2:** $C_0$ is the pre-industrial reference concentration (280 ppm).

**Step 3:** $C$ is the instantaneous atmospheric CO₂ concentration.

**Step 4:** For a doubling of CO₂ from 280 → 560 ppm:

$$ \Delta F_{2\times} = 5.35 \cdot \ln(2) = 5.35 \times 0.6931 $$

$$ \Delta F_{2\times} \approx 3.71 \text{ W/m}^2 $$

**Step 5:** This **3.7 W/m²** is the canonical climate sensitivity forcing used in nearly all IPCC General Circulation Models (GCMs).

**Step 6:** Combining with the climate sensitivity parameter $\lambda$ (typically 0.5–1.2 K per W/m²), the equilibrium temperature rise from a CO₂ doubling is:

$$ \Delta T_{2\times} = \lambda \cdot \Delta F_{2\times} \approx 0.8 \times 3.71 \approx 3.0 \text{ K} $$

This is the well-known **Climate Sensitivity** range of **2.5 K – 4.0 K** per CO₂ doubling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – CO₂-Equivalent Emissions

For a multi-gas emission inventory:

$$ E_{\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}} = \sum_{i} m_i \times \text{GWP}_i $$

**Worked example** for a typical Indian household annual emission inventory:

| Gas | Mass emitted (kg) | GWP-100 | CO₂-eq (kg) |
|:----|:-----------------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| CO₂ (electricity, transport) | 4,500 | 1 | 4,500 |
| CH₄ (LPG leakage, waste) | 1.2 | 28 | 33.6 |
| N₂O (fertiliser, combustion) | 0.3 | 265 | 79.5 |
| HFC-134a (refrigerant leak) | 0.05 | 1,430 | 71.5 |

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$ E_{\text{CO}_2\text{-eq}} = (4500 \times 1) + (1.2 \times 28) + (0.3 \times 265) + (0.05 \times 1430) $$

$$ = 4500 + 33.6 + 79.5 + 71.5 $$

$$ = 4684.6 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per year} $$

**Conclusion:** Even though the **mass** of CH₄, N₂O, and HFC is tiny, their **CO₂-equivalent impact** is ~4% of the total — a key insight in **GHG accounting**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 – Vibrational Frequency of CO₂ (Asymmetric Stretch)

Using the harmonic oscillator approximation for a diatomic *effective* mode:

$$ \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} $$

**Step 1:** The CO₂ asymmetric stretch has force constant $k \approx 1550$ N/m.

**Step 2:** Reduced mass for the C–O bond (relative atomic masses: C = 12, O = 16):

$$ \mu = \frac{m_C \cdot m_O}{m_C + m_O} = \frac{12 \times 16}{12 + 16} = \frac{192}{28} = 6.857 \text{ u} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to kg: $\mu = 6.857 \times 1.661 \times 10^{-27} = 1.139 \times 10^{-26}$ kg.

**Step 4:** Compute $k/\mu = 1550 / (1.139 \times 10^{-26}) = 1.361 \times 10^{29}$ s⁻².

**Step 5:** $\sqrt{k/\mu} = 1.167 \times 10^{14.5} \approx 3.689 \times 10^{13}$ rad/s? Let me recompute carefully:

$$ \sqrt{1.361 \times 10^{29}} = 1.167 \times 10^{14.5} \approx 3.69 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 6:** Convert to wavenumber: $\tilde{\nu} = 3.69 \times 10^{14} / (2\pi \times 3 \times 10^{10}) = 3.69 \times 10^{14} / (1.885 \times 10^{11})$

$$ \tilde{\nu} \approx 1957 \text{ cm}^{-1} $$

**Step 7:** The experimental value for the asymmetric stretch of CO₂ is **2349 cm⁻¹**. The discrepancy arises because the harmonic oscillator underestimates anharmonic coupling; the more accurate value reflects Fermi resonance between ν₃ and 2ν₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Knowing *which vibrational mode* of a GHG falls in the **8–12 µm IR window** (where Earth's surface emits) is more important than the exact numerical value, because that overlap determines the molecule's potency as a greenhouse gas.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 – Methane's Atmospheric Lifetime via ·OH Oxidation

The dominant tropospheric sink of CH₄ is:

$$ \text{CH}_4 + \cdot\text{OH} \rightarrow \cdot\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (k = 6.3 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^3/\text{molecule/s at 298 K}) $$

**Step 1:** Pseudo-first-order rate constant (assuming [·OH] = 1 × 10⁶ molecules/cm³):

$$ k' = k \times [\cdot\text{OH}] = (6.3 \times 10^{-15})(1 \times 10^6) = 6.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 2:** Atmospheric lifetime:

$$ \tau_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{1}{k'} = \frac{1}{6.3 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.587 \times 10^{8} \text{ s} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to years: $1.587 \times 10^{8} / (3.156 \times 10^7) \approx 5.0$ years.

The actual observed lifetime (~12 years) is longer because tropospheric [·OH] is lower in the Southern Hemisphere and seasonally variable; a global average of ~0.4 × 10⁶ molecules/cm³ gives τ ≈ 12.6 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why short lifetime = high GWP:** Methane has a high GWP because although it survives only ~12 years, its **instantaneous radiative efficiency per molecule** is **~28× that of CO₂** over a 100-year horizon (and ~84× over 20 years).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Greenhouse Gases – Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: The Greenhouse Effect Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: 5778 K Blackbody] --> B[S0 = 1361 W/m2 Solar Constant]
    B --> C[Top of Atmosphere]
    C --> D{Atmospheric Window}
    D -- Transmitted --> E[Earth Surface: 288 K]
    D -- Reflected alpha = 0.30 --> F[Outer Space]
    E --> G[Outgoing Longwave Radiation OLR ~ 10 micrometer]
    G --> H{GHG Molecules}
    H -- CO2 --> I[Absorb at 667 and 2349 cm-1]
    H -- CH4 --> J[Absorb at 1306 and 3019 cm-1]
    H -- H2O --> K[Absorb broadly 0-20000 cm-1]
    H -- N2O --> L[Absorb at 1285 and 2224 cm-1]
    I --> M[Vibrational Excitation]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Isotropic Re-emission]
    N -- 50 percent to space --> F
    N -- 50 percent back to Earth --> E
    E --> O[Net Surface Warming Delta T]
    O --> P[Enhanced Greenhouse Effect]
    P --> Q[Climate Change Impacts]
```

## 4.2 GHG Emission Sector Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Global Anthropogenic GHG Sources] --> B[Energy Sector 73 percent]
    A --> C[Agriculture 18 percent]
    A --> D[Industrial Processes 5 percent]
    A --> E[Waste 3 percent]
    A --> F[Land Use Change 1 percent]

    B --> B1[Electricity Generation]
    B --> B2[Transport]
    B --> B3[Buildings Heating]
    B --> B4[Manufacturing Fuels]

    C --> C1[Enteric Fermentation CH4]
    C --> C2[Rice Cultivation CH4]
    C --> C3[Synthetic Fertiliser N2O]
    C --> C4[Manure Management]

    D --> D1[Cement Production CO2]
    D --> D2[Adipic Acid N2O]
    D --> D3[Refrigerant Leaks HFCs]
    D --> D4[Aluminium Smelting PFCs]

    E --> E1[Landfill CH4]
    E --> E2[Wastewater N2O]

    F --> F1[Deforestation CO2]
    F --> F2[Soil Degradation]
```

## 4.3 Atmospheric Residence Time vs GWP Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GHG Comparison] --> B[Short Lifetime less than 20 years]
    A --> C[Medium Lifetime 20 to 200 years]
    A --> D[Very Long Lifetime greater than 1000 years]

    B --> B1[CH4: 12 years, GWP 28]
    B --> B2[N2O: 114 years, GWP 265]
    B --> B3[HFC-134a: 14 years, GWP 1430]

    C --> C1[CFC-11: 52 years, GWP 4750]
    C --> C2[CFC-12: 102 years, GWP 10900]
    C --> C3[N2O: 114 years, GWP 265]

    D --> D1[SF6: 3200 years, GWP 23500]
    D --> D2[CF4: 50000 years, GWP 6630]
    D --> D3[CCl4: 26 years, GWP 1400]
```

## 4.4 IR Absorption Spectrum of Major GHGs (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR Spectrum 4 to 100 micrometer] --> B[Earth Emission Peak 10 micrometer]
    B --> C[Atmospheric IR Window 8 to 12 micrometer]

    C --> D[CO2 strong at 15 micrometer]
    C --> E[O3 strong at 9.6 micrometer]
    C --> F[CH4 strong at 7.7 micrometer]
    C --> G[CFC-12 strong at 8.7 to 9.1 micrometer]
    C --> H[N2O strong at 7.8 and 8.6 micrometer]

    D --> I[Effective at trapping Earth radiation]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J[Net Radiative Forcing Delta F]
    J --> K[Surface Temperature Increase]
```

## 4.5 Mitigation Hierarchy Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Climate Mitigation Strategies] --> B[Avoid Avoid emissions]
    A --> C[Reduce Improve efficiency]
    A --> D[Substitute Low carbon energy]
    A --> E[Capture CCUS]

    B --> B1[Behavioural change]
    B --> B2[Demand reduction]

    C --> C1[LED lighting]
    C --> C2[High efficiency motors]
    C --> C3[Building insulation]

    D --> D1[Solar PV]
    D --> D2[Wind power]
    D --> D3[Green hydrogen]
    D --> D4[Nuclear]

    E --> E1[Post combustion capture amine]
    E --> E2[Pre combustion capture]
    E --> E3[Oxy fuel combustion]
    E --> E4[Direct air capture DAC]

    A --> F[Sequester Geological storage]
    A --> G[Offset Forests soil]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Greenhouse Gases – Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

> Define greenhouse gases. Give two examples and explain why diatomic molecules like N₂ and O₂ are **not** greenhouse gases.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Greenhouse gases are atmospheric gases that absorb and re-emit infrared (IR) radiation, contributing to the greenhouse effect and warming of the Earth's surface.

**Examples (1 mark):** Carbon dioxide (CO₂) and methane (CH₄).

**Why N₂ and O₂ are not GHGs (1 mark):** Both are **homonuclear diatomic molecules** that possess no permanent dipole moment. Their vibrational modes do **not** produce a change in dipole moment ($\partial \mu / \partial Q = 0$), making them **IR-inactive**. They cannot absorb Earth's outgoing longwave radiation, so they do not contribute to the greenhouse effect.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

> What is **Global Warming Potential (GWP)**? Why is methane's GWP-100 (28) higher than CO₂'s (1) even though it is present in much smaller concentration?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** GWP is an index measuring the **time-integrated radiative forcing** of a unit mass of a greenhouse gas relative to the same mass of CO₂ over a chosen time horizon (typically 100 years).

**Calculation basis (1 mark):** $\text{GWP}_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot [x(t)]\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_r \cdot [r(t)]\,dt}$, where $a$ is radiative efficiency and $[x(t)]$ is the time-decaying concentration.

**Why CH₄ has higher GWP (1 mark):** Although CH₄ concentration is ~2.0 ppm (vs CO₂'s 422 ppm), each CH₄ molecule has a much **higher instantaneous radiative efficiency** (~28× CO₂ per molecule over 100 years) due to its strong IR absorption bands in the **8–12 µm atmospheric window**. Thus on a per-mass basis, 1 kg of CH₄ traps 28× more heat than 1 kg of CO₂.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each module in the End Semester Examination carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two full alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive: CO₂ Chemistry & Climate Sensitivity

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** [Module 4]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of neat labelled diagrams, explain the **molecular mechanism of the greenhouse effect** for CO₂. Discuss the role of asymmetric stretching and bending vibrational modes. How does IR activity relate to dipole moment change?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Derive the **radiative equilibrium temperature of the Earth** with and without the greenhouse effect. Show that a doubling of pre-industrial CO₂ concentration (280 → 560 ppm) produces a radiative forcing of **3.71 W/m²**, and estimate the resulting equilibrium temperature rise.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) – Molecular Mechanism (7 marks)

**Step 1: Introduction to molecular vibrations (1 mark)**
CO₂ is a linear triatomic molecule with 3N – 5 = **4 normal vibrational modes**: symmetric stretch (ν₁, ~1388 cm⁻¹), asymmetric stretch (ν₃, ~2349 cm⁻¹), and two degenerate bending modes (ν₂, ~667 cm⁻¹). CO₂ is a **nonpolar molecule** in its equilibrium geometry, but vibrations can induce a transient dipole moment.

**Step 2: IR-active modes (2 marks)**
According to the **IR selection rule** ($\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$):
- The symmetric stretch ν₁ produces **no change in dipole moment** → **IR-inactive** (Raman-active).
- The asymmetric stretch ν₃ produces a **change in dipole moment along the molecular axis** → **IR-active** at 2349 cm⁻¹ (~4.3 µm).
- The bending modes ν₂ produce a **perpendicular dipole moment** → **IR-active** at 667 cm⁻¹ (~15 µm).

**Step 3: Coupling to Earth's IR emission (2 marks)**
Earth's surface at 288 K emits IR radiation peaking near **10 µm** (Wien's law: $\lambda_{\max} = b/T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} / 288 = 10.06$ µm). The 15 µm band (ν₂) overlaps strongly with this emission — this is the primary "**15 µm CO₂ band**" responsible for CO₂'s greenhouse effect. The 4.3 µm band is less effective because little Earth radiation is emitted at that wavelength.

**Step 4: Absorption and re-emission (2 marks)**
When an outgoing IR photon at 667 cm⁻¹ strikes a CO₂ molecule, it excites the bending mode. The excited molecule undergoes **vibrational–rotational energy transfer** via collisions, then spontaneously emits a photon in a **random direction** (isotropic). Approximately **half** of these re-emitted photons return to Earth, providing the **downward IR flux** that warms the surface. This trapping of outgoing energy is the **greenhouse effect**.

---

##### Part (b) – Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Blackbody assumption (0.5 mark)**
Treat Earth as a blackbody at equilibrium temperature $T_e$.

**Step 2: Incoming absorbed flux (1 mark)**
The fraction of solar constant absorbed by Earth, accounting for geometry and albedo:

$$ F_{\text{in}} = (1 - \alpha)\frac{S_0}{4} = (1 - 0.30)\frac{1361}{4} = 238.2 \text{ W/m}^2 $$

**Step 3: Equilibrium without atmosphere (1 mark)**
Without GHGs, all absorbed energy is re-radiated:

$$ 238.2 = \sigma T_e^4 $$

$$ T_e = \left(\frac{238.2}{5.670 \times 10^{-8}}\right)^{1/4} = 254.6 \text{ K} = -18.5 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 4: Natural greenhouse addition (1 mark)**
With natural GHGs (H₂O, CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, O₃), the additional downwelling IR flux raises the surface temperature to the observed 288 K (15 °C). Natural greenhouse warming = 288 – 254.6 = **33.4 K**.

**Step 5: CO₂ doubling forcing (1.5 marks)**
Using the Myhre equation:

$$ \Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\frac{560}{280}\right) = 5.35 \ln(2) = 5.35 \times 0.6931 = 3.71 \text{ W/m}^2 $$

**Step 6: Equilibrium temperature rise (1 mark)**
Using climate sensitivity parameter $\lambda \approx 0.8$ K/(W/m²):

$$ \Delta T_{2\times} = \lambda \cdot \Delta F_{2\times} = 0.8 \times 3.71 = 2.97 \text{ K} $$

**Step 7: Conclusion (1 mark)**
A doubling of atmospheric CO₂ would, in equilibrium, raise the mean global surface temperature by approximately **3.0 K** (range 2.5–4.0 K across IPCC models). This is the cornerstone **climate sensitivity** parameter underpinning all IPCC projections.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: GWP, Methane & Mitigation

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** [Module 4]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** with its mathematical definition. Compare the GWP-100 of CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, and SF₆, and discuss why SF₆ (used as an insulating gas in high-voltage switchgear) is considered the most potent synthetic GHG.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **major sources, sinks, and lifetime** of methane (CH₄) in the atmosphere. How does methane chemistry differ from CO₂ in terms of its long-term warming impact? Outline **three engineering strategies** to reduce methane emissions from Indian agriculture and waste sectors.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) – GWP and SF₆ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Definition of GWP (1 mark)**
The Global Warming Potential is the cumulative radiative forcing of 1 kg of a GHG over a time horizon (TH) relative to 1 kg of CO₂ over the same horizon:

$$ \text{GWP}_x = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot [x(t)]\,dt}{\displaystyle\int_0^{TH} a_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot [\text{CO}_2(t)]\,dt} $$

where $a_x$ is the **radiative efficiency** (W/m² per kg) and $[x(t)]$ is the time-decaying atmospheric abundance of gas *x*.

**Step 2: Time horizon and integration (1 mark)**
The standard TH is **100 years** (per IPCC AR6). The integration captures the fact that short-lived gases like CH₄ have a large instantaneous effect but disappear, while long-lived gases like SF₆ accumulate. The choice of TH critically affects GWP values.

**Step 3: Comparison table (2 marks)**

| Gas | Lifetime | GWP-100 (AR6) | Dominant Sink |
|:----|:--------:|:-------------:|:--------------|
| CO₂ | Indefinite | 1 | Ocean, biosphere, weathering |
| CH₄ | 12 years | 27.9 | Tropospheric ·OH oxidation |
| N₂O | 109 years | 273 | Stratospheric photolysis |
| SF₆ | 3,200 years | 25,200 | None (only very slow photodissociation) |

**Step 4: Why SF₆ is the most potent (3 marks)**

- **Extreme stability:** The **octahedral S–F bonds** in SF₆ are kinetically inert. SF₆ does not react with ·OH, O₃, or any common atmospheric species.
- **No atmospheric sink:** With a lifetime of 3,200 years, every kg emitted today is **still warming** the planet in the year 5226 CE.
- **Strong IR absorption:** SF₆ has a vibrational mode at **948 cm⁻¹** (~10.5 µm) that overlaps almost exactly with peak Earth emission — an "ideal" blackbody absorber in the atmospheric window.
- **High radiative efficiency per molecule:** The S–F symmetric stretch is IR-inactive (octahedral symmetry, no dipole change), but the ν₃ mode produces a strong IR band.
- **Engineering relevance:** SF₆ is used as an insulating gas in **gas-insulated switchgear (GIS)**, high-voltage circuit breakers, and magnesium die-casting. Even small leaks contribute massively to GWPs; modern substations are moving to **g³ (CO₂-based) or vacuum interrupters** as substitutes.

---

##### Part (b) – Methane Chemistry & Mitigation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Atmospheric sources of CH₄ (1.5 marks)**
Methane sources are **biogenic** (wetlands, ruminants, rice paddies, termites, oceans), **thermogenic** (natural gas seeps, fossil fuel extraction), and **pyrogenic** (biomass burning). Global emissions: ~**570 Tg CH₄/year** (IPCC AR6, top-down estimate ~600 Tg).

**Step 2: Atmospheric sink (1 mark)**
The dominant sink is reaction with tropospheric hydroxyl radicals (·OH):

$$ \text{CH}_4 + \cdot\text{OH} \rightarrow \cdot\text{CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (k = 6.3 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^3/\text{molecule/s}) $$

The ·CH₃ radical ultimately oxidises to CO₂ via formaldehyde and CO intermediates. Minor sinks include soil uptake by methanotrophs (~5%) and stratospheric loss (~10%).

**Step 3: Difference from CO₂ (1.5 marks)**

| Property | CO₂ | CH₄ |
|:---------|:----|:----|
| Lifetime | Effectively infinite | ~12 years |
| GWP-100 | 1 | 27.9 |
| GWP-20 | 1 | 81.2 |
| Dominant sink | Oceanic + biological absorption | Atmospheric ·OH oxidation |
| Short-term climate effect | Slow, cumulative | Strong, short-lived |

Methane is a **"flow pollutant"** — its concentration responds rapidly to changes in emissions — whereas CO₂ is a **"stock pollutant"** that accumulates. Cutting CH₄ emissions yields fast climate benefits (within decades); cutting CO₂ yields permanent long-term benefits.

**Step 4: Engineering mitigation strategies for India (3 marks)**

1. **Livestock dietary additives:** Adding **3-nitrooxypropanol (3-NOP)** to cattle feed reduces enteric fermentation CH₄ by ~30%. India's ICAR-NIANP is piloting this in dairy cooperatives.

2. **Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) in paddy cultivation:** Traditional continuous flooding generates large CH₄ fluxes. AWD — periodic drying of paddies — can cut CH₄ by **40–70%** while maintaining yields. Indian state agricultural departments (e.g., Tamil Nadu, Odisha) have scaled AWD via drip and laser-levelled fields.

3. **Landfill methane capture with energy recovery:** Installing **anaerobic digesters** and **landfill gas (LFG) collection systems** at municipal solid waste sites captures CH₄ (50% CH₄ + 50% CO₂) for electricity generation or CNG upgrading. This converts a potent GHG into useful energy. The Indore and Lucknow smart-city projects have implemented this.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "greenhouse effect" with "ozone depletion":** These are *different* problems. CFCs cause ozone depletion (stratospheric chemistry), AND act as GHGs (tropospheric IR absorption). Do not mix the mechanisms.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the forcing equation:** $\Delta F$ must be quoted in **W/m²**, not "watts". Always include **per square metre**.
> 3. **Mixing GWP-20, GWP-100, GWP-500:** Each is valid; always specify the time horizon. For "long-term" policies, GWP-100 is the IPCC standard; for "near-term" mitigation (e.g., methane), GWP-20 is more relevant.
> 4. **Writing CO₂ as "Carbon Dioxide" without chemical context:** Examiners expect linkage between **molecular structure (linear, IR-active bending mode at 15 µm) and the macroscopic greenhouse effect**.
> 5. **Mistaking residence time for half-life:** Atmospheric lifetime τ ≠ radioactive half-life t₁/₂. For exponential decay, $t_{1/2} = \tau \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- **Greenhouse gases** are IR-active polyatomic molecules (with $\partial \mu / \partial Q \neq 0$) that absorb Earth's outgoing longwave radiation and re-emit it, causing warming.
- The **major natural GHGs** are **H₂O (60%), CO₂ (20%), O₃ (7%), N₂O (4%)**; **anthropogenic additions** include **CCl₂F₂, HFCs, SF₆**.
- Earth's natural greenhouse warming is **+33 K** (from 255 K to 288 K).
- **CO₂ is a stock pollutant** (long-lived, cumulative); **CH₄ is a flow pollutant** (short-lived, high GWP-20).
- The **15 µm CO₂ band** (ν₂ bending) is the dominant absorber of Earth radiation.
- **GWP-100 reference values (IPCC AR6):** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 27.9, N₂O = 273, HFC-134a = 1,430, SF₆ = 25,200.
- **CO₂ doubling forcing:** $\Delta F_{2\times} = 5.35 \ln 2 = 3.71$ W/m².
- **Equilibrium temperature rise per CO₂ doubling:** ~3.0 K (range 2.5–4.0 K).
- **Albedo of Earth** ≈ **0.30**; **solar constant** $S_0 = 1361$ W/m².
- **Carbon footprinting** uses CO₂-equivalent (CO₂-eq) = $\sum m_i \cdot \text{GWP}_i$.
- **Mitigation hierarchy:** Avoid → Reduce → Substitute → Capture → Sequester → Offset.
- **CFCs** are regulated under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**; HFCs under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)**.
- **Methane sinks** rely on tropospheric ·OH — a renewable but vulnerable oxidant (polluted air may reduce ·OH, increasing CH₄ lifetime).
- **N₂O** is the largest current ozone-depleting substance emitted by humans (stratospheric NO production).
- **Carbon capture technologies:** Post-combustion amine scrubbing, pre-combustion shift conversion, oxy-fuel combustion, and direct air capture (DAC).
- For KTU numerical problems, **always state the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W m⁻² K⁻⁴** and the **solar constant $S_0 = 1361$ W/m²** explicitly.
- The **IR atmospheric window** (8–12 µm) is the critical spectral region for greenhouse effect efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ozone Depletion. Control Methods. Sewage Water Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ozone Depletion. Control Methods. Sewage Water Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ozone Depletion — The Formal Definition

**Stratospheric Ozone Depletion** is the localized, anthropogenic reduction in the concentration of ozone ($\text{O}_3$) molecules within the Earth's stratosphere (the **ozone layer**, situated roughly 15–35 km above the surface). This depletion is caused primarily by the catalytic action of free radical species — most notably **chlorine (Cl·) and bromine (Br·) atoms** — liberated from photodissociated, human-emitted halogenated organics such as **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)**, **Halons**, and **Carbon Tetrachloride ($\text{CCl}_4$)**.

The phenomenon is quantified using the **Dobson Unit (DU)**, where 1 DU = $2.69 \times 10^{16}$ ozone molecules per cm².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Good Ozone vs. Bad Ozone**
> - **Stratospheric Ozone (Good):** Naturally forms the UV-shielding layer at ~25 km altitude. Absorbs harmful **UV-B (280–315 nm)** and **UV-C (100–280 nm)** radiation.
> - **Tropospheric Ozone (Bad):** A photochemical pollutant formed by NOx–VOC–sunlight reactions; a major component of urban smog and a respiratory irritant.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Broken Umbrella

Imagine the stratosphere as a giant **glasshouse umbrella** shielding every living being on Earth from the Sun's ultraviolet "sting." Ozone molecules are the tightly woven threads of this umbrella. When we released **CFCs (the chemical equivalent of industrial scissors)** from aerosols, refrigerators, and foam, these molecules drifted upward over **decades**. Once in the stratosphere, intense UV radiation snapped them, releasing atomic chlorine. **A single chlorine atom is a pair of scissors that can snip ~100,000 ozone threads** before being neutralized — a process called a **chain reaction**. The "Antarctic Ozone Hole" is simply a giant tear in the umbrella, observed every spring since the 1980s.

## 1.3 Sewage Water Treatment — The Formal Definition

**Sewage (or wastewater) Treatment** is the engineered, multistage physico-chemical-biological process by which municipal/industrial wastewater is purified of **suspended solids, dissolved organics, pathogenic microorganisms, and nutrient pollutants (N, P)** before safe discharge into natural water bodies or reuse. The treatment is conventionally divided into **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary (Advanced)** stages, each targeting progressively finer classes of contaminants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quality Parameters (Syllabus Highlights)**
> - **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand, 5-day, 20°C):** mg $\text{O}_2$/L consumed by microbes. **Standard BOD₅ limit for discharge: ≤ 30 mg/L (CPCB, India).**
> - **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** mg $\text{O}_2$/L consumed by strong oxidant ($\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$).
> - **TSS (Total Suspended Solids):** mg/L, removed mainly in primary treatment.
> - **DO (Dissolved Oxygen):** Healthy surface water ≥ **5 mg/L**.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Cleaning a Cup of Tea

Think of sewage as a **muddy, oily cup of tea filled with floating leaves and sugar**. **Primary treatment** is using a sieve to lift out the big leaves (gross solids). **Secondary treatment** is adding microbes that "eat" the dissolved sugar (organic carbon), making the liquid clear. **Tertiary treatment** is polishing the tea by removing the last traces of color, fine sediments, and adding a pinch of chlorine to kill the remaining germs — making it safe to drink (reuse) or release back to the river.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stratospheric Ozone — Natural Formation

Ozone is naturally formed and destroyed in a dynamic steady-state cycle known as the **Chapman Cycle (1930)**.

**Step 1 — Photolysis of Oxygen:**

$$\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu \, (\lambda \leq 242 \text{ nm})} \text{O}· + \text{O}·$$

**Step 2 — Ozone Formation:**

$$\text{O}_2 + \text{O}· + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}^*$$

(*M* is a third body — typically $\text{N}_2$ or $\text{O}_2$ — that absorbs excess energy.)

**Step 3 — Natural Ozone Destruction:**

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{O}· \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2$$

This is a **balanced photocatalytic steady state**, with no net loss of stratospheric ozone.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Anthropogenic Ozone Depletion (CFC Pathway)

The chlorofluorocarbons (e.g., **CFC-11, $\text{CFCl}_3$**; **CFC-12, $\text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2$**) are extremely stable in the troposphere (lifetimes of 50–100+ years) due to the strength of their **C–F bonds** (~485 kJ/mol). They slowly diffuse into the stratosphere, where they undergo **UV photolysis** at $\lambda \approx 220$ nm.

**Initiation Step:**

$$\text{CFCl}_3 \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{CFCl}_2· + \text{Cl}·$$

**Propagation Step 1 (Ozone Attack):**

$$\text{Cl}· + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{ClO}· + \text{O}_2$$

**Propagation Step 2 (Regeneration of Cl·):**

$$\text{ClO}· + \text{O}· \longrightarrow \text{Cl}· + \text{O}_2$$

**Net Chain Reaction:**

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{O}· \xrightarrow{\text{Cl}·} 2\text{O}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chlorine radical is a **true catalyst** — it is **regenerated** and can destroy up to **~10⁵ ozone molecules** before being sequestered by reservoir species such as $\text{HCl}$ or $\text{ClONO}_2$.

## 2.3 Consequences of Ozone Depletion

| Sector | Effect of Enhanced UV-B |
|---|---|
| **Human Health** | Skin cancer (melanoma, non-melanoma), cataracts, immune suppression |
| **Marine Ecosystem** | Destruction of **phytoplankton** at the base of the food chain |
| **Agriculture** | Reduced photosynthesis in rice, wheat, soybean |
| **Materials** | Polymer degradation, fading of dyes |
| **Climate** | Altered stratospheric temperature gradients |

## 2.4 Control Methods of Ozone Depletion

1. **Montreal Protocol (1987):** Multilateral treaty phasing out CFCs, halons, $\text{CCl}_4$, methyl bromide.
2. **Kigali Amendment (2016):** Phase-down of **HFCs** (potent greenhouse gases, non-ozone-depleting but high GWP).
3. **Substitutes:** **HCFCs** (interim, ODP < 1), **HFCs** (zero ODP, but high GWP), **HFOs** (fourth-generation, ultra-low GWP).
4. **Recovery & Recycling:** Capture of refrigerants from ACs, foams.
5. **Sustainable Alternatives:** Ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$), $\text{CO}_2$, hydrocarbons as natural refrigerants.

> [!NOTE]
> **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** of $\text{CFC-11} = 1.0$ (reference). **ODP of $\text{HFC-134a} = 0$.** **GWP (Global Warming Potential, 100-yr)** of $\text{CFC-11} = 4750$, $\text{HFC-134a} = 1430$, $\text{CO}_2 = 1$.

## 2.5 Sewage Water Treatment — Stages and Process

### A. Preliminary & Primary Treatment (Physical)

- **Screening:** Removal of rags, sticks, plastics via bar screens.
- **Grit Chamber:** Settles sand, gravel (velocity ~0.3 m/s).
- **Primary Clarifier:** 60–70 % TSS removal, 25–40 % BOD removal via sedimentation (2–3 hr retention).
- **Skimming:** Floating oils/grease removed by skimmers.

### B. Secondary Treatment (Biological)

Aerobic microbial oxidation of dissolved organics. The mixed liquor operates on the equation:

$$\text{Organic matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{new biomass}$$

**Common Reactors:**

1. **Activated Sludge Process (ASP):** Sewage + returned sludge aerated in an aeration tank; biomass flocs settle in a secondary clarifier; excess sludge wasted.
2. **Trickling Filter:** Wastewater sprayed over a bed of stones coated with biofilm.
3. **Oxidation Pond / Aerated Lagoon:** Low-cost, large retention times.
4. **Anaerobic Digestion (Sludge Treatment):**

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobes}} \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{NH}_3$$

**Biogas (~60 % CH₄)** is recovered for energy.

### C. Tertiary (Advanced) Treatment

- **Filtration:** Sand/activated carbon removes residual TSS.
- **Nutrient Removal:** Nitrification–Denitrification (biological); chemical precipitation of phosphates.
- **Disinfection:** Chlorination, $\text{O}_3$ or UV to inactivate pathogens.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO):** For water reuse.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **BOD₅ (dilution method)** | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$ | Standard 5-day BOD at 20°C |
| **COD (dichromate)** | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{8 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg $\text{O}_2$/L |
| **BOD/COD Ratio** | $\text{BOD}_5 / \text{COD}$ | Biodegradability index; > 0.5 = biodegradable |
| **ODP (relative)** | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Mass O}_3 \text{ destroyed by } x}{\text{Mass O}_3 \text{ destroyed by CFC-11}}$ | Reference scale |
| **Removal Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100 \%$ | Performance metric |
| **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS}}$ | mL/g; sludge settleability |
| **Montreal Production Cap** | Phase-out schedule by year/chemical | Legal compliance |
| **Heterotrophic Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{biomass produced}}{\text{substrate consumed}}$ | Stoichiometric coefficient |
| **F/M Ratio** | $F/M = \dfrac{S_0}{\theta \cdot X}$ | Food-to-microorganism ratio |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = V / Q$ | Time fluid stays in reactor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the BOD formula above: $D_1, D_2$ = DO of diluted sample on day 0 and day 5; $B_1, B_2$ = DO of dilution water blank; $f$ = dilution factor; $P$ = decimal fraction of sample used.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Net Ozone Destruction by Chlorine

We start with the propagation cycle and add it algebraically to extract the net reaction.

**Step 1: Write the two propagation steps.**

$$\text{Cl}· + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{ClO}· + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\text{ClO}· + \text{O}· \longrightarrow \text{Cl}· + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2: Add equations (i) and (ii) and cancel the chlorine species appearing on both sides.**

$$\text{Cl}· + \text{O}_3 + \text{ClO}· + \text{O}· \longrightarrow \text{ClO}· + \text{O}_2 + \text{Cl}· + \text{O}_2$$

**Step 3: Cancel $\text{Cl}·$ and $\text{ClO}·$ from both sides (intermediates).**

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{O}· \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

- The chlorine radical is fully regenerated (a true catalyst).
- The net effect is identical to the natural Chapman destruction, but **catalyzed** by a foreign species.
- The catalyst converts an otherwise slow reaction into a rapid, kinetically significant one.

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Ozone Concentration (Chapman)

In the stratosphere, formation and destruction reach a **photostationary state**.

**Rate of formation:**

$$R_f = k_f [\text{O}_2][\text{O}·][\text{M}]$$

**Rate of natural destruction:**

$$R_d = k_d [\text{O}_3][\text{O}·]$$

**At steady state:** $R_f = R_d$.

$$k_f [\text{O}_2][\text{O}·][\text{M}] = k_d [\text{O}_3][\text{O}·]$$

**Cancel $[\text{O}·]$ (assumed steady) and solve for $[\text{O}_3]$:**

$$[\text{O}_3]_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{k_f [\text{M}]}{k_d} [\text{O}_2]$$

This shows ozone concentration is proportional to molecular oxygen concentration and inversely related to the destruction rate constant. Adding **catalytic chlorine** enlarges $k_d$ (effective) → **lowers $[\text{O}_3]_{\text{ss}}$**.

## 3.3 BOD Numerical — Sample KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A 5 mL sewage sample is diluted to 300 mL with aerated dilution water. The initial DO ($D_1$) = 8.0 mg/L; the DO after 5 days ($D_2$) = 4.0 mg/L. The dilution water blank shows $B_1 = 8.0$ mg/L and $B_2 = 7.9$ mg/L. Calculate $\text{BOD}_5$.

**Step 1: Dilution factor.**

$$f = \dfrac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{diluted}}} = \dfrac{5}{300} = 0.01667$$

**Step 2: Decimal fraction of sample in the diluted bottle.**

$$P = f = 0.01667$$

**Step 3: Oxygen depletion of the sample bottle.**

$$\Delta D = D_1 - D_2 = 8.0 - 4.0 = 4.0 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Correction for dilution water blank depletion.**

$$\Delta B = B_1 - B_2 = 8.0 - 7.9 = 0.1 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 5: Apply the BOD formula.**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{\Delta D - \Delta B \times (1 - P)}{P}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{4.0 - 0.1 \times (1 - 0.01667)}{0.01667}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{4.0 - 0.0983}{0.01667} = \dfrac{3.9017}{0.01667}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 \approx 234.1 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such a high BOD indicates **strong sewage** — typical domestic sewage has $\text{BOD}_5$ ≈ 200–300 mg/L; industrial effluents can exceed 1000 mg/L.

## 3.4 COD Numerical

**Problem:** 25 mL of sewage is refluxed with 0.25 N $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$. The blank titration requires 24.6 mL of 0.1 N FAS (Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate); the sample titration requires 18.0 mL. Calculate COD.

**Step 1: Difference in volume.**

$$\Delta V = V_b - V_s = 24.6 - 18.0 = 6.6 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2: Apply the COD formula.**

$$\text{COD} = \dfrac{8 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

$$\text{COD} = \dfrac{8 \times 6.6 \times 0.1 \times 1000}{25}$$

$$\text{COD} = \dfrac{5280}{25} = 211.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD} \approx 211.2 \text{ mg O}_2 / \text{L}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Stratospheric Ozone | Tropospheric Ozone |
|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 15–35 km | 0–10 km |
| Origin | Natural (Chapman) | Photochemical smog |
| Function | UV shield (beneficial) | Pollutant (harmful) |
| CFCs effect | Depletes | None direct |
| NOx effect | Forms $\text{NO}/\text{NO}_2$ reservoir cycles | Forms smog |
| Health effect | Reduces UV-induced cancers | Respiratory illness |

| Treatment Stage | Process Type | Target | Typical Removal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Physical (sedimentation) | TSS, oils | 50–70 % TSS, 25–40 % BOD |
| **Secondary (Aerobic)** | Biological | Dissolved organics | 80–95 % BOD |
| **Secondary (Anaerobic)** | Biological (sludge) | Sludge stabilization + biogas | 30–50 % VS |
| **Tertiary** | Chemical / Advanced | N, P, pathogens | 99 % coliform |
