# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model 1]
**Define Dobson Unit. State the average and minimum Dobson values observed over the Antarctic during the ozone hole event.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Dobson Unit (DU)** is the standard measure of total columnar atmospheric ozone; 1 DU corresponds to a layer of pure ozone 0.01 mm thick at STP, equivalent to $2.69 \times 10^{16}$ molecules/cm². The **global average** stratospheric ozone column is ~ **300 DU**; the minimum over the **Antarctic ozone hole** falls to **~ 100 DU** or lower.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model 2]
**What is the Montreal Protocol? Mention two of its key success chemicals that have been phased out.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** is a multilateral environmental agreement under the **UNEP** designed to phase out the production and consumption of ozone-depleting substances (ODS). Two key substances phased out: **CFC-11 ($\text{CFCl}_3$)** and **CFC-12 ($\text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2$)**. Halons, carbon tetrachloride, and methyl bromide were also brought under its umbrella.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion by CFCs** with balanced chemical equations. Explain why one Cl atom can destroy ~10⁵ ozone molecules. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **stages of municipal sewage water treatment** with a flow diagram. Compare BOD and COD as pollution indicators. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) CFC Mechanism

**Step 1 — Long residence of CFCs:**
CFCs like $\text{CFCl}_3$ are chemically inert in the troposphere due to the high C–F bond energy (~485 kJ/mol), giving atmospheric lifetimes of 50–100 years. This allows them to drift into the stratosphere.

**Step 2 — UV photolysis (initiation):**

$$\text{CFCl}_3 \xrightarrow{h\nu \, (\lambda \approx 220 \text{ nm})} \text{CFCl}_2· + \text{Cl}·$$

**Step 3 — Propagation 1 (ozone attack):**

$$\text{Cl}· + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{ClO}· + \text{O}_2$$

**Step 4 — Propagation 2 (Cl regeneration):**

$$\text{ClO}· + \text{O}· \longrightarrow \text{Cl}· + \text{O}_2$$

**Step 5 — Net chain reaction:**

$$\text{O}_3 + \text{O}· \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2$$

**Step 6 — Why ~10⁵ factor?**
The Cl radical is **regenerated** in step 4 and re-enters the cycle, behaving as a **true catalyst**. Termination occurs when Cl is locked into reservoir species $\text{HCl}$ or $\text{ClONO}_2$. On average, **a single Cl atom destroys 100,000 ozone molecules** before termination.

**Step 7 — Why polar regions?**
In the Antarctic stratosphere, **Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs)** at ~ −78 °C provide surfaces that liberate Cl from reservoir species (heterogeneous reactions), maximizing the catalytic chain in spring (September–October) when sunlight returns.

#### Model Solution — (b) Sewage Treatment

**Step 1 — Primary Treatment (Physical):**
Raw sewage → bar screen (removes rags, plastics) → grit chamber (sand, gravel) → primary clarifier (settles suspended solids; removes 50–70 % TSS and 25–40 % BOD over 2–3 hours). Floating scum is skimmed off.

**Step 2 — Secondary Treatment (Biological):**
Primary effluent enters an **Activated Sludge Process (ASP)**:
$$\text{Organics} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{aerobic microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{biomass}$$
Aeration tank (4–8 hr) → secondary clarifier (settles biomass). Return Activated Sludge (RAS) is recycled; Waste Activated Sludge (WAS) goes to the digester. **80–95 % BOD removal** is typical. Alternatives: trickling filter, oxidation pond, RBC.

**Step 3 — Tertiary Treatment:**
Effluent is sand-filtered, chlorinated (or treated with $\text{O}_3$ / UV), and nutrients (N, P) are removed. Suitable for reuse in irrigation or industrial cooling.

**Step 4 — BOD vs COD comparison:**

| Parameter | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidant | Microbes (5 days, 20°C) | $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ (2 hr) |
| Time | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Measures | Biodegradable organics | Total organics (bio + non-bio) |
| Value | Always ≤ COD | ≥ BOD |
| Ratio | $\text{BOD/COD} > 0.5$ = biodegradable |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** What is **ODP**? Compare the ODP and GWP of CFC-11, HCFC-22, and HFC-134a. Justify why HFCs are being phased out under the **Kigali Amendment**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With balanced equations, describe the **biological processes in the activated sludge reactor and anaerobic sludge digester**. Why is sludge digestion important in a sewage treatment plant? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) ODP and Kigali Amendment

**Step 1 — Definition:**
**Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** is the relative amount of ozone destruction a substance can cause compared to **CFC-11** (which is assigned an ODP of 1.0). It is calculated as the ratio of the integrated perturbation to total ozone for the substance vs. CFC-11.

**Step 2 — Numerical comparison:**

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP (100-yr) |
|---|---|---|
| **CFC-11 ($\text{CFCl}_3$)** | 1.0 | 4750 |
| **HCFC-22 ($\text{CHClF}_2$)** | 0.055 | 1810 |
| **HFC-134a ($\text{CH}_2\text{FCF}_3$)** | 0 | 1430 |

**Step 3 — Justification of Kigali phase-out:**
HFCs have **zero ODP** (do not harm ozone) but are **potent greenhouse gases** with GWP 1000–4000 times that of $\text{CO}_2$. The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol commits nations to an **80–85 % phase-down of HFCs by 2045** to mitigate climate change.

#### Model Solution — (b) Activated Sludge & Anaerobic Digestion

**Step 1 — Activated Sludge Process (Aerobic):**
In the aeration tank, dissolved oxygen (~1.5–2.5 mg/L) supports heterotrophic bacteria. Reaction:
$$\text{Organics} + \text{O}_2 + \text{NH}_3 + \text{PO}_4^{3-} \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{new biomass} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
Biomass flocs settle in the secondary clarifier; clear supernatant is the treated effluent. RAS is recycled to maintain MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids) of 2000–4000 mg/L.

**Step 2 — Anaerobic Sludge Digestion (Anaerobic):**
In the absence of $\text{O}_2$ at ~35 °C (mesophilic), the sludge undergoes a 4-stage process: hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis. The overall reaction is:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z\text{N}_w\text{S}_v \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} + \text{stabilized sludge}$$

**Step 3 — Importance of sludge digestion:**

- **Volume reduction:** 30–50 % reduction in volatile solids; less landfill.
- **Biogas production:** ~60 % $\text{CH}_4$ — used for heating, electricity (energy recovery).
- **Pathogen destruction:** Thermophilic digestion (>55 °C) inactivates helminths and coliforms.
- **Stabilization:** Eliminates putrescible organics, prevents odor and re-fermentation.
- **Resource recovery:** Digested biosolids can be used as a soil conditioner (regulated).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** write "CFCs deplete ozone" without showing the **chain mechanism and Cl regeneration.** Examiners specifically look for the two-step propagation cycle and the net reaction.
> - **Do not** confuse BOD and COD — BOD measures only **biodegradable** organics (microbes, 5 days), while COD measures **all organics** (strong oxidant, 2 hr). Always conclude **COD ≥ BOD**.
> - **Do not** skip the **regeneration step** when explaining why one Cl destroys many O₃ molecules — this is the most-missed conceptual question.
> - **Do not** list sewage treatment stages without the **sludge line** — most students ignore anaerobic digestion and biogas.
> - **Avoid** the error of stating "HFCs cause ozone depletion" — they **do not**; they cause **global warming** (which is why the Kigali Amendment was needed).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ozone Layer:** Stratosphere 15–35 km; maximum concentration near 25 km; measures ~300 DU globally.
- **Chapman Cycle:** $\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{UV} 2\text{O}·$; $\text{O}_2 + \text{O}· \to \text{O}_3$. Natural steady state.
- **CFC destruction:** Initiation (UV photolysis at 220 nm) + Propagation (Cl + $\text{O}_3$ → ClO + $\text{O}_2$; ClO + O → Cl + $\text{O}_2$) + Termination (reservoir species $\text{HCl}$, $\text{ClONO}_2$).
- **Net reaction:** $\text{O}_3 + \text{O}· \to 2\text{O}_2$ catalyzed by Cl.
- **Antarctic ozone hole:** First observed 1985; maximum in September–October; due to PSCs and spring sunlight.
- **Consequences:** Skin cancer, cataracts, immune suppression, phytoplankton damage, polymer degradation.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** Phased out CFCs, halons, $\text{CCl}_4$. **Kigali (2016):** Phases down HFCs.
- **Substitutes:** HCFCs (interim, ODP < 1) → HFCs (ODP = 0, GWP high) → HFOs / natural refrigerants.
- **BOD:** mg $\text{O}_2$/L consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20°C. Indicates biodegradable organics.
- **COD:** mg $\text{O}_2$/L consumed by $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ in 2 hr. Indicates total organics.
- **BOD/COD > 0.5 = biodegradable** (typical for municipal sewage).
- **Primary Treatment:** Physical — screening, grit removal, sedimentation (50–70 % TSS, 25–40 % BOD).
- **Secondary Treatment:** Biological — ASP, trickling filter, oxidation pond (80–95 % BOD).
- **Tertiary Treatment:** Filtration, N/P removal, disinfection (chlorination, $\text{O}_3$, UV).
- **Sludge Digestion:** Anaerobic → $\text{CH}_4$ + $\text{CO}_2$ (biogas) + stabilized biosolids.
- **DO** ≥ 5 mg/L = healthy river; < 3 mg/L = hypoxic; < 1 mg/L = dead zone.
- **SVI** (mL/g): 50–150 = good settleability; > 200 = bulking sludge problem.
- **ODP (CFC-11) = 1.0**; **GWP ($\text{CO}_2$) = 1.0**; reference scales for refrigerant evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary, Secondary and Tertiary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Primary, Secondary and Tertiary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hierarchical Organization of Proteins

In biochemistry and physical chemistry, the terms **Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary** refer to the **three fundamental hierarchical levels of protein architecture**. A protein is a biological macromolecule composed of one or more polypeptide chains, and its 3D functional shape is built up in a step-wise (hierarchical) manner. Each level of structure depends on, and arises from, the level before it. This hierarchy was first systematically proposed by **Linderstrøm-Lang (1951)** and is the cornerstone of structural biology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The primary structure is the covalent, linear sequence of amino acids. The secondary structure refers to the local, regular spatial arrangement of the polypeptide backbone (stabilized mainly by hydrogen bonds). The tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional folding of an entire polypeptide chain (stabilized by R-group interactions).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Beaded Necklace, the Coiled Spring, and the Crumpled Paper"

To understand the hierarchy instantly, think of a **long string of beads**:

| Structure Level | Everyday Analogy | What it Means |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | A **straight beaded necklace** laid on a table | The *order* of beads (amino acids) is fixed |
| **Secondary** | The same necklace **coiled into a spring** or **zig-zag pleated** | The chain twists locally into helices or sheets |
| **Tertiary** | The spring **crumpled into a tight ball** and stuffed into a pocket | The whole chain folds into a compact 3D shape |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as the *type* of beads determines how the spring coils, the *R-groups* (side chains) of amino acids determine how the helix folds into a 3D shape. The function of a protein (e.g., enzyme catalysis) emerges from the **tertiary** structure, which is determined by the **primary** sequence.

## 1.3 The Twenty Standard α-Amino Acids (Building Blocks)

Every protein is a polymer of **α-amino acids** of the general formula:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N}-\underset{\underset{\text{R}}{\vert}}{\text{C}}\text{H}-\text{COOH}$$

where **R** is the variable side chain. There are **20 standard amino acids** encoded by the genetic code, each differing only in the **R group** (e.g., H for glycine, CH₃ for alanine, etc.). The α-carbon (Cα) is the central chiral carbon (except in glycine).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** At physiological pH ≈ **7.4**, amino acids exist predominantly as **zwitterions**: H₃N⁺–CHR–COO⁻, with no net charge but possessing both positive and negative charges.

## 1.4 Why the Hierarchy Matters in Engineering & Physical Science

Proteins are not just biological curiosities. They are central to:
- **Biosensors & enzyme-based electrodes** (electrochemistry)
- **Biopolymers & biodegradable materials** (materials science)
- **Drug delivery & pharmaceutical engineering** (chemical engineering)
- **Spectroscopic analysis** (UV, CD, IR, NMR of biomolecules)

Understanding primary, secondary, and tertiary structures allows engineers to design **synthetic polypeptides**, **denaturation-resistant enzymes**, and **biomimetic materials**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PRIMARY STRUCTURE — The Covalent Backbone

### 2.1.1 Definition
The **primary structure** of a protein is the **linear, covalently-bonded sequence of amino acid residues** joined together by **peptide (amide) bonds**. It is the most fundamental level and dictates all higher levels.

### 2.1.2 The Peptide Bond
A peptide bond is formed by a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the **α-carboxyl group (–COOH)** of one amino acid and the **α-amino group (–NH₂)** of the next, releasing one molecule of **water (H₂O)**.

$$\text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_1-\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_2-\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_1-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-\text{CHR}_2-\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanger's Contribution:** Frederick Sanger (Nobel Prize, 1958) determined the complete primary structure of **insulin (51 amino acids, two chains)** in 1955, establishing the methodology for sequencing proteins.

### 2.1.3 Properties of the Peptide Bond
- It is a **partial double bond** (≈40% double-bond character) due to **resonance** between C=O and C–N.
- The bond is **planar** (sp²-hybridized C and N), which restricts rotation.
- The bond length is **≈ 1.32 Å**, intermediate between a C–N single bond (1.47 Å) and a C=N double bond (1.27 Å).
- The **C–N** bond is **rigid**, while **Cα–N** and **Cα–C** are **rotatable** (angles φ and ψ, Ramachandran plot).

### 2.1.4 N-terminus and C-terminus
By convention, the primary sequence is written from the **free α-amino group (N-terminus, H₂N–)** on the left to the **free α-carboxyl group (C-terminus, –COOH)** on the right.

## 2.2 SECONDARY STRUCTURE — Local Regular Folding

### 2.2.1 Definition
The **secondary structure** is the **local, regular spatial arrangement of the polypeptide backbone** stabilized primarily by **hydrogen bonds** between the **C=O of one peptide bond** and the **N–H of another** peptide bond. The R-groups do NOT participate directly.

### 2.2.2 The Two Canonical Secondary Structures

**(a) α-Helix (Pauling & Corey, 1951)**
- A **right-handed** helical structure with **3.6 amino acid residues per turn**.
- Rise per residue = **1.5 Å**; pitch (per turn) = **5.4 Å**.
- Hydrogen bonds form between the **C=O of residue *i*** and the **N–H of residue *i+4***.
- R-groups project **outward** from the helical axis, avoiding steric clash.
- Stabilized by **maximal intramolecular H-bonding**.

**(b) β-Pleated Sheet (Pauling & Corey, 1951)**
- Polypeptide chains (β-strands) lie **side-by-side** in a zig-zag (pleated) pattern.
- H-bonds form **between adjacent strands** (inter-strand), perpendicular to the strand direction.
- Two types: **Parallel** (N→C same direction) and **Antiparallel** (N→C opposite directions; stronger H-bonds).

### 2.2.3 Other Secondary Structures
- **β-Turns (β-bends):** Reverse the chain direction in 4 residues; stabilized by a single H-bond (residue *i* C=O to residue *i+3* N–H).
- **Random Coil:** Irregular, fluctuating conformations; lacks a defined H-bond pattern.
- **310-helix, π-helix:** Less common helical variants.

## 2.3 TERTIARY STRUCTURE — The 3D Functional Fold

### 2.3.1 Definition
The **tertiary structure** is the **overall three-dimensional folding of an entire polypeptide chain** in 3D space, including the spatial arrangement of all secondary structural elements and R-groups. It is the level at which the protein becomes **biologically active**.

### 2.3.2 Bonds Stabilizing Tertiary Structure (Four Types of R-group Interactions)

| Interaction Type | Nature | Strength | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrophobic interactions** | Nonpolar R-groups cluster in the interior | Weak (~5 kJ/mol) | Leu, Ile, Val, Phe side chains |
| **Hydrogen bonds** | Polar R-groups (–OH, –NH, –C=O) | Moderate (~20 kJ/mol) | Ser–OH, Thr–OH, Tyr–OH |
| **Ionic (salt) bridges** | Oppositely charged R-groups | Moderate (~20 kJ/mol) | Lys⁺ … Asp⁻ |
| **Disulfide bridges** | Covalent S–S bond between two Cys | **Strong** (~200 kJ/mol) | Cys–S–S–Cys |
| **Van der Waals** | Transient dipoles in nonpolar groups | Very weak (~1 kJ/mol) | Throughout the core |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disulfide bridges** are the only **covalent** R-group interactions and are responsible for the extreme stability of proteins like **keratin** (hair, nails) and **ribonuclease**.

### 2.3.3 Domains and Motifs
- A **domain** is a compact, independently-folding region of a protein (typically 100–200 residues).
- A **motif (supersecondary structure)** is a recurring small fold (e.g., helix-loop-helix, zinc finger, Greek key).

## 2.4 Denaturation vs. Renaturation

- **Denaturation** = loss of tertiary (and secondary) structure while the **primary structure remains intact**. Caused by heat, pH change, urea, heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺), organic solvents.
- **Renaturation** = recovery of native structure after removal of the denaturant. **Anfinsen's experiment (1961, Nobel 1972)** with ribonuclease proved that **primary sequence alone determines tertiary structure** (the "thermodynamic hypothesis").

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Amino acid general formula | H₂N–CHR–COOH | – |
| Peptide bond length | $\approx 1.32 \,\text{Å}$ | Å (10⁻¹⁰ m) |
| α-Helix: residues per turn | $3.6$ | – |
| α-Helix: pitch | $5.4 \,\text{Å}$ | – |
| α-Helix: rise per residue | $1.5 \,\text{Å}$ | – |
| α-Helix: H-bond span | residue $i$ to $i+4$ | – |
| β-Sheet: H-bond direction | Inter-strand, perpendicular to strand | – |
| Disulfide bond energy | $\approx 200 \,\text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol |
| Hydrogen bond energy | $\approx 20 \,\text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol |
| Hydrophobic interaction | $\approx 5 \,\text{kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol |
| Number of standard amino acids | $20$ | – |
| Peptide bond C–N character | $\approx 40\%$ double bond | – |
| Zwitterion pH | $\approx 7.4$ (physiological) | – |
| Water released per peptide bond | $1$ molecule H₂O | – |
| Molecular mass of amino acid (avg) | $\approx 110 \,\text{Da}$ | Dalton (g/mol) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The rule **"sequence → structure → function"** (Anfinsen's dogma) is exploited in **directed evolution** and **protein engineering** to design thermostable enzymes for industrial bioreactors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Peptide Bond Formation in a Dipeptide

**Problem:** Two amino acids, **Glycine (Gly, R = H)** and **Alanine (Ala, R = CH₃)**, form the dipeptide **Gly–Ala**. Write the reaction, the full structural formula, and the molecular mass of the dipeptide. State which end is the N-terminus and which is the C-terminus.

### Step 1: Write the two amino acid structures

$$\text{Glycine:} \quad \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH}$$
$$\text{Alanine:} \quad \text{H}_2\text{N}-\underset{\underset{\text{CH}_3}{\vert}}{\text{CH}}-\text{COOH}$$

### Step 2: Identify the reactive groups
- **Glycine** contributes its **–COOH** group (C-terminus partner).
- **Alanine** contributes its **–H₂N** group (N-terminus partner).

### Step 3: Perform the condensation reaction
A water molecule is eliminated; the **α-COOH of Gly** joins the **α-NH₂ of Ala**.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}_2-\text{COOH} &+ \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{COOH} \\
&\longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{COOH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Identify the peptide bond
The bond **–CO–NH–** is the **peptide bond**. In Gly–Ala, it lies between the **carbonyl C of Gly** and the **amide N of Ala**.

### Step 5: Label the termini
- **N-terminus:** the free **–H₂N** group on the **Glycine** side (left end).
- **C-terminus:** the free **–COOH** group on the **Alanine** side (right end).

### Step 6: Calculate the molecular mass of the dipeptide

| Component | Formula | Mass (g/mol) |
|---|---|---|
| Glycine | C₂H₅NO₂ | 75.07 |
| Alanine | C₃H₇NO₂ | 89.09 |
| **Sum** | – | **164.16** |
| **Less: 1 H₂O** | H₂O | **–18.02** |
| **Dipeptide Gly–Ala** | C₅H₁₀N₂O₃ | **≈ 146.14 g/mol** |

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rule:** For a peptide of *n* amino acid residues, the number of peptide bonds = (*n* − 1), and the number of water molecules released = (*n* − 1). Total mass = (sum of residue masses) − 18.02 × (*n* − 1).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Analyzing a Tripeptide's Primary Structure

**Problem:** A tripeptide has the sequence **Ser–Gly–Ala** at physiological pH. (a) Write its full structure. (b) Identify the H-bond donor and acceptor sites for secondary structure. (c) How many peptide bonds and water molecules are released?

### Step 1: Write the full structural formula

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{H}_2\text{N}-\underset{\underset{\text{CH}_2\text{OH}}{\vert}}{\text{CH}}-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CO}-\text{NH}-\underset{\underset{\text{CH}_3}{\vert}}{\text{CH}}-\text{COOH}
\end{aligned}$$

Here, **Ser** (R = CH₂OH) is the N-terminal residue, and **Ala** (R = CH₃) is the C-terminal residue.

### Step 2: Count peptide bonds
- Bond 1: between Ser and Gly (–CO–NH–).
- Bond 2: between Gly and Ala (–CO–NH–).
- **Total peptide bonds = 2**.
- **Water molecules released = 2**.

### Step 3: Identify H-bond donors and acceptors in the backbone
- **H-bond donors (N–H):** the amide N–H of **every peptide bond** (here, 2 N–H groups).
- **H-bond acceptors (C=O):** the carbonyl O of **every peptide bond** (here, 2 C=O groups).
- In an **α-helix**, the C=O of residue *i* H-bonds to the N–H of residue *i+4*.

### Step 4: Calculate molecular mass

| Component | Mass (g/mol) |
|---|---|
| Serine (C₃H₇NO₃) | 105.09 |
| Glycine (C₂H₅NO₂) | 75.07 |
| Alanine (C₃H₇NO₂) | 89.09 |
| **Sum** | **269.25** |
| Less 2 H₂O (2 × 18.02) | **–36.04** |
| **Tripeptide mass** | **≈ 233.21 g/mol** |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Determining the Number of Hydrogen Bonds in an α-Helix

**Problem:** A polypeptide has 30 amino acid residues arranged in a continuous α-helix. (a) How many residues per turn? (b) How many complete turns? (c) How many intramolecular H-bonds are present in this helix?

### Step 1: Residues per turn in α-helix
From the formula sheet: **n = 3.6 residues per turn**.

### Step 2: Calculate the number of turns

$$\text{Number of turns} = \frac{\text{Total residues}}{\text{Residues per turn}} = \frac{30}{3.6} \approx 8.33 \,\text{turns}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the axial length (pitch × number of turns)

$$L = (\text{pitch per turn}) \times (\text{number of turns}) = 5.4 \,\text{Å} \times 8.33 \approx 44.98 \,\text{Å}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the number of H-bonds
In an α-helix, **H-bonds form between residue *i* (C=O) and residue *i+4* (N–H)**. For a helix with *N* residues:

$$\text{Number of H-bonds} = N - 4 = 30 - 4 = 26$$

(Only the first 4 residues cannot H-bond to a residue 4 ahead; the last 4 lack a C=O partner 4 residues behind.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Identifying Tertiary-Structure Stabilizing Bonds

**Problem:** A protein contains two cysteine residues (Cys) that are far apart in the primary sequence but close in 3D space. Name the type of bond that holds them together. Predict the effect of adding a reducing agent like β-mercaptoethanol (which cleaves S–S bonds).

### Step 1: Identify the bond
Two cysteine side chains (–CH₂–SH) oxidized together form a **disulfide bridge (–CH₂–S–S–CH₂–)**. This is a **covalent** R-group bond, the strongest tertiary-stabilizing interaction.

### Step 2: Effect of the reducing agent
β-Mercaptoethanol reduces the S–S bond back to two –SH groups. The tertiary structure is disrupted (or altered), and the protein may undergo **denaturation** or loss of function. However, the **primary structure (sequence) remains intact** because peptide bonds are not cleaved.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Level-by-Level Comparison

| Feature | Primary | Secondary | Tertiary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What is defined** | Amino acid sequence | Local backbone geometry | Overall 3D fold |
| **Bond type** | Covalent peptide bond | Hydrogen bonds (backbone) | H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, VdW, disulfide |
| **Disrupted by** | Acid/alkali hydrolysis, proteases | Heat, urea, guanidinium | Denaturants, reducing agents |
| **Spatial scale** | ~Å (linear) | ~Å–nm (local) | ~nm (global) |
| **Determined by** | Genetic code (DNA) | Primary structure + H-bonding | Primary structure + R-group chemistry |
| **Example feature** | Sequence of insulin | α-helix of myoglobin | Globin fold of hemoglobin subunit |
| **Loss called** | Hydrolysis | Denaturation (partial) | Denaturation (full) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Protein Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Genetic Code] --> B[Primary Structure: AA1-AA2-AA3... AAn]
    B --> C[Secondary Structure: alpha Helix]
    B --> D[Secondary Structure: beta Pleated Sheet]
    B --> E[Secondary Structure: beta Turn]
    C --> F[Tertiary Structure: 3D Fold]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G1[Stabilized by Hydrophobic Interactions]
    F --> G2[Stabilized by Hydrogen Bonds]
    F --> G3[Stabilized by Ionic Salt Bridges]
    F --> G4[Stabilized by Disulfide Bridges]
    F --> G5[Stabilized by Van der Waals Forces]
    F --> H[Functional Protein Active Site]
    H --> I[Biological Function: Catalysis, Transport, Signaling]

```

    S3 --> S5[Inter strand H bonds]

    T1[Tertiary Level] --> T2[Hydrophobic Core]

## 4.3 Schematic: α-Helix Geometry (Mermaid Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Residue 1: C=O] --> H2[Residue 2: N-H]
    H2 --> H3[Residue 3: C=O]
    H3 --> H4[Residue 4: N-H]
    H4 --> H5[Residue 5: C=O and N-H of residue 1 H bond]
    H5 --> H6[Residue 5: C=O]
    H6 --> H7[Residue 6: N-H]
    H7 --> H8[Residue 7: C=O]
    H8 --> H9[Residue 8: N-H]
    H9 --> H10[Residue 9: C=O and N-H of residue 5 H bond]

    H1 -.H bond i to i+4.-> H5
    H5 -.H bond i to i+4.-> H10

    style H5 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H10 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow: Sequence → Structure → Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DNA[DNA Codon] --> mRNA[mRNA Translation]
    mRNA --> AA[Primary AA Sequence]
    AA --> H20[Removal of Water via Peptide Bonds]
    H20 --> SS[Secondary Structure: Helix and Sheet]
    SS --> TS[Tertiary Structure: 3D Globular Fold]
    TS --> ACT[Active Site Formation]
    ACT --> FN[Specific Biological Function]

    style DNA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AA fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style SS fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TS fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FN fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Translation:** In enzyme engineering, mutating a single amino acid in the **primary** structure can collapse an entire **tertiary** fold, abolishing function. This is the basis of diseases like **sickle-cell anemia** (Glu→Val at position 6 of β-globin).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**(3 Marks)** *Define the primary structure of a protein. Explain the nature of the peptide bond with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer:**
The **primary structure** of a protein is the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acid residues** in a polypeptide chain, joined together by **peptide (amide) bonds**. The peptide bond is formed by a **condensation reaction** between the α-carboxyl (–COOH) group of one amino acid and the α-amino (–NH₂) group of another, releasing a molecule of water. It is a **partial double bond** (≈40% double-bond character) due to resonance, making the C–N bond **planar** (sp²-hybridized) and **rigid** (bond length ≈ 1.32 Å). Example: In **glycylalanine (Gly–Ala)**, the peptide bond is the –CO–NH– linkage between the two residues.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**(3 Marks)** *Distinguish between the secondary and tertiary structures of a protein. Mention the bonds responsible for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Secondary Structure | Tertiary Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Local, regular folding of the backbone | Overall 3D folding of the entire chain |
| **Bond type** | Hydrogen bonds (backbone C=O ··· H–N) | R-group interactions (H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, disulfide, VdW) |
| **Examples** | α-helix, β-pleated sheet, β-turns | Globular fold of myoglobin, lysozyme |
| **R-group role** | Not directly involved | Decides the fold |

Thus, the secondary structure is a **local** phenomenon stabilized by **main-chain hydrogen bonds**, whereas the tertiary structure is a **global** 3D fold stabilized by **side-chain (R-group) interactions**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ONE)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`  **(14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the formation of a peptide bond with a suitable reaction. Discuss the salient features of the peptide bond, including its geometry and bond order.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Describe the α-helix structure of proteins. State its characteristic features: residues per turn, pitch, rise per residue, and the nature of hydrogen bonding.*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — The Reaction:**
A peptide bond is formed by the **condensation** between the –COOH group of one amino acid and the –NH₂ group of another, with elimination of water.

$$\text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_1-\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_2-\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N}-\text{CHR}_1-\overset{\text{Peptide bond}}{\text{CO}-\text{NH}}-\text{CHR}_2-\text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry:**
The six atoms of the peptide unit (Cα₁, C(=O), N–H, Cα₂) lie in **one plane**. The C=O and N–H are **trans** to each other.

**Step 3 — Bond Order & Resonance:**
The C–N bond has a bond order of ≈ 1.4, intermediate between a single (1.0) and double bond (2.0). This is due to **resonance delocalization**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{O}=\text{C}-\text{N}-\text{H} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{O}^- -\text{C} = \text{N}^+ -\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Salient Features:**
- **Planar** (sp² at C and N); restricted rotation.
- **Bond length** ≈ 1.32 Å.
- **Uncharged** under physiological conditions.
- **Trans configuration** is preferred (cis is rare, except for Pro).
- The peptide bond is **stable at physiological pH** but hydrolyzed by strong acid/base or proteases.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
The **α-helix** is a right-handed helical secondary structure proposed by **Pauling & Corey (1951)** and is the most common motif in globular proteins (e.g., myoglobin, hemoglobin).

**Step 2 — Geometric Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Residues per turn | **3.6** |
| Rise per residue (translation along axis) | **1.5 Å** |
| Pitch (rise per turn) | **5.4 Å** |
| H-bond span | **i → i + 4** (C=O of residue *i* to N–H of residue *i+4*) |
| Handedness | **Right-handed** (L-amino acids) |
| R-group orientation | Project **outward** from the helix axis |

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Bonding:**
Every peptide C=O forms a hydrogen bond with the N–H four residues ahead. This **maximizes backbone H-bonding**, making the α-helix highly stable.

**Step 4 — Stabilizing and Destabilizing Residues:**
- **Helix formers:** Ala, Leu, Met, Glu, Lys, Gln.
- **Helix breakers:** Pro (rigid ring, no N–H donor) and Gly (too flexible).

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The α-helix is a compact, regular, and predictable secondary structure, fundamental to fibrous proteins (keratin, collagen) and globular proteins (enzymes, transport proteins).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`  **(14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 Marks)** *What is meant by the tertiary structure of a protein? Explain the different types of bonds/interactions that stabilize the tertiary structure with examples.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *Explain Anfinsen's experiment on ribonuclease. What conclusion did he draw regarding the relationship between primary structure and tertiary structure?*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The **tertiary structure** is the **overall three-dimensional folding** of a polypeptide chain in space. It brings distant R-groups close together to create the **active site** of an enzyme.

**Step 2 — Types of Stabilizing Interactions:**

1. **Hydrophobic interactions** (nonpolar R-groups cluster inward away from water, e.g., Leu, Ile, Val, Phe).
2. **Hydrogen bonds** (between polar R-groups, e.g., –OH of Ser and C=O of Asn).
3. **Ionic (salt) bridges** (between oppositely charged R-groups, e.g., Lys⁺ ··· Asp⁻).
4. **Disulfide bridges** (covalent S–S bond between two Cys residues; the strongest, e.g., in insulin, keratin, ribonuclease).
5. **Van der Waals forces** (weak, transient; abundant in the densely packed hydrophobic core).

**Step 3 — Engineering Analogy:**
The hydrophobic core of a protein is analogous to the **micelle** of a soap — nonpolar groups buried, polar groups exposed to water. The disulfide bond is analogous to a **rivet** in engineering — the strongest fastener holding the structure together.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The tertiary structure is the result of the **cooperative action of all five interactions** and is uniquely determined by the primary sequence (Anfinsen's thermodynamic hypothesis).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Background:**
**Christian B. Anfinsen (1916–1995)** studied **bovine pancreatic ribonuclease A**, a 124-residue enzyme with **4 disulfide bridges** and full enzymatic activity.

**Step 2 — The Experiment:**

1. **Denaturation:** Ribonuclease was treated with **8 M urea** (breaks H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions) and **β-mercaptoethanol** (reduces S–S bonds to –SH). The enzyme lost all activity, and the polypeptide became a random coil (primary structure intact).
2. **Removal of denaturants:** Urea and β-mercaptoethanol were removed by **dialysis**.
3. **Result:** The ribonuclease spontaneously **refolded** into its native 3D shape, the **4 disulfide bonds re-formed correctly**, and **>90% enzymatic activity was recovered**.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (Nobel Prize 1972):**
> *The amino acid sequence (primary structure) of a protein contains all the information needed to specify its native three-dimensional (tertiary) structure.*

This is the **thermodynamic hypothesis** or **Anfinsen's dogma**: **Sequence → Structure → Function**.

**Step 4 — Significance in Engineering:**
Anfinsen's principle underlies **protein engineering**, **directed evolution**, and **rational drug design** — all aimed at designing sequences that fold into desired 3D structures for industrial biocatalysis and therapeutics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing "denaturation" with "hydrolysis"** — Denaturation disrupts secondary/tertiary but **preserves the primary structure**; hydrolysis cleaves peptide bonds.
> 2. **Writing R-groups involved in secondary structure** — R-groups are **NOT** directly involved in secondary structure H-bonding; only backbone C=O and N–H participate.
> 3. **Citing the wrong number of residues per turn** — α-helix = **3.6**, not 3.6 per *amino acid*. Memorize: 3.6, 1.5 Å, 5.4 Å.
> 4. **Forgetting that the disulfide bond is covalent** — It is the **only covalent** interaction among R-group forces. The others (H-bond, ionic, hydrophobic, VdW) are **non-covalent**.
> 5. **Missing N- and C-termini labelling** — Always label both termini in a peptide drawing.
> 6. **Conflating α-helix with DNA double helix** — α-helix is a **single-chain** H-bonded coil; DNA is a **double-stranded** antiparallel helix with **base pairing**, not peptide H-bonds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Primary structure** = **linear covalent sequence** of amino acids; bond = **peptide (amide) bond**; **Sanger sequenced insulin**.
- **Peptide bond** is **partial double bond** (resonance), **planar**, **1.32 Å**, formed by **condensation** releasing **H₂O**.
- For *n* amino acids: peptide bonds = (*n* − 1), water released = (*n* − 1).
- **Secondary structure** = local, regular backbone folding; **α-helix** (3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 Å pitch, 1.5 Å rise, i→i+4 H-bond) and **β-pleated sheet** (inter-strand H-bonds, parallel/antiparallel).
- **Secondary structure is stabilized by H-bonds between backbone C=O and N–H**; R-groups do NOT participate.
- **Tertiary structure** = overall 3D fold; stabilized by **hydrophobic**, **H-bond**, **ionic**, **van der Waals**, and **disulfide (covalent)** interactions among R-groups.
- **Denaturation** disrupts secondary & tertiary, **not** primary.
- **Anfinsen's experiment (ribonuclease)** proved **primary sequence → tertiary structure** (Nobel 1972).
- **Sequence → Structure → Function** is the central dogma of structural biochemistry.
- **Engineering applications:** enzyme bioreactors, biosensors, drug design, biomimetic materials, biodegradable polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering theory questions, always **draw the structure**, **label both termini**, **state the bond type**, and **give a numerical example** (e.g., 3.6 residues/turn). Examiners reward precision and diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Flow Diagram # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Flow Diagram A **Flow Diagram** (also called a **Process Flow Diagram** or **PFD**) is a standardized graphical representation that uses standardized symbols, arrows, and interconnected blocks to illustrate the sequence of unit operations, unit processes, material streams, and energy transfers occurring in a chemical, industrial, or analytical process. In the context of **GCCYT122 (Chemistry for Physical Science)**, flow diagrams are predominantly used to depict: - Industrial water treatment processes (treatment of raw water for potable supply) - Wastewater treatment sequences (sewage treatment plants) - Manufacture of industrial chemicals (e.g., nitric acid, sulphuric acid, ammonia, cement, glass) - Chromatographic separation schemes - Phase-rule based thermal analysis workflows > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A flow diagram in this course is **not** an artistic sketch. It is a **standardized engineering drawing** that uses **rectangles for unit operations/processes**, **arrows for material flow direction**, and **labeled streams** to identify inputs (raw materials), outputs (products, by-products), and recycle streams. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a flow diagram as a **"recipe card on a city map"** 🗺️. Imagine you are following a recipe to bake a cake: - You put flour, sugar, and eggs into a **mixer** (Unit Operation 1). - The batter goes into an **oven** (Unit Operation 2). - The baked cake comes out as the **final product**. Now imagine the same recipe, but instead of one kitchen, it happens in a **giant factory** with pipes, tanks, pumps, and reactors spread across a large area. A flow diagram is the **"blueprint"** that tells every worker, engineer, and chemist exactly where each material comes from, which machine it enters next, what comes out, and where the by-products are sent. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Definition:** A flow diagram is simply a **"map showing how raw materials transform step-by-step into final products"** in a chemical or industrial process. Each box = one step. Each arrow = one stream of material flowing in a specific direction. ## 1.3 Why Flow Diagrams Matter in Engineering | Reason | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Process Visualization** | Converts complex multi-step processes into a single comprehensible visual | | **Mass Balance** | Allows tracking of every kg of input and output material | | **Troubleshooting** | Pinpoints exact process location when a product fails quality control | | **Communication** | Universal language understood by chemists, engineers, and operators globally | | **Design & Scale-up** | Essential blueprint for designing pilot plants and industrial-scale facilities | | **Safety Analysis** | Helps in identifying hazardous stream locations and emergency shutdown zones | ## 1.4 Standardized Symbols Used in KTU-Level Flow Diagrams > [!NOTE] > For the KTU 2024 examination, students are expected to use **simplified block-level symbols** rather than the strict ANSI/ISA S5.1 instrumentation symbols (which are reserved for advanced Process & Instrumentation Diagrams). The following standardized components are widely accepted: - **Rectangle / Box** → Represents a Unit Operation or Unit Process - **Arrow (→)** → Represents direction of material flow - **Circle** → Represents a pump, compressor, or blower - **Diamond** → Represents a decision point (e.g., quality check) - **Cylinder** → Represents a storage tank or accumulator - **Conical (funnel) shape** → Represents a settling tank or hopper - **Parallel horizontal lines (≡)** → Represents a heat exchanger - **Labeled stream lines** → Show what material is flowing (e.g., Raw water, Steam, Sludge, Brine) ## 1.5 Types of Flow Diagrams Encountered in GCCYT122 1. **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** – Simplest, uses only boxes and arrows; used for overview 2. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** – Includes major equipment, stream information, operating conditions 3. **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** – Includes pipes, valves, instruments, control loops (advanced, not required for KTU) 4. **Schematic Flow Diagram** – Combines process flow with basic instrumentation # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing a Flow Diagram A flow diagram is governed by two fundamental physical laws that are **always implicitly applied** during its construction: ### 2.1.1 Law of Conservation of Mass (Mass Balance) The total mass entering any process unit **must equal** the total mass leaving that unit, plus any accumulation (steady-state assumption means accumulation = 0). $$\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}} + m_{\text{accumulation}}$$ For **steady-state operation** (which is the standard assumption in KTU problems): $$\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$$ ### 2.1.2 Law of Conservation of Energy (Energy Balance) Energy in (as feed, heat, work) = Energy out (as products, waste heat, work output) + Accumulation. $$\sum E_{\text{in}} = \sum E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{accumulated}}$$ ### 2.1.3 Stream Classification in a Flow Diagram > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three mandatory stream types** that every KTU flow diagram must identify: | Stream Type | Symbol Convention | Description | |---|---|---| | **Input (Feed) Stream** | Arrow pointing **into** the box | Raw materials entering the process | | **Output (Product) Stream** | Arrow pointing **out** of the box | Desired product leaving the process | | **Recycle Stream** | Arrow looping **back** to a previous unit | Unreacted reactants sent back for reprocessing | | **By-product / Waste Stream** | Arrow pointing **out** (usually downward) | Unwanted material that must be disposed | ## 2.2 Essential Components of a KTU-Acceptable Flow Diagram A complete flow diagram submitted in an examination or assignment **must** include the following six elements: 1. **Title Block** – Identifying the process name (e.g., "Flow Diagram for Municipal Water Treatment Plant") 2. **Unit Operation Boxes** – Rectangles labeled with the operation (e.g., Sedimentation, Filtration, Chlorination) 3. **Directional Arrows** – Clear, single-headed arrows showing material flow 4. **Stream Labels** – Every major input and output stream must be named 5. **Operating Conditions** (optional but marks-winning) – Temperature, pressure, pH where relevant 6. **Legend / Symbol Key** – A small box explaining any non-standard symbols used ## 2.3 Application: Flow Diagram for Municipal Water Treatment Plant > [!NOTE] > This is a **frequently-asked 14-mark question** in KTU 2024 scheme for GCCYT122. Memorize this sequence. A typical municipal water treatment plant (drinking water supply) follows this sequence: | Step | Unit Operation | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Intake (Raw Water Pump)** | Draws water from source (river/lake/reservoir) | | 2 | **Screening** | Removes large debris (sticks, leaves, plastic) | | 3 | **Aeration** | Removes dissolved gases (H₂S, CO₂), oxidizes Fe²⁺ and Mn²⁺ | | 4 | **Coagulation & Flocculation** | Adds alum [KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O] or FeCl₃ to form flocs that trap colloidal impurities | | 5 | **Sedimentation (Clarifier)** | Heavy flocs settle as sludge at the bottom; clarified water moves up | | 6 | **Filtration (Rapid Sand Filter)** | Removes remaining suspended particles | | 7 | **Disinfection (Chlorination)** | Kills pathogenic microorganisms using Cl₂, O₃, or UV | | 8 | **Storage & Distribution** | Treated water is stored in overhead tanks and supplied to consumers | ### 2.3.1 Chemical Reaction During Coagulation $$KAl(SO_4)_2 \cdot 12H_2O \rightarrow K^+ + Al^{3+} + 2SO_4^{2-} + 12H_2O$$ $$Al^{3+} + 3H_2O \rightarrow Al(OH)_3 \downarrow + 3H^+$$ The gelatinous **Al(OH)₃ precipitate** entrains colloidal dirt particles and settles as floc. ### 2.3.2 Disinfection Chemistry $$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HOCl + HCl$$ $$HOCl \rightarrow HCl + [O] \quad \text{(nascent oxygen — kills bacteria)}$$ ## 2.4 Application: Flow Diagram for Sewage Treatment Plant | Step | Unit Process | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Preliminary Treatment** | Screening and grit removal | | 2 | **Primary Treatment (Sedimentation)** | ~50–70% of suspended solids removed as sludge | | 3 | **Secondary Treatment (Biological)** | Activated sludge process or trickling filter — uses aerobic bacteria to oxidize organic matter | | 4 | **Tertiary Treatment** | Advanced removal (N, P) using coagulation, adsorption, ion exchange | | 5 | **Disinfection** | Chlorination before discharge | | 6 | **Sludge Treatment** | Anaerobic digestion → biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) | > [!NOTE] > **Sewage vs. Water Treatment:** The **direction of cleaning is opposite**. Water treatment cleans raw water *to make it potable*. Sewage treatment cleans wastewater *before discharging it back to the environment*. In flow diagrams, the **arrow direction** clearly distinguishes them. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations and rules appear in flow-diagram-based numerical problems. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass Balance (general) | $\sum m_{\text{in}} - \sum m_{\text{out}} = m_{\text{accumulation}}$ | kg, kg/hr, kg/day | Tracking material flow | | 2 | Steady-state mass balance | $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$ | kg, kg/hr | Simplification for steady processes | | 3 | Component mass balance | $\sum m_{\text{in}} \cdot x_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{\text{out}}$ | kg of component | Tracking individual species | | 4 | Conversion (X) | $X = \dfrac{n_{\text{reacted}}}{n_{\text{fed}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Reactor efficiency | | 5 | Recycle ratio (R) | $R = \dfrac{\text{Recycle stream}}{\text{Fresh feed}}$ | dimensionless | Recycle design | | 6 | Yield (Y) | $Y = \dfrac{n_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{reactant,limiting}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Process economics | | 7 | Selectivity (S) | $S = \dfrac{\text{Desired product}}{\text{Total products}} \times 100\%$ | % | Multi-product reactions | | 8 | Coagulant dose | $\text{Dose} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of alum}}{\text{Volume of water}}$ | mg/L or ppm | Water treatment | | 9 | Chlorine demand | $Cl_2 \text{ demand} = Cl_2 \text{ applied} - Cl_2 \text{ residual}$ | mg/L | Disinfection | | 10 | BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) | $BOD_5 = (DO_i - DO_f)$ at 5 days, 20°C | mg/L | Wastewater strength indicator | > [!NOTE] > For the KTU 2024 examination, students **must remember** that pipes carrying **gases** are typically drawn at the **top** of the diagram, **liquids** in the **middle**, and **solids (sludge)** at the **bottom** of the flow diagram — following standard chemical-engineering drawing convention. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Flow Diagram Application | |---|---| | **Petrochemical** | Crude oil refining, catalytic cracking units | | **Pharmaceutical** | Drug synthesis, purification sequences | | **Food Processing** | Pasteurization, fermentation, packaging | | **Power Plants** | Boiler feed water treatment, cooling tower cycles | | **Cement & Glass** | Raw material blending, kiln operations | | **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETP)** | Industrial wastewater handling | | **Reverse Osmosis Plants** | Desalination processes | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Mass Balance Across a Two-Stage Process > **Problem Statement:** > A water treatment plant processes **10,000 L/hr** of raw water. After the coagulation-flocculation unit, **15% of the inlet water mass is removed as sludge**. The remaining water enters the filtration unit, where an additional **5% of incoming water** is removed as backwash waste. Calculate the flow rate of the final treated water, the sludge from coagulation, and the backwash waste from filtration. ### Step 1: Define the Streams and Variables Let: - $F_1$ = Raw water feed = **10,000 L/hr** - $F_2$ = Water leaving coagulation unit (entering filtration) - $F_3$ = Final treated water (leaving filtration) - $S_1$ = Sludge from coagulation - $S_2$ = Backwash waste from filtration ### Step 2: Apply Mass Balance at Coagulation Unit At the coagulation unit, **15% of $F_1$ is removed as sludge**: $$S_1 = 0.15 \times F_1$$ $$S_1 = 0.15 \times 10{,}000 = 1{,}500 \text{ L/hr}$$ Water entering filtration: $$F_2 = F_1 - S_1 = 10{,}000 - 1{,}500 = 8{,}500 \text{ L/hr}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Mass Balance at Filtration Unit At the filtration unit, **5% of $F_2$ is removed as backwash waste**: $$S_2 = 0.05 \times F_2 = 0.05 \times 8{,}500 = 425 \text{ L/hr}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate Final Treated Water $$F_3 = F_2 - S_2 = 8{,}500 - 425 = 8{,}075 \text{ L/hr}$$ ### Step 5: Verification Using Overall Mass Balance $$\sum m_{\text{in}} = F_1 = 10{,}000 \text{ L/hr}$$ $$\sum m_{\text{out}} = F_3 + S_1 + S_2 = 8{,}075 + 1{,}500 + 425 = 10{,}000 \text{ L/hr} \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > The **overall mass balance** must always close perfectly. This is the **single most important sanity check** for any flow-diagram-based numerical problem in KTU exams. ### Final Result Table | Stream | Flow Rate (L/hr) | % of Feed | |---|---|---| | Raw water feed ($F_1$) | 10,000 | 100.00% | | Sludge from coagulation ($S_1$) | 1,500 | 15.00% | | Water to filtration ($F_2$) | 8,500 | 85.00% | | Backwash waste ($S_2$) | 425 | 4.25% | | **Final treated water ($F_3$)** | **8,075** | **80.75%** | ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Recycle Stream Calculation > **Problem Statement:** > In a reactor, fresh feed of **100 kmol/hr** of reactant A enters. The conversion per pass is **40%**. The unreacted A is **completely separated** and **recycled** back to mix with the fresh feed. Calculate: (a) the recycle stream flow rate, (b) the combined feed to the reactor, (c) the overall conversion of the process. ### Step 1: Define Variables - $F_{\text{fresh}}$ = Fresh feed of A = **100 kmol/hr** - $X_{\text{pass}}$ = Conversion per pass = **40% = 0.40** - $F_{\text{recycle}}$ = Recycle stream - $F_{\text{combined}}$ = Combined feed entering reactor - $F_{\text{exit}}$ = Stream leaving reactor ### Step 2: Mass Balance Across the Reactor The unreacted material leaving the reactor becomes the recycle stream: $$F_{\text{recycle}} = F_{\text{combined}} \times (1 - X_{\text{pass}})$$ $$F_{\text{recycle}} = F_{\text{combined}} \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.60 \cdot F_{\text{combined}}$$ ### Step 3: Mass Balance at the Mixer (before reactor) The combined feed is the sum of fresh feed and recycle: $$F_{\text{combined}} = F_{\text{fresh}} + F_{\text{recycle}}$$ Substituting: $$F_{\text{combined}} = 100 + 0.60 \cdot F_{\text{combined}}$$ $$F_{\text{combined}} - 0.60 \cdot F_{\text{combined}} = 100$$ $$0.40 \cdot F_{\text{combined}} = 100$$ $$F_{\text{combined}} = \frac{100}{0.40} = 250 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate Recycle Stream $$F_{\text{recycle}} = 0.60 \times 250 = 150 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ ### Step 5: Verify the Reactor Output $$F_{\text{exit}} = F_{\text{combined}} \times X_{\text{pass}} = 250 \times 0.40 = 100 \text{ kmol/hr}$$ This is the **net product**, which equals the fresh feed (as expected for a steady-state recycle system with complete separation). ### Step 6: Calculate Overall Conversion For a recycle system with complete separation and recycle of unreacted material, the overall conversion is theoretically **100%** — every molecule of A eventually reacts. $$X_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\text{A reacted (overall)}}{\text{A fed (fresh)}} = \frac{100}{100} = 100\%$$ ### Final Result Table | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Fresh feed | 100 kmol/hr | | Conversion per pass | 40% | | Combined feed to reactor | 250 kmol/hr | | Recycle stream | 150 kmol/hr | | Net product | 100 kmol/hr | | Overall conversion | 100% | ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Coagulant Dosage Calculation > **Problem Statement:** > A municipality treats **5 million liters per day (MLD)** of water using **alum** at a dose of **30 mg/L**. Calculate the **daily consumption of alum** in kg. ### Step 1: Convert Units $$\text{Volume} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$ $$\text{Dose} = 30 \text{ mg/L}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Dosage Formula $$\text{Mass of alum per day} = \text{Volume} \times \text{Dose}$$ $$\text{Mass} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day} \times 30 \text{ mg/L}$$ $$\text{Mass} = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/day}$$ ### Step 3: Convert mg to kg Since $1 \text{ kg} = 10^6 \text{ mg}$: $$\text{Mass} = \frac{150 \times 10^6 \text{ mg/day}}{10^6 \text{ mg/kg}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Mass of alum} = 150 \text{ kg/day}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Flow Diagram Mistakes | # | Common Mistake | Correct Practice | Marks Lost | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drawing arrows in **both directions** between two boxes | Arrows are **unidirectional**; show net flow direction | 1–2 | | 2 | **Forgetting to label** streams (e.g., "raw water", "sludge") | Every major stream must have a name | 1–2 | | 3 | Drawing flow diagram **without a title** | Always write the process name at the top | 0.5–1 | | 4 | Confusing **water treatment** with **sewage treatment** in the diagram | Know the difference; water = purification, sewage = cleaning before discharge | 2–3 | | 5 | **No mention of recycle streams** where applicable (e.g., activated sludge in sewage treatment) | Draw recycle arrow back to previous unit | 1–2 | | 6 | **No operating conditions** mentioned | Add temperature, pressure, pH in parentheses for marks | 0.5–1 | | 7 | Drawing **input/output on the wrong side** of the box | Standard convention: input from left, output to right | 0.5 | | 8 | Skipping **chemical equations** that accompany each unit | Write the key reaction (e.g., alum hydrolysis, chlorination) | 2–3 | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Municipal Water Treatment Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Water Source
River or Reservoir] --> B[Intake Pump Station] B --> C[Screening Unit
Removes large debris] C --> D[Aeration Tank
Removes dissolved gases and oxidizes Fe2+ Mn2+] D --> E[Coagulation and Flocculation Tank
Alum or FeCl3 added as coagulant] E --> F[Sedimentation Clarifier
Flocs settle as sludge] F --> G[Rapid Sand Filter
Removes remaining suspended particles] G --> H[Disinfection Unit
Chlorination with Cl2 gas] H --> I[Clear Water Storage Tank
Overhead reservoir] I --> J[Distribution Network
Supplied to consumers] F --> K[Sludge Treatment Unit
Dewatering and disposal] E --> K K --> L[Landfill or Sludge Disposal Site] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style D fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style E fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style F fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style G fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style H fill:#d5a6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 style I fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style K fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style L fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Sewage Treatment Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sewage Collection
From households and industry] --> B[Preliminary Treatment
Screening and grit removal] B --> C[Primary Clarifier
Sedimentation removes 50 to 70 percent suspended solids] C --> D[Secondary Biological Treatment
Aeration tank with activated sludge] D --> E[Secondary Clarifier
Biomass separation] E --> F[Tertiary Treatment
Nutrient removal N and P] F --> G[Disinfection
Chlorination] G --> H[Treated Effluent Discharge
Into river or sea] C --> I[Primary Sludge] E --> J[Secondary Sludge or Waste Activated Sludge] I --> K[Sludge Thickener] J --> K K --> L[Anaerobic Digester
Produces biogas CH4 plus CO2] L --> M[Biogas Storage and Utilization] L --> N[Digested Sludge Dewatering] N --> O[Landfill or Soil Conditioner] style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style D fill:#d5a6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 style E fill:#d5a6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 style F fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style G fill:#d5a6ff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 style H fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style J fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style K fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style L fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style M fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600,color:#000 style N fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 style O fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Flow Diagram Components ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Raw Material Input Stream] --> B[Unit Operation 1
Physical or chemical change] B --> C[Unit Operation 2
Separation or purification] C --> D[Unit Operation 3
Final treatment or finishing] D --> E[Desired Product Output Stream] B --> F[By product 1 Stream] C --> G[By product 2 Stream] D --> H[Waste Stream] E -.Recycle.-> B F -.Optional Recycle.-> A style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style D fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000 style F fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600,color:#000 style G fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600,color:#000 style H fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The dashed arrow labeled `Recycle` represents a **recycle stream** that loops material back to a previous unit. This is a critical feature in real industrial flow diagrams, especially for unreacted reactants in chemical reactors and for activated sludge in sewage treatment. ## 4.4 Reading the Diagram — Key Engineering Insights | Stage | Color Code | Real Significance | |---|---|---| | **Blue boxes** | Inputs/feeds | Material entering the process | | **Orange boxes** | Unit operations | Where transformation happens | | **Purple boxes** | Biological/chemical reaction | Where chemistry drives the process | | **Green boxes** | Final products/desired output | Material of value leaving the system | | **Yellow boxes** | By-products (recoverable) | Could be sold or reused | | **Red/Pink boxes** | Waste/sludge streams | Material requiring disposal | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **Define a flow diagram. List any four standard symbols used in flow diagrams with their meaning.** **Model Answer:** A **flow diagram** is a graphical representation of a chemical or industrial process that uses standardized symbols, arrows, and labeled streams to illustrate the sequence of unit operations, material flows, and transformations from raw material to final product. **Four standard symbols:** 1. **Rectangle / Box** → Represents a unit operation or unit process 2. **Arrow (→)** → Represents the direction of material flow 3. **Circle** → Represents a pump, compressor, or blower 4. **Cylinder** → Represents a storage tank or accumulator --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **Distinguish between the flow diagrams of a water treatment plant and a sewage treatment plant (any three points).** **Model Answer:** | # | Water Treatment Plant | Sewage Treatment Plant | |---|---|---| | 1 | Processes **raw water from natural sources** (rivers, lakes) to make it **potable** | Processes **wastewater** to make it **safe for discharge** into the environment | | 2 | Primary goal is **removal of turbidity, hardness, and pathogens** for **drinking** | Primary goal is **reduction of BOD, COD, and pathogens** for **environmental safety** | | 3 | Main steps: Screening → Aeration → Coagulation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Chlorination | Main steps: Screening → Primary sedimentation → Biological treatment → Secondary clarification → Disinfection | | 4 | Produces **potable water** for distribution to consumers | Produces **treated effluent** for safe disposal; generates **biogas** as a by-product | --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **Question A:** **(a)** Draw a neat, labeled flow diagram for the **municipal water treatment plant** used for the supply of drinking water. Mention the function of **any five** unit operations used in the process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During the chlorination step, **5 kg of chlorine gas (Cl₂)** is added per day to treat **2 million liters** of water. If the **chlorine demand** of the water is **1.8 mg/L**, calculate: **(i)** the **chlorine applied** in mg/L, (ii) the **chlorine residual** in mg/L, and (iii) whether the water is **safe for distribution** as per WHO standards (residual should be ≥ 0.2 mg/L and ≤ 0.5 mg/L). **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — Flow Diagram and Functions **Flow Diagram (to be drawn in the answer sheet):** ``` Raw Water Source ↓ [ Intake Pump ] ↓ [ Screening ] ↓ [ Aeration ] ↓ [ Coagulation-Flocculation (Alum added) ] ↓ [ Sedimentation (Clarifier) ] ↓ [ Rapid Sand Filtration ] ↓ [ Chlorination (Disinfection) ] ↓ [ Clear Water Storage ] ↓ [ Distribution ] ``` **Functions of any five unit operations:** 1. **Screening:** Removes large floating debris such as leaves, sticks, plastics, and rags using bar screens. 2. **Aeration:** Removes dissolved gases like H₂S and CO₂; oxidizes soluble Fe²⁺ and Mn²⁺ to insoluble hydroxides; also improves taste and reduces odor. 3. **Coagulation-Flocculation:** Addition of alum [KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O] or ferric chloride destabilizes colloidal impurities, which aggregate into larger flocs. 4. **Sedimentation:** Allows heavy flocs to settle under gravity in a clarifier; the settled sludge is removed for further treatment; clarified water overflows. 5. **Filtration:** Removes remaining suspended and colloidal particles by passing water through sand and gravel layers; produces crystal-clear water. 6. **Chlorination:** Adds Cl₂ to kill pathogenic bacteria and viruses through the formation of HOCl and nascent oxygen [O]. 7. **Storage:** Provides a buffer supply to meet fluctuating demand and allows time for any residual reactions to complete. **Chemical Reactions (for additional marks):** $$KAl(SO_4)_2 + 3H_2O \rightarrow Al(OH)_3 \downarrow + K^+ + 2SO_4^{2-} + 3H^+$$ $$Cl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow HOCl + HCl$$ $$HOCl \rightarrow HCl + [O]$$ #### Part (b) — Chlorination Numerical **Given data:** - Mass of Cl₂ applied per day = 5 kg/day = $5 \times 10^6$ mg/day - Volume of water treated per day = 2,000,000 L/day = $2 \times 10^6$ L/day - Chlorine demand = 1.8 mg/L **(i) Chlorine applied (mg/L):** $$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ applied} = \frac{\text{Mass of Cl}_2}{\text{Volume of water}} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \text{ mg}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ L}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Cl}_2 \text{ applied} = 2.5 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **(ii) Chlorine residual (mg/L):** The fundamental relationship is: $$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ residual} = \text{Cl}_2 \text{ applied} - \text{Cl}_2 \text{ demand}$$ $$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ residual} = 2.5 - 1.8$$ $$\boxed{\text{Cl}_2 \text{ residual} = 0.7 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **(iii) Safety Assessment as per WHO Standards:** WHO recommends that chlorine residual in drinking water should be: - **Minimum:** 0.2 mg/L (to ensure disinfection continues in the distribution network) - **Maximum:** 0.5 mg/L (to avoid taste and odor complaints) Our calculated residual = **0.7 mg/L > 0.5 mg/L** ❌ > **Conclusion:** The water is **NOT safe for distribution** as per WHO standards. The chlorine dose is too high and should be reduced. The plant operator must decrease the chlorine input so that the residual lies in the range 0.2–0.5 mg/L. **To find the correct applied dose for residual = 0.5 mg/L:** $$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ applied (corrected)} = 0.5 + 1.8 = 2.3 \text{ mg/L}$$ --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice ### [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4] **Question B:** **(a)** With the help of a neat, labeled flow diagram, explain the **sewage treatment process** highlighting the role of **primary, secondary, and tertiary treatment** stages. Mention **any two** chemical/biological reactions involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sewage treatment plant receives **8 million liters per day (MLD)** of wastewater. The **initial dissolved oxygen (DO)** is **8.0 mg/L** and the **DO after 5 days** is **2.0 mg/L**. Calculate: **(i)** the **5-day BOD (BOD₅)** of the wastewater, (ii) whether the wastewater is suitable for direct discharge into a river (standard BOD₅ for discharge should be < 30 mg/L), and (iii) the **percentage reduction in BOD** if secondary treatment removes **85% of the BOD**. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — Sewage Treatment Flow Diagram **Flow Diagram (to be drawn in the answer sheet):** ``` Sewage Influent ↓ [ Preliminary Treatment: Screening + Grit Removal ] ↓ [ Primary Treatment: Primary Clarifier (Sedimentation) ] ↓ (Primary Sludge) [ Secondary Treatment: Aeration Tank (Activated Sludge Process) ] ↓ [ Secondary Clarifier ] ↓ ↑ (Recycle of activated sludge) [ Tertiary Treatment: Nutrient Removal (N, P), Filtration ] ↓ [ Disinfection: Chlorination ] ↓ Treated Effluent (Discharge to River/Sea) ↓ Primary Sludge + Secondary Sludge → [ Sludge Thickener ] → [ Anaerobic Digester ] → Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) ``` **Stages Explained:** 1. **Primary Treatment (Physical):** Removes settleable suspended solids by gravity sedimentation in a primary clarifier. Removes **50–70%** of suspended solids and **25–40%** of BOD. 2. **Secondary Treatment (Biological):** Uses **aerobic microorganisms** in an aeration tank to oxidize dissolved organic matter into CO₂, H₂O, and new biomass. The mixed liquor flows to a secondary clarifier where biomass settles as **activated sludge**, which is partly recycled back to the aeration tank. 3. **Tertiary Treatment (Advanced):** Removes residual nutrients (nitrogen by nitrification-denitrification; phosphorus by chemical precipitation or biological uptake), and any remaining suspended solids by filtration. **Two Key Reactions:** $$(i) \text{ Nitrification: } NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} NO_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$ $$(ii) \text{ Anaerobic digestion of sludge: } \text{Organic matter} \xrightarrow{\text{Anaerobic bacteria}} CH_4 \uparrow + CO_2 \uparrow + H_2S$$ #### Part (b) — BOD Numerical **Given data:** - Volume of wastewater = 8 MLD = $8 \times 10^6$ L/day - Initial DO ($DO_i$) = 8.0 mg/L - DO after 5 days ($DO_f$) = 2.0 mg/L **(i) BOD₅ Calculation:** The BOD₅ is the **oxygen consumed** by microorganisms during 5 days of incubation at 20°C: $$BOD_5 = DO_i - DO_f$$ $$BOD_5 = 8.0 - 2.0$$ $$\boxed{BOD_5 = 6.0 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **(ii) Suitability for Discharge:** Standard for discharge into rivers: **BOD₅ < 30 mg/L** Our calculated BOD₅ = **6.0 mg/L < 30 mg/L** ✅ > **Conclusion:** The wastewater **IS suitable for direct discharge** into a river as per the BOD₅ standard. **(iii) BOD Reduction by Secondary Treatment:** If secondary treatment removes 85% of BOD: $$BOD_{\text{after}} = BOD_5 \times (1 - 0.85)$$ $$BOD_{\text{after}} = 6.0 \times 0.15$$ $$\boxed{BOD_{\text{after}} = 0.9 \text{ mg/L}}$$ **Percentage reduction:** $$\text{Reduction} = 85\% \text{ (as given)}$$ **Final BOD after secondary treatment = 0.9 mg/L**, which is **well within the discharge standard** of < 30 mg/L. --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Flow Diagram Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to label stream arrows** (e.g., writing "sludge", "treated water") — costs **1–2 marks** out of 14. > 2. **Drawing the boxes but not writing the chemical reaction** inside or below the unit operation — this is the **single most common mistake**. The examiner expects at least **one balanced chemical equation per process** (e.g., alum hydrolysis, chlorination). Cost: **2–3 marks**. > 3. **Confusing water treatment and sewage treatment sequences** — water treatment does NOT have an "aeration tank for biological oxidation" (it has aeration for gas removal and Fe²⁺ oxidation). Sewage treatment DOES have an aeration tank for biological BOD reduction. Mixing these two costs **3–4 marks**. > 4. **Numerical part: wrong unit conversion** — e.g., forgetting to convert kg to mg, or L/day to L/hr. Always write the unit-conversion step **explicitly**. > 5. **Not drawing the recycle stream** in sewage treatment (activated sludge recycle) — the examiner awards **dedicated marks** for the recycle arrow. > 6. **Missing the title of the diagram** — write "Flow Diagram for Municipal Water Treatment Plant" at the top. > 7. **Writing the answer as continuous paragraphs** instead of using **clear headings, sub-points, and a neatly drawn diagram** — presentation matters in KTU valuation. > 8. **Forgetting to state the WHO/standard limits** in numerical problems (e.g., 0.2–0.5 mg/L for chlorine residual, < 30 mg/L for BOD₅ discharge) — this is a **favourite examiner trap** to test student awareness of standards. ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Flow Diagrams (Module 4):** - ✅ A **flow diagram** is a **graphical map** of an industrial/chemical process showing **inputs, transformations, and outputs** using boxes and arrows. - ✅ Standard symbols: **Rectangle** = unit operation, **Arrow** = material flow, **Circle** = pump, **Cylinder** = storage tank. - ✅ Every KTU flow diagram must have a **title, labeled boxes, labeled arrows, and (optionally) operating conditions**. - ✅ **Municipal Water Treatment sequence:** Intake → Screening → Aeration → Coagulation-Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Chlorination → Storage → Distribution. - ✅ **Sewage Treatment sequence:** Screening → Primary Sedimentation → Secondary (Biological Aeration) → Secondary Clarification → Tertiary → Disinfection → Discharge. - ✅ **Coagulant chemistry:** Alum hydrolyzes to **gelatinous Al(OH)₃** which traps colloidal particles. - ✅ **Disinfection chemistry:** Cl₂ + H₂O → HOCl + HCl → HCl + [O] (nascent oxygen kills pathogens). - ✅ **Mass balance (steady-state):** $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}}$ — must always close perfectly in numerical problems. - ✅ **Chlorine residual = Chlorine applied − Chlorine demand.** WHO range: **0.2–0.5 mg/L**. - ✅ **BOD₅ = DO_initial − DO_final (after 5 days at 20°C).** Discharge standard: **< 30 mg/L** for surface water bodies. - ✅ **Recycle streams** must be shown with **dashed or curved arrows** looping back to a previous unit (very important in sewage treatment for activated sludge). - ✅ **Convention:** Gases on top, liquids in middle, solids/sludge at bottom of the flow diagram. - ✅ **Activated sludge** in sewage treatment is **partially recycled** back to the aeration tank to maintain a high microbial population. - ✅ **Anaerobic digestion of sludge** produces **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** as a useful by-product — a mark-winning point. - ✅ Always include **at least one chemical equation** in the answer for a 14-mark question to score full marks. - ✅ Always end the answer with a **clear conclusion or safety/economic statement** (e.g., "Thus the water is safe for distribution" or "Thus the wastewater meets discharge norms").

topics.title: Trickling Filter and UASB Process. Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GCCYT122 - CHEMISTRY FOR PHYSICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Trickling Filter and UASB Process. Solid Waste # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Trickling Filter — Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)** > A **Trickling Filter (TF)** is a fixed-film biological wastewater treatment reactor in which sewage is distributed over a bed of coarse, inert, highly porous media (rock, slag, or plastic). A *biofilm* of aerobic microorganisms (zoogleal film) grows on the media surface, and as the wastewater trickles down, organic matter is metabolised by the film using the natural updraft of oxygen supplied through the underdrain vent. It is also called a *biological bed* or *percolating filter*. ### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Strawberry Tower" Imagine a vertical tower filled with hollow strawberry planters stacked on top of each other. As you pour syrup (wastewater) from the top, the syrup rolls over the surface of every strawberry (microbial film). The strawberries "eat" the sugar (organic matter), and what drips out at the bottom is much cleaner. Meanwhile, air naturally rises from the bottom vents — exactly the same way oxygen is supplied in a trickling filter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A trickling filter is classified as a **secondary (biological) treatment unit** that follows primary sedimentation and is followed by secondary clarifier in a typical wastewater treatment train. --- ## 1.2 UASB Process — Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)** > The **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactor is a high-rate, suspended-growth, *anaerobic* wastewater treatment system. Influent is introduced at the bottom of the reactor, distributed uniformly by an inlet system, and flows **upward** through a dense *granular sludge blanket*. Anaerobic bacteria degrade the organic matter in the absence of molecular oxygen, producing **biogas** (CH₄ + CO₂) that is collected in a gas–solid–liquid (GSL) separator at the top. ### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Bubbler" Think of brewing a cup of Turkish coffee in a copper *cezve*. The dark, granular coffee grounds settle at the bottom. As you heat it, bubbles of aroma rise upward, pushing the lighter brewed coffee above, while a frothy "froth" layer sits on top capturing gases. In a UASB: - Coffee grounds = **granular anaerobic sludge** - Bubbles = **biogas (methane)** - Froth layer = **GLS (Gas–Liquid–Solid) separator dome** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** UASB is a **zero-aeration** process — it **does NOT require electricity for aeration** and yields a useful energy by-product (biogas). It is classified as a *tertiary / advanced secondary* biological treatment. --- ## 1.3 Solid Waste — Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)** > **Solid Waste (SW)** refers to any discarded material arising from human and animal activities that is essentially solid and is incapable of flowing like a liquid. It encompasses **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)**, industrial, biomedical, electronic (e-waste), construction & demolition, and agricultural residues. **Solid Waste Management (SWM)** is the discipline associated with the control of generation, storage, collection, transfer and transport, processing, and disposal of solid wastes. ### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garbage Sorting Mandir" A South Indian temple has *mandaps* (halls), each with a specific role: one hall for offerings, one for distribution, one for recycling flowers, and a final sacred disposal area. SWM follows a *5-stage mandap* hierarchy — **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Residual Disposal** — exactly mirroring how the temple responsibly handles floral waste. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The **5R Principle** (Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover) is the cornerstone of sustainable solid waste management as per the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** (India) — a frequently asked concept in KTU exams. ### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics The following values are KTU-recommended standard parameters frequently used in numericals: | Parameter | Trickling Filter | UASB Process | |---|---|---| | **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** | **≥ 2.0 mg/L** in effluent | **0 mg/L** (anaerobic) | | **BOD Removal Efficiency** | **60 – 90 %** | **60 – 80 %** | | **COD Removal Efficiency** | **50 – 85 %** | **65 – 90 %** | | **Energy Input** | Low (gravity-driven) | Negligible (produces energy) | | **Sludge Yield** | Moderate (excess biofilm sloughed) | Low (anaerobic yield is low) | # 2.
