# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Solution Policy:** All algebraic steps, numerical substitutions, and intermediate simplifications are shown explicitly. Multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — C/N Ratio Adjustment (Composting)

### Problem Statement
A municipal composting plant receives **400 kg/day** of food waste with a C/N ratio of **15:1** and **600 kg/day** of dry leaves with a C/N ratio of **80:1**. Determine the C/N ratio of the **blended feedstock** and recommend whether bulking is required.

### Step 1: Set Up Mass and Composition
Let waste A (food) = 400 kg/day, C/N_A = 15.
Let waste B (leaves) = 600 kg/day, C/N_B = 80.

Let $C_A, N_A$ be the carbon and nitrogen masses in food waste.
Let $C_B, N_B$ be the carbon and nitrogen masses in leaves.

### Step 2: Assume a Basis for N (Easiest Path)
Assume the food waste contributes a nitrogen content. A typical food waste has C ≈ **50%** and N ≈ **3.3%** of fresh mass (this gives C/N ≈ 15/1). We will derive N from the ratio:

$$
C_A = 15 \, N_A \quad \text{and} \quad C_B = 80 \, N_B
$$

Also, assuming typical dry-basis values, food waste has $C_A = 0.50 \times 400 = 200 \text{ kg}$, so:

$$
N_A = \frac{C_A}{15} = \frac{200}{15} = 13.333 \text{ kg}
$$

For leaves, $C_B = 0.50 \times 600 = 300 \text{ kg}$ (approx.), so:

$$
N_B = \frac{C_B}{80} = \frac{300}{80} = 3.75 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Carbon and Total Nitrogen

$$
C_\text{total} = C_A + C_B = 200 + 300 = 500 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
N_\text{total} = N_A + N_B = 13.333 + 3.75 = 17.083 \text{ kg/day}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Blended C/N Ratio

$$
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_\text{blend} = \frac{C_\text{total}}{N_\text{total}} = \frac{500}{17.083} \approx 29.27
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

$$
29.27 \approx 29{:}1
$$

This lies within the optimal range of **25:1 to 35:1**, so **no further adjustment** is needed. The blend is ideal.

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut (Weighted C/N formula):** When C and N percentages are known, use $C/N_\text{blend} = \frac{\sum m_i C_i}{\sum m_i N_i}$. This avoids solving for absolute masses.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stoichiometric Air for Incineration

### Problem Statement
A municipal solid waste has the following ultimate (elemental) analysis by mass:
- Carbon (C): **35%**
- Hydrogen (H): **5%**
- Oxygen (O): **25%**
- Sulfur (S): **1%**
- Nitrogen (N): **1%**
- Moisture + Ash + Cl: balance (**33%**, inert)

Calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen demand** (kg O₂ per kg waste) and the **stoichiometric air** (Nm³ per kg waste).

### Step 1: Apply the Stoichiometric O₂ Formula

$$
n_{O_2} = c + \frac{h}{4} + s - \frac{o}{2} \quad \text{(kg O}_2\text{/kg waste)}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.35 + \frac{0.05}{4} + 0.01 - \frac{0.25}{2}
$$

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.35 + 0.0125 + 0.01 - 0.125
$$

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.2475 \text{ kg O}_2\text{/kg waste}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Moles

$$
\text{moles of O}_2 = \frac{0.2475 \times 1000}{32} = 7.734 \text{ mol/kg waste}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Volume (STP, 0 °C, 1 atm)

$$
V_{O_2} = 7.734 \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.17324 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Stoichiometric Air (Air = 21% O₂ by volume)

$$
A_s = \frac{V_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.17324}{0.21} = 0.8250 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

### Step 6: Express in Mass Terms (Optional)

$$
A_s(\text{kg/kg}) = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{0.2475}{0.232} = 1.067 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_s \approx 0.825 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste} \approx 1.07 \text{ kg air/kg waste}}
$$

### Step 7: Actual Air with 80% Excess

$$
A_\text{actual} = 1.80 \times A_s = 1.80 \times 0.825 = 1.485 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Combustion Efficiency from Flue Gas Analysis

### Problem Statement
A municipal incinerator flue gas shows: **CO₂ = 9.8%**, **CO = 0.05%**, **O₂ = 8.2%**, **N₂ = 82.95%** (dry basis). Determine the **dry-basis combustion efficiency** $\eta_c$ and comment on the result.

### Step 1: Apply the Combustion Efficiency Formula

$$
\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
\eta_c = \frac{9.8}{9.8 + 0.05} \times 100\% = \frac{9.8}{9.85} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3: Compute

$$
\eta_c = 0.99492 \times 100\% = 99.49\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
\eta_c = 99.49\% \quad (\text{Target} \geq 99.5\%)
$$

The incinerator is **just below the EPA benchmark**. Slight improvement in air-fuel mixing (turbulence) or reduced excess air is needed.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE) for a Hazardous Constituent

### Problem Statement
A hazardous waste incinerator feeds **200 kg/h** of a principal organic hazardous constituent (POHC). The stack gas after APC devices emits **0.012 kg/h** of the same POHC. Calculate the DRE.

### Step 1: Apply the DRE Formula

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{W_\text{in} - W_\text{out}}{W_\text{in}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{200 - 0.012}{200} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3: Compute

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{199.988}{200} \times 100\% = 99.994\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

$$
99.994\% < 99.99\% \text{ threshold is NOT met (it equals "four-nines")}
$$

Wait — **99.994% is greater than 99.99%**, so it **does meet** the EPA four-nines threshold. The student should double-check threshold comparisons carefully.

$$
\boxed{\text{DRE} = 99.994\% \;\;\text{(meets the 99.99\% standard)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** DRE = 99.994% is **above** 99.99% (which equals 99.9900%). The student must compare to **exactly** 99.99%, not round off. Always retain at least 4–5 significant figures for DRE calculations.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Landfill Methane Generation & CO₂-equivalent

### Problem Statement
A landfill contains **50,000 tonnes** of waste, of which **60%** is biodegradable volatile solids. By a first-order decay model with $k = 0.05 \text{ yr}^{-1}$ and $L_0 = 100 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{tonne}$ VS, estimate the **methane generated in the first year** and the **CO₂-equivalent emissions** if all of it escapes.

### Step 1: Mass of Volatile Solids

$$
M_{VS} = 50{,}000 \times 0.60 = 30{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### Step 2: Annual Methane (First-Order Decay)

$$
M_\text{CH}_4(1) = M_{VS} \times L_0 \times (1 - e^{-k t})
$$

For $t = 1$ yr:

$$
M_\text{CH}_4 = 30{,}000 \times 100 \times (1 - e^{-0.05 \times 1})
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Exponential

$$
e^{-0.05} = 0.95123
$$

$$
(1 - e^{-0.05}) = 0.04877
$$

### Step 4: Substitute

$$
M_\text{CH}_4 = 30{,}000 \times 100 \times 0.04877 = 146{,}310 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4
$$

### Step 5: Convert to Mass (CH₄ density at STP = 0.716 kg/m³)

$$
m_\text{CH}_4 = 146{,}310 \times 0.716 = 104{,}758 \text{ kg} \approx 104.76 \text{ tonnes}
$$

### Step 6: Convert to CO₂-equivalent (GWP₁₀₀ = 28)

$$
E_{CO_2e} = 104.76 \times 28 = 2{,}933.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_\text{CH}_4 \approx 146{,}310 \text{ m}^3 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 104.76 \text{ tonnes} \;\;\to\;\; \approx 2{,}933 \text{ t CO}_2\text{e in 1 year}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Volume and Mass Reduction in Incineration

### Problem Statement
A batch incinerator processes **10 m³ of loose MSW (density 150 kg/m³)**. The bottom ash produced is **0.6 m³ (density 850 kg/m³)**. Calculate the **volume reduction** and the **mass reduction** (assuming 25% of mass is in fly ash and not counted in bottom ash).

### Step 1: Initial Mass

$$
m_\text{in} = 10 \times 150 = 1{,}500 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2: Bottom Ash Mass

$$
m_\text{ash} = 0.6 \times 850 = 510 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3: Volume Reduction

$$
VR = \left(1 - \frac{V_\text{ash}}{V_\text{in}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{0.6}{10}\right) \times 100\% = 94\%
$$

### Step 4: Mass Reduction

$$
MR = \left(1 - \frac{m_\text{ash}}{m_\text{in}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{510}{1500}\right) \times 100\% = 66\%
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

- **Volume reduced by 94%** — the headline figure used by incinerator operators.
- **Mass reduced by 66%** — the remaining 34% is mostly water that evaporated and CO₂ released; the bottom ash is just a small fraction of the original mass.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip:** *Volume reduction (90%+) always exceeds mass reduction (70%) in incineration, because the gas-phase products (CO₂, H₂O vapor) leave with the flue gas.*

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection at a Glance

| Waste Property | Best Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| High organic (> 60%), low LHV | Composting | Energy content too low; biology preferred |
| High LHV (> 1,500 kcal/kg), low organic | Incineration | Volumetric + energy recovery efficient |
| Heterogeneous mixed MSW with no separation | Sanitary Landfill | Most flexible, no pretreatment needed |
| Clinical / hazardous waste | High-temperature Incineration (1100 °C) + DRE | Pathogen + toxic destruction |
| Sludge, market waste, food waste | Anaerobic Digestion | Biogas + digestate dual benefit |
| Construction & demolition | Recycling + Landfill (C&D cell) | Combustion gives little energy; high ash |
| E-waste | Specialized recycling (NOT landfill/incineration) | Toxic metals, valuable materials |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme pattern. Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice, 7+7 sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
Define **composting**. List the **three key parameters** that govern aerobic composting and state the **optimum value of C/N ratio**.

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-driven):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Composting is the **controlled aerobic biological decomposition** of organic solid waste by microorganisms (bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi) under thermophilic conditions, yielding a humus-like product called **compost**.
- **Three Key Parameters (1 mark):**
  1. **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) ratio** of feed
  2. **Moisture content** of the pile
  3. **Aeration / Oxygen supply** (and resultant temperature)
- **Optimum C/N (1 mark):** **25:1 to 35:1** (canonical optimum = **30:1**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "C/N ratio of compost" instead of "C/N ratio of the feed." The C/N ratio of **mature compost** is **10:1 to 15:1**, much lower than the feed. Make sure to specify "of feed" or "of the substrate."

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
Differentiate between **sanitary landfill** and **open dumpsite**. List **two engineering features** of a sanitary landfill that are absent in an open dump.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sanitary Landfill | Open Dump |
|---|---|---|
| **Liner system (HDPE + clay)** | Present | Absent (waste in direct contact with soil) |
| **Leachate collection & treatment** | Present | Leachate freely percolates to groundwater |
| **Daily soil cover** | Yes (15 cm) | None (scavengers, flies, fires) |
| **Landfill gas (LFG) capture** | Yes (flared or utilized) | Fugitive CH₄ emissions |
| **Site selection & design** | Engineered (geology, hydrogeology) | Often in low-lying, unplanned sites |
| **Closure & post-closure care** | Mandatory (cap, monitoring, 15–30 yr) | None |

**Any two of the above features carry 1.5 marks each.** Common KTU accepted answers: (1) bottom liner system; (2) leachate collection; (3) LFG management.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A sanitary landfill is **not** "a pit where waste is buried." It is an **engineered structure with engineered barriers.** The examiner expects at least one barrier-related feature (liner, gas collection, leachate system) for full marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **process of aerobic composting** with reference to the **biochemical phases**, the **C/N ratio** and its significance, the **role of temperature**, and the **operational methods** (windrow, in-vessel, aerated static pile).

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen demand** and **stoichiometric air requirement** for an MSW sample with the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): C = **28%**, H = **4%**, O = **22%**, S = **0.5%**, N = **1.0%**, Moisture + Ash = **44.5%**. Assume the waste is combusted in an incinerator with **100% excess air**. What is the **actual air supplied per kg waste**?

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice 1)

#### Part (a) — Aerobic Composting [7 marks]

**(i) Biochemical Phases (2 marks)**
- **Active Phase (Mesophilic → Thermophilic, 20–65 °C, 3–6 weeks):**
  - Microbes consume soluble substrates (sugars, starches, proteins, amino acids).
  - Heat is released; temperature rises from ambient to **45–65 °C**.
  - Thermophilic microbes (actinomycetes, *Bacillus* spp.) dominate.
  - Pathogens, weed seeds, and fly larvae are destroyed.
- **Curing / Maturation Phase (mesophilic, ambient, 4–12 weeks):**
  - Slow degradation of lignin and humic precursors.
  - Final C/N ratio drops to **10:1 to 15:1**.
  - Mature, stable humus is produced.

**(ii) C/N Ratio and Its Significance (2 marks)**

The C/N ratio is the **ratio of total mass of carbon to total mass of nitrogen** in the feed.

$$
\left(\frac{C}{N}\right)_\text{feed} = \frac{m_C}{m_N}
$$

- **Optimum:** **25:1 to 35:1** (ideal = **30:1**).
- If C/N > 35:1, microbes are **nitrogen-limited** → slow composting, N immobilization.
- If C/N < 20:1, excess N is released as **ammonia (NH₃)** → odor, pH rise, N loss.
- Mature compost has C/N ≈ **10:1**, suitable for soil incorporation.

**(iii) Role of Temperature (1.5 marks)**
- Temperature rise is **direct evidence** of microbial activity.
- Thermophilic range (45–65 °C) destroys pathogens and weed seeds.
- > 70 °C inhibits most microbes; **pile must be turned** to cool.
- Temperature profile is used as the **primary control parameter** in monitoring.

**(iv) Operational Methods (1.5 marks)**

| Method | Description | Merits |
|---|---|---|
| **Windrow** | Long trapezoidal piles; turned periodically with a loader | Low cost; simple |
| **In-vessel** | Closed drum / silo / tunnel reactor | Fastest; better odor control |
| **Aerated Static Pile** | Pipes embedded for forced air; no turning | Mechanized; large scale |
| **Vermicomposting** | Earthworm (*Eisenia foetida*) action | Excellent for kitchen waste; low energy |

---

#### Part (b) — Stoichiometric Air Calculation [7 marks]

**Given (dry basis):**
- C = 28% → $c = 0.28$
- H = 4% → $h = 0.04$
- O = 22% → $o = 0.22$
- S = 0.5% → $s = 0.005$
- N = 1.0% → does not enter O₂ demand

**Step 1: Stoichiometric O₂ Demand per kg waste (1 mark)**

$$
n_{O_2} = c + \frac{h}{4} + s - \frac{o}{2}
$$

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.28 + \frac{0.04}{4} + 0.005 - \frac{0.22}{2}
$$

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.28 + 0.010 + 0.005 - 0.110
$$

$$
n_{O_2} = 0.185 \text{ kg O}_2\text{/kg waste}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to Moles (1 mark)**

$$
\text{moles of O}_2 = \frac{0.185 \times 1000}{32} = 5.781 \text{ mol/kg waste}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of O₂ at STP (1 mark)**

$$
V_{O_2} = 5.781 \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1295 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

**Step 4: Stoichiometric Air (1 mark)**

$$
A_s = \frac{V_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.1295}{0.21} = 0.6167 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

**Step 5: Air in Mass Terms (1 mark)**

$$
A_s(\text{kg/kg}) = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{0.185}{0.232} = 0.7974 \text{ kg air/kg waste}
$$

**Step 6: Actual Air with 100% Excess (2 marks)**

$$
A_\text{actual} = 2.00 \times A_s = 2 \times 0.6167 = 1.2334 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_\text{actual} \approx 1.23 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/kg waste} \;\;(\text{or } \approx 1.59 \text{ kg air/kg waste})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Many students forget to **subtract the oxygen already present in the waste** (the $-o/2$ term). This causes a *systematic over-estimate* of O₂ demand.
> **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** **100% excess air** means actual air = 2 × stoichiometric. Some students misinterpret "excess air" as an absolute value; it is a **ratio** (EAR = 2).
> **Examiner's Pitfall 3:** Confusing mass and volumetric units. Be consistent: state both Nm³/kg and kg/kg.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Alternative for Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Describe a **sanitary landfill** with a neat sketch (describe in words) of its components. Explain the **five degradation phases** (Tchobanoglous model) and the **composition of landfill gas (LFG)**. Discuss **two methods of LFG management** (capture & utilization / flaring / energy recovery).

**(b) [7 marks]** A municipal incinerator operates at a flue gas composition (dry basis) of **CO₂ = 10.2%**, **CO = 0.04%**, **O₂ = 7.5%**, **N₂ = 82.26%**.
- (i) Determine the **dry-basis combustion efficiency** $\eta_c$.
- (ii) The incinerator feeds a POHC at **50 kg/h** and the stack emits **0.0008 kg/h** of the same. Calculate the **DRE** and state whether the **EPA "four-nines"** standard is met.
- (iii) Comment on the role of **excess air** and the **3 T's** in maintaining $\eta_c$ above 99.5%.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice 2)

#### Part (a) — Sanitary Landfill [7 marks]

**(i) Components of a Sanitary Landfill (2.5 marks)**

A sanitary landfill is composed of the following elements (from top to bottom):

1. **Final Cover System (Closure Cap):** Multi-layer — top soil + clay + geomembrane + drainage layer + gas venting layer.
2. **Gas Collection System:** Vertical wells (or horizontal trenches) embedded in the waste, connected to a manifold header.
3. **Waste Mass:** Compacted waste in cells, with **daily cover of 15 cm** soil (or 6 inches alternative cover).
4. **Leachate Collection System:** Perforated pipes within a granular blanket, sloping to a sump.
5. **Composite Liner System:** **HDPE geomembrane (1.5–2.5 mm)** overlying a **compacted clay liner (≥ 0.6 m, $k < 1 \times 10^{-9}$ m/s)**.
6. **Groundwater Monitoring Wells:** Up-gradient and down-gradient, sampled semi-annually.
7. **Leachate Treatment Plant:** Biological + physico-chemical treatment before discharge.

**(ii) Five Degradation Phases — Tchobanoglous Model (3 marks)**

| Phase | Duration | Dominant Process | pH | Gas |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Initial Adjustment** | Days–weeks | Aerobic decomposition of readily biodegradable matter | 6–7 | CO₂ |
| **II — Transition** | Weeks–months | Hydrolysis begins; O₂ depletion | 6 | CO₂ ↑, CH₄ trace |
| **III — Acid Formation** | Months–years | Acidogenesis; VFAs accumulate | 5–6 | CO₂ high, CH₄ low |
| **IV — Methane Fermentation** | 5–50 years | Methanogenesis dominates | 7–8 | **~50% CH₄ + 50% CO₂** |
| **V — Final Maturation** | Decades | Slow decline, humification | 7–8 | CO₂ > CH₄ |

**(iii) LFG Composition and Management (1.5 marks)**

**LFG Composition:**
- **CH₄:** 40–60% (typical 50%)
- **CO₂:** 40–60% (typical 50%)
- **Trace:** H₂S, NMOCs, VOCs, siloxanes, mercury vapor

**Two LFG Management Methods:**
1. **Flaring:** Collected LFG is combusted in an enclosed flare, converting CH₄ to CO₂ (GWP reduced from 28 to 1).
2. **Energy Recovery:** LFG is dried, filtered, and burned in a gas engine or gas turbine to generate **electricity** (typical yield: **1–2 MWh per 1,000 m³ of LFG**). Alternatively, upgraded LFG can be injected into a natural-gas grid after CO₂ and H₂S removal.

---

#### Part (b) — Combustion Calculations [7 marks]

**(i) Combustion Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$
\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}]} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_c = \frac{10.2}{10.2 + 0.04} \times 100\% = \frac{10.2}{10.24} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta_c = 99.61\%
$$

**Above the 99.5% benchmark — combustion is satisfactory.**

**(ii) Destruction and Removal Efficiency (3 marks)**

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{W_\text{in} - W_\text{out}}{W_\text{in}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{50 - 0.0008}{50} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{49.9992}{50} \times 100\% = 99.9984\%
$$

**Comparison with EPA "four-nines" (99.9900%):**

$$
99.9984\% > 99.9900\% \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DRE} = 99.9984\% \;\;\text{(exceeds the 99.99\% EPA threshold)}}
$$

**(iii) Role of Excess Air and the 3 T's (2 marks)**

- **Excess Air (EAR = 1.6–2.0 typical):** Sufficient O₂ ensures complete combustion; insufficient air leads to **CO and soot** (lowering $\eta_c$); too much air **cools the chamber** and increases flue gas volume (lowering heat recovery).
- **The 3 T's:**
  - **Temperature (≥ 850 °C):** Ensures destruction of dioxins and POHCs.
  - **Time (≥ 2 s residence at ≥ 850 °C):** Allows complete oxidation.
  - **Turbulence:** Adequate air-fuel mixing to eliminate pockets of incomplete combustion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — DRE Threshold Comparison:** A common error is to write "99.9984% is below 99.99%". This is **false** because 99.9984% > 99.99%. Always carry 4+ decimal places and compare numerically.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Universal Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 reasons students lose marks on this topic (compiled from KTU 2023 valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "open dump" with "sanitary landfill"** — Always mention *engineered liner*, *leachate collection*, and *gas capture*.
> 2. **Writing "C/N ratio of compost = 30:1"** — That is the *feed* value; *finished* compost is **10:1 to 15:1**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $-o/2$ term in the stoichiometric O₂ formula** — Over-estimates O₂ demand.
> 4. **Treating "100% excess air" as "EAR = 1.0"** — It is **EAR = 2.0**.
> 5. **DRE comparison rounding errors** — Always keep 4 decimal places.
> 6. **Listing incineration as a "renewable energy" process** — It is *energy recovery* from waste; the CO₂ emitted is mostly **biogenic** (counted separately in carbon accounting) but the process is *not* classified as renewable in the strict sense.
> 7. **Missing the "3 T's" (Temperature, Time, Turbulence)** — This is the single most-asked 7-mark sub-question in ESE for Module 4.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this twice before the exam.**

### A. Composting
- ✔️ Composting = **aerobic** biological decomposition → product = **compost / humus**
- ✔️ Optimum **C/N = 25–35 (ideal 30)**; moisture = **50–60%**; O₂ > **5%**
- ✔️ Two phases: **Active (thermophilic, 45–65 °C)** → **Curing (maturation)**
- ✔️ Methods: **Windrow, In-vessel, Aerated Static Pile, Vermicomposting**
- ✔️ Vermicomposting agent: **Eisenia foetida** (red wiggler)
- ✔️ Anaerobic variant = **anaerobic digestion (AD)** → **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**
- ✔️ Stoichiometry: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$

### B. Landfill
- ✔️ Engineered structure with **composite liner (HDPE + clay)** and **leachate + gas collection**
- ✔️ **Daily cover = 15 cm soil**; final cover is multi-layer
- ✔️ **Five phases (Tchobanoglous):** Initial → Transition → Acid → Methane → Maturation
- ✔️ **LFG ≈ 50% CH₄ + 50% CO₂**; CH₄ GWP₁₀₀ = **28**; LEL = **5% v/v**
- ✔️ Leachate BOD₅ in young landfill = **5,000–30,000 mg/L**
- ✔️ LFG calorific value ≈ **18 MJ/m³**; **flaring or electricity generation** are management options
- ✔️ Landfill is **not** a simple dump; it is an *engineered bioreactor*

### C. Incineration
- ✔️ Combustion of waste at **≥ 850 °C**, residence time **≥ 2 s**
- ✔️ **3 T's:** Temperature, Time, Turbulence
- ✔️ Stoichiometric O₂: $n_{O_2} = c + h/4 + s - o/2$ (kg/kg)
- ✔️ Stoichiometric air: $A_s \approx 4.31 \times n_{O_2}$ (Nm³/kg)
- ✔️ **Excess air ratio (EAR) = 1.6–2.0** typical
- ✔️ **Combustion efficiency** $\eta_c \geq 99.5\%$
- ✔️ **DRE ≥ 99.99%** ("four nines") for hazardous waste
- ✔️ **Volume reduction ≈ 90%**, **Mass reduction ≈ 70%**
- ✔️ **APC train:** ESP → Scrubber → SNCR/SCR → Stack (with CEMS)
- ✔️ Dioxins, HCl, NOₓ, SO₂, Hg are the critical pollutants to control

### D. Comparative & Cross-Topic
- ✔️ Composting has **no** volume reduction; Incineration has the **highest** volume reduction
- ✔️ Landfill is the **last resort** in the waste hierarchy (after Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recovery)
- ✔️ CH₄ from landfill is **25–28 times** more potent as GHG than CO₂
- ✔️ Biogas & LFG are **complementary** — both are methane-rich, but from different feedstocks
- ✔️ **DRE and ηc** are the two key incinerator performance metrics

### E. KTU 2024 Likely "Trigger" Topics
- ✔️ Numerical on C/N ratio adjustment (3–7 marks)
- ✔️ Numerical on stoichiometric air for incineration (7 marks)
- ✔️ Numerical on DRE and ηc from flue gas data (7 marks)
- ✔️ Descriptive on 3 T's of combustion, phases of landfill, phases of composting (7 marks)
- ✔️ Comparison table of the three methods (3–7 marks)
- ✔️ Landfill gas composition & GWP calculation (3 marks)
